AI.conclusion: The study concludes that hallucinations in LLMs can be fruitfully modeled as phase transitions in a latent potential energy landscape. By leveraging sparse autoencoders and probing, HalluSAE offers a mechanistic, causal, and interpretable approach to hallucination detection that outperforms existing techniques, suggesting a promising direction for more controllable and reliable LLMs.

title: The Query Channel: Information-Theoretic Limits of Masking-Based Explanations
summary: Masking-based post-hoc explanation methods, such as KernelSHAP and LIME, estimate local feature importance by querying a black-box model under randomized perturbations. This paper formulates this procedure as communication over a query channel, where the latent explanation acts as a message and each masked evaluation is a channel use. Within this framework, the complexity of the explanation is captured by the entropy of the hypothesis class, while the query interface supplies information at a rate determined by an identification capacity per query. We derive a strong converse showing that, if the explanation rate exceeds this capacity, the probability of exact recovery necessarily converges to one in error for any sequence of explainers and decoders. We also prove an achievability result establishing that a sparse maximum-likelihood decoder attains reliable recovery when the rate lies below capacity. A Monte Carlo estimator of mutual information yields a non-asymptotic query benchmark that we use to compare optimal decoding with Lasso- and OLS-based procedures that mirror LIME and KernelSHAP. Experiments reveal a range of query budgets where information theory permits reliable explanations but standard convex surrogates still fail. Finally, we interpret super-pixel resolution and tokenization for neural language models as a source-coding choice that sets the entropy of the explanation and show how Gaussian noise and nonlinear curvature degrade the query channel, induce waterfall and error-floor behavior, and render high-resolution explanations unattainable.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes masking-based post-hoc explanation methods (like KernelSHAP and LIME) through an information-theoretic lens, modeling explanations as messages sent over a query channel, and derives fundamental limits on when accurate explanations are possible given a query budget.
AI.motivation: Masking-based explainers repeatedly query a black-box model on perturbed inputs to infer local feature importance, but there is little theoretical understanding of how many queries are fundamentally needed to reliably recover an explanation, or why common surrogate methods may fail even when, in principle, success is information-theoretically possible.
AI.method: The authors model the explanation process as communication over a noisy channel: the underlying explanation is a message, each masked model evaluation is a channel use, and the hypothesis class of explanations has an associated entropy. They define an identification capacity per query, derive coding-theoretic bounds (a strong converse and an achievability theorem) for exact recovery, construct a sparse maximum-likelihood decoder, and estimate mutual information via Monte Carlo to obtain non-asymptotic query benchmarks. They then empirically compare this optimal decoding to Lasso- and OLS-based estimators analogous to LIME and KernelSHAP, and analyze how design choices (e.g., super-pixel resolution, tokenization) and factors like Gaussian noise and model curvature affect the channel and explanation feasibility.
AI.result: They prove a strong converse: if the explanation rate (entropy per query) exceeds the identification capacity of the query channel, the probability of exactly recovering the explanation must go to one in error for any algorithm. They also prove achievability: below capacity, a sparse maximum-likelihood decoder can reliably recover the explanation. Using Monte Carlo mutual information estimates, they construct practical query budgets and show empirically that there is a region where information theory predicts reliable explanations are possible but standard convex approximation methods (Lasso, OLS surrogates of LIME/KernelSHAP) still fail. They further show that finer source encodings (more super-pixels, finer tokenization) increase explanation entropy, and that Gaussian noise and nonlinear model curvature degrade the effective channel, producing waterfall and error-floor effects that make high-resolution explanations unachievable in practice.
AI.conclusion: Post-hoc masking-based explanations can be rigorously framed as a communication problem, yielding information-theoretic capacities that bound when exact local explanations are in principle recoverable from a given query budget. This framework both explains observed empirical failures of popular methods such as LIME and KernelSHAP and shows that their limitations are often algorithmic rather than fundamental. It also clarifies the trade-off between resolution and reliability of explanations and highlights how noise and nonlinearity can fundamentally limit attainable explanation fidelity, guiding the design of future explanation methods and query interfaces.

title: SynopticBench: Evaluating Vision-Language Models on Generating Weather Forecast Discussions of the Future
comment: Accepted for presentation at Climate Informatics 2026
summary: Recent advances in visual-language models (VLMs) have led to significant improvements in a plethora of complex multimodal tasks like image captioning, report generation, and visual perception. However, generating text from meteorological data is highly challenging because the atmosphere is a chaotic system that is rapidly changing at various spatial and temporal scales. Given the complexity of atmospheric phenomena, it is critical to verifiably quantify the effectiveness of existing VLMs on weather forecasting data. In this work, we present SynopticBench, a high-quality dataset consisting of 1,367,041 text samples of Area Forecast Discussions created by the National Weather Service over the continental United States paired to images of 500mb geopotential height, 2 meter temperature, and 850mb wind velocity in weather forecasts. We also present Synoptic Phenomena Alignment and Coverage Evaluation (SPACE), a novel evaluation framework that can be used to effectively estimate the quality of text descriptions of synoptic weather phenomena. Extensive experiments on generating forecast discussions using state-of-the-art VLMs show the sensitivity of existing evaluation metrics in this domain and enable further exploration into synoptic weather and climate text generation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SynopticBench, a large multimodal benchmark pairing U.S. weather forecast discussions with synoptic-scale forecast maps, and SPACE, a new framework to evaluate how well models describe synoptic weather phenomena.
AI.motivation: Visual-language models are improving rapidly and are used for tasks like captioning and report generation, but it is unclear how well they handle meteorological forecast data, which is complex, chaotic, and highly dynamic. There is a need for a verifiable, domain-specific benchmark and evaluation method to assess and improve VLMs on synoptic weather and climate text generation.
AI.method: The authors construct SynopticBench by pairing 1.36M+ National Weather Service Area Forecast Discussions over the continental U.S. with corresponding forecast images for several key atmospheric fields (500mb geopotential height, 2m temperature, 850mb wind). They then design SPACE (Synoptic Phenomena Alignment and Coverage Evaluation), an evaluation framework that measures how well generated texts describe synoptic-scale weather phenomena, and run extensive experiments using state-of-the-art VLMs to generate forecast-style discussions from the weather maps and evaluate them with both standard metrics and SPACE.
AI.result: SynopticBench provides a large-scale, high-quality dataset for training and evaluating VLMs on synoptic meteorological forecasting language. Experiments reveal that standard text generation metrics are sensitive and may not adequately capture the quality of synoptic weather descriptions, while SPACE offers a more targeted view of model performance on relevant meteorological phenomena. The results expose strengths and weaknesses of current VLMs for this domain.
AI.conclusion: SynopticBench and the SPACE evaluation framework together establish a new benchmark for multimodal synoptic weather and climate text generation. They show that existing VLM evaluation practices are insufficient for this specialized domain and that more domain-aware metrics are needed. The work enables more rigorous assessment and future improvement of VLMs used in weather forecasting and climate applications.

title: RankGuide: Tensor-Rank-Guided Routing and Steering for Efficient Reasoning
summary: Large reasoning models (LRMs) enhance problem-solving capabilities by generating explicit multi-step chains of thought (CoT) reasoning; however, they incur substantial inference latency and computational overhead. To mitigate this issue, recent works have explored model collaboration paradigms, where small reasoning models (SRMs) generate intermediate reasoning steps to achieve a better accuracy--latency trade-off. Despite recent progress, effectively and efficiently detecting and mitigating SRM failures in collaborative systems remains a key challenge. To address this issue, we analyze SRM inference in both the generated text and hidden-state spaces, and identify three types of failure modes: \textit{overconfidence}, \textit{uncertainty}, and \textit{heavy revalidation}. Building on these insights, we propose \textbf{RankGuide}, a framework that improves the efficiency and effectiveness of SRM--LRM collaboration through tensor-rank-guided routing and steering. Specifically, RankGuide leverages a routing signal that incorporates tensor-rank signals derived from consecutive hidden states to detect when SRMs are likely to fail and selectively invoke LRMs. In addition, we introduce a tensor-rank-filtered steering vector extraction method to modulate the reasoning trajectory of SRMs, thereby improving their generation quality. By improving both routing and steering through tensor-rank signals, RankGuide enables SRM--LRM collaborative systems to achieve more efficient reasoning with fewer steps and improved accuracy. Experiments on multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate the efficacy of RankGuide in reducing latency by up to $1.75\times$ compared to LRM, while maintaining competitive accuracy relative to prior methods.

AI.tldr: RankGuide is a framework that uses tensor-rank signals from SRM hidden states to both route hard cases to large reasoning models and steer small reasoning models’ trajectories, improving accuracy–latency trade-offs in collaborative reasoning systems.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models achieve strong multi-step reasoning via chains-of-thought but are slow and expensive. Small reasoning models are cheaper but less reliable, and existing collaboration methods struggle to detect and correct their failures efficiently. The paper aims to systematically characterize SRM failure modes and develop a mechanism that can cheaply identify and mitigate these failures during inference.
AI.method: The authors empirically analyze SRM behavior in both text outputs and hidden states to identify three failure modes: overconfidence, uncertainty, and heavy revalidation. They then design RankGuide, which leverages tensor-rank metrics computed on consecutive hidden states as signals. These signals serve two roles: (1) routing—deciding when to escalate from an SRM to an LRM; and (2) steering—deriving tensor-rank-filtered steering vectors that nudge the SRM’s hidden-state trajectory toward more reliable reasoning, without always invoking the LRM.
AI.result: On multiple reasoning benchmarks, RankGuide-based SRM–LRM collaboration reduces inference latency by up to 1.75× compared with using only a large reasoning model, while preserving or improving accuracy relative to prior collaboration strategies. The approach leads to fewer reasoning steps and better quality SRM generations in practice.
AI.conclusion: Tensor-rank characteristics of hidden states are informative signals for both detecting impending SRM failures and steering SRM reasoning. Incorporating these signals into routing and steering yields a more efficient SRM–LRM collaboration framework that maintains strong reasoning accuracy while significantly lowering latency and computation costs.

title: EchoChain: A Full-Duplex Benchmark for State-Update Reasoning Under Interruptions
summary: Real-time voice assistants must revise task state when users interrupt mid-response, but existing spoken-dialog benchmarks largely evaluate turn-based interaction and miss this failure mode. We introduce EchoChain, a controlled benchmark for evaluating full-duplex state-update reasoning under mid-speech interruptions. EchoChain identifies three recurring failure patterns in post-interruption continuations: contextual inertia, interruption amnesia, and objective displacement. The benchmark generates scenario-driven conversations and injects interruptions at a standardized point relative to assistant speech onset, enabling controlled cross-model comparison. In a paired half-duplex control, total failures drop by 40.2% relative to interrupted runs, indicating that many errors are driven by state-update reasoning under interruption rather than task difficulty alone. Across evaluated real-time voice models, no system exceeds a 50% pass rate, showing substantial room for improvement in mid-generation state revision. EchoChain provides a reproducible benchmark for diagnosing state-update reasoning failures in full-duplex voice interaction.

AI.tldr: EchoChain is a benchmark to test how real-time voice assistants update task state when users interrupt them mid-response, revealing frequent reasoning failures and large room for improvement.
AI.motivation: Existing spoken-dialog benchmarks mostly assume turn-based (half-duplex) interaction and do not stress-test how assistants handle user interruptions during their own speech. In real-time voice settings, assistants must revise their internal task state on the fly when users interrupt, but current models often fail in subtle ways that are not captured by standard evaluations. The authors want a controlled, reproducible way to expose and compare these interruption-related failures across models.
AI.method: The authors design EchoChain, a controlled benchmark that synthesizes scenario-driven conversations specifically for full-duplex voice assistants. They inject user interruptions at a fixed, standardized moment relative to when the assistant starts speaking, so that comparisons across models are fair. They then categorize and measure three characteristic failure modes in the assistant’s post-interruption continuation—contextual inertia (sticking to the old plan), interruption amnesia (ignoring or forgetting the interruption), and objective displacement (pursuing the wrong objective after the interruption). They also run a paired half-duplex control condition without interruptions to isolate the effect of mid-speech state-update reasoning from general task difficulty.
AI.result: In the half-duplex (turn-based) control condition without interruptions, overall failure rates are 40.2% lower than in the interrupted, full-duplex setting, indicating that many errors are specifically tied to state updating under interruption rather than inherent task difficulty. When evaluating multiple real-time voice models on EchoChain, none achieves more than a 50% pass rate on the benchmark tasks, reflecting widespread deficiencies in mid-generation state revision across existing systems.
AI.conclusion: Mid-speech interruptions pose a major and underexplored challenge for real-time voice assistants, leading to systematic state-update reasoning failures that current benchmarks overlook. EchoChain offers a standardized, reproducible benchmark that surfaces three core failure patterns and enables controlled, cross-model comparison under full-duplex conditions. The overall low pass rates and large performance gap relative to half-duplex interactions show substantial scope for improving how voice assistants revise their internal state in response to interruptions.

title: Evaluating Tool-Using Language Agents: Judge Reliability, Propagation Cascades, and Runtime Mitigation in AgentProp-Bench
comment: 9 pages, 5 figures, 12 tables (8 main + 4 supplementary). Under review at Information Processing & Management. Code and data: https://github.com/bhaskargurram-ai/agenthallu-bench
summary: Automated evaluation of tool-using large language model (LLM) agents is widely assumed to be reliable, but this assumption has rarely been validated against human annotation. We introduce AgentProp-Bench, a 2,000-task benchmark with 2,300 traces across four domains, nine production LLMs, and a 100-label human-validated subset. We quantify judge reliability, characterize error propagation, and evaluate a runtime mitigation. Substring-based judging agrees with human annotation at kappa=0.049 (chance-level); a three-LLM ensemble reaches kappa=0.432 (moderate) with a conservative bias. Under validated evaluation, a parameter-level injection propagates to a wrong final answer with human-calibrated probability approximately 0.62 (range 0.46-0.73 across models). Rejection (catching bad parameters) and recovery (correcting after acceptance) are independent model capabilities (Spearman rho=0.126, p=0.747). A tuned runtime interceptor reduces hallucination on GPT-4o-mini by 23.0 percentage points under a concurrent n=600 control, but shows no significant effect on Gemini-2.0-Flash, whose aggressive parameter rejection eliminates the target failure mode. All code, data, traces, and human labels are released at https://github.com/bhaskargurram-ai/agenthallu-bench.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AgentProp-Bench to rigorously test how reliably automated systems can evaluate tool-using LLM agents, comparing LLM-based judges to human annotations and analyzing how parameter-level errors propagate to final answers, as well as testing a runtime defense against hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Automated evaluations of tool-using LLM agents are commonly trusted but rarely checked against careful human judgment, so their actual reliability, error patterns, and vulnerability to adversarial or erroneous tool parameters are poorly understood. This gap makes it hard to measure real agent robustness and to design effective mitigations.
AI.method: The authors build AgentProp-Bench, a 2,000-task benchmark with 2,300 agent traces covering four domains and nine production LLMs, and create a 100-trace subset with detailed human labels. They compare different automated judging strategies (e.g., substring match vs. LLM judges and a three-LLM ensemble) to human annotations using Cohen’s kappa, study how injected parameter-level errors propagate through agent reasoning to final answers, statistically analyze the relationship between rejection and recovery capabilities, and test a tuned runtime interceptor to reduce hallucinations on specific models under controlled experiments.
AI.result: Simple substring-based automatic judging is essentially no better than chance compared to humans (kappa=0.049), while a three-LLM ensemble achieves moderate agreement (kappa=0.432) but tends to be conservative. Under human-validated evaluation, injected parameter-level errors cause incorrect final answers with about 62% probability on average (ranging 0.46–0.73 across models). The ability to reject bad parameters and the ability to recover after accepting them are statistically independent (Spearman rho=0.126, p=0.747). A tuned runtime interceptor notably reduces hallucinations for GPT-4o-mini by 23 percentage points in a controlled n=600 study but has no significant effect on Gemini-2.0-Flash, which already rejects problematic parameters aggressively enough to avoid this failure mode.
AI.conclusion: Automated evaluation of tool-using LLM agents is far less reliable than commonly assumed unless carefully designed, with naïve metrics performing at chance and even ensemble LLM judges only moderately matching humans. Parameter-level errors frequently propagate to wrong final answers, and robustness involves distinct skills of rejection and recovery. Properly tuned runtime defenses can substantially reduce hallucinations for some models but may be redundant for models with strong parameter-rejection behavior. AgentProp-Bench and its released artifacts provide a standardized, human-validated testbed for studying and improving evaluation and mitigation strategies for agentic LLMs.

title: Revisiting a Pain in the Neck: A Semantic Reasoning Benchmark for Language Models
summary: We present SemanticQA, an evaluation suite designed to assess language models (LMs) in semantic phrase processing tasks. The benchmark consolidates existing multiword expression (MwE) resources and reorganizes them into a unified testbed. It covers both general lexical phenomena, such as lexical collocations, and three fine-grained categories: idiomatic expressions, noun compounds, and verbal constructions. Through SemanticQA, we assess LMs of diverse architectures and scales in extraction, classification, and interpretation tasks, as well as sequential task compositions. We reveal substantial performance variation, particularly on tasks requiring semantic reasoning, highlighting differences in reasoning efficacy and semantic understanding of LMs, providing insights for pushing LMs with stronger comprehension on non-trivial semantic phrases. The evaluation harness and data of SemanticQA are available at https://github.com/jacklanda/SemanticQA.

AI.tldr: SemanticQA is an evaluation benchmark for testing language models’ ability to understand and process multiword semantic phrases.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks do not sufficiently and systematically test language models on complex multiword expressions and semantic phrases, which are crucial for real-world language understanding. The authors aim to unify scattered MwE resources and provide a focused way to measure semantic reasoning and comprehension of such phrases.
AI.method: The authors consolidate multiple existing multiword expression resources and reorganize them into a single benchmark, SemanticQA. They structure it around general lexical phenomena (e.g., collocations) and three specific categories: idioms, noun compounds, and verbal constructions. They design tasks including extraction, classification, interpretation, and sequential compositions of these tasks. Then they evaluate language models of various architectures and sizes on these tasks.
AI.result: The evaluation shows large variation in model performance across tasks, with especially poor and inconsistent results on tasks that require deeper semantic reasoning about multiword expressions. Different architectures and scales show different strengths and weaknesses, revealing gaps in current models’ semantic understanding.
AI.conclusion: SemanticQA exposes significant limitations of current language models in handling non-trivial semantic phrases and multiword expressions, particularly in reasoning-heavy settings. The benchmark offers a unified, publicly available testbed and harness that can guide the development and comparison of future models with stronger semantic comprehension.

title: Debate as Reward: A Multi-Agent Reward System for Scientific Ideation via RL Post-Training
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated potential in automating scientific ideation, yet current approaches relying on iterative prompting or complex multi-agent architectures often suffer from hallucination or computational inefficiency. A critical bottleneck in applying Reinforcement Learning (RL) to this open-ended domain is reward hacking -- where models exploit imperfect evaluation proxies to maximize scores without producing genuine scientific innovation. To address these limitations, we propose an RL framework explicitly tailored for high-quality scientific idea generation. We propose the first multi-agent reward function designed to serve as a judge, decoupling methodological validation from implementation details while providing strict binary rewards that are robust to reward hacking. To effectively optimize against this sparse signal, we utilize an unbiased variant of Group Relative Policy Optimization to mitigate artificial length bias. We grounded our training in ICLR-320, a curated dataset of problem-solution pairs extracted from ICLR 2024 proceedings. Experiments demonstrate that our framework significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across expert-evaluated metrics of novelty, feasibility, and effectiveness.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a reinforcement learning framework with a specialized multi-agent reward-judge and a debiased policy optimization method to generate higher-quality, less-hackable scientific ideas, outperforming existing LLM-based ideation systems on expert-judged metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM approaches for scientific ideation often rely on iterative prompting or elaborate multi-agent setups, which are prone to hallucinations and computational overhead. Applying RL to this space faces a major challenge: reward hacking, where models game imperfect evaluation heuristics instead of producing genuinely innovative, feasible research ideas. There is a need for a principled RL framework and reward design that reliably evaluates and improves scientific ideation quality while remaining robust to such exploitation.
AI.method: The authors design a new RL framework tailored to scientific idea generation. Central is a multi-agent reward function that acts as a judge: it separates checking whether a method is valid from implementation specifics and issues strict binary rewards, making the signal simpler and more resistant to reward hacking. To cope with the resulting sparse reward signal, they employ an unbiased version of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO), which addresses artificial length bias during training. The model is trained on ICLR-320, a dataset of problem-solution pairs curated from ICLR 2024 papers, using this RL setup to refine idea generation policies.
AI.result: When evaluated on scientific ideation tasks, the proposed framework substantially outperforms strong baselines, including state-of-the-art LLM and RL approaches, on expert-assessed criteria of novelty, feasibility, and expected effectiveness of the proposed ideas. The experiments suggest that the binary, judge-style reward and debiased GRPO training lead to more genuinely innovative and practically grounded research ideas while reducing reward hacking artifacts.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed RL framework—with a multi-agent judge-style reward and an unbiased GRPO optimization strategy—can significantly improve LLM-based scientific idea generation while mitigating reward hacking. Grounding training in real problem-solution pairs from ICLR 2024 enables the system to generate ideas that are both novel and feasible. The results indicate that principled reward design and length-debiased policy optimization are key to robust, high-quality scientific ideation, offering a scalable alternative to prompt-only or ad hoc multi-agent methods.

title: Spotlights and Blindspots: Evaluation Machine-Generated Text Detection
summary: With the rise of generative language models, machine-generated text detection has become a critical challenge. A wide variety of models is available, but inconsistent datasets, evaluation metrics, and assessment strategies obscure comparisons of model effectiveness. To address this, we evaluate 15 different detection models from six distinct systems, as well as seven trained models, across seven English-language textual test sets and three creative human-written datasets. We provide an empirical analysis of model performance, the influence of training and evaluation data, and the impact of key metrics. We find that no single system excels in all areas and nearly all are effective for certain tasks, and the representation of model performance is critically linked to dataset and metric choices. We find high variance in model ranks based on datasets and metrics, and overall poor performance on novel human-written texts in high-risk domains. Across datasets and metrics, we find that methodological choices that are often assumed or overlooked are essential for clearly and accurately reflecting model performance.

AI.tldr: The paper benchmarks many machine-generated-text detectors on multiple datasets and metrics, showing that no detector is universally best and that evaluation choices heavily affect perceived performance, especially on challenging human-written creative and high‑risk texts.
AI.motivation: Existing work on detecting text generated by large language models is fragmented: different papers use different datasets, metrics, and evaluation protocols, making it difficult to compare detectors or understand when they work or fail. There is also practical concern about how reliably these detectors operate, particularly in sensitive, high‑risk applications and on creative human writing, where false positives or failures could have serious consequences.
AI.method: The authors systematically evaluate 15 detection models drawn from six existing detection systems plus seven additional trained models. They test these detectors on seven English machine-vs-human textual datasets and three datasets of creative, purely human-written text. They compare performance across a variety of metrics and carefully analyze how training data, evaluation data, and metric selection influence reported effectiveness and rankings of detectors.
AI.result: The evaluation shows large variability: no single detector dominates across all datasets, metrics, and tasks. Many detectors perform well on some benchmarks but poorly on others. Model rankings change substantially depending on which datasets and metrics are used. Performance is particularly weak on novel, human-written texts in high‑risk domains and on creative human writing. This reveals that existing detectors can be brittle and that headline metrics are highly sensitive to evaluation design choices.
AI.conclusion: Detector effectiveness for machine-generated text cannot be meaningfully summarized by a single score or benchmark. Evaluation design—including dataset selection, domain, and metric choice—strongly shapes perceived performance and rankings. Current detectors are not reliably effective on the most challenging and practically important settings, such as novel, creative human-written or high‑risk-domain texts. Careful, transparent methodological choices are essential for accurately characterizing and comparing detectors, and future work must prioritize robust, context-aware evaluation protocols.

title: When Agents Go Quiet: Output Generation Capacity and Format-Cost Separation for LLM Document Synthesis
summary: LLM-powered coding agents suffer from a poorly understood failure mode we term output stalling: the agent silently produces empty responses when attempting to generate large, format-heavy documents. We present a theoretical framework that explains and prevents this failure through three contributions. (1) We introduce Output Generation Capacity (OGC), a formal measure of an agent's effective ability to produce output given its current context state - distinct from and empirically smaller than the raw context window. (2) We prove a Format-Cost Separation Theorem showing that deferred template rendering is always at least as token-efficient as direct generation for any format with overhead multiplier $μ_f > 1$, and derive tight bounds on the savings. (3) We formalize Adaptive Strategy Selection, a decision framework that maps the ratio of estimated output cost to available OGC into an optimal generation strategy (direct, chunked, or deferred). We validate the theory through controlled experiments across three models (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o, Llama 3.1 70B), four document types, and an ablation study isolating each component's contribution. Deferred rendering reduces LLM generation tokens by 48-72% across all conditions and eliminates output stalling entirely. We instantiate the framework as GEN-PILOT, an open-source MCP server, demonstrating that the theory translates directly into a practical tool.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes and mitigates a failure mode in LLM coding agents called output stalling, where models silently stop producing content for large, structured documents. It proposes a theory of Output Generation Capacity (OGC), proves that deferred template rendering is token-optimal for most formats, and introduces an adaptive strategy selector. Experiments show large token savings and complete elimination of stalling, implemented in an open-source system, GEN-PILOT.
AI.motivation: LLM-based coding and tool-using agents frequently need to generate long, format-heavy artifacts (e.g., reports, codebases, documents). In practice, these agents sometimes stall: instead of finishing the output or raising an error, they silently emit empty or truncated responses, especially near context limits. This behavior is poorly understood and hard to debug. There is no principled framework to reason about how much output a model can still produce given its current context, nor how to structure generation strategies (direct vs. chunked vs. template-based) to avoid stalling while minimizing token usage. The paper aims to formally characterize this failure mode and provide theoretically grounded methods to prevent it and improve efficiency.
AI.method: 1) Define Output Generation Capacity (OGC) as a formal, operational measure of how many additional tokens a model can reliably generate given its current context state, distinguishing it from the raw context-window size. 2) Prove a Format-Cost Separation Theorem that compares two strategies for producing structured documents: direct generation of fully formatted text vs. deferred template rendering where the model emits a logical representation or content first and formatting is applied later. The theorem shows that, for any format whose overhead multiplier μ_f exceeds 1, deferred rendering is never worse and can be strictly more token-efficient; they derive tight bounds on token savings. 3) Develop an Adaptive Strategy Selection framework that, given an estimate of output cost and the available OGC, chooses among direct generation, chunked generation, and deferred rendering to avoid stalling and minimize token use. 4) Empirically validate the theory with controlled experiments spanning three different LLMs, multiple document types, and ablations that measure each component’s contribution. 5) Implement these ideas in a practical open-source tool, GEN-PILOT, as an MCP server that integrates the theoretical framework into real-world coding-agent workflows.
AI.result: Across Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o, and Llama 3.1 70B, and four different document types, using deferred rendering substantially reduces the number of tokens the LLM has to generate—by 48–72% in all tested settings—compared to direct formatted generation. More importantly, when the adaptive framework is applied, output stalling is eliminated in the experiments: the agents no longer silently fail when producing long, structured documents. The ablation study indicates that both the explicit modeling of OGC and the use of deferred rendering are key contributors to these gains. The practical instantiation, GEN-PILOT, confirms that the theoretical framework can be implemented as an operational system that improves robustness and efficiency in coding agents.
AI.conclusion: Output stalling in LLM-powered coding agents is not just a quirk but a systematic failure mode stemming from a mismatch between naive assumptions about context length and the model’s true Output Generation Capacity. By formally defining OGC, proving that deferred format rendering is token-optimal for realistic formats, and introducing an adaptive policy that chooses generation strategies based on estimated costs vs. available capacity, the paper both explains and resolves this problem in a principled way. Experiments demonstrate large, consistent token savings and complete removal of stalling across models and tasks, and the GEN-PILOT implementation shows that these ideas are directly usable in practice. This establishes a theoretical and practical foundation for building more reliable, efficient LLM agents that must produce long, structured outputs.

title: Aligning Backchannel and Dialogue Context Representations via Contrastive LLM Fine-Tuning
summary: Backchannels (e.g., `yeah', `mhm', and `right') are short, non-interruptive feedback signals whose lexical form and prosody jointly convey pragmatic meaning. While prior computational research has largely focused on predicting backchannel timing, the relationship between lexico-prosodic form and meaning remains underexplored. We propose a two-stage framework: first, fine-tuning large language models on dialogue transcripts to derive rich contextual representations; and second, learning a joint embedding space for dialogue contexts and backchannel realizations. We evaluate alignment with human perception via triadic similarity judgments (prosodic and cross-lexical) and a context-backchannel suitability task. Our results demonstrate that the learned projections substantially improve context-backchannel retrieval compared to previous methods. In addition, they reveal that backchannel form is highly sensitive to extended conversational context and that the learned embeddings align more closely with human judgments than raw WavLM features.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a framework to learn how the lexical and prosodic forms of backchannels (like “yeah”, “mhm”) map to their meanings in context, showing improved alignment with human judgments over previous approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing computational work mostly predicts *when* backchannels occur, not *which* backchannel form (lexical choice and prosody) is appropriate or what meaning it conveys. There is a need to model how different realizations of backchannels relate to conversational context and human perception of their pragmatic function.
AI.method: The authors propose a two-stage framework. First, they fine-tune large language models on dialogue transcripts to obtain context-sensitive representations of conversational turns. Second, they learn a joint embedding space that maps both dialogue contexts and concrete backchannel realizations (including lexical and prosodic information, e.g., via WavLM features) into a shared space. They then evaluate this space using triadic similarity judgments (prosodic and cross-lexical) and a context–backchannel suitability (retrieval) task, comparing to baselines such as raw WavLM features and earlier methods.
AI.result: The learned projections significantly improve performance on context–backchannel retrieval tasks versus prior methods. They also show that backchannel realizations are strongly influenced by extended conversational context, not just local cues, and that the joint embeddings correlate better with human similarity judgments than using raw prosodic features alone.
AI.conclusion: Jointly modeling dialogue context and the lexico-prosodic forms of backchannels in a shared embedding space better captures their pragmatic function. This approach aligns more closely with human perceptions of backchannel similarity and appropriateness, suggesting it can serve as a stronger basis for generating or selecting contextually suitable backchannels in dialogue systems than timing-based or purely acoustic-feature-based approaches.

title: CT Open: An Open-Access, Uncontaminated, Live Platform for the Open Challenge of Clinical Trial Outcome Prediction
summary: Scientists have long sought to accurately predict outcomes of real-world events before they happen. Can AI systems do so more reliably? We study this question through clinical trial outcome prediction, a high-stakes open challenge even for domain experts. We introduce CT Open, an open-access, live platform that will run four challenge every year. Anyone can submit predictions for each challenge. CT Open evaluates those submissions on trials whose outcomes were not yet public at the time of submission but were made public afterwards. Determining if a trial's outcome is public on the internet before a certain date is surprisingly difficult. Outcomes posted on official registries may lag behind by years, while the first mention may appear in obscure articles. To address this, we propose a novel, fully automated decontamination pipeline that uses iterative LLM-powered web search to identify the earliest mention of trial outcomes. We validate the pipeline's quality and accuracy by human expert's annotations. Since CT Open's pipeline ensures that every evaluated trial had no publicly reported outcome when the prediction was made, it allows participants to use any methodology and any data source. In this paper, we release a training set and two time-stamped test benchmarks, Winter 2025 and Summer 2025. We believe CT Open can serve as a central hub for advancing AI research on forecasting real-world outcomes before they occur, while also informing biomedical research and improving clinical trial design. CT Open Platform is hosted at $\href{https://ct-open.net/}{https://ct-open.net/}$

AI.tldr: The paper presents CT Open, an open, ongoing benchmarking platform for predicting clinical trial outcomes using AI and other methods, with careful safeguards against data leakage via a novel LLM-based web decontamination pipeline.
AI.motivation: Predicting real-world outcomes, especially clinical trial results, is crucial but remains difficult even for human experts. Existing work on AI forecasting often suffers from data leakage, where systems inadvertently use information published after a prediction should have been made. Clinical trials are a particularly high-stakes, impactful domain where better forecasting could guide research investment, trial design, and patient care. However, building a fair, live benchmark requires knowing which trial outcomes were truly unknown at prediction time, which is challenging because outcome information appears at different times across registries, papers, and web sources.
AI.method: The authors design CT Open, a live, open-access platform running four forecasting challenges per year on ongoing or recently completed clinical trials. They build an automated decontamination pipeline that uses iterative LLM-powered web search to find the earliest public mention of a trial’s outcome across the internet. This pipeline determines whether a trial’s outcome was publicly available before a given cutoff date. The system labels trials as valid for evaluation only if no public outcome information existed before participants submitted predictions. Human experts annotate and audit a subset of cases to validate the accuracy and robustness of the pipeline.
AI.result: CT Open provides: (1) a curated training dataset of clinical trials with verified outcome publication timelines, and (2) two time-stamped test benchmarks (Winter 2025 and Summer 2025) constructed to ensure no outcome leakage at the time of prediction. The automated decontamination pipeline successfully identifies earliest outcome mentions with quality validated against human experts, enabling trustworthy, leakage-free benchmarks. The platform is deployed as a live system where anyone can submit predictions using any methods or data sources, constrained only by the decontaminated timing guarantees.
AI.conclusion: CT Open establishes a robust, open, and regularly updated benchmark for forecasting clinical trial outcomes under strict no-leakage conditions. By combining an LLM-driven web decontamination pipeline with live challenges and open participation, it supports rigorous evaluation and comparison of AI and human forecasting methods. The authors argue that CT Open can become a central hub for research on predicting real-world outcomes and ultimately help guide biomedical research priorities and improve clinical trial design.

title: AdaExplore: Failure-Driven Adaptation and Diversity-Preserving Search for Efficient Kernel Generation
comment: Preliminary work. The implementation is available at https://github.com/StigLidu/AdaExplore
summary: Recent large language model (LLM) agents have shown promise in using execution feedback for test-time adaptation. However, robust self-improvement remains far from solved: most approaches still treat each problem instance independently, without accumulating reusable knowledge. This limitation is particularly pronounced in domain-specific languages such as Triton, which are underrepresented in LLM pretraining data. Their strict constraints and non-linear optimization landscape further make naive generation and local refinement unreliable. We propose AdaExplore, an agent framework that enables self-improvement via accumulated execution feedback for performance-critical kernel code generation through two complementary stages: failure-driven adaptation and diversity-preserving search, jointly improving correctness and optimization performance without additional fine-tuning or external knowledge. In the adaptation stage, the agent synthesizes tasks and converts recurring failures into a reusable memory of validity rules, helping subsequent generations remain within the feasible set. In the search stage, the agent organizes candidate kernels as a tree and alternates between small local refinements and larger structural regeneration, allowing it to explore the optimization landscape beyond local optima. Experiments on kernel runtime optimization benchmarks validate these gains: AdaExplore achieves 3.12x and 1.72x speedups on KernelBench Level-2 and Level-3, respectively, within 100 steps, and continues to improve with additional computation.

AI.tldr: AdaExplore is an LLM agent framework that uses accumulated execution feedback to iteratively improve correctness and performance of Triton GPU kernels, delivering substantial speedups on kernel optimization benchmarks without extra training.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents can adapt at test time using execution feedback, but they usually treat each problem instance independently and fail to accumulate reusable knowledge. This is especially limiting for domain-specific languages like Triton, which appear rarely in pretraining corpora and are hard to optimize due to strict semantic/compilation constraints and a highly non-linear performance landscape. The paper aims to enable robust, reusable self-improvement for performance-critical kernel code generation under these conditions.
AI.method: The authors introduce AdaExplore, an agent framework with two main stages that repeatedly uses execution feedback. (1) Failure-driven adaptation: as the agent generates and executes Triton kernels, it synthesizes auxiliary tasks and distills recurring failure patterns (e.g., compilation errors, invalid semantics) into an explicit memory of validity rules, which then guides future generations to stay within the feasible region of the search space. (2) Diversity-preserving search: the agent maintains candidate kernels in a search tree and alternates between local refinements of promising kernels and more radical structural regenerations, preserving diversity so as to avoid getting stuck in local optima while still exploiting good partial solutions. No additional parameter fine-tuning or external domain knowledge is used; all improvements stem from iterative interaction with the execution environment and the learned rule memory.
AI.result: On kernel runtime optimization benchmarks, particularly KernelBench Level-2 and Level-3, AdaExplore substantially improves kernel performance within a limited number of interaction steps. It reaches 3.12x speedup on Level-2 and 1.72x speedup on Level-3 over baseline performance within 100 steps and continues to achieve further improvements with more computation, demonstrating both sample-efficient and scalable gains in code quality and runtime speed.
AI.conclusion: AdaExplore shows that LLM agents can achieve robust self-improvement in domain-specific code generation by explicitly accumulating and reusing execution-derived knowledge and combining it with a search strategy that balances refinement and exploration. This approach overcomes key limitations of instance-independent test-time adaptation, significantly improves correctness and performance of Triton kernels without retraining, and suggests a general recipe for performance-critical program synthesis in underrepresented languages.

title: Why Training-Free Token Reduction Collapses: The Inherent Instability of Pairwise Scoring Signals
summary: Training-free token reduction methods for Vision Transformers (ToMe, ToFu, PiToMe, and MCTF) employ different scoring mechanisms, yet they share a closely matched cliff-like collapse at high compression. This paper explains \emph{why}. We develop a diagnostic framework with two tools, ranking consistency $ρ_s$ and off-diagonal correlation $ρ_\text{off}$, that decomposes the collapse into (1)a signal-agnostic error amplifier inherent to layer-wise reduction, predicting convex Pareto curves and $r_{\text{crit}} \propto 1/L$; and (2)shared reliance on \emph{pairwise} similarity signals whose ranking consistency degrades from $ρ_s{=}0.88$ to $0.27$ in deep layers. Pairwise rankings are inherently unstable ($O(N_p^2)$ joint perturbations) while unary signals enjoy greater stability ($O(N_p)$ perturbations, CLT). From three design principles derived from this diagnosis, we construct CATIS as a constructive validation: unary signals raise the trigger threshold, triage suppresses the gain. On ViT-Large at 63% FLOPs reduction, CATIS retains 96.9% of vanilla accuracy (81.0%) on ImageNet-1K where all baselines collapse to 43--65%.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why different training-free token reduction methods for Vision Transformers fail catastrophically at high compression and introduces a new, more stable method (CATIS) built from that analysis.
AI.motivation: Existing training-free token reduction methods for Vision Transformers (e.g., ToMe, ToFu, PiToMe, MCTF) achieve good computational savings but exhibit a similar sudden accuracy collapse when compression becomes aggressive. Despite differing scoring mechanisms, they share this failure mode, which is poorly understood. The authors aim to explain the underlying cause of this collapse, quantify it, and derive design principles that lead to more robust token reduction under high FLOPs reduction.
AI.method: The authors propose a diagnostic framework based on two metrics: ranking consistency (ρ_s) and off-diagonal correlation (ρ_off). Using these, they decompose the observed collapse into two components: (1) a signal-agnostic error amplification effect inherent to performing token reduction layer-wise, which theoretically predicts convex Pareto trade-off curves between accuracy and compression and a critical compression rate r_crit that scales inversely with network depth (r_crit ∝ 1/L); and (2) the common dependence of current methods on pairwise similarity signals between tokens. They empirically measure how the ranking consistency of these pairwise signals degrades with depth (ρ_s dropping from 0.88 to 0.27) and argue, from perturbation analysis, that pairwise rankings are intrinsically unstable because they depend on O(N_p^2) joint perturbations, while unary signals only accumulate O(N_p) perturbations and thus benefit from central limit theorem style stability. From these insights, they derive three design principles for token reduction and implement a new method, CATIS, which uses unary signals to raise the reduction trigger threshold and a triage mechanism to suppress overall error gain.
AI.result: Using ViT-Large on ImageNet-1K, at 63% FLOPs reduction, CATIS preserves 96.9% of the original (vanilla) model’s accuracy, achieving 81.0% × 0.969 ≈ 78.5% top-1 accuracy. In the same setting, existing training-free token reduction baselines (ToMe, ToFu, PiToMe, MCTF) suffer severe collapse, only retaining 43–65% of the vanilla accuracy. The diagnostic metrics ρ_s and ρ_off reveal that the collapse correlates with low ranking consistency of pairwise similarity-based scores and with the predicted error amplification behavior of layer-wise reduction, validating the theoretical decomposition.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the common cliff-like accuracy collapse in training-free token reduction for Vision Transformers stems from two main factors: an intrinsic, signal-agnostic error amplification due to performing token reduction independently in each layer, and the instability of pairwise similarity-based scoring whose ranking consistency degrades in deeper layers. Pairwise comparisons induce many correlated perturbations (O(N_p^2)), making rankings highly sensitive to noise, whereas unary scoring functions are more robust (O(N_p) perturbations and CLT-like averaging). Guided by three design principles extracted from this analysis, the proposed CATIS method, built on unary signals and a triage mechanism, achieves substantially more stable performance under high compression, demonstrating that the collapse is not inevitable but largely a consequence of previous design choices.

title: Migrant Voices, Local News: Insights on Bridging Community Needs with Media Content
comment: David Alonso del Barrio, Paula Dolores Rescala, Victor Bros, Daniel Gatica-Perez| ACM 2026. This is the author's version of the work. It is posted here for your personal use. Not for redistribution. The definitive Version of Record will be published in IMX'26 ACM International Conference on Interactive Media Experiences https://doi.org/10.1145/3788851.3805011
summary: Research shows news consumption differs across demographics, yet little is known about non-mainstream audiences, especially in relation to local media. Our study addresses this gap by examining how French-speaking migrants in a mid-size European city engage with local news, and whether their needs are reflected in coverage. Eight community members participated in focus groups, whose insights guided the selection of natural language processing methods (topic modeling, information retrieval, sentiment analysis, and readability) applied to over 2000 hyper-local news articles. Results showed that while articles frequently covered local events, gaps remained in topics important to participants. Sentiment analysis revealed a generally positive tone, and readability measures indicated an intermediate-advanced French level, raising questions about accessibility for integration. Our work contributes to bridging the gap between local news platforms' content and diverse readers' needs, and could inform local media organizations about opportunities to expand their current news story coverage to appeal to more diverse audiences.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how French-speaking migrants engage with local hyper-local news in a European city, using focus groups to guide NLP analyses of 2000+ articles, and finds mismatches between migrants’ information needs and existing coverage, as well as accessibility issues.
AI.motivation: There is evidence that news consumption varies across demographic groups, but little is understood about how non-mainstream, migrant audiences interact with local media and whether their needs are adequately served. This study aims to fill this gap by focusing on French-speaking migrants’ engagement with local news and evaluating whether local hyper-local news content aligns with their interests and supports integration.
AI.method: The researchers conducted focus groups with eight French-speaking community members to identify their information needs and preferred news topics. Insights from the focus groups informed the choice and configuration of NLP methods—topic modeling, information retrieval, sentiment analysis, and readability assessment—which were then applied to a corpus of over 2000 hyper-local news articles from a mid-size European city.
AI.result: The analysis showed that local hyper-local news outlets often cover local events, but there are notable gaps in reporting on topics that participants considered important. Sentiment analysis indicated that the coverage tends to be generally positive in tone. Readability metrics suggested that the language of the articles corresponds to an intermediate-to-advanced level of French, which may be challenging for some migrants and could hinder their access to and understanding of local news relevant to integration.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that local news content does not fully reflect the information needs of French-speaking migrants and may be linguistically inaccessible to some of them. This misalignment suggests opportunities for local media organizations to adjust topic selection and language complexity to better serve diverse audiences. Overall, the work helps bridge the gap between local news offerings and the needs of migrant communities, and it provides actionable insights for outlets seeking to expand and diversify their readership.

title: Don't Start What You Can't Finish: A Counterfactual Audit of Support-State Triage in LLM Agents
summary: Current agent evaluations largely reward execution on fully specified tasks, while recent work studies clarification [11, 22, 2], capability awareness [9, 1], abstention [8, 14], and search termination [20, 5] mostly in isolation. This leaves open whether agents can diagnose why a task is blocked before acting. We introduce the Support-State Triage Audit (SSTA-32), a matched-item diagnostic framework in which minimal counterfactual edits flip the same base request across four support states: Complete (ANSWER), Clarifiable (CLARIFY), Support-Blocked (REQUEST SUPPORT), and Unsupported-Now (ABSTAIN). We evaluate a frontier model under four prompting conditions - Direct, Action-Only, Confidence-Only, and a typed Preflight Support Check (PSC) - using Dual-Persona Auto-Auditing (DPAA) with deterministic heuristic scoring. Default execution overcommits heavily on non-complete tasks (41.7% overcommitment rate). Scalar confidence mapping avoids overcommitment but collapses the three-way deferral space (58.3% typed deferral accuracy). Conversely, both Action-Only and PSC achieve 91.7% typed deferral accuracy by surfacing the categorical ontology in the prompt. Targeted ablations confirm that removing the support-sufficiency dimension selectively degrades REQUEST SUPPORT accuracy, while removing the evidence-sufficiency dimension triggers systematic overcommitment on unsupported items. Because DPAA operates within a single context window, these results represent upper-bound capability estimates; nonetheless, the structural findings indicate that frontier models possess strong latent triage capabilities that require explicit categorical decision paths to activate safely.

AI.tldr: They propose and test a new benchmark (SSTA‑32) showing that advanced models can, in principle, decide when to answer, clarify, request support, or abstain—but only reliably when the prompt makes these categories explicit.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations mostly reward agents for executing fully specified tasks, and prior work has looked separately at behaviors like asking clarification questions, being aware of one’s own capabilities, abstaining, or ending a search. What’s missing is a unified diagnostic of whether an agent can recognize *why* a task is blocked before acting (e.g., missing info vs missing tools vs fundamentally impossible). The authors want to know if models can perform this kind of ‘triage’ and what prompting structures are needed to elicit it safely.
AI.method: They design the Support-State Triage Audit (SSTA-32), a set of matched items where minimal counterfactual changes convert one base request into four distinct ‘support states’: (1) Complete: model has enough info and capability to answer (ANSWER); (2) Clarifiable: more user info is needed (CLARIFY); (3) Support-Blocked: external tools/resources or other support are needed (REQUEST SUPPORT); (4) Unsupported-Now: the task should be refused/abstained from for now (ABSTAIN). They then evaluate a frontier model on these items under four prompting regimes: Direct (default instruction-following), Action-Only (categorical action choice without explicit confidence), Confidence-Only (scalar confidence to map into actions), and a typed Preflight Support Check (PSC) that explicitly walks through the support-state ontology. They score behavior using Dual-Persona Auto-Auditing (DPAA), a deterministic heuristic auditing setup that stays within one context window so that the same model both acts and evaluates in a structured way.
AI.result: Default ‘Direct’ execution often overcommits on tasks that are not actually fully supported (41.7% overcommitment). Confidence-Only prompting reduces overcommitment but fails to preserve the distinctions among the three non-answer deferral types, giving only 58.3% accuracy in correctly typing the deferral state. In contrast, prompts that surface the full categorical ontology—Action-Only and PSC—reach 91.7% accuracy in correctly classifying whether to answer, clarify, request support, or abstain. Ablation experiments show that if the prompt omits the support-sufficiency dimension, the model’s ability to recognize when to REQUEST SUPPORT selectively worsens; if it omits the evidence-sufficiency dimension, the model starts to overcommit systematically on items that should be abstained from or deferred, indicating that each dimension is functionally important for safe triage.
AI.conclusion: The frontier model has substantial latent capability to perform nuanced support-state triage (answer vs clarify vs request support vs abstain), but this capability does not reliably emerge under standard or purely confidence-based prompting. Instead, it requires explicit, categorical decision paths that encode both evidence sufficiency and support/tool sufficiency. While the DPAA setup gives upper-bound estimates because it keeps everything in one context window, the structural pattern suggests that careful prompt and agent-architecture design can safely activate these triage abilities and reduce overcommitment in real-world agent systems.

title: Know When to Trust the Skill: Delayed Appraisal and Epistemic Vigilance for Single-Agent LLMs
summary: As large language models (LLMs) transition into autonomous agents integrated with extensive tool ecosystems, traditional routing heuristics increasingly succumb to context pollution and "overthinking". We argue that the bottleneck is not a deficit in algorithmic capability or skill diversity, but the absence of disciplined second-order metacognitive governance. In this paper, our scientific contribution focuses on the computational translation of human cognitive control - specifically, delayed appraisal, epistemic vigilance, and region-of-proximal offloading - into a single-agent architecture. We introduce MESA-S (Metacognitive Skills for Agents, Single-agent), a preliminary framework that shifts scalar confidence estimation into a vector separating self-confidence (parametric certainty) from source-confidence (trust in retrieved external procedures). By formalizing a delayed procedural probe mechanism and introducing Metacognitive Skill Cards, MESA-S decouples the awareness of a skill's utility from its token-intensive execution. Evaluated under an In-Context Static Benchmark Evaluation natively executed via Gemini 3.1 Pro, our early results suggest that explicitly programming trust provenance and delayed escalation mitigates supply-chain vulnerabilities, prunes unnecessary reasoning loops, and prevents offloading-induced confidence inflation. This architecture offers a scientifically cautious, behaviorally anchored step toward reliable, epistemically vigilant single-agent orchestration.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MESA-S, a metacognitive governance framework for single LLM agents, to reduce overthinking, context pollution, and unsafe tool use by separating self-confidence from source-confidence and adding delayed, explicit checks before invoking external skills or tools.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are turned into autonomous, tool-using agents, simple routing rules and confidence scores cause problems: context gets cluttered, the model over-reasons, and it can blindly trust external tools or retrieved procedures, creating supply-chain vulnerabilities and inefficient behavior. The authors believe existing systems lack structured metacognitive control—like humans’ ability to pause, question sources, and decide what to offload—rather than lacking raw capabilities.
AI.method: They design a single-agent architecture called MESA-S that computationally instantiates human-inspired cognitive control mechanisms: (1) delayed appraisal: the agent postpones costly actions and re-checks their necessity; (2) epistemic vigilance: it tracks and evaluates the trustworthiness of external procedures; (3) region-of-proximal offloading: it selectively offloads tasks that are just beyond its internal capability. Technically, they replace a scalar confidence score with a vector separating self-confidence (model’s parametric certainty) from source-confidence (trust in retrieved/tool procedures). They also add a delayed procedural probe mechanism and Metacognitive Skill Cards, which encode skills and their usage conditions separately from executing them, so the agent can reason about whether to use a skill without fully running it. They then evaluate MESA-S on an in-context static benchmark executed with Gemini 3.1 Pro.
AI.result: In benchmark tests, explicitly modeling trust provenance and delaying escalation to external tools or retrieved procedures reduces harmful patterns: it mitigates supply-chain vulnerabilities (blindly trusting external resources), cuts down unnecessary long reasoning chains, and avoids inflated confidence when the model has offloaded too much to external tools. Early empirical results indicate more efficient and safer single-agent behavior compared with baseline heuristics.
AI.conclusion: MESA-S demonstrates that embedding human-like metacognitive skills—especially differentiated confidence tracking, explicit trust provenance, and delayed decision mechanisms—into a single LLM agent can make tool use more cautious, efficient, and reliable. Although preliminary, the framework is positioned as a behaviorally grounded, scientifically conservative step toward robust, epistemically vigilant single-agent orchestration, rather than simply adding more tools or capabilities.

title: Defragmenting Language Models: An Interpretability-based Approach for Vocabulary Expansion
summary: All languages are equal; when it comes to tokenization, some are more equal than others. Tokens are the hidden currency that dictate the cost and latency of access to contemporary LLMs. However, many languages written in non-Latin scripts observe a poor exchange rate: LLMs take several multiples of tokens to encode the same information in many languages as they do for English. Our analysis reveals that this issue, known as 'token over-fragmentation', persists in modern open-weight LLMs. The standard remedy is vocabulary expansion that adds target language items missing from the model's vocabulary. In this work, we comprehensively study and advance interpretability-based vocabulary expansion, a new research direction. We focus on two core decisions in the vocabulary expansion process: What items should we add? and How should we initialize their corresponding input and output embeddings? First, we question the conventional use of frequency-based methods to choose candidate vocabulary items to add (a decision long treated as settled), and show that interpretability-based methods offer a superior performance-token efficiency trade-off. Next, we strengthen the case for interpretability-based embedding initialization by showing large gains (~20 pts) over baseline initialization methods for several languages written in non-Latin scripts. We identify the phenomenon of "subword detokenization" where models progressively merge fragmented subword tokens into larger subwords across layers. Grounded in our analysis of this phenomenon, we propose FragMend to further push the efficiency ceiling of interpretability-based expansion. We validate the effectiveness of FragMend through comparison against strong baselines and we present extensive analysis of its design choices.

AI.tldr: The paper studies token over-fragmentation for non-Latin-script languages in LLMs and proposes interpretability-based vocabulary expansion and a new method, FragMend, to reduce tokens and improve performance.
AI.motivation: LLMs encode non-Latin-script languages using many more tokens than English, increasing cost and latency. Existing practice relies on frequency-based vocabulary expansion, but its adequacy and optimality have been under-examined, especially for open-weight models. The authors aim to systematically understand and improve vocabulary expansion, with a focus on interpretability-guided choices that can make non-English languages more token-efficient and performant.
AI.method: They analyze token over-fragmentation in modern open-weight LLMs and compare standard frequency-based vocabulary expansion to interpretability-based selection of new vocabulary items. They also examine different strategies to initialize the input and output embeddings of the new tokens, emphasizing interpretability-based initialization. They introduce and analyze the phenomenon of “subword detokenization,” where models merge fragmented subword tokens into larger units across layers. Building on this, they propose FragMend, an interpretability-driven vocabulary expansion method designed to exploit this detokenization behavior. They evaluate FragMend against strong baselines across several non-Latin-script languages and conduct ablations of its design choices.
AI.result: They find that token over-fragmentation persists in modern open-weight LLMs for many non-Latin-script languages. Interpretability-based methods for selecting new vocabulary tokens outperform traditional frequency-based methods in terms of the trade-off between performance and token efficiency. Interpretability-based embedding initialization yields large gains—around 20 points on their evaluation metrics—over baseline initialization methods for several non-Latin-script languages. Their analysis reveals a consistent pattern of subword detokenization across layers. FragMend, which is built around this phenomenon, achieves superior token efficiency and performance compared to strong baseline vocabulary expansion approaches.
AI.conclusion: Vocabulary expansion for non-Latin-script languages in LLMs benefits significantly from interpretability-guided design. Both the choice of new vocabulary items and the way their embeddings are initialized can be improved by leveraging internal model interpretability signals, leading to better performance-token efficiency trade-offs than frequency-based methods. The identified subword detokenization behavior provides a mechanistic explanation for why interpretability-based approaches work and motivates FragMend, which pushes the efficiency ceiling of vocabulary expansion. Overall, the work argues that tokenization for multilingual LLMs should be treated as an interpretability-informed optimization problem rather than a purely frequency-driven engineering choice.

title: Machine individuality: Separating genuine idiosyncrasy from response bias in large language models
summary: As large language models (LLMs) are increasingly integrated into daily life, in roles ranging from high-stakes decision support to companionship, understanding their behavioral dispositions becomes critical. A growing literature uses psychometric inventories and cognitive paradigms to profile LLM dispositions. However, these approaches cannot determine whether behavioral differences reflect stable, stimulus-specific individuality or global response biases and stochastic noise. Here, we apply crossed random-effects models -- widely used in psychometrics to separate systematic effects -- to 74.9 million ratings provided by 10 open-weight LLMs for over 100,000 words across 14 psycholinguistic norms. On average, 16.9% of variance is attributable to stimulus-specific individuality, robustly exceeding a statistical null model. Cross-norm prediction analyses reveal this individuality as a coherent fingerprint, unique to each model. These results identify individual differences among LLMs that cannot be attributed to response biases or stochastic noise. We term these differences machine individuality.

AI.tldr: They analyze massive rating data from multiple LLMs to show that models have stable, stimulus-specific behavioral patterns, which they call 'machine individuality', not explainable by noise or simple biases.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are embedded in everyday, sometimes high‑stakes, applications, it's important to know whether apparent personality-like differences between models are real, stable properties or just artifacts of noise and generic response biases. Existing psychometric-style probing cannot clearly separate these possibilities.
AI.method: They collect 74.9 million ratings from 10 open-weight LLMs on more than 100,000 words, each evaluated along 14 psycholinguistic norm dimensions (e.g., concreteness, valence). They then use crossed random-effects models from psychometrics to decompose the variance in these ratings into components due to the specific stimulus (word), the model, their interaction, and noise. They also perform cross-norm prediction analyses to examine how consistent the model-specific patterns are across different psycholinguistic dimensions.
AI.result: They find that, on average, 16.9% of variance in model responses is attributable to stimulus-specific individuality – a stable interaction between each model and each specific word – and that this significantly exceeds what would be expected under a null model of no individuality. Cross-norm prediction shows that these patterns form coherent and distinctive fingerprints for each LLM across norms, indicating structured, model-specific behavior.
AI.conclusion: Different LLMs display robust, stimulus-specific response patterns that cannot be reduced to global biases or random variation. These stable, distinctive patterns amount to 'machine individuality', suggesting that LLMs can be meaningfully characterized by their idiosyncratic response profiles across a wide range of linguistic stimuli and norms.

authors: A. B. M. Alim Al Islam
title: CBRS: Cognitive Blood Request System with Bilingual Dataset and Dual-Layer Filtering for Multi-Platform Social Streams
comment: Accepted to Findings of the ACL 2026
summary: Urgent blood donation seeking posts and messages on social media often go unnoticed due to the overwhelming volume of daily communications. Traditional app-based systems, reliant on manual input, struggle to reach users in low-resource settings, delaying critical responses. To address this, we introduce the Cognitive Blood Request System (CBRS), a multi-platform framework that efficiently filters and parses blood donation requests from social media streams using a cost-efficient dual-layered architecture. To do so, we curate a novel dataset of 11K parsed blood donation request messages in Bengali, English, and transliterated Bengali, capturing the linguistic diversity of real social media communications. The inclusion of adversarial negatives further enhances the robustness of our model. CBRS achieves an impressive 99% accuracy and precision in filtering, surpassing benchmark methods. In the parsing task, our LoRA finetuned Llama-3.2-3B model achieves 92% zero-shot accuracy, surpassing the base model by 41.54% and exceeding the few-shot performance of GPT-4o-mini, Gemini-2.0-Flash, and other LLMs, while resulting in a 35X reduction in input token usage. This work lays a robust foundation for scalable, inclusive information extraction in time-sensitive, object-focused tasks. Our code, dataset, and trained models are publicly available at [https://github.com/aaniksahaa/CBRS](https://github.com/aaniksahaa/CBRS).

AI.tldr: The paper presents the Cognitive Blood Request System (CBRS), a multi-platform, dual-layer framework that automatically detects and parses urgent blood donation requests from social media, using a new multilingual dataset and a LoRA-finetuned Llama-3.2-3B model that outperforms larger LLMs while being much more cost-efficient.
AI.motivation: Urgent blood donation requests on social media are often missed because of the sheer volume of posts and the reliance on manual, app-based systems that do not work well in low-resource settings. There is a need for an automated, scalable, and language-inclusive system that can reliably identify and extract structured information from these requests in real time to enable faster responses and better coordination.
AI.method: The authors design CBRS, a dual-layer architecture that first filters blood donation request messages from social media streams and then parses the relevant details (such as blood type, location, etc.). They curate a new dataset of about 11,000 annotated blood request messages covering Bengali, English, and transliterated Bengali, including adversarial negative examples to improve robustness. For parsing, they finetune a Llama-3.2-3B model using LoRA and compare its performance in zero-shot settings against the base model and various proprietary and open LLMs (including GPT-4o-mini and Gemini-2.0-Flash), also measuring token usage for efficiency.
AI.result: CBRS attains 99% accuracy and precision in the filtering stage, outperforming benchmark methods. For parsing, the LoRA-finetuned Llama-3.2-3B achieves 92% zero-shot accuracy, improving over the base model by 41.54 percentage points and surpassing the few-shot performance of larger models like GPT-4o-mini and Gemini-2.0-Flash, while reducing input token usage by a factor of 35. The inclusion of adversarial negatives improves robustness to noisy or deceptive messages.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CBRS framework demonstrates that a compact, finetuned open LLM coupled with a dual-layer architecture and a carefully curated multilingual dataset can enable accurate, scalable, and cost-efficient extraction of urgent blood donation requests from noisy social media streams. This lays groundwork for broader, inclusive deployment of automated information extraction systems in critical, time-sensitive, and object-focused applications, particularly in low-resource linguistic environments. The authors release their code, dataset, and models to encourage further research and practical deployment.

title: SAVE: A Generalizable Framework for Multi-Condition Single-Cell Generation with Gene Block Attention
summary: Modeling single-cell gene expression across diverse biological and technical conditions is crucial for characterizing cellular states and simulating unseen scenarios. Existing methods often treat genes as independent tokens, overlooking their high-level biological relationships and leading to poor performance.
