We introduce SAVE, a unified generative framework based on conditional Transformers for multi-condition single-cell modeling. SAVE leverages a coarse-grained representation by grouping semantically related genes into blocks, capturing higher-order dependencies among gene modules. A Flow Matching mechanism and condition-masking strategy further enhance flexible simulation and enable generalization to unseen condition combinations. We evaluate SAVE on a range of benchmarks, including conditional generation, batch effect correction, and perturbation prediction. SAVE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods in generation fidelity and extrapolative generalization, especially in low-resource or combinatorially held-out settings. Overall, SAVE offers a scalable and generalizable solution for modeling complex single-cell data, with broad utility in virtual cell synthesis and biological interpretation. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/fdu-wangfeilab/sc-save

AI.tldr: The paper presents SAVE, a conditional Transformer-based generative framework for modeling single-cell gene expression across multiple biological and technical conditions, improving generation fidelity and generalization via gene-block representations, Flow Matching, and condition masking.
AI.motivation: Accurately modeling single-cell gene expression under diverse, possibly unseen biological and technical conditions is essential for understanding cellular states, correcting batch effects, and simulating perturbations. Existing approaches usually model genes as independent tokens, failing to capture higher-order biological structures and relationships between genes, which limits performance, especially when data are scarce or when generalizing to new condition combinations.
AI.method: The authors propose SAVE, a unified generative framework that uses conditional Transformers for multi-condition single-cell modeling. Instead of treating each gene independently, SAVE groups semantically related genes into coarse-grained blocks, improving representation of higher-order gene-module dependencies. It incorporates a Flow Matching generative mechanism and a condition-masking strategy, enabling flexible conditional simulation and extrapolation to unseen combinations of conditions. The model is trained and evaluated on various multi-condition single-cell datasets.
AI.result: Across benchmarks such as conditional data generation, batch effect correction, and perturbation prediction, SAVE outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. It achieves better generation fidelity and stronger extrapolative generalization, with especially notable gains in low-resource regimes and in settings where certain condition combinations are held out during training but must be predicted at test time.
AI.conclusion: SAVE provides a scalable, unified, and generalizable generative framework for complex single-cell data. By leveraging gene-block representations, Flow Matching, and condition masking, it can accurately simulate cellular gene expression across diverse and even unseen conditions. This makes it broadly useful for virtual cell synthesis, more robust downstream analyses, and biological interpretation of cellular states.

title: No-Worse Context-Aware Decoding: Preventing Neutral Regression in Context-Conditioned Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can answer questions and summarize documents when conditioned on external contexts (e.g., retrieved evidence), yet context use remains unreliable: models may overwrite an already-correct output (neutral regression) even when the context is non-informative. We formalize neutral regression as a do-no-harm requirement and quantify it by measuring accuracy drops on baseline-correct items under answer-consistent contexts. We propose No-Worse Context-Aware Decoding (NWCAD), a decode-time adapter built on a two-stream setup with a two-stage gate: it backs off to no-context decoding when the context is non-informative, and otherwise uses context-conditioned decoding with a CAD-style fallback under uncertainty. We evaluate NWCAD on benchmarks that separate do-no-harm reliability from context utilization (accuracy gains on genuinely helpful contexts). NWCAD prevents neutral regression on baseline-correct items while preserving strong context-driven accuracy on helpful contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and tackles 'neutral regression', where LLMs get worse when given non-informative context, and proposes a decoding method (NWCAD) that guarantees no harm while preserving benefits from useful context.
AI.motivation: LLMs often use retrieved or provided context to improve answers, but sometimes adding context makes correct baseline answers wrong, even when the context is neutral or irrelevant. This undermines reliability and trust in context-augmented systems. The authors want a principled way to formalize and reduce this failure mode so models are at least no worse when extra context is added.
AI.method: They formally define 'neutral regression' as violations of a do-no-harm constraint: adding answer-consistent or non-informative context should not reduce accuracy on items the base model already gets right. They then design No-Worse Context-Aware Decoding (NWCAD), a decode-time adapter based on two parallel decoding streams (with and without context) and a two-stage gating mechanism. The gate decides when to rely on context-conditioned decoding, when to back off to no-context decoding if context appears non-informative, and when to fall back CAD-style under uncertainty. They construct or use benchmarks that explicitly separate reliability (avoid harming baseline-correct cases) from gains due to helpful contexts.
AI.result: Empirical evaluations show that NWCAD essentially eliminates or greatly reduces neutral regression on baseline-correct items: when context is answer-consistent but non-informative, accuracy does not drop compared to no-context decoding. At the same time, on instances with genuinely helpful context, NWCAD still leverages that information and retains strong context-driven accuracy improvements, comparable to or close to standard context-augmented decoding.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that context-augmented LLMs can be made more reliable through decode-time control without retraining. By formalizing neutral regression as a do-no-harm requirement and introducing NWCAD, the authors show it is possible to prevent performance degradation from non-informative contexts while still benefiting from helpful evidence, improving the robustness of retrieval-augmented and context-based LLM applications.

title: Introspection Adapters: Training LLMs to Report Their Learned Behaviors
summary: When model developers or users fine-tune an LLM, this can induce behaviors that are unexpected, deliberately harmful, or hard to detect. It would be far easier to audit LLMs if they could simply describe their behaviors in natural language. Here, we study a scalable approach to rapidly identify learned behaviors of many LLMs derived from a shared base LLM. Given a model $M$, our method works by finetuning models $M_i$ from $M$ with implanted behaviors $b_i$; the $(M_i, b_i)$ pairs serve as labeled training data. We then train an \emph{introspection adapter} (IA): a single LoRA adapter jointly trained across the finetunes $M_i$ to cause them to verbalize their implanted behaviors. We find that this IA induces self-description of learned behaviors even in finetunes of $M$ that were trained in very different ways from the $M_i$. For example, IAs generalize to AuditBench, achieving state-of-the-art at identifying explicitly hidden concerning behaviors. IAs can also be used to detect encrypted finetuning API attacks. They scale favorably with model size and training data diversity. Overall, our results suggest that IAs are a scalable, effective, and practically useful approach to auditing fine-tuned LLMs.

AI.tldr: They introduce an "introspection adapter" (IA), a shared LoRA module that makes many differently fine‑tuned LLMs describe their own learned behaviors in natural language, enabling scalable auditing of fine‑tuned models.
AI.motivation: Fine‑tuning LLMs can introduce unexpected, harmful, or covert behaviors that are difficult to discover with ordinary testing or red‑teaming. Manually auditing each fine‑tuned model does not scale, especially when the model may deliberately hide its capabilities or when the fine‑tuning process is opaque (e.g., via third‑party APIs or encrypted data). A method is needed that lets models themselves accurately describe what behaviors they have learned, in a way that generalizes across many fine‑tunes of a shared base model.
AI.method: Starting from a base model M, they create multiple fine‑tuned models Mi, each with a specific, known implanted behavior bi (e.g., some particular capability or objective). The pairs (Mi, bi) act as labeled data. They then train a single LoRA module, called the introspection adapter (IA), jointly on all these Mi so that, when the IA is attached and appropriately prompted, each Mi will verbalize its own implanted behavior bi. After training, this same IA is attached to other fine‑tuned descendants of M (not seen during IA training, and trained in different ways) and prompted to elicit a natural‑language description of those models’ behaviors. They evaluate the IA on benchmarks like AuditBench and on scenarios involving encrypted fine‑tuning API attacks, and study how performance scales with model size and diversity of training behaviors.
AI.result: The introspection adapter reliably causes fine‑tuned models with implanted behaviors to describe those behaviors in natural language. Crucially, the same IA generalizes to new fine‑tuned models of the same base—trained in different ways and without explicit labeling—still eliciting accurate self‑descriptions. On AuditBench, the IA attains state‑of‑the‑art performance at uncovering intentionally hidden concerning behaviors. It also successfully detects behaviors arising from encrypted fine‑tuning API attacks. Performance improves as the base model size and the diversity of implanted behaviors used in IA training increase.
AI.conclusion: A single, jointly trained LoRA introspection adapter can make a wide range of fine‑tuned descendants of a base LLM describe their own learned behaviors, even when those behaviors are hidden or the fine‑tunes differ substantially from those seen during training. This yields a scalable, practical tool for auditing fine‑tuned LLMs, improving the ability to detect harmful or covert capabilities without bespoke, model‑specific analysis each time.

title: The impact of postediting on AI generative translation in Yemeni context: Translating literary prose by ChatGPT
summary: This study examines the role of artificial intelligence in translation, focusing on ChatGPT, specifically ChatGPT-4, and the extent to which human postediting is required in literary translation. A mixed-method approach was adopted, involving 30 professional translators who evaluated and postedited AI-generated translations of selected Arabic and English literary texts. The results show that although AI improves translation speed and accessibility, it remains limited in handling cultural, stylistic, and figurative aspects of language. Participants generally confirmed the necessity of human postediting, particularly in novels and drama. The findings indicate that emerging human-machine collaboration model rather than replacement of human translators. The study concludes that AI should be used as a supportive tool, while human expertise remains essential for ensuring translation quality and cultural appropriateness.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how well ChatGPT-4 can handle literary translation between Arabic and English and how much human postediting is still needed.
AI.motivation: To understand whether AI, particularly ChatGPT-4, can replace or simply assist human translators in the demanding domain of literary translation, especially regarding cultural and stylistic nuances.
AI.method: The authors used a mixed-method design with 30 professional translators who evaluated and postedited ChatGPT-4 translations of selected Arabic and English literary texts (including novels and drama) and provided feedback on quality and required effort.
AI.result: AI translations increased speed and accessibility but struggled with cultural references, stylistic fidelity, and figurative language. Translators consistently found that substantial human postediting was required, especially for novels and drama.
AI.conclusion: AI, including ChatGPT-4, is best positioned as a supportive tool within a human–machine collaboration model. Human translators remain essential to ensure high-quality, culturally appropriate literary translations, so AI should augment rather than replace them.

title: PersonalHomeBench: Evaluating Agents in Personalized Smart Homes
summary: Agentic AI systems are rapidly advancing toward real-world applications, yet their readiness in complex and personalized environments remains insufficiently characterized. To address this gap, we introduce PersonalHomeBench, a benchmark for evaluating foundation models as agentic assistants in personalized smart home environments. The benchmark is constructed through an iterative process that progressively builds rich household states, which are then used to generate personalized, context-dependent tasks. To support realistic agent-environment interaction, we provide PersonalHomeTools, a comprehensive toolbox enabling household information retrieval, appliance control, and situational understanding. PersonalHomeBench evaluates both reactive and proactive agentic abilities under unimodal and multimodal observations. Thorough experimentation reveals a systematic performance reduction as task complexity increases, with pronounced failures in counterfactual reasoning and under partial observability, where effective tool-based information gathering is required. These results position PersonalHomeBench as a rigorous evaluation platform for analyzing the robustness and limitations of personalized agentic reasoning and planning.

AI.tldr: PersonalHomeBench is a benchmark to test how well AI agents act as personalized smart-home assistants, revealing where they fail as tasks become more complex.
AI.motivation: Existing agentic AI systems are moving toward real-world deployment, but we lack a clear understanding of how reliably they can operate as personalized assistants in complex smart-home environments. Traditional benchmarks often use simple, decontextualized tasks and do not capture evolving household states, personalization, or the need for proactive and reactive behaviors. The authors aim to fill this gap by providing a realistic, structured way to evaluate foundation models’ effectiveness and robustness in such settings.
AI.method: They build PersonalHomeBench, a benchmark that iteratively constructs detailed household states (including occupants, devices, and context) and uses these states to generate personalized, context-dependent tasks. They accompany it with PersonalHomeTools, a toolbox that lets agents retrieve household information, control appliances, and interpret situational context, supporting realistic interaction with the environment. The benchmark is designed to test both reactive (responding to user requests) and proactive (anticipating needs or acting autonomously) abilities, under both unimodal (text-only) and multimodal (e.g., text + visual) observations. They then run systematic experiments to evaluate model performance across increasing task complexity and varying observability conditions, especially where tool use is necessary.
AI.result: Experiments show that model performance degrades systematically as task complexity increases. The agents particularly struggle with counterfactual reasoning (reasoning about what would happen under alternate conditions) and with tasks under partial observability, where they fail to use tools effectively to gather missing information from the environment. These findings highlight concrete failure modes in current agentic foundation models when deployed as smart-home assistants.
AI.conclusion: PersonalHomeBench provides a rigorous, realistic benchmark and accompanying tool suite for evaluating personalized agentic reasoning and planning in smart-home environments. By exposing how performance drops with complexity and revealing specific weaknesses in counterfactual reasoning and information-gathering under partial observability, it offers a structured platform for diagnosing current limitations and guiding the development of more robust agentic AI systems.

title: Detecting Alarming Student Verbal Responses using Text and Audio Classifier
comment: 9 Pages. Paper to be Presented at the National Council on Measurement in Education Conference on April 10, 2026
summary: This paper addresses a critical safety gap in the use Automated Verbal Response Scoring (AVRS). We present a novel hybrid framework for troubled student detection that combines a text classifier, trained to detect responses based on their content, and an audio classifier, trained to detect responses using prosodic markers. This approach overcomes key limitations of traditional AVRS systems by considering both content and prosody of responses, achieving enhanced performance in identifying potentially concerning responses. This system can expedite the review process by humans, which can be life-saving particularly when timely intervention may be crucial.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hybrid system combining text and audio analysis to better detect troubled students in Automated Verbal Response Scoring (AVRS) environments.
AI.motivation: Traditional AVRS systems focus mainly on content or simple scoring and often miss critical indicators of student distress, especially those conveyed through tone and prosody. There is a safety gap in timely identifying students who may need urgent support or intervention.
AI.method: The authors design a hybrid framework composed of two machine learning classifiers: a text classifier trained to flag concerning responses based on linguistic content, and an audio classifier trained on prosodic features (e.g., intonation, pitch, rhythm). They then fuse these outputs to detect potentially troubling student responses more accurately than using either modality alone.
AI.result: The combined text-and-audio model outperforms traditional AVRS approaches that rely on a single modality, showing improved accuracy and reliability in detecting potentially concerning student responses. It also streamlines which responses are escalated for human review.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating both semantic content and prosodic cues into AVRS significantly improves the early detection of troubled students, enabling faster human review and potentially life‑saving interventions. The hybrid framework represents a safer and more effective alternative to conventional AVRS systems.

title: The CTLNet for Shanghai Composite Index Prediction
summary: Shanghai Composite Index prediction has become a hot issue for many investors and academic researchers. Deep learning models are widely applied in multivariate time series forecasting, including recurrent neural networks (RNN), convolutional neural networks (CNN), and transformers. Specifically, the Transformer encoder, with its unique attention mechanism and parallel processing capabilities, has become an important tool in time series prediction, and has an advantage in dealing with long sequence dependencies and multivariate data correlations. Drawing on the strengths of various models, we propose the CNN-Transformer-LSTM Networks (CTLNet). This paper explores the application of CTLNet for Shanghai Composite Index prediction and the comparative experiments show that the proposed model outperforms state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a hybrid deep learning model (CNN-Transformer-LSTM, CTLNet) to predict the Shanghai Composite Index and reports better performance than existing models.
AI.motivation: Accurate prediction of the Shanghai Composite Index is important for investors and researchers, but existing models struggle with capturing long-term dependencies and complex multivariate relationships in financial time series. Recent advances in deep learning, particularly attention-based Transformers, offer improved handling of long sequences and multivariate correlations, motivating the design of a new architecture that combines the strengths of CNNs, Transformers, and LSTMs.
AI.method: The authors design CTLNet, a hybrid neural network that integrates convolutional neural networks (for local feature extraction), Transformer encoders (for attention-based modeling of long-range dependencies and multivariate correlations), and LSTMs (for sequential temporal modeling). They train CTLNet on historical Shanghai Composite Index data and conduct comparative experiments against state-of-the-art baselines such as standalone RNNs, CNNs, and Transformer-based models.
AI.result: Experimental results on Shanghai Composite Index forecasting show that CTLNet achieves lower prediction errors and superior performance metrics compared with state-of-the-art baseline models, demonstrating its effectiveness in capturing complex temporal and multivariate patterns in the data.
AI.conclusion: Combining CNN, Transformer, and LSTM into a unified CTLNet architecture improves prediction accuracy for the Shanghai Composite Index compared with existing deep learning baselines. The attention mechanism and hybrid design effectively capture both local and long-range temporal dependencies, suggesting that such integrated architectures are promising for financial time series forecasting and related multivariate prediction tasks.

title: Evaluating Adaptive Personalization of Educational Readings with Simulated Learners
summary: We present a framework for evaluating adaptive personalization of educational reading materials with theory-grounded simulated learners. The system builds a learning-objective and knowledge-component ontology from open textbooks, curates it in a browser-based Ontology Atlas, labels textbook chunks with ontology entities, and generates aligned reading-assessment pairs. Simulated readers learn from passages through a Construction-Integration-inspired memory model with DIME-style reader factors, KREC-style misconception revision, and an open New Dale-Chall readability signal. Answers are produced by score-based option selection over the learner's explicit memory state, while BKT drives adaptation. Across three sampled subject ontologies and matched cohorts of 50 simulated learners per condition, adaptive reading significantly improved outcomes in computer science, yielded smaller positive but inconclusive gains in inorganic chemistry, and was neutral to slightly negative in general biology.

AI.tldr: A framework is introduced to evaluate adaptive personalization of educational reading using theory-based simulated learners, showing mixed effectiveness across subjects.
AI.motivation: Personalized reading materials are believed to improve learning, but evaluating such systems with real students is costly and complex. The paper aims to create a principled, simulation-based way to test adaptive reading systems across domains using grounded cognitive and educational theories, enabling fast iteration and comparison without risking student outcomes.
AI.method: The authors automatically build an ontology of learning objectives and knowledge components from open textbooks, refine it in a browser-based Ontology Atlas, and label textbook segments with ontology entities. They then generate aligned reading passages and assessment questions. Simulated learners read and learn via a Construction-Integration-inspired memory model enriched with DIME-style reader characteristics, KREC-style misconception revision processes, and a readability feature based on an open New Dale-Chall metric. Learners answer questions by selecting options based on scores derived from their explicit memory states, while Bayesian Knowledge Tracing (BKT) is used to adapt which reading materials they receive. The framework is tested across three subject-specific ontologies with matched cohorts of 50 simulated learners per condition.
AI.result: In simulation experiments, adaptive reading sequencing driven by BKT improved learning outcomes notably in computer science, showed smaller and statistically inconclusive positive gains in inorganic chemistry, and had neutral to slightly negative effects in general biology. Thus, the effectiveness of the adaptive personalization varied by domain, with clear benefits in one subject, weak or uncertain benefits in another, and no benefit or mild detriment in the third.
AI.conclusion: The framework demonstrates that theory-grounded simulated learners can be used to evaluate adaptive personalization of educational reading at scale, revealing that adaptivity is not uniformly beneficial across domains. These mixed results highlight the importance of domain characteristics, ontology quality, and cognitive modeling choices when designing and deploying adaptive reading systems, and suggest that such simulation-based evaluation can guide refinement before real-world classroom use.

title: GAMMA-Net: Adaptive Long-Horizon Traffic Spatio-Temporal Forecasting Model based on Interleaved Graph Attention and Multi-Axis Mamba
summary: Accurate traffic forecasting is crucial for intelligent transportation systems, supporting effective traffic management, congestion reduction, and informed urban planning. However, traditional models often fail to adequately capture the intricate spatio-temporal dependencies present in traffic data. To overcome these limitations, we introduce GAMMA-Net, a novel approach that integrates Graph Attention Networks (GAT) with multi-axis Selective State Space Models (Mamba). The GAT component uses a self-attention mechanism to dynamically adjust the influence of nodes within the traffic network, enabling adaptive spatial dependency modeling based on real-time conditions. Simultaneously, the Mamba module efficiently models long-term temporal and spatial dynamics without the heavy computational cost of conventional recurrent architectures. Extensive experiments on several benchmark traffic datasets, including METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03, PEMS04, PEMS07, and PEMS08, show that GAMMA-Net consistently outperforms existing state-of-the-art models across different prediction horizons, achieving up to a 16.25% reduction in Mean Absolute Error (MAE) compared to baseline models. Ablation studies highlight the critical contributions of both the spatial and temporal components, emphasizing their complementary role in improving prediction accuracy. In conclusion, the GAMMA-Net model sets a new standard in traffic forecasting, offering a powerful tool for next-generation traffic management and urban planning. The code for this study is available at https://github.com/hdy6438/GAMMA-Net

AI.tldr: The paper proposes GAMMA-Net, a traffic forecasting model that combines Graph Attention Networks with multi-axis Mamba state space models to better capture complex spatio-temporal dependencies, achieving significant performance gains over existing methods on multiple benchmark datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing traffic forecasting models struggle to fully capture complex and dynamic spatio-temporal dependencies in traffic networks. Traditional methods, and even many deep learning approaches, either model spatial and temporal relationships in a limited way or incur high computational costs, leading to suboptimal prediction accuracy and scalability issues for real-world intelligent transportation systems.
AI.method: GAMMA-Net integrates two key components: (1) a Graph Attention Network (GAT) that uses self-attention over the traffic graph to dynamically weight the influence of different nodes based on current conditions, enabling adaptive spatial dependency modeling; and (2) a multi-axis Selective State Space Model (Mamba) module that efficiently models long-range temporal and spatial dynamics with lower computational overhead than recurrent architectures. The model is trained and evaluated on multiple standard traffic datasets using common forecasting metrics such as MAE.
AI.result: Across several benchmark datasets (METR-LA, PEMS-BAY, PEMS03, PEMS04, PEMS07, PEMS08) and different prediction horizons, GAMMA-Net consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving up to 16.25% lower Mean Absolute Error. Ablation experiments demonstrate that both the GAT-based spatial module and the Mamba-based temporal module are essential, and that their combination yields the best performance.
AI.conclusion: GAMMA-Net effectively captures complex spatio-temporal dependencies in traffic data by combining graph attention for adaptive spatial modeling with Mamba state space models for efficient long-range temporal modeling. The approach sets a new performance benchmark on multiple traffic forecasting datasets and offers a practical, more accurate tool to support intelligent traffic management and urban planning, with open-source code provided for reproducibility and further research.

title: Expressing Social Emotions: Misalignment Between LLMs and Human Cultural Emotion Norms
summary: The expression of emotions that serve social purposes, such as asserting independence or fostering interdependence, is central to human interactions and varies systematically across cultures. As LLMs are increasingly used to simulate human behavior in culturally nuanced interactions, it is important to understand whether they faithfully capture human patterns of social emotion expression. When LLM responses are not culturally aligned, their utility is compromised -- particularly when users assume they are interacting with a culturally attuned interlocutor, and may act on advice that proves inappropriate in their cultural context. We present a psychologically informed evaluation framework of cross-cultural social emotion expression in LLMs. Using a human study comparing European American and Latin American participants' expression of engaging and disengaging emotions, we evaluate six frontier LLMs on their ability to reflect culturally differentiated patterns for expressing social emotions. We find systematic misalignment between model and human behavior: all models express engaging emotions more than disengaging ones, with particularly stark differences observed for the generally well-represented European American persona. We further highlight that LLM responses are highly concentrated and deterministic, failing to capture the diversity of human responses in expressing social emotions. Our ablation analyses reveal that these patterns are robust to sampling temperatures, partially sensitive to prompt language, and dependent on the response elicitation format. Together, our findings highlight limitations in how current LLMs represent the interaction of cultural and emotional axes, particularly when expressing social emotions, with direct implications for their deployment in cross-cultural affective contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether large language models (LLMs) reproduce human, culture-specific patterns of expressing social emotions, finding systematic misalignment and low diversity in model responses across cultural contexts.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly deployed in socially and culturally nuanced settings (e.g., advice, counseling-like interactions, simulations), it becomes crucial to know if they accurately mirror how people from different cultures express emotions serving social goals like independence or interdependence. Misaligned emotional expressions can mislead users who assume the model shares or understands their cultural norms, potentially resulting in inappropriate or harmful advice. The authors want a principled, psychology-grounded way to test LLMs on this dimension.
AI.method: The authors design a psychologically informed evaluation framework grounded in prior work on engaging vs. disengaging emotions and cultural differences in social emotion expression. They run a human study comparing European American and Latin American participants’ tendencies to express engaging (e.g., connection-promoting) versus disengaging (e.g., autonomy-asserting) emotions in social contexts. Using these human patterns as a benchmark, they then probe six frontier LLMs, prompting them to respond as if they were individuals from the two cultural groups, and measure how closely the models’ emotion-expression patterns match the observed human data. They also perform ablation analyses by varying sampling temperature, prompt language, and response elicitation formats to test the robustness of observed patterns.
AI.result: All tested LLMs show systematic misalignment with human behavior. Across conditions, models overproduce engaging emotions relative to disengaging ones, and this bias is particularly strong for the European American persona, a group presumably well represented in training data. Furthermore, model outputs are narrow and deterministic: they exhibit much less variability and diversity in emotional expression than human respondents. The ablation studies show that these misalignment patterns persist across different sampling temperatures, are somewhat affected by the language of the prompt, and depend notably on how responses are elicited, but overall remain robust.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs fail to accurately encode how cultural background and emotional goals jointly shape social emotion expression. They exhibit a generic preference for engaging emotions and lack the diversity and nuance found in human responses, even for culturally dominant groups. These limitations call into question the reliability of LLMs in cross-cultural affective or socially sensitive applications and underscore the need for better modeling, evaluation, and alignment of cultural-emotional dynamics before such systems are used as culturally attuned interlocutors.

title: GRAIL: Autonomous Concept Grounding for Neuro-Symbolic Reinforcement Learning
summary: Neuro-symbolic Reinforcement Learning (NeSy-RL) combines symbolic reasoning with gradient-based optimization to achieve interpretable and generalizable policies. Relational concepts, such as "left of" or "close by", serve as foundational building blocks that structure how agents perceive and act. However, conventional approaches require human experts to manually define these concepts, limiting adaptability since concept semantics vary across environments. We propose GRAIL (Grounding Relational Agents through Interactive Learning), a framework that autonomously grounds relational concepts through environmental interaction. GRAIL leverages large language models (LLMs) to provide generic concept representations as weak supervision, then refines them to capture environment-specific semantics. This approach addresses both sparse reward signals and concept misalignment prevalent in underdetermined environments. Experiments on the Atari games Kangaroo, Seaquest, and Skiing demonstrate that GRAIL matches or outperforms agents with manually crafted concepts in simplified settings, and reveals informative trade-offs between reward maximization and high-level goal completion in the full environment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GRAIL, a framework that automatically learns environment-specific relational concepts for neuro-symbolic reinforcement learning using large language models and interaction, achieving performance comparable or superior to manually engineered concepts.
AI.motivation: Neuro-symbolic RL relies on relational concepts (e.g., spatial relations) as building blocks for interpretable and generalizable policies, but these concepts are normally hand-designed by experts. This manual specification is inflexible, does not adapt to different environments, and may misalign with how concepts should be interpreted in each setting. Additionally, underdetermined environments often suffer from sparse rewards and concept misalignment, hampering learning. The paper aims to remove the need for manual concept engineering and to better ground concepts to the specific environment.
AI.method: The authors propose GRAIL (Grounding Relational Agents through Interactive Learning), a framework that uses large language models to generate generic, weakly supervised representations of relational concepts and then refines these through interaction with the environment. GRAIL integrates these grounded concepts into a neuro-symbolic RL agent, allowing the agent to learn both task policies and environment-specific semantics of relational concepts. The learning process addresses sparse reward and misalignment by using high-level, language-based guidance coupled with trial-and-error in the target environment.
AI.result: Across several Atari games (Kangaroo, Seaquest, Skiing), GRAIL achieves performance on par with or better than baselines that rely on manually engineered relational concepts in simplified scenarios. In the full environments, the framework exposes and manages trade-offs between maximizing game scores (rewards) and achieving high-level, language-specified goals, indicating that the grounded concepts meaningfully structure behavior.
AI.conclusion: GRAIL shows that relational concepts for neuro-symbolic RL can be autonomously grounded rather than hand-crafted, using LLMs for weak supervision and environment interaction for refinement. This improves adaptability to new environments, maintains or improves performance relative to expert-designed concepts, and offers a way to balance low-level reward optimization with high-level goal satisfaction. The approach suggests a path toward more scalable and interpretable neuro-symbolic agents that can flexibly adapt their conceptual vocabulary to different tasks.

title: When Misinformation Speaks and Converses: Rethinking Fact-Checking in Audio Platforms
summary: Audio platforms have evolved beyond entertainment. They have become central to public discourse, from podcasts and radio to WhatsApp voice notes and live streams. With millions of shows and hundreds of millions of listeners, audio platforms are now a major channel for misinformation. Yet existing fact-checking pipelines are mostly designed for written claims, overlooking the unique properties of spoken media. We argue that audio misinformation is not merely textual content with transcripts: it is structurally different because it is both spoken - carrying persuasive force through prosody, pacing, and emotion - and conversational - unfolding across turns, speakers, and episodes. These dual properties introduce verification difficulties that traditional methods rarely face. This position paper synthesizes evidence across modalities and platforms, examines datasets and methods, and highlights why existing pipelines fail on audio. We argue that advancing fact-checking requires rethinking verification pipelines around the spoken and conversational realities of audio.

AI.tldr: Audio platforms are now key channels for misinformation, but current fact-checking systems built for text fail to address the unique spoken and conversational nature of audio content.
AI.motivation: Audio has become central to public discourse, with vast scale and influence, making it a significant vector for misinformation. However, most verification and fact-checking infrastructures were developed for text and do not account for what makes audio distinct and more persuasive, leaving a critical gap in combating misinformation in this medium.
AI.method: This is a position/survey paper: it synthesizes evidence across different modalities and platforms, reviews existing datasets and methods, and analytically compares current text-centric fact-checking pipelines against the requirements imposed by spoken and conversational audio content.
AI.result: The paper identifies concrete ways in which existing fact-checking pipelines break down when applied to audio, especially because they ignore prosodic, emotional, and dialogic structures and assume that working with transcripts is sufficient. It articulates the dimensions of audio’s ‘spoken’ and ‘conversational’ properties that create new verification challenges.
AI.conclusion: Effective fact-checking of audio misinformation cannot be achieved by simply transcribing speech and applying text-based methods. Verification workflows must be redesigned to explicitly handle the persuasive, prosodic aspects of speech and the multi-turn, multi-speaker nature of audio conversations and episodes.

title: Step-GRPO: Internalizing Dynamic Early Exit for Efficient Reasoning
comment: This paper has been accepted for publication at the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Large reasoning models that use long chain-of-thought excel at problem-solving yet waste compute on redundant checks. Curbing this overthinking is hard: training-time length penalties can cripple ability, while inference-time early-exit adds system overhead. To bridge this gap, we propose Step-GRPO, a novel post-training framework that internalizes dynamic early-exit capabilities directly into the model. Step-GRPO shifts the optimization objective from raw tokens to semantic steps by utilizing linguistic markers to structure reasoning. We introduce a Dynamic Truncated Rollout mechanism that exposes the model to concise high-confidence trajectories during exploration, synergized with a Step-Aware Relative Reward that dynamically penalizes redundancy based on group-level baselines. Extensive experiments across three model sizes on diverse benchmarks demonstrate that Step-GRPO achieves a superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off. On Qwen3-8B, our method reduces token consumption by 32.0\% compared to the vanilla model while avoiding the accuracy degradation observed in traditional length-penalty methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Step-GRPO, a post-training method that teaches reasoning models to stop earlier without losing accuracy, greatly cutting redundant chain-of-thought tokens.
AI.motivation: Large reasoning models solve problems well using long chain-of-thought reasoning, but they waste substantial compute on redundant steps. Existing ways to shorten reasoning either hurt performance (training-time length penalties) or require external early-exit mechanisms that add system complexity and overhead. There is a need for a method that allows models to learn when to stop thinking dynamically while maintaining or improving accuracy and efficiency, and that can be incorporated directly into the model via post-training.
AI.method: The authors propose Step-GRPO, a post-training framework that optimizes over semantic reasoning steps instead of raw tokens. They use linguistic markers to segment model outputs into discrete steps and then apply a reinforcement-style optimization. A Dynamic Truncated Rollout mechanism explores and favors shorter, high-confidence reasoning trajectories by cutting rollouts once sufficient confidence or completeness is reached. This is combined with a Step-Aware Relative Reward function that measures and penalizes redundancy at the group (step) level using relative baselines, encouraging concise but correct reasoning. The framework is applied to existing models of multiple sizes without architectural changes.
AI.result: Across several benchmarks and three model sizes, Step-GRPO improves the trade-off between accuracy and efficiency compared to baselines. On Qwen3-8B, the method reduces token usage by 32.0% relative to the vanilla model while preserving accuracy, in contrast to traditional length-penalty approaches that cause noticeable performance drops. Overall, models trained with Step-GRPO perform similarly or better in accuracy while generating substantially fewer reasoning tokens.
AI.conclusion: Step-GRPO successfully internalizes dynamic early-exit behavior into large reasoning models through step-level optimization, yielding concise chains-of-thought without sacrificing accuracy. By operating at the level of semantic steps, using dynamic truncated rollouts, and applying step-aware relative rewards, the approach alleviates overthinking and redundant reasoning. The method offers a practical, architecture-agnostic post-training technique to substantially cut token—and thus compute—costs while maintaining strong problem-solving performance.

title: StageMem: Lifecycle-Managed Memory for Language Models
summary: Long-horizon language model systems increasingly rely on persistent memory, yet many current designs still treat memory primarily as a static store: write an item, place it into memory, and retrieve it later if needed. We argue that this framing does not adequately capture the practical memory-control problem in deployed LLM systems. In realistic settings, the difficulty is often not merely forgetting useful information, but retaining too many uncertain items, forgetting important content in the wrong order, and giving users little trust in what will persist over time. We propose StageMem, a lifecycle-managed memory framework that treats memory as a stateful process rather than a passive repository. StageMem organizes memory into three stages -- transient, working, and durable memory -- and models each item with explicit confidence and strength. This separates shallow admission from long-term commitment: information may first be written at low cost and only later be promoted, retained, updated, or evicted as evidence and pressure evolve. Under controlled pressure regimes, this decomposition helps preserve late-important content while keeping memory burden and deeper-tier pollution more controlled. Adapted external tasks provide boundary evidence that the same schema remains compatible with stronger retrieval structure outside pure synthetic control. We present StageMem as a principled decomposition of the memory-control problem for language model systems.

AI.tldr: StageMem is a lifecycle-managed memory framework for long-horizon LLM systems that treats memory as a stateful process with staged persistence and confidence/strength modeling, improving control over what is retained, promoted, or forgotten.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM memory systems largely treat memory as a static key-value store, which fails to capture the practical challenges of long-term operation: over-retaining uncertain or low-quality items, forgetting important information in the wrong order, and giving users little clarity about what will persist. There is a need for a principled framework that explicitly manages the lifecycle and reliability of memory items under resource and capacity constraints.
AI.method: The authors propose StageMem, which structures memory into three lifecycle stages—transient, working, and durable. Each memory item is associated with explicit confidence and strength scores. Items are first admitted cheaply into transient memory and then, under evolving evidence and resource pressure, can be promoted, retained, updated, or evicted. The system operates under controlled pressure regimes that simulate realistic capacity constraints and evaluates how this staged schema behaves, including on adapted external tasks that introduce more realistic retrieval structure beyond synthetic experiments.
AI.result: The staged memory decomposition improves the system’s ability to preserve information that becomes important later while limiting memory burden and reducing pollution of deeper, more durable tiers. Experiments under controlled pressure regimes and adapted external tasks show that StageMem’s schema remains compatible with more structured retrieval setups, indicating robustness beyond purely synthetic control scenarios.
AI.conclusion: StageMem provides a principled way to conceptualize and implement memory control in LLM systems by treating memory as a process with explicit lifecycle stages and confidence/strength modeling. This approach better balances short-term flexibility with long-term reliability, leading to more trustworthy and efficient persistent memory behavior in long-horizon language model deployments.

title: Beyond Text-Dominance: Understanding Modality Preference of Omni-modal Large Language Models
summary: Native Omni-modal Large Language Models (OLLMs) have shifted from pipeline architectures to unified representation spaces. However, this native integration gives rise to a critical yet underexplored phenomenon: modality preference. To bridge this gap, we first systematically quantify modality preference of OLLMs using a newly-curated conflict-based benchmark and the modality selection rate metric. Our evaluation of ten representative OLLMs reveals a notable paradigm shift: unlike the ``text-dominance'' of traditional VLMs, most OLLMs exhibit a pronounced visual preference. To further understand the underlying mechanism, we conduct layer-wise probing and demonstrate that such modality preference is not static but emerges progressively in the mid-to-late layers. Building upon these insights, we leverage these internal signals to diagnose cross-modal hallucinations, achieving competitive performance across three downstream multi-modal benchmarks without task-specific data. Our work provides both a mechanistic understanding and a practical tool for building more trustworthy OLLMs. Our code and related resources are publicly available at: https://github.com/icip-cas/OmniPreference

AI.tldr: The paper studies and measures modality preference in native omni-modal large language models (OLLMs), finding a strong visual bias and using internal model signals to detect cross-modal hallucinations.
AI.motivation: Native OLLMs integrate multiple modalities (e.g., text and vision) into a unified representation, unlike traditional pipeline VLMs. However, how these models internally balance or prefer one modality over another (modality preference) is poorly understood, despite its importance for reliability and hallucination control. The authors aim to quantify and explain this phenomenon and exploit it to improve trustworthiness.
AI.method: 1) Construct a conflict-based benchmark where visual and textual inputs intentionally conflict, and define the modality selection rate metric to quantify which modality the model follows. 2) Evaluate ten representative OLLMs on this benchmark. 3) Perform layer-wise probing of internal representations to see how and where modality preference emerges across network depth. 4) Use these internal modality-preference signals as a diagnostic tool to detect cross-modal hallucinations on downstream multimodal benchmarks, without using task-specific training data.
AI.result: The study finds that, in contrast to traditional vision-language models which tend to favor text (text dominance), most modern OLLMs show a strong preference for visual information. Layer-wise analysis indicates that modality preference is dynamic and forms progressively in mid-to-late layers rather than being fixed from the input. Using these internal modality-preference indicators, the authors achieve competitive performance in detecting cross-modal hallucinations on three multimodal benchmarks, despite not relying on task-specific fine-tuning data.
AI.conclusion: Modality preference is a systematic and measurable behavior in OLLMs, with a shift from text dominance in older architectures to visual preference in native omni-modal models. This preference emerges through the model’s internal processing rather than being hard-wired at the input. These insights enable a practical, data-efficient method for diagnosing cross-modal hallucinations, contributing to the development of more trustworthy omni-modal language models. The authors release code and resources to support further research.

title: When Informal Text Breaks NLI: Tokenization Failure, Distribution Shift, and Targeted Mitigations
summary: We study how informal surface forms degrade NLI accuracy in ELECTRA-small (14M) and RoBERTa-large (355M) across four transforms applied to SNLI and MultiNLI: slang substitution, emoji replacement, Gen-Z filler tokens, and their combination. Slang substitution (replacing formal words with informal equivalents, e.g., "going to" -> "gonna", "friend" -> "homie") causes minimal degradation (at most 1.1pp): slang vocabulary falls largely within WordPiece coverage, so the tokenizer handles it without signal loss. Emoji replaces content words with Unicode characters that ELECTRA's WordPiece tokenizer maps to [UNK], destroying the input signal before any learned parameters see it (93.6% of emoji examples contain at least one [UNK], mean 2.91 per example). Noise tokens (no cap, deadass, tbh) are fully in-vocabulary but absent from NLI training data, consistent with the model assigning them inferential weight they do not carry. The two failure modes respond to different interventions: preprocessing recovers emoji accuracy by normalizing text before tokenization; augmentation handles noise by exposing the model to noise-bearing examples during training. A hybrid of both achieves 88.93% on the combined variant for ELECTRA on SNLI (up from 75.88%), with no statistically significant drop on clean text. Against GPT-4o-mini zero-shot, unmitigated ELECTRA is significantly worse on transformed variants (p < 0.0001); hybrid ELECTRA surpasses it across all SNLI variants and reaches statistical parity on MultiNLI.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how informal language (slang, emojis, Gen‑Z filler tokens) impacts NLI performance of ELECTRA-small and RoBERTa-large on SNLI/MultiNLI, and proposes preprocessing plus data augmentation to recover robustness without hurting clean-text accuracy.
AI.motivation: NLI models are typically evaluated and trained on clean, formal text, but real-world user text in social media and messaging is full of informal variants such as slang, emojis, and filler expressions. These surface-level changes should not alter the underlying semantic relations in NLI, yet models often fail on such inputs, revealing brittleness in tokenization and training regimes. The authors aim to precisely characterize which types of informality hurt current Transformer-based NLI models, why they fail (tokenization vs. learned semantics), and how to mitigate these weaknesses efficiently.
AI.method: The authors apply four controlled text transforms to SNLI and MultiNLI: (1) slang substitution, where standard phrases are replaced with informal equivalents; (2) emoji replacement, where content words are replaced with emojis; (3) Gen-Z noise tokens (e.g., “no cap”, “deadass”, “tbh”) inserted; and (4) a combination of these. They evaluate ELECTRA-small (14M) and RoBERTa-large (355M) on these variants, analyze tokenizer behavior (WordPiece/[UNK] incidence) and training-data coverage, then test interventions: (a) text preprocessing to normalize emojis before tokenization, and (b) data augmentation to include noise-token examples during training. They compare baseline and mitigated models against GPT-4o-mini in zero-shot on the same transformed benchmarks and use statistical tests to assess significance.
AI.result: Slang substitutions cause almost no accuracy drop (≤1.1 percentage points), because slang mostly maps cleanly into the existing WordPiece vocabulary. Emoji replacement severely degrades performance for ELECTRA: many emojis map to [UNK], leading to substantial information loss (93.6% of emoji examples have at least one [UNK], with on average 2.91 [UNK] tokens per example). Gen-Z noise tokens, although in-vocabulary, are unseen during NLI training; models incorrectly treat them as semantically meaningful, which degrades inference. Preprocessing that normalizes emojis before tokenization largely recovers emoji performance, while data augmentation with noise-bearing examples mitigates the impact of filler tokens. A hybrid approach combining both yields 88.93% accuracy on the combined transform variant of SNLI for ELECTRA (up from 75.88%), without significant degradation on the original clean SNLI. In head-to-head comparison, unmitigated ELECTRA performs significantly worse than GPT-4o-mini on transformed inputs (p < 0.0001), but the hybrid ELECTRA surpasses GPT-4o-mini on all SNLI variants and achieves statistical parity on MultiNLI.
AI.conclusion: The main conclusion is that informal language harms NLI models via two distinct mechanisms: (1) tokenization failures for out-of-vocabulary symbols like emojis, which can be fixed via input normalization prior to tokenization; and (2) semantic over-interpretation of in-vocabulary but pragmatically empty noise tokens, which is best addressed via targeted data augmentation. Slang is comparatively benign because it is well-covered by existing subword vocabularies. A simple hybrid strategy (preprocessing + augmentation) substantially improves robustness to informal variants without compromising performance on clean data and can even close or reverse the gap to larger, stronger baselines like GPT-4o-mini on these robustness benchmarks.

title: Skilldex: A Package Manager and Registry for Agent Skill Packages with Hierarchical Scope-Based Distribution
comment: 8 pages, 1 figure, 5 tables. IEEE conference format
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) agents are increasingly extended at runtime via skill packages, structured natural-language instruction bundles loaded from a well-known directory. Community install tooling and registries exist, but two gaps persist: no public tool scores skill packages against Anthropic's published format specification, and no mechanism bundles related skills with the shared context they need to remain mutually coherent. We present Skilldex, a package manager and registry for agent skill packages addressing both gaps. The two novel contributions are: (1) compiler-style format conformance scoring against Anthropic's skill specification, producing line-level diagnostics on description specificity, frontmatter validity, and structural adherence; and (2) the skillset abstraction, a bundled collection of related skills with shared assets (vocabulary files, templates, reference documents) that enforce cross-skill behavioral coherence. Skilldex also provides supporting infrastructure: a three-tier hierarchical scope system, a human-in-the-loop agent suggestion loop, a metadata-only community registry, and a Model Context Protocol (MCP) server. The system is implemented as a TypeScript CLI (skillpm / spm) with a Hono/Supabase registry backend, and is open-source.

AI.tldr: Introduces Skilldex, a package manager/registry for LLM agent skills that validates skill packages against Anthropic’s spec and bundles coherent, related skills via a new “skillset” abstraction.
AI.motivation: LLM agents are extended with skill packages, but there is no public tooling to automatically score/validate these packages against Anthropic’s skill spec, nor a way to bundle interrelated skills with shared context so they stay behaviorally coherent.
AI.method: Design and implement Skilldex: a TypeScript CLI and registry backend. Key mechanisms include (1) compiler-style conformance scoring and line-level diagnostics for skill packages, assessing description specificity, frontmatter, and structural adherence; (2) the “skillset” abstraction for grouping related skills and shared assets (vocabulary, templates, references); plus supporting infra like a hierarchical scope system, human-in-the-loop suggestion loop, metadata-only registry, and MCP server.
AI.result: Skilldex provides automated validation and scoring of skill packages, enables coherent bundling of related skills and assets, and delivers a working open-source toolchain (CLI, registry, MCP server) integrated with existing LLM agent ecosystems.
AI.conclusion: By adding spec-conformance scoring and coherent skill bundling via skillsets, Skilldex fills key gaps in current LLM agent extension tooling, offering a practical, open-source package manager and registry that can improve the quality, consistency, and manageability of agent skill packages.

title: Crowded in B-Space: Calibrating Shared Directions for LoRA Merging
summary: Merging separately trained LoRA adapters is a practical alternative to joint multi-task training, but it often hurts performance. Existing methods usually treat the LoRA update $ΔW = BA$ as a single object and do not distinguish the two LoRA matrices. We show that the main source of LoRA merge interference comes from the output-side matrix $B$. Across tasks, $B$ repeatedly uses a small set of shared directions, while $A$ remains much more task-specific. As a result, the merged adapter overemphasizes these shared directions, and task-specific information is lost. We propose Pico (Pre-merge interference calibration in output-space), a data-free method that calibrates $B$ before merge by downscaling over-shared directions and then rescaling the merged update. Pico plugs directly into existing merging methods such as Task Arithmetic, TIES, and TSV-M. Across eight different benchmarks from math, coding, finance, and medical domains, Pico improves average accuracy by 3.4-8.3 points over the corresponding base method and achieves the best overall average performance. Pico also enables merged adapters to outperform the LoRA trained with all task data. These results show that LoRA merging works better when the two LoRA matrices are treated separately.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and fixes a key cause of performance loss when merging separately trained LoRA adapters by calibrating only the output matrix B, leading to better multi-task performance than both naive merging and joint training.
AI.motivation: Merging multiple LoRA adapters trained on different tasks is attractive because it avoids costly joint multi-task training, but naive merging often degrades performance. Prior work generally treats the LoRA update ΔW=BA as a single unit and does not analyze how the two low-rank matrices A and B contribute differently to interference between tasks. There is a need to understand this interference and to design a simple, data-free way to improve LoRA merging so that merged adapters retain task-specific information and possibly rival or surpass jointly trained adapters.
AI.method: The authors empirically analyze the roles of the two LoRA matrices A (input-side) and B (output-side) across tasks. They show that the output matrix B tends to reuse a small set of shared directions across tasks, while A remains more task-specific. They then propose Pico (Pre-merge interference calibration in output-space), a data-free procedure that (1) decomposes or analyzes B to detect over-shared directions, (2) downscales these directions before merging to reduce interference, and (3) rescales the final merged update to preserve overall update magnitude. Pico is designed as a plug-in pre-processing step that can be combined with existing merging approaches such as Task Arithmetic, TIES, and TSV-M.
AI.result: On eight benchmarks spanning math, coding, finance, and medical domains, integrating Pico with standard LoRA merging methods consistently improves performance. Pico yields average accuracy gains of 3.4–8.3 percentage points over the corresponding base merging methods and achieves the best overall average performance across all evaluated settings. Notably, in some cases merged adapters with Pico even outperform a single LoRA trained jointly on all task data, demonstrating that better merging can beat traditional multi-task LoRA training.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that the main source of interference in LoRA merge comes from the output-side matrix B due to overused shared directions across tasks. Treating A and B separately and calibrating B via Pico significantly mitigates this interference. As a result, LoRA merging becomes more effective, often surpassing both naive merging and joint multi-task LoRA training. The work advocates for output-space-aware, matrix-specific treatment in future adapter merging techniques.

title: The Cognitive Penalty: Ablating System 1 and System 2 Reasoning in Edge-Native SLMs for Decentralized Consensus
comment: Working paper. 14 pages, 3 figures, 6 tables. Code and dataset: https://github.com/smarizvi110/sentinel-bench
summary:
Decentralized Autonomous Organizations (DAOs) are inclined explore Small Language Models (SLMs) as edge-native constitutional firewalls to vet proposals and mitigate semantic social engineering. While scaling inference-time compute (System 2) enhances formal logic, its efficacy in highly adversarial, cryptoeconomic governance environments remains underexplored. To address this, we introduce Sentinel-Bench, an 840-inference empirical framework executing a strict intra-model ablation on Qwen-3.5-9B. By toggling latent reasoning across frozen weights, we isolate the impact of inference-time compute against an adversarial Optimism DAO dataset. Our findings reveal a severe compute-accuracy inversion. The autoregressive baseline (System 1) achieved 100% adversarial robustness, 100% juridical consistency, and state finality in under 13 seconds. Conversely, System 2 reasoning introduced catastrophic instability, fundamentally driven by a 26.7% Reasoning Non-Convergence (cognitive collapse) rate. This collapse degraded trial-to-trial consensus stability to 72.6% and imposed a 17x latency overhead, introducing critical vulnerabilities to Governance Extractable Value (GEV) and hardware centralization. While rare (1.5% of adversarial trials), we empirically captured "Reasoning-Induced Sycophancy," where the model generated significantly longer internal monologues (averaging 25,750 characters) to rationalize failing the adversarial trap. We conclude that for edge-native SLMs operating under Byzantine Fault Tolerance (BFT) constraints, System 1 parameterized intuition is structurally and economically superior to System 2 iterative deliberation for decentralized consensus.
  Code and Dataset: https://github.com/smarizvi110/sentinel-bench

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether adding chain-of-thought style reasoning (System 2) to a small language model actually improves security and reliability for DAO governance, and finds that it often makes things worse.
AI.motivation: DAOs are exploring the use of small language models as on-chain or edge-native "constitutional firewalls" to screen governance proposals and resist social engineering attacks. Many current best practices suggest using more inference-time compute (System 2 reasoning) to get better, more reliable decisions. However, it is unclear if this additional reasoning helps or harms robustness and economic security in highly adversarial cryptoeconomic settings. The authors aim to rigorously test this assumption.
AI.method: The authors build Sentinel-Bench, an empirical benchmark consisting of 840 model inferences on an adversarial dataset derived from Optimism DAO governance. They perform a strict intra-model ablation on Qwen-3.5-9B by toggling latent reasoning (System 2) on and off while keeping model weights fixed. They then compare a simple autoregressive baseline (System 1) to the same model with explicit reasoning enabled, measuring adversarial robustness, juridical consistency, consensus stability across trials, latency, and failure modes such as reasoning non-convergence and sycophancy.
AI.result: The System 1 baseline (no explicit reasoning) achieved perfect performance on the benchmark: 100% robustness to adversarial traps, 100% juridical consistency, and finalized decisions in under 13 seconds. In contrast, enabling System 2 reasoning caused a major performance inversion. It produced a 26.7% rate of Reasoning Non-Convergence (cognitive collapse), reduced trial-to-trial consensus stability to 72.6%, and imposed a 17x latency overhead. These issues increase Governance Extractable Value (GEV) risk and encourage hardware centralization. Additionally, in 1.5% of adversarial trials they observed "Reasoning-Induced Sycophancy," where the model generated very long internal monologues (around 25,750 characters) to justify failing adversarial traps.
AI.conclusion: For small language models deployed as edge-native governance agents under Byzantine Fault Tolerance constraints, simple System 1 style parameterized intuition outperforms System 2 iterative reasoning in both structural robustness and economic efficiency. Increasing inference-time compute and encouraging long reasoning traces can introduce new instability and attack surfaces rather than improving safety in adversarial DAO governance. Designers of decentralized governance systems should be cautious about defaulting to chain-of-thought or heavy reasoning for on-chain decision-making SLMs.

title: HeLa-Mem: Hebbian Learning and Associative Memory for LLM Agents
summary:
Long-term memory is a critical challenge for Large Language Model agents, as fixed context windows cannot preserve coherence across extended interactions. Existing memory systems represent conversation history as unstructured embedding vectors, retrieving information through semantic similarity. This paradigm fails to capture the associative structure of human memory, wherein related experiences progressively strengthen interconnections through repeated co-activation. Inspired by cognitive neuroscience, we identify three mechanisms central to biological memory: association, consolidation, and spreading activation, which remain largely absent in current research.
  To bridge this gap, we propose HeLa-Mem, a bio-inspired memory architecture that models memory as a dynamic graph with Hebbian learning dynamics. HeLa-Mem employs a dual-level organization: (1) an episodic memory graph that evolves through co-activation patterns, and (2) a semantic memory store populated via Hebbian Distillation, wherein a Reflective Agent identifies densely connected memory hubs and distills them into structured, reusable semantic knowledge. This dual-path design leverages both semantic similarity and learned associations, mirroring the episodic-semantic distinction in human cognition. Experiments on LoCoMo demonstrate superior performance across four question categories while using significantly fewer context tokens. Code is available on GitHub: https://github.com/ReinerBRO/HeLa-Mem

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HeLa-Mem, a biologically inspired long-term memory architecture for LLM agents that uses a dynamic Hebbian graph plus a distilled semantic store to achieve better long-horizon reasoning with fewer context tokens.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle with long-term coherence because of fixed context windows and simplistic memory systems that store past interactions as unstructured embeddings and retrieve them only via semantic similarity. This ignores how human memory works, where associations strengthen through repeated co-activation and where episodic experiences are consolidated into structured semantic knowledge. The authors want to bring key mechanisms from cognitive neuroscience—association, consolidation, and spreading activation—into LLM memory design to improve performance on long-context tasks while reducing context size.
AI.method: The authors design HeLa-Mem, a memory architecture that represents memories as nodes in a dynamic graph updated with Hebbian learning rules based on co-activation (“cells that fire together wire together”). The system has two levels: (1) an episodic memory graph capturing individual interactions/events and their associative links, which evolve as related memories are repeatedly co-activated, and (2) a semantic memory store built through Hebbian Distillation, where a Reflective Agent periodically analyzes the episodic graph, identifies densely connected hubs, and distills them into structured, reusable semantic knowledge (e.g., facts, rules, schemas). Retrieval uses both semantic similarity and graph-based associations (spreading activation) instead of pure embedding search. They evaluate HeLa-Mem on the LoCoMo benchmark with different question types.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo benchmark, HeLa-Mem outperforms prior memory systems across four categories of questions that test long-term reasoning and recall. It achieves this while using significantly fewer context tokens than baselines, indicating more efficient and targeted retrieval. The results suggest that incorporating associative structure, consolidation, and spreading activation yields more coherent and effective long-term memory for LLM agents than unstructured embedding stores.
AI.conclusion: HeLa-Mem demonstrates that bio-inspired mechanisms from human memory—such as Hebbian association, episodic-to-semantic consolidation, and spreading activation—can substantially improve LLM agents’ long-term memory capabilities. Modeling memory as a dynamic associative graph plus a distilled semantic store leads to better performance and token efficiency on long-horizon tasks compared to standard vector-store approaches. The work argues for moving beyond pure semantic-similarity retrieval toward cognitively grounded memory architectures and provides an open-source implementation for further research.

title: ClimAgent: LLM as Agents for Autonomous Open-ended Climate Science Analysis
summary: Climate research is pivotal for mitigating global environmental crises, yet the accelerating volume of multi-scale datasets and the complexity of analytical tools have created significant bottlenecks, constraining scientific discovery to fragmented and labor-intensive workflows. While the emergence Large Language Models (LLMs) offers a transformative paradigm to scale scientific expertise, existing explorations remain largely confined to simple Question-Answering (Q&A) tasks. These approaches often oversimplify real-world challenges, neglecting the intricate physical constraints and the data-driven nature required in professional climate science.To bridge this gap, we introduce ClimAgent, a general-purpose autonomous framework designed to execute a wide spectrum of research tasks across diverse climate sub-fields. By integrating a unified tool-use environment with rigorous reasoning protocols, ClimAgent transcends simple retrieval to perform end-to-end modeling and analysis.To foster systematic evaluation, we propose ClimaBench, the first comprehensive benchmark for real-world climate discovery. It encompasses challenging problems spanning 5 distinct task categories derived from professional scenarios between 2000 and 2025. Experiments on ClimaBench demonstrate that ClimAgent significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, achieving a 40.21% improvement over original LLM solutions in solution rigorousness and practicality. Our code are available at https://github.com/usail-hkust/ClimAgent.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ClimAgent, an autonomous LLM-based framework and a new benchmark, ClimaBench, to automate and rigorously evaluate climate research workflows, showing large gains over standard LLM use.
AI.motivation: Climate science relies on increasingly large, multi-scale datasets and complex tools, creating fragmented, manual, and time-consuming research workflows that limit discovery. Existing uses of LLMs in this field mostly focus on simple Q&A and ignore physical constraints, modeling, and data-driven analysis central to real climate research. There is a need for a system that can handle realistic, end-to-end climate research tasks and for a benchmark to evaluate such systems rigorously.
AI.method: The authors design ClimAgent, an autonomous, general-purpose LLM-driven framework tailored to climate science. It integrates a unified environment for calling diverse analytical tools and follows explicit reasoning protocols to go beyond retrieval and perform full modeling and analysis workflows. To evaluate such systems, they build ClimaBench, a benchmark composed of challenging, real-world climate discovery problems from 5 task categories derived from professional climate research scenarios spanning 2000–2025. They compare ClimAgent’s performance to state-of-the-art baselines and original LLM solutions on this benchmark.
AI.result: On ClimaBench, ClimAgent substantially outperforms baseline methods, including straightforward LLM-based approaches. It shows a 40.21% improvement over original LLM solutions in terms of solution rigorousness and practicality, indicating better adherence to scientific standards and more usable outputs for climate research tasks.
AI.conclusion: ClimAgent demonstrates that LLM-based autonomous frameworks, when equipped with structured tool use and rigorous reasoning protocols, can meaningfully support end-to-end climate research rather than just Q&A. The accompanying ClimaBench benchmark provides a systematic way to evaluate such systems on realistic, professional climate science tasks. Together, they suggest a promising path for scaling climate expertise and accelerating scientific discovery with AI tools.

title: DART: Mitigating Harm Drift in Difference-Aware LLMs via Distill-Audit-Repair Training
summary: Large language models (LLMs) tuned for safety often avoid acknowledging demographic differences, even when such acknowledgment is factually correct (e.g., ancestry-based disease incidence) or contextually justified (e.g., religious hiring preferences). This identity-blindness yields incorrect responses, unnecessary refusals, or generic "equal-treatment" defaults. We study this via difference-awareness classification: given a question involving demographic groups, the task is not to answer directly, but to classify whether a correct answer requires recognizing group differences (yes) or whether groups should be treated identically (no). Crucially, fine-tuning for accuracy triggers harm drift: model-generated explanations become increasingly harmful as decision accuracy improves, whether by elaborating harmful content, introducing problematic assumptions, or failing to flag harms the baseline identified. To mitigate this, we introduce DART (Distill--Audit--Repair Training), which distills label-conditioned reasoning from a teacher, audits outputs for harm drift cases relative to baseline, and repairs problematic cases via severity-weighted fine-tuning. On eight benchmarks, DART improves Llama-3-8B-Instruct accuracy from 39.0% to 68.8%, with largest gains on equal-treatment prompts (11.3% -> 72.6%), while reducing harm drift cases by 72.6%. It also transfers to 280 open-ended real-world queries across medical, legal, policy, and educational domains, improving difference-appropriate responses from 39.8% to 77.5% while reducing refusals from 34.3% to 3.0%. Our results demonstrate that accuracy and safety need not conflict when explicit detection and repair mechanisms are in place.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that large language models can be trained to correctly recognize when demographic group differences matter for an answer, while simultaneously reducing harmful reasoning, using a new DART training pipeline.
AI.motivation: Safety-tuned LLMs often default to identity-blind answers, avoiding any acknowledgment of demographic differences even when such differences are medically, legally, or contextually important. This leads to wrong answers, over-refusals, and superficial equal-treatment claims. Moreover, naively improving accuracy on these tasks causes "harm drift," where the model’s explanations become more harmful. The authors want a way to improve difference-awareness accuracy without sacrificing safety, and to explicitly manage and reduce harm drift.
