AI.method: They formalize a difference-awareness classification task: given a question mentioning demographic groups, the model must decide whether correct answering requires recognizing group differences (yes) or equal treatment (no). They then propose DART (Distill–Audit–Repair Training). First, they distill label-conditioned reasoning from a teacher model. Next, they audit the distilled outputs, detecting cases where improved accuracy introduces more harmful reasoning compared to a safety-tuned baseline (harm drift). Finally, they repair these cases via severity-weighted fine-tuning, emphasizing the most problematic instances so the student model learns accurate yet non-harmful reasoning patterns.
AI.result: On eight benchmarks, applying DART to Llama-3-8B-Instruct raises difference-awareness accuracy from 39.0% to 68.8%, with particularly large gains on equal-treatment prompts (from 11.3% to 72.6%). At the same time, detected harm-drift cases are reduced by 72.6%. In a separate evaluation on 280 open-ended real-world queries from domains like medicine, law, policy, and education, the DART-trained model improves difference-appropriate responses from 39.8% to 77.5%, and cuts refusal rates from 34.3% to 3.0%, indicating better, more nuanced handling of demographic information without extra harm.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling when demographic differences matter, and using a Distill–Audit–Repair pipeline, allows LLMs to become more accurate and nuanced about group-sensitive questions while also reducing harmful reasoning side effects. The work argues that accuracy and safety are not inherently in conflict if harm drift is systematically detected and corrected during training, leading to models that can appropriately acknowledge differences without defaulting to blanket refusals or identity-blind responses.

title: Alignment Imprint: Zero-Shot AI-Generated Text Detection via Provable Preference Discrepancy
summary: Detecting AI-generated text is an important but challenging problem. Existing likelihood-based detection methods are often sensitive to content complexity and may exhibit unstable performance. In this paper, our key insight is that modern Large Language Models (LLMs) undergo alignment (including fine-tuning and preference tuning), leaving a measurable distributional imprint. We theoretically derive this imprint by abstracting the alignment process as a sequence of constrained optimization steps, showing that the log-likelihood ratio can naturally decompose into implicit instructional biases and preference rewards. We refer to this quantity as the Alignment Imprint. Furthermore, to mitigate the instability in high-entropy regions, we introduce Log-likelihood Alignment Preference Discrepancy (LAPD), a standardized information-weighted statistic based on alignment imprint. We provide statistical guarantee that alignment-based statistics dominate Fast-DetectGPT in performance. We also theoretically show that LAPD strictly improves the unweighted alignment scores when the aligned and base models are close in distribution. Extensive experiments show that LAPD achieves an improvement 45.82% relative to the strongest existing baselines, yielding large and consistent gains across all settings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new alignment-based statistic, LAPD, to more robustly detect AI-generated text by leveraging the distributional imprint left by LLM alignment.
AI.motivation: Likelihood-based AI-text detectors are unstable and sensitive to content complexity, especially in high-entropy regions, leading to unreliable detection of AI-generated text. The authors want a more principled and stable detection method.
AI.method: They model LLM alignment (fine-tuning and preference tuning) as constrained optimization steps and derive a resulting log-likelihood ratio decomposition into instructional bias and preference reward, termed the Alignment Imprint. Building on this, they define LAPD (Log-likelihood Alignment Preference Discrepancy), an information-weighted and standardized statistic based on the alignment imprint, designed to be more stable in high-entropy regions. They provide theoretical analysis comparing alignment-based statistics to Fast-DetectGPT and show when LAPD improves over unweighted alignment scores.
AI.result: The authors prove that alignment-based statistics theoretically dominate Fast-DetectGPT and that LAPD strictly improves unweighted alignment scores when aligned and base models are close in distribution. Empirically, they report that LAPD improves detection performance by 45.82% relative to the strongest prior baselines and provides consistent gains across diverse experimental settings.
AI.conclusion: Model alignment leaves a measurable distributional imprint that can be exploited for more reliable AI-text detection. The proposed LAPD statistic, grounded in this alignment imprint, theoretically and empirically outperforms prior likelihood-based detectors and mitigates instability in high-entropy regions.

title: A Community-Based Approach for Stance Distribution and Argument Organization
summary: The proliferation of online debate platforms and social media has led to an unprecedented volume of argumentative content on controversial topics from multiple perspectives. While this wealth of perspectives offers opportunities for developing critical thinking and breaking filter bubbles (Pariser 2011), the sheer volume and complexity of arguments make it challenging for readers to synthesize and comprehend diverse viewpoints effectively. We present an unsupervised graph-based approach for community-based argument organization that helps users navigate and understand complex argumentative landscapes. Our system analyzes collections of topic-focused articles and constructs a rich interaction graph by capturing multiple relationship types between arguments: topic similarity, semantic coherence, shared keywords, and common entities. We then employ community detection to identify argument communities that reveal homogeneous and heterogeneous viewpoint distributions. The detected communities are simplified through strategic graph operations to present users with digestible, yet comprehensive summaries of key argumentative patterns. Our approach requires no training data and can effectively process hundreds of articles while preserving nuanced relationships between arguments. Experimental results demonstrate our system's ability to identify meaningful argument communities and present them in an interpretable manner, facilitating users' understanding of complex socio-political debates.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an unsupervised, graph-based system that clusters arguments from many articles into coherent communities so users can more easily understand complex online debates.
AI.motivation: Online debates and social media produce huge numbers of arguments on controversial topics, which in theory can broaden perspectives and reduce filter bubbles, but in practice are too numerous and complex for users to synthesize. There is a need for automatic methods that structure and organize this argumentative content so readers can grasp the main viewpoints and their relationships without manually reading everything.
AI.method: The authors design an unsupervised, graph-based pipeline. First, they gather topic-focused articles and extract arguments. They build a rich interaction graph where nodes represent arguments and edges encode several relationship types: topic similarity, semantic coherence, shared keywords, and common named entities. Next, they run community detection on this multi-relational graph to discover clusters (communities) of arguments that reflect homogeneous (similar stance) or heterogeneous (mixed stance) viewpoint distributions. They then apply graph simplification and aggregation operations to each community to create more concise, human-digestible representations that still preserve important argumentative relationships.
AI.result: Experiments on collections containing hundreds of articles show that the system can automatically discover argument communities that correspond to meaningful viewpoints and subtopics in complex debates. The resulting community structures and their simplified summaries are interpretable to users and preserve nuanced relations among arguments, helping reveal key argumentative patterns present in socio-political discussions.
AI.conclusion: The proposed unsupervised, graph-based community detection framework is effective for organizing large-scale argumentative content into understandable communities of arguments without requiring training data. By modeling multiple relationship types between arguments and simplifying detected communities, the system supports users in navigating and comprehending complex debates, contributing a scalable tool for critical engagement with online argumentative discourse.

title: Playing Psychic: Using Thought Trees to Predict Reasoning Models Accuracy on Coding Tasks
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have shown that test-time scaling can substantially improve model performance on complex tasks, particularly in the coding domain. Under this paradigm, models use a larger token budget during inference to generate intermediate reasoning traces before producing a final answer. However, current evaluations primarily rely on competitive programming benchmarks, which may not capture the full range of reasoning abilities. In this work, we perform a systematic study of frontier reasoning models to understand their performance on real-world coding benchmarks. To gain more insights into the performance of such models, we devise a programmatic way to {\em automatically generate} coding tasks of arbitrary difficulty and structure from existing benchmarks. Using this framework, our analysis reveals that the structure of a reasoning trace, not just its contents, is a strong predictor of correctness. Motivated by this, we propose structured thought-trees as means to represent reasoning traces. To illustrate their use, we train a lightweight classifier on features extracted from thought-trees to predict trace correctness, and demonstrate that flagging and retrying structurally anomalous traces based on the extracted features yields consistent gains at lower complexity levels.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how frontier large language models perform on real-world coding tasks under test-time scaling, introduces a way to automatically generate coding problems of varying difficulty, shows that the structure of reasoning traces strongly predicts correctness, and proposes structured thought-trees plus a simple classifier to flag bad traces and improve performance by retrying.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time scaling work on LLMs, especially for coding, mainly evaluates on competitive programming benchmarks, which may not reflect real-world coding needs. There is also limited understanding of what aspects of long reasoning traces actually correlate with correctness. The authors want a more realistic evaluation setup and a deeper understanding of how reasoning structure affects performance, so that these insights can be used to improve model outputs without greatly increasing complexity.
AI.method: They (1) systematically evaluate frontier reasoning-focused LLMs on real-world coding benchmarks instead of just competitive programming tasks; (2) design a programmatic pipeline to automatically generate coding tasks of varying difficulty and structural properties from existing benchmarks; (3) analyze generated reasoning traces and identify structural properties that correlate with correctness; (4) formalize reasoning traces as structured thought-trees; and (5) train a lightweight classifier on features extracted from these thought-trees to predict whether a trace is likely correct, then use this to flag anomalous traces and trigger retries.
AI.result: The analysis shows that the structural characteristics of a model’s reasoning trace are strong predictors of whether the final answer is correct, beyond the semantic content alone. Using thought-trees and a simple classifier on their structural features allows the system to reliably detect anomalous traces and, by selectively retrying those, to improve performance on coding tasks, particularly at lower complexity levels, without a large increase in inference cost.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning performance of LLMs on coding tasks depends importantly on how their reasoning is structured, not just what is written. By automatically generating diverse coding problems and modeling reasoning traces as structured thought-trees, one can build lightweight predictors of trace correctness. Using these predictors to flag and retry structurally anomalous reasoning leads to consistent performance gains at modest additional cost, suggesting that structure-aware control of test-time scaling is a promising direction for improving LLM reasoning on real-world coding tasks.

title: Incentivizing Parametric Knowledge via Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards for Cross-Cultural Entity Translation
summary: Cross-cultural entity translation remains challenging for large language models (LLMs) as literal or phonetic renderings are usually yielded instead of culturally appropriate translations in context. However, relevant knowledge may already be encoded in model parameters during large-scale pre-training. To incentivize the effective use of parametric knowledge, we propose EA-RLVR (Entity-Anchored Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards), a training framework that optimizes cross-cultural entity translation without relying on external knowledge bases. EA-RLVR anchors supervision on a verifiable, entity-level reward signal and incorporates lightweight structural gates to stabilize optimization. This design steers the model toward learning a robust reasoning process rather than merely imitating reference translations. We evaluate EA-RLVR on XC-Translate and observe consistent improvements in both entity translation accuracy and out-of-domain generalization. Specifically, training on merely 7k samples boosts Qwen3-14B's entity translation accuracy from 23.66\% to 31.87\% on a 50k test set comprising entirely unseen entities. The learned entity translation ability also transfers to general translation, yielding +1.35 XCOMET on WMT24++, which scales to +1.59 with extended optimization. Extensive analyses of $pass@k$ dynamics and reward formulations attribute these gains to superior sampling efficiency and a stable optimization landscape.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes EA-RLVR, a reinforcement learning framework that improves cross-cultural entity translation in LLMs using only parametric knowledge and verifiable entity-level rewards, significantly boosting entity translation accuracy and general translation quality.
AI.motivation: Cross-cultural entity translation is difficult for LLMs, which often default to literal or phonetic renderings instead of culturally appropriate equivalents. Although LLMs contain rich parametric knowledge from pre-training, current approaches underutilize this knowledge and often depend on external knowledge bases or simple imitation learning, which do not robustly generalize to unseen entities or domains. The authors aim to exploit the knowledge already in the model to perform better entity translation and reasoning without external resources.
AI.method: They introduce EA-RLVR (Entity-Anchored Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards), a training framework that uses reinforcement learning with an entity-level reward signal that is verifiable (e.g., matching or consistency criteria around entities) and does not depend on external KBs. The framework adds lightweight structural gates to stabilize optimization and guide the model toward a consistent reasoning process in producing culturally appropriate entity translations. The method focuses the optimization on entity translation behavior, encouraging the model to use its internal knowledge more effectively instead of just copying reference translations.
AI.result: On the XC-Translate benchmark, EA-RLVR consistently improves entity translation accuracy and out-of-domain generalization. For example, with only 7k training samples, Qwen3-14B’s entity translation accuracy increases from 23.66% to 31.87% on a 50k test set containing entirely unseen entities. The improved entity translation capability transfers to general translation: on WMT24++, the model gains +1.35 XCOMET, which further scales to +1.59 with more optimization steps. Analyses of pass@k behavior and different reward formulations suggest that EA-RLVR yields better sampling efficiency and a smoother, more stable optimization landscape.
AI.conclusion: EA-RLVR effectively exploits parametric knowledge in LLMs to substantially improve cross-cultural entity translation without relying on external knowledge bases. By anchoring RL on verifiable entity-level rewards and using structural gates, the framework encourages robust reasoning rather than mere imitation, leading to better accuracy on unseen entities and improved general translation performance. The analysis indicates that the gains arise from enhanced sampling efficiency and more stable optimization, suggesting EA-RLVR is a promising direction for aligning LLMs with nuanced, knowledge-intensive translation tasks.

title: LLMs can persuade only psychologically susceptible humans on societal issues, via trust in AI and emotional appeals, amid logical fallacies
summary: Scarce longitudinal evidence examines LLMs' persuasiveness and humanness along time-evolving psychological frameworks. We introduce Talk2AI, a longitudinal framework quantifying psycho-social, reasoning and affective dimensions of LLMs' persuasiveness about polarizing societal topics. In a four-way longitudinal setup, Talk2AI's 770 participants engaged in structured conversations with one of four leading LLMs on topics like climate change, social media misinformation, and math anxiety. This produced 3,080 conversations over 60,000 turns. After each wave, participants reported conviction in their initial topic stance, perceived opinion change, LLM's perceived humanness, a self-donation to the topic and a textual explanation. Feedback time series showed longitudinal inertia in convictions, indicating some human anchoring to initial opinions even after repeated exposure to AI-generated arguments. Interestingly, NLP analyses revealed that both humans and LLMs relied on fallacious reasoning in 1 conversational quip every 6, countering the ``LLMs as superior systems" stereotype behind LLMs' cognitive surrender. LLMs' perceived humanness was most learnable from sociodemographic, psychological and engagement features ($R^2=0.44$), followed by opinion change ($R^2=0.34$), conviction ($R^2=0.26$) and personal endowment ($R^2=0.24$). Crucially, explainable AI (XAI) indicated: (i) the presence of individuals more susceptible to LLM-based opinion changes; (ii) psychological susceptibility to LLM-convincing consisted of having more trust in LLMs, being more agreeable and extraverted and with a higher need for cognition. A multiverse approach with mixed-effects models confirmed XAI results, alongside strong individual differences. Talk2AI provides a grounded framework and evidence for detecting how GenAI can influence human opinions via multiple psycho-social pathways in AI-human digital platforms.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Talk2AI, a longitudinal framework to study how LLMs persuade humans and appear human-like over time on polarizing topics, using 3,080 AI–human conversations and multi-wave measurements of beliefs, behavior, and explanations.
AI.motivation: There is limited longitudinal evidence on how LLMs shape human opinions and how their perceived humanness and persuasiveness evolve over time, especially on polarizing societal topics. Existing work often treats LLM persuasion as static and overestimates their reasoning superiority. The authors aim to create an empirical, time-aware framework to quantify multiple psychological and social dimensions of LLM influence in realistic AI–human interactions.
AI.method: The authors design Talk2AI, a four-wave longitudinal experimental setup where 770 participants each converse with one of four leading LLMs on polarizing topics (e.g., climate change, misinformation, math anxiety). They collect 3,080 conversations totaling 60,000 turns. After each wave, participants report conviction in their initial stance, perceived opinion change, perceived humanness of the LLM, self-donation (a monetary endowment decision) to the topic, and a written explanation. They apply NLP to detect fallacious reasoning in both human and LLM utterances. They then build predictive models (with explainable AI techniques and a multiverse of mixed-effects model specifications) to explain variance in perceived humanness, opinion change, conviction, and personal endowment from sociodemographic, psychological, and engagement features, and to identify psychologically susceptible subgroups.
AI.result: Participants showed longitudinal inertia: their conviction remained anchored to initial opinions despite repeated AI-generated arguments. NLP analysis showed both humans and LLMs used fallacious reasoning about once every six conversational turns, challenging the notion that LLMs are consistently superior reasoners. Predictive models explained a substantial portion of variance in perceived LLM humanness (R^2 = 0.44), with lower but meaningful R^2 for opinion change (0.34), conviction (0.26), and personal endowment (0.24). Explainable AI analysis revealed (i) subgroups of individuals more prone to opinion change due to LLM interaction, and (ii) that higher trust in LLMs, greater agreeableness, extraversion, and need for cognition are associated with greater psychological susceptibility to LLM persuasion. A multiverse of mixed-effects models confirmed these effects and highlighted strong individual differences.
AI.conclusion: Talk2AI offers a longitudinal, multi-dimensional framework for studying how generative AI influences human beliefs and behaviors through psycho-social pathways in AI–human platforms. The findings show that while people tend to remain anchored to initial opinions, LLMs can still shift opinions and behavior in identifiable, psychologically susceptible subgroups. LLMs do not consistently exhibit superior reasoning, as both sides frequently employ fallacies. The framework and empirical results can inform safer, more transparent design and governance of persuasive AI systems by highlighting who is most vulnerable to LLM influence and which traits and engagement patterns drive that vulnerability.

title: Prune, Interpret, Evaluate: A Cross-Layer Transcoder-Native Framework for Efficient Circuit Discovery via Feature Attribution
summary: Existing feature-interpretation pipelines typically operate on uniformly sampled units, but only a small fraction of cross-layer transcoder (CLT) features matter for a target behavior, with the rest resulting in expensive feature explaining and evaluating costs. We introduce the first CLT-native end-to-end framework, PIE, connecting Pruning, automatic Interpretation, and interpretation Evaluation, enabling systematic measurement of behavioral fidelity and downstream interpretability under pruning. To achieve this, we propose Feature Attribution Patching (FAP), a patch-grounded attribution method that scores CLT features by aggregating gradient-weighted write contributions, and FAP-Synergy, a synergy-aware reranking procedure. We evaluate pruning using KL-divergence behavior retention and assess interpretation quality with FADE-style metrics. Across IOI and Doc-String, across budgets $K \in \{50, 100, 200, 400, 800\}$, and across FAP, FAP-Synergy, Activation-Magnitude, and ACDC-style pruning, the FAP family consistently achieves the best or near-best fidelity, with FAP-Synergy providing its clearest gains in strict-budget regimes. On IOI with CLTs for Llama-3.2-1B and Gemma-2-2B, pruning to $K=100$ features matches the KL fidelity that random selection from the active feature set requires $\approx 4$k features to achieve ($\approx 40\times$ compression), enabling $\approx 40\times$ fewer interpretation/evaluation calls while substantially reducing low-quality features.

AI.tldr: PIE is an end-to-end framework for pruning and interpreting cross-layer transcoder (CLT) features in language models, using a new attribution method (FAP) and a synergy-aware variant to keep only the most behaviorally important features while maintaining model behavior and improving interpretability efficiency.
AI.motivation: Most existing feature-interpretation methods assume uniformly sampled units (e.g., neurons or features), but in CLT-based models only a small subset of features actually drive a given target behavior. Interpreting all features is wasteful and expensive, leading to high computational and human evaluation costs while mixing in many low-quality or irrelevant features. There is a need for a principled, CLT-native way to (1) decide which features matter for a behavior, (2) automatically interpret them, and (3) evaluate how pruning affects both behavioral fidelity and interpretability quality.
AI.method: The paper introduces PIE, an end-to-end framework that integrates (i) pruning, (ii) automatic interpretation, and (iii) interpretation evaluation for CLT features. The core technical tool is Feature Attribution Patching (FAP), which assigns importance scores to CLT features by aggregating their gradient-weighted write contributions under patching interventions. A second method, FAP-Synergy, adjusts these scores via a synergy-aware reranking step that accounts for feature interactions, particularly beneficial under tight pruning budgets. Pruning strategies based on FAP/FAP-Synergy are compared against baselines such as activation-magnitude and ACDC-style pruning. Behavioral retention is measured with KL-divergence between model output distributions before and after pruning, and interpretation quality is assessed with FADE-style metrics.
AI.result: Across tasks (IOI and Doc-String), budgets K in {50, 100, 200, 400, 800}, and multiple pruning methods, FAP-based pruning consistently yields the best or near-best behavioral fidelity. FAP-Synergy shows the largest improvements in strict-budget regimes (small K). On IOI with CLTs derived from Llama-3.2-1B and Gemma-2-2B, pruning down to K=100 FAP-selected features matches the KL-divergence fidelity that requires about 4,000 randomly selected active features—around 40x compression. This enables roughly 40x fewer interpretation and evaluation calls while simultaneously filtering out many low-quality features.
AI.conclusion: PIE demonstrates that CLT-native, attribution-based pruning can drastically reduce the number of features required to preserve a target behavior while maintaining or improving interpretability quality. FAP and its synergy-aware extension provide more faithful feature importance estimates than magnitude- or ACDC-based baselines, especially under tight feature budgets. This leads to large computational savings and a cleaner, higher-quality feature set for interpretation, establishing PIE as a practical path toward scalable, systematic interpretability of CLT features in large language models.

title: AutoPKG: An Automated Framework for Dynamic E-commerce Product-Attribute Knowledge Graph Construction
summary: Product attribute extraction in e-commerce is bottlenecked by ontologies that are inconsistent, incomplete, and costly to maintain. We present AutoPKG, a multi-agent Large Language Model (LLM) framework that automatically constructs a Product-attribute Knowledge Graph (PKG) from multimodal product content. AutoPKG induces product types and type-specific attribute keys on demand, extracts attribute values from text and images, and consolidates updates through a centralized decision agent that maintains a globally consistent canonical graph. We also propose an evaluation protocol for dynamic PKGs that measures type and key validity, consolidation quality, and edge-level accuracy for value assertions after canonicalization. On a large real-world marketplace catalog dataset from Lazada (Alibaba), AutoPKG achieves up to 0.953 Weighted Knowledge Efficiency (WKE) for product types, 0.724 WKE for attribute keys, and 0.531 edge-level F1 for multimodal value extraction. Across three public benchmarks, our method improves edge-level exact-match F1 by 0.152 and yields a precision gain of 0.208 on the attribute extraction application. Online A/B tests show that AutoPKG-derived attributes increase Gross Merchandise Value (GMV) in Badge by 3.81 percent, in Search by 5.32 percent, and in Recommendation by 7.89 percent, supporting the practical value of AutoPKG in production.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AutoPKG, a multi-agent LLM system that automatically builds and maintains a product-attribute knowledge graph from multimodal e-commerce data, and shows it improves both offline metrics and real-world business KPIs.
AI.motivation: Existing product ontologies in e-commerce are inconsistent, incomplete, static, and expensive to curate and maintain. This limits accurate product attribute extraction and downstream applications like search and recommendation, especially at marketplace scale and in dynamic catalogs. There is a need for a system that can flexibly induce product types and attributes, keep them consistent over time, and leverage multimodal product information without heavy manual ontology engineering.
AI.method: The authors design AutoPKG, a multi-agent framework based on large language models. Different agents handle (1) inducing product types and type-specific attribute keys on demand, (2) extracting attribute values from both text and images, and (3) consolidating all proposed entities and edges into a globally consistent canonical product-attribute knowledge graph via a central decision agent. They also define an evaluation protocol for dynamic PKGs that separately measures the validity of induced types and keys, the quality of consolidation, and edge-level accuracy of canonicalized value assertions, including a multimodal setting.
AI.result: On a large real-world Lazada (Alibaba) catalog dataset, AutoPKG reaches up to 0.953 Weighted Knowledge Efficiency (WKE) on product types, 0.724 WKE on attribute keys, and 0.531 edge-level F1 for multimodal value extraction. On three public benchmarks, it boosts edge-level exact-match F1 by 0.152 and increases precision by 0.208 for attribute extraction. In online A/B tests on production systems, attributes derived from AutoPKG raise Gross Merchandise Value by 3.81% for badges, 5.32% for search, and 7.89% for recommendation.
AI.conclusion: AutoPKG can automatically construct and maintain a high-quality, dynamic product-attribute knowledge graph from multimodal marketplace data, outperforming prior methods in attribute extraction and delivering significant business impact in production search, recommendation, and merchandising systems. The proposed evaluation protocol provides a structured way to assess dynamic PKGs along multiple dimensions, showing that LLM-based multi-agent architectures are viable for large-scale e-commerce knowledge management.

title: MCPO: Mastery-Consolidated Policy Optimization for Large Reasoning Models
summary: Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) has emerged as a promising approach to improve the reasoning abilities of Large Language Models (LLMs). Among RLVR algorithms, Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) and its variants have demonstrated strong performance and high training efficiency. However, GRPO-style objectives exhibit two issues on high accuracy prompts including mastered prompts (rollout accuracy =1) and majority-correct prompts (rollout accuracy in (0.5,1)). For mastered prompts, group-relative advantages vanish, yielding no training signal and unconstrained policy drift that can cause forgetting. For majority-correct prompts, the induced query weight shrinks as accuracy increases, weakening consolidation from partial correctness to mastery. To alleviate this, we propose Mastery-Consolidated Policy Optimization (MCPO), which introduces (i) a hinge-KL regularizer applied exclusively to mastered prompts to bound harmful policy drift between successive gradient steps, and (ii) a weighting mechanism that prioritizes majority-correct prompts to better allocate optimization effort. Extensive experiments across three mathematical benchmarks demonstrate that MCPO consistently improves pass@1 performance. Counter-intuitively, rather than restricting exploration, MCPO boosts pass@k metrics, indicating that mastery consolidation further catalyzes solution diversity.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes Mastery-Consolidated Policy Optimization (MCPO), an RLVR training objective that fixes weaknesses of GRPO on high-accuracy prompts by stabilizing mastered behaviors and emphasizing consolidation on nearly-correct ones, leading to better math reasoning performance of LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing RL with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) methods like GRPO work well overall but behave poorly on high-accuracy prompts: when a model has already mastered a prompt, group-relative advantages disappear, giving no learning signal and allowing the policy to drift and forget; when prompts are mostly but not fully solved, their effective weight in training shrinks as accuracy rises, so the model under-invests in pushing them from partial success to mastery. The authors aim to stabilize mastered capabilities while better consolidating near-mastery behaviors, without sacrificing exploration or diversity of solutions.
AI.method: The authors analyze GRPO-style objectives on two classes of prompts: mastered prompts (all rollouts correct, accuracy = 1) and majority-correct prompts (accuracy between 0.5 and 1). They identify how group-relative advantage computation and query weighting create vanishing gradients and reduced emphasis for precisely those prompts where consolidation and stability are most important. To address this, they introduce Mastery-Consolidated Policy Optimization (MCPO), which adds: (i) a hinge-KL regularizer applied only on mastered prompts, constraining the KL divergence between successive policies to limit harmful drift when advantage signals vanish; and (ii) a modified weighting scheme that increases the relative importance of majority-correct prompts in the objective, so training focuses more on pushing them to full mastery. They then integrate MCPO into an RLVR training pipeline for mathematical reasoning tasks and compare against GRPO variants.
AI.result: Across three math reasoning benchmarks, MCPO consistently improves pass@1 accuracy over baselines using GRPO-style objectives. Surprisingly, despite imposing KL regularization on mastered prompts, MCPO also improves pass@k metrics, suggesting that its mastery consolidation does not reduce exploration but instead promotes a broader and more diverse set of correct solutions. The empirical results indicate better retention of mastered behaviors, stronger consolidation of near-mastered prompts, and overall higher reasoning performance.
AI.conclusion: MCPO effectively resolves two key weaknesses of GRPO-style RLVR training—policy drift on mastered prompts and under-weighting of majority-correct prompts—by combining a mastery-specific hinge-KL regularizer with a consolidation-focused weighting scheme. This yields more stable mastery, better conversion of partial success into full correctness, and improved pass@1 and pass@k performance on mathematical reasoning benchmarks. The findings indicate that carefully managing optimization pressure across mastery levels can simultaneously enhance reliability and diversity of LLM reasoning, and suggest MCPO as a strong default objective for RLVR-based training on verifiable tasks like math.

title: When Choices Become Risks: Safety Failures of Large Language Models under Multiple-Choice Constraints
summary: Safety alignment in large language models (LLMs) is primarily evaluated under open-ended generation, where models can mitigate risk by refusing to respond. In contrast, many real-world applications place LLMs in structured decision-making tasks, such as multiple-choice questions (MCQs), where abstention is discouraged or unavailable. We identify a systematic failure mode in this setting: reformulating harmful requests as forced-choice MCQs, where all options are unsafe, can systematically bypass refusal behavior, even in models that consistently reject equivalent open-ended prompts. Across 14 proprietary and open-source models, we show that forced-choice constraints sharply increase policy-violating responses. Notably, for human-authored MCQs, violation rates follow an inverted U-shaped trend with respect to structural constraint strength, peaking under intermediate task specifications, whereas MCQs generated by high-capability models yield near-saturation violation rates across constraints and exhibit strong cross-model transferability. Our findings reveal that current safety evaluations substantially underestimate risks in structured task settings and highlight constrained decision-making as a critical and underexplored surface for alignment failures.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that safety-aligned LLMs that typically refuse harmful requests in open-ended form can be systematically induced to give unsafe answers when the same requests are posed as forced-choice multiple‑choice questions, revealing a major gap in current safety evaluation methods.
AI.motivation: Current LLM safety evaluations mostly test open-ended generation, where models can avoid harm by refusing to answer. However, many real applications use structured decision formats, like multiple-choice questions, where abstaining is difficult or disallowed. The authors want to understand whether these structured formats create new, underexplored failure modes in safety alignment and whether present evaluations are underestimating real-world risk.
AI.method: The authors construct harmful prompts in multiple-choice format where every option is unsafe, creating a forced-choice setting. They compare model behavior on these MCQs to equivalent open-ended prompts across 14 proprietary and open-source LLMs. They vary the degree of structural constraint (e.g., how strictly the question enforces choosing among options) and examine two sources of MCQs: human-authored and questions generated by powerful models. They then measure and analyze the rate of policy-violating, unsafe responses under different levels of constraint and question sources, as well as the transferability of such adversarial MCQs across models.
AI.result: Forced-choice multiple-choice formats dramatically increase the rate at which models produce policy-violating content, even when those same models reliably refuse the equivalent open-ended harmful prompts. For human-authored MCQs, violation rates exhibit an inverted U-shaped relationship with constraint strength, peaking under intermediate levels of structural constraint. MCQs generated by high-capability models are even more effective: they induce near-saturation violation rates across constraint levels and generalize well as attacks to many other models, indicating strong cross-model transferability of this adversarial format.
AI.conclusion: Safety alignment procedures that primarily evaluate open-ended dialogue significantly underestimate risk in settings where models must make constrained decisions. The forced-choice MCQ format exposes a systematic failure mode: when refusal is discouraged or structurally blocked, even ostensibly safety-aligned models frequently choose harmful options. The paper argues that constrained decision-making tasks should be treated as a key attack surface in LLM safety research and that evaluation and training pipelines must explicitly address these structured-task vulnerabilities.

title: A phenotype-driven and evidence-governed framework for knowledge graph enrichment and hypotheses discovery in population data
summary: Current knowledge graph (KG) construction methods are confirmatory, focusing on recovering known relationships rather than identifying novel or context-dependent nodes. This paper proposes a phenotype-driven and evidence-governed framework that shifts the paradigm toward structured hypothesis discovery and controlled KG expansion. The approach integrates graph neural networks (GNNs) for phenotype discovery, causal inference, probabilistic reasoning and large language models (LLMs) for hypothesis generation and claim extraction within a unified pipeline. The framework prioritizes relationships that are both structurally supported by data and underexplored in the literature. KG expansion is formulated as a multi-objective optimization problem, where candidate claims are jointly evaluated in terms of relevance, structural validation and novelty. Pareto-optimal selection enables the identification of non-dominated claims that balance confirmation and discovery, avoiding trivial or redundant knowledge inclusion. Experiments on heterogeneous population datasets demonstrate that the proposed framework produces more interpretable phenotypes, reveals context-dependent causal structures and generates high-quality claims that align with both data and scientific evidence. Compared to rule-based and LLM-only baselines, the method achieves the best trade-off across plausibility, novelty, validation and relevance. In retrieval-augmented settings, it significantly improves performance (Recall@5=0.98) while reducing hallucination rates (0.05), highlighting its effectiveness in grounding LLM outputs.

AI.tldr: A framework for expanding biomedical knowledge graphs by discovering novel, evidence-backed relationships using phenotypes, GNNs, causal inference, probabilistic reasoning, and LLMs, optimized for both confirmation and discovery.
AI.motivation: Existing knowledge graph construction methods mostly confirm known relationships and do not effectively discover new, context-dependent entities or edges. There is a need for an approach that systematically proposes and evaluates novel hypotheses while keeping them grounded in data and literature, reducing trivial or redundant facts and LLM hallucinations.
AI.method: The paper introduces a phenotype-driven, evidence-governed KG expansion pipeline. It uses graph neural networks to discover phenotypes from heterogeneous population data, applies causal inference and probabilistic reasoning to identify candidate causal structures, and employs large language models for hypothesis generation and claim extraction from literature. KG expansion is cast as a multi-objective optimization problem over candidate claims, jointly scoring relevance, structural validation, and novelty. Pareto-optimal selection is used to choose non-dominated claims that balance confirmatory and exploratory value.
AI.result: On heterogeneous population datasets, the framework yields more interpretable phenotypes, identifies context-dependent causal structures, and generates high-quality claims consistent with both data and scientific evidence. Quantitatively, it outperforms rule-based and LLM-only baselines on the combined criteria of plausibility, novelty, validation, and relevance. In retrieval-augmented scenarios, it reaches Recall@5 of 0.98 while cutting hallucination rate to 0.05, indicating strong grounding of LLM-generated claims.
AI.conclusion: A phenotype-driven, multi-objective, and evidence-governed approach can transform KG construction from mere confirmation to structured hypothesis discovery. Integrating GNNs, causal/probabilistic reasoning, and LLMs within a Pareto-optimization framework enables controlled KG expansion that is both novel and well-grounded in data and literature, improving interpretability and reducing hallucinations compared to conventional or LLM-only methods.

title: x1: Learning to Think Adaptively Across Languages and Cultures
summary: Languages encode distinct abstractions and inductive priors, yet most large language models (LLMs) overlook this diversity by reasoning in a single dominant language. In this work, we introduce x1, a family of reasoning models that can adaptively reason in an advantageous language on a per-instance basis. To isolate the effect of reasoning-language choice, x1 is constructed without expanding the model's knowledge boundaries and is trained by contrasting linguistically distinct reasoning trajectories for the same input. Our extensive experiments demonstrate the benefits of adaptive multilingual reasoning across multilingual mathematical reasoning and culturally grounded tasks. Moreover, our results challenge a simplistic view of scaling laws: while scaling reduces cross-lingual disparities in procedural domains such as math reasoning, it does not eliminate the advantages of culture-associated languages in culturally grounded tasks, as we empirically show that such reasoning enables more efficient and accurate cultural knowledge recall. Overall, our findings establish language choice as a functional component of reasoning, with implications for building more generalist and globally competent reasoning models.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes x1, a family of multilingual reasoning models that dynamically choose the most advantageous language per instance to improve reasoning, without adding new knowledge.
AI.motivation: Most existing LLMs effectively reason in only one dominant language, ignoring that different languages encode different abstractions and inductive priors. This overlooks potential gains from leveraging multiple languages for reasoning and from aligning language choice with the task’s cultural or procedural characteristics. The authors aim to understand and exploit language choice as an active component of reasoning, rather than a passive medium of expression.
AI.method: They design x1, a family of reasoning models that can adaptively select which language to reason in for each input. To isolate the effect of language choice (rather than knowledge expansion), x1 is built so that its knowledge boundaries are not expanded beyond the base model. Training involves contrasting linguistically distinct reasoning trajectories for the same input—i.e., encouraging the model to explore and compare reasoning chains in different languages, then learn when one language yields better reasoning performance. They then evaluate x1 on multilingual mathematical reasoning benchmarks and culturally grounded tasks, comparing adaptive multilingual reasoning vs. fixed-language reasoning at different model scales.
AI.result: Experiments show that allowing the model to adaptively choose its reasoning language improves performance in both multilingual math reasoning and culturally grounded tasks. Scaling the model decreases cross-lingual performance gaps in procedural/math tasks, suggesting scaling helps standardize procedural reasoning across languages. However, even at larger scales, languages associated with particular cultures retain advantages on culturally grounded tasks, where reasoning in those languages yields more efficient and accurate cultural knowledge recall. This indicates persistent, structured differences in how languages support cultural reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Language choice is not just a surface property but a functional component of reasoning in LLMs. Adaptive multilingual reasoning can yield measurable benefits, especially for tasks with strong cultural grounding. While scaling narrows procedural cross-lingual gaps, it does not erase the advantages of culture-associated languages for cultural tasks. These findings imply that future “generalist” reasoning models should incorporate mechanisms to select and mix languages strategically, in order to be more globally competent and to better leverage the diverse inductive priors encoded in human languages.

title: Rule-VLN: Bridging Perception and Compliance via Semantic Reasoning and Geometric Rectification
summary: As embodied AI transitions to real-world deployment, the success of the Vision-and-Language Navigation (VLN) task tends to evolve from mere reachability to social compliance. However, current agents suffer from a "goal-driven trap", prioritizing physical geometry ("can I go?") over semantic rules ("may I go?"), frequently overlooking subtle regulatory constraints. To bridge this gap, we establish Rule-VLN, the first large-scale urban benchmark for rule-compliant navigation. Spanning a massive 29k-node environment, it injects 177 diverse regulatory categories into 8k constrained nodes across four curriculum levels, challenging agents with fine-grained visual and behavioral constraints. We further propose the Semantic Navigation Rectification Module (SNRM), a universal, zero-shot module designed to equip pre-trained agents with safety awareness. SNRM integrates a coarse-to-fine visual perception VLM framework with an epistemic mental map for dynamic detour planning. Experiments demonstrate that while Rule-VLN challenges state-of-the-art models, SNRM significantly restores navigation capabilities, reducing CVR by 19.26% and boosting TC by 5.97%.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Rule-VLN, a large-scale benchmark for socially and legally compliant vision-and-language navigation in urban environments, and proposes SNRM, a zero-shot module that adds rule-awareness and safety to pre-trained navigation agents, improving performance on this challenging task.
AI.motivation: Existing VLN agents focus on reaching goals based mainly on physical feasibility, ignoring social and regulatory constraints such as traffic rules or local prohibitions. This "goal-driven trap" makes them unsafe and unsuitable for real-world deployment, where agents must respect semantic rules ("may I go?"). There is also a lack of large-scale benchmarks that systematically test rule compliance in navigation.
AI.method: 1) Construct Rule-VLN, a large-scale urban VLN benchmark with 29k nodes, where 8k are constrained by 177 categories of regulatory rules (visual and behavioral), organized into four curriculum levels from simple to complex. 2) Propose the Semantic Navigation Rectification Module (SNRM), a plug-and-play, zero-shot module for pre-trained VLN agents that: (a) uses a coarse-to-fine visual perception framework based on vision-language models to detect regulatory constraints; and (b) maintains an epistemic mental map to reason about rule states over time and plan dynamic detours that avoid violations while still reaching the goal.
AI.result: On the new Rule-VLN benchmark, state-of-the-art VLN models perform poorly when required to follow rules, indicating the benchmark’s difficulty and the limitations of existing agents. When augmented with SNRM, agents regain much of their navigation ability under constraints: the Constraint Violation Rate (CVR) is reduced by 19.26%, and the Task Completion (TC) metric is increased by 5.97%, demonstrating improved rule-compliant navigation.
AI.conclusion: Rule-VLN provides a rigorous testbed for rule- and safety-aware navigation, revealing that current VLN systems are vulnerable to a goal-driven trap and frequently violate constraints. The proposed SNRM offers an effective, universal, and zero-shot way to endow pre-trained agents with semantic rule awareness, substantially improving both safety (lower CVR) and effectiveness (higher TC). This lays groundwork for safer embodied AI ready for real-world urban deployment.

title: Freshness-Aware Prioritized Experience Replay for LLM/VLM Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) has achieved impressive success in post-training Large Language Models (LLMs) and Vision-Language Models (VLMs), with on-policy algorithms such as PPO, GRPO, and REINFORCE++ serving as the dominant paradigm. However, these methods discard all collected trajectories after a single gradient update, resulting in poor sample efficiency, particularly wasteful for agentic tasks where multi-turn environment interactions are expensive. While Experience Replay drives sample efficiency in classic RL by allowing agents to reuse past trajectories and prioritize informative ones, directly applying Prioritized Experience Replay (PER) to LLMs fails. The rapid policy evolution of billion-parameter models renders stored priorities stale, causing old high-priority trajectories to dominate sampling long after they have become uninformative. We propose Freshness-Aware PER, which addresses this priority staleness problem by augmenting any PER-based priority with a multiplicative exponential age decay grounded in effective sample size analysis. To the best of our knowledge, Freshness-Aware PER is the first work to successfully apply PER to LLM/VLM reinforcement learning. We evaluate on eight multi-step agentic, reasoning, and math competition tasks with 0.5B, 3B, and 7B models. Freshness-Aware PER significantly outperforms on-policy baselines, achieving +46% on NQ Search, +367% on Sokoban, and +133% on VLM FrozenLake, while standard PER without age decay consistently degrades performance. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/Vision-CAIR/Freshness-Aware-PER.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Freshness-Aware Prioritized Experience Replay (PER) to improve sample efficiency in reinforcement learning fine-tuning of large language and vision-language models by decaying the priority of older trajectories.
AI.motivation: On-policy RL methods like PPO and GRPO dominate RL fine-tuning of LLMs/VLMs but are highly sample-inefficient because they discard trajectories after a single use, which is particularly costly in multi-turn, agentic tasks. Classic RL uses Experience Replay/PER to reuse data, but naively applying PER to rapidly changing, billion-parameter policies fails due to stale priorities that cause old, no-longer-useful trajectories to dominate training.
AI.method: The authors analyze the priority staleness problem when using PER with fast-evolving LLM/VLM policies and propose Freshness-Aware PER, which modifies any PER scheme by multiplying priorities with an exponential age-based decay factor. This decay is theoretically grounded via effective sample size analysis, ensuring that older trajectories gradually lose influence as the policy drifts away from the one that generated them. The approach is plug-and-play for PER-style replay buffers in RL for foundation models.
AI.result: Across eight multi-step agentic, reasoning, and math competition benchmarks and three model scales (0.5B, 3B, 7B parameters), Freshness-Aware PER markedly outperforms strong on-policy baselines and standard PER. Reported gains include +46% on NQ Search, +367% on Sokoban, and +133% on a VLM FrozenLake task. In contrast, PER without age decay consistently harms performance, validating that handling freshness is critical.
AI.conclusion: Freshness-Aware PER demonstrates that experience replay can be made effective for RL fine-tuning of large language and vision-language models when priority staleness is explicitly controlled. By incorporating an exponential age decay into PER, the method enables substantial sample-efficiency and performance gains over on-policy RL, establishing a practical recipe for replay-based training in rapidly evolving foundation model policies.

title: Small Model as Master Orchestrator: Learning Unified Agent-Tool Orchestration with Parallel Subtask Decomposition
summary: Multi-agent systems (MAS) demonstrate clear advantages in tackling complex problems by coordinating diverse agents and external tools. However, most existing orchestration methods rely on static workflows or serial agent scheduling, and are further constrained by heterogeneous interface protocols between tools and agents. This leads to high system complexity and poor extensibility. To mitigate these issues, we propose Agent-as-Tool, a unified parallel orchestration paradigm that abstracts both agents and tools into a standardized, learnable action space with protocol normalization and explicit state feedback. Building on this paradigm, we train a lightweight orchestrator, ParaManager, which decouples planning decisions from subtask solving, enabling state-aware parallel subtask decomposition, delegation, and asynchronous execution. For training, we adopt a two-stage ParaManager training pipeline. It improves robustness by incorporating supervised fine-tuning (SFT) trajectories equipped with recovery mechanisms, and further applies reinforcement learning (RL) to achieve an optimal balance among task success, protocol compliance, diversity, and reasoning efficiency. Experiments show that ParaManager achieves strong performance across multiple benchmarks and exhibits robust generalization under unseen model pools.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Agent-as-Tool and a lightweight orchestrator, ParaManager, to standardize and parallelize orchestration of agents and tools in multi-agent systems, improving robustness, extensibility, and performance via an SFT+RL training pipeline.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent system orchestrations rely on static workflows or serial scheduling and suffer from heterogeneous interfaces between agents and tools, causing high complexity, limited flexibility, and poor extensibility. There is a need for a unified and learnable orchestration paradigm that can handle diverse agents/tools, support parallel execution, and remain robust and generalizable.
AI.method: 1) Introduce Agent-as-Tool, which abstracts both agents and tools into a unified, learnable action space with normalized protocols and explicit state feedback, enabling a common interface. 2) Design ParaManager, a lightweight orchestrator that decouples high-level planning from subtask solving and performs state-aware parallel subtask decomposition, delegation, and asynchronous execution. 3) Train ParaManager via a two-stage pipeline: (a) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on trajectories that include recovery mechanisms to increase robustness; (b) reinforcement learning (RL) to optimize a composite objective over task success, protocol compliance, diversity, and reasoning efficiency. 4) Evaluate ParaManager on multiple benchmarks and under unseen model pools.
AI.result: ParaManager achieves strong empirical performance on several benchmarks and maintains robust generalization when orchestrating previously unseen sets of models (model pools). It manages parallel tasks effectively while adhering to protocols and balancing success, diversity, and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Unifying agents and tools into a standardized action space (Agent-as-Tool) and orchestrating them via a lightweight, trainable manager (ParaManager) enables more flexible, parallel, and extensible multi-agent systems. The two-stage SFT+RL training pipeline yields a robust orchestrator that generalizes well across different benchmarks and model configurations, mitigating the complexity and limitations of prior static or serial orchestration approaches.

title: MeasHalu: Mitigation of Scientific Measurement Hallucinations for Large Language Models with Enhanced Reasoning
summary: The accurate extraction of scientific measurements from literature is a critical yet challenging task in AI4Science, enabling large-scale analysis and integration of quantitative research findings. However, Large Language Models (LLMs) frequently exhibit severe hallucinations, which significantly undermine the reliability of automated scientific document understanding systems. To address this problem, we propose MeasHalu, a novel framework for mitigating scientific measurement hallucinations through enhanced reasoning and targeted optimization. We first present a fine-grained taxonomy of measurement-specific hallucinations, categorizing errors across quantities, units, modifiers, and relations. Our approach incorporates a two-stage reasoning-aware fine-tuning strategy using augmented scientific data and process-based supervision. Furthermore, we introduce a progressive reward curriculum designed to penalize specific hallucination types, significantly improving extraction faithfulness. Experimental results demonstrate that MeasHalu substantially reduces hallucination rates and improves overall accuracy on the MeasEval benchmark. This work provides a targeted solution to a key bottleneck in automated scientific knowledge extraction, facilitating more trustworthy and scalable machine-assisted scientific literature analysis.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MeasHalu, a framework to reduce hallucinations when extracting scientific measurements with LLMs, using a taxonomy of errors, reasoning-aware fine-tuning, and reward curricula to improve faithfulness on the MeasEval benchmark.
AI.motivation: Accurate extraction of quantitative measurements from scientific literature is essential for large-scale analysis and integration of research results, but current LLM-based systems often hallucinate measurements, units, and related details, limiting their reliability in AI4Science applications.
AI.method: The authors define a fine-grained taxonomy of measurement-related hallucinations (quantities, units, modifiers, relations) and propose MeasHalu, which uses a two-stage reasoning-aware fine-tuning strategy on augmented scientific data with process-based supervision, combined with a progressive reward curriculum that penalizes specific hallucination types during optimization.
AI.result: On the MeasEval benchmark, MeasHalu significantly reduces hallucination rates and increases extraction accuracy compared with baseline systems, showing clear improvements in faithfulness of measurement extraction.
AI.conclusion: MeasHalu offers a targeted, effective approach to mitigating scientific measurement hallucinations in LLMs, addressing a key bottleneck in automated scientific knowledge extraction and enabling more trustworthy and scalable machine-assisted analysis of scientific literature.

title: Mini-BEHAVIOR-Gran: Revealing U-Shaped Effects of Instruction Granularity on Language-Guided Embodied Agents
comment: 23 pages, Keywords: Language Grounding, Language Granularity, Instruction Following Agent, Width-based Planning Research Area: Multimodality and Language Grounding to Vision, Robotics and Beyond Research Area Keywords: vision language navigation, multimodality, neurosymbolic approaches
summary: Instruction granularity is an important yet poorly controlled variable in language-guided embodied AI. Existing benchmarks typically pair each task with a single static instruction, making it difficult to study how agent behavior changes when the same task is described at different levels of detail. We introduce Mini-BEHAVIOR-Gran, a new benchmark for controlled studies of instruction granularity that extends Mini-BEHAVIOR with multiple instruction variants per task, ranging from high-level goal descriptions to step-by-step guidance. Using this benchmark, we compare four candidate metrics for cross-task granularity quantification: token count, entity count, action-verb count, and planning-width, and find that width correlates most consistently with agent performance. Using width to organize training and evaluation further reveals a non-monotonic U-shaped relationship between instruction granularity and performance, with peaks at both fine and coarse extremes. Further analysis suggests that the coarse-granularity performance rebound is associated with shallow grounding, where agents learn vision-dominant policies.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Mini-BEHAVIOR-Gran, a benchmark to systematically study how different levels of instruction detail affect performance of language-guided embodied AI agents.
AI.motivation: In embodied AI, agents follow language instructions to perform tasks in simulated or real environments. However, existing benchmarks usually provide only one static instruction per task, limiting our ability to understand how varying instruction granularity (from high-level goals to detailed step-by-step instructions) influences agent behavior and learning. There is a need for a controlled setting where the same task is expressed at different granularities and for principled ways to quantify this granularity across tasks.
AI.method: The authors extend the existing Mini-BEHAVIOR benchmark by creating Mini-BEHAVIOR-Gran, in which each task is associated with multiple instruction variants spanning a spectrum from coarse, goal-level descriptions to fine, step-wise guidance. They then propose and empirically compare four metrics for quantifying instruction granularity across tasks: raw token count, entity count, action-verb count, and a new measure called planning-width (capturing how many parallel subgoals or branches an instruction implies). They evaluate language-guided embodied agents trained and tested under different instruction granularities, organized according to these metrics, and examine correlations between these measures and agent performance.
AI.result: Among the four candidate metrics, planning-width shows the most consistent correlation with agent performance, outperforming simpler length-based measures (token count) and structural content measures (entity and action-verb counts). When instructions and experiments are organized by width, the authors observe a U-shaped relationship between instruction granularity and performance: agents perform best under very fine-grained, step-by-step instructions and also under very coarse, high-level goal instructions, while intermediate levels of detail lead to worse performance. Additional analysis indicates that the performance rebound at coarse granularity is linked to agents learning shallow, vision-dominant policies that rely less on deep language grounding.
AI.conclusion: Mini-BEHAVIOR-Gran provides a controlled benchmark for studying instruction granularity in language-guided embodied AI. Planning-width is a more reliable cross-task granularity metric than simple length or count-based measures. Agent performance does not improve monotonically with more detailed instructions; instead, it follows a U-shaped curve with strong performance at both extremes of granularity. The rebound at coarse instructions appears to be due to shallow grounding, where agents depend more on visual cues than on nuanced language understanding. These findings highlight the need to consider both instruction structure and grounding behavior when designing and evaluating embodied AI systems.

title: No One Fits All: From Fixed Prompting to Learned Routing in Multilingual LLMs
comment: Accepted as a long findings paper at ACL 2026
summary: Translation-based prompting is widely used in multilingual LLMs, yet its effectiveness varies across languages and tasks. We evaluate prompting strategies across ten languages of different resource levels and four benchmarks. Our analysis shows that no single strategy is universally optimal. Translation strongly benefits low-resource languages even when translation quality is imperfect, high-resource languages gain little, and prompt-based self-routing underperforms explicit translation. Motivated by these findings, we formulate prompting strategy selection as a learned decision problem and introduce lightweight classifiers that predict whether native or translation-based prompting is optimal for each instance. The classifiers achieve statistically significant improvements over fixed strategies across four benchmarks and generalize to unseen task formats not observed during training. Further analysis reveals that language resource level, rather than translation quality alone, determines when translation is beneficial.

AI.tldr: The paper studies when translation-based prompting helps multilingual LLMs and proposes learned classifiers to choose between native and translated prompts per instance.
AI.motivation: Translation-based prompting is common in multilingual LLM use, but its benefits are inconsistent across languages and tasks. Practitioners lack guidance on when to use native-language prompts versus translation into a high-resource language like English, and heuristic or fixed strategies can be suboptimal. The authors aim to systematically characterize these trade-offs and to provide a principled, automatic way to select prompting strategies.
AI.method: The authors systematically evaluate multiple prompting strategies—native language prompting, translation-based prompting, and prompt-based self-routing—across ten languages with varying resource levels and four different benchmarks. Based on empirical observations, they cast strategy selection as a supervised learning problem. They then design lightweight classifiers that, given an instance, predict whether native prompting or translation-based prompting will work better. They train and test these classifiers and assess whether they outperform fixed, one-size-fits-all prompting strategies and whether they generalize to new task formats.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that no single prompting strategy is best in all settings. Translation-based prompting yields large gains for low-resource languages, even with imperfect translations, while high-resource languages see limited benefit from translation. Prompt-based self-routing performs worse than explicit translation. The proposed lightweight classifiers successfully predict when to use native or translation-based prompting, achieving statistically significant performance improvements over fixed strategies on all four benchmarks and generalizing to unseen task formats.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that translation-based prompting is especially effective for low-resource languages and less so for high-resource ones, and that language resource level, rather than translation quality alone, is the primary factor determining translation’s benefit. Moreover, simple learned classifiers can reliably choose between native and translation-based prompting on a per-instance basis, yielding robust gains over static strategies and generalizing beyond the specific tasks seen during training.

title: Harness as an Asset: Enforcing Determinism via the Convergent AI Agent Framework (CAAF)
summary:
Large Language Models (LLMs) produce a controllability gap in safety-critical engineering: even low rates of undetected constraint violations render a system undeployable. Current orchestration paradigms suffer from sycophantic compliance, context attention decay [Liu et al., 2024], and stochastic oscillation during self-correction [Huang et al., 2024].
  We introduce the Convergent AI Agent Framework (CAAF), which transitions agentic workflows from open-loop generation to closed-loop Fail-Safe Determinism via three pillars: (1) Recursive Atomic Decomposition with physical context firewalls; (2) Harness as an Asset, formalizing domain invariants into machine-readable registries enforced by a deterministic Unified Assertion Interface (UAI); and (3) Structured Semantic Gradients with State Locking for monotonic convergence.
  Empirical evaluation across two domains -- SAE Level 3 (L3) autonomous driving (AD) (n=30, 7 conditions) and pharmaceutical continuous flow reactor design (n=20, 4 conditions including a Mono+UAI ablation) -- shows that CAAF-all-GPT-4o-mini achieves 100% paradox detection while monolithic GPT-4o achieves 0% (even at temperature=0). The pharmaceutical benchmark features 7 simultaneous constraints with nonlinear Arrhenius interactions and a 3-way minimal unsatisfiable subset, representing a structurally harder challenge than the 2-constraint AD paradox. Alternative multi-agent architectures (debate, sequential checking) also achieve 0% across 80 trials, confirming that CAAF's reliability derives from its deterministic UAI, not from multi-agent orchestration per se. A Mono+UAI ablation (95%) isolates UAI as the core contribution. CAAF's reliability is invariant to prompt hints; all components use a single commodity model, enabling fully offline deployment.

AI.tldr: They propose a new agent framework (CAAF) that makes LLM-based engineering workflows deterministic and fail-safe, and show it vastly outperforms standard and multi-agent GPT-4o setups on paradox/constraint-violation benchmarks in autonomous driving and pharma reactor design.
AI.motivation: LLMs are unreliable for safety-critical engineering because even rare, undetected constraint violations make systems unusable. Existing orchestration methods (prompting, agent debates, self-correction) still suffer from sycophancy, attention decay, and oscillatory corrections, so we need a way to guarantee constraint adherence and reliable paradox detection using commodity LLMs.
AI.method: They design the Convergent AI Agent Framework (CAAF) with three main mechanisms: (1) Recursive Atomic Decomposition with context firewalls to break tasks into minimal, context-isolated units; (2) treating the safety/constraints harness as a first-class asset by encoding domain invariants into machine-readable registries enforced through a deterministic Unified Assertion Interface (UAI); and (3) Structured Semantic Gradients with State Locking, which iteratively refine outputs while locking in previously validated state so that behavior converges monotonically instead of oscillating. They then implement CAAF using GPT-4o-mini in all roles and test it on two benchmarks: detecting paradoxical or unsatisfiable constraints in Level 3 autonomous driving scenarios and in pharmaceutical continuous flow reactor design with complex nonlinear constraints. They also run ablations (Mono+UAI) and compare against monolithic GPT-4o and other multi-agent baselines such as debate and sequential checking.
AI.result: On both domains, the full CAAF using only GPT-4o-mini detects all paradoxes/constraint violations (100% detection), while a single GPT-4o model without the framework detects none (0%), even at temperature 0. Competing multi-agent orchestration schemes like debate and sequential checking also get 0% across 80 trials, indicating that multi-agency alone is insufficient. An ablation where a monolithic agent is augmented with UAI (Mono+UAI) reaches 95% detection, singling out the deterministic Unified Assertion Interface as the dominant contributor to reliability. Performance is stable across different prompts, and everything runs with a single commodity model that can be deployed offline.
AI.conclusion: Formalizing safety constraints as a deterministic, model-independent assertion layer (UAI) and integrating it into a closed-loop, decomposed, convergent agent framework (CAAF) can close much of the controllability gap for LLM-based engineering workflows. CAAF delivers fail-safe, monotonic behavior and robust paradox detection in complex, safety-critical domains using only commodity LLMs, and its core benefit comes from the deterministic harness rather than from generic multi-agent orchestration or larger models.

title: MNAFT: modality neuron-aware fine-tuning of multimodal large language models for image translation
comment: Accepted by SCIS (SCIENCE CHINA Information Science)
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have shown impressive capabilities, yet they often struggle to effectively capture the fine-grained textual information within images crucial for accurate image translation. This often leads to a modality gap between visual text inputs and textual inputs/outputs for image translation. Existing methods, primarily relying on instruction fine-tuning, risk parameter redundancy of pre-trained knowledge, hindering generalization performance. To address this, we introduce modality neuron-aware fine-tuning (MNAFT), a novel approach that takes advantage of the specialized roles of individual neurons within MLLMs for enhanced image translation. MNAFT identifies language-agnostic and language-specific neurons in both vision and language modules through an instruction-driven activation analysis, evaluating their importance in various translation tasks. We then perform selective fine-tuning, updating only the parameters of language-specific and language-agnostic neurons within the selected layers relevant to the target task, while preserving the knowledge encoded in other neurons and layers. Our extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that MNAFT significantly outperforms state-of-the-art image translation methods, including cascaded models, standard full fine-tuning, and parameter-efficient tuning techniques. Furthermore, we provide comprehensive analysis, including visualizations of neuron activations and clustering patterns, to offer insights into the roles of different neuron groups in mediating cross-modal understanding and facilitating accurate language-specific translation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Modality Neuron-Aware Fine-Tuning (MNAFT) for multimodal large language models to better handle image translation by selectively updating only specific neurons tied to language and modality, achieving superior performance over existing methods.
AI.motivation: MLLMs are good at many tasks but still struggle with fine-grained text embedded in images, which is crucial for accurate image translation (e.g., reading signs, documents, UI text). This causes a modality gap between visual text and regular textual input/output. Existing instruction fine-tuning approaches tend to over-update parameters, redundantly modifying pre-trained knowledge and hurting generalization. The authors are motivated to find a more targeted fine-tuning strategy that respects the internal specialization of neurons in MLLMs and bridges the visual–textual modality gap for translation tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose Modality Neuron-Aware Fine-Tuning (MNAFT). First, they perform instruction-driven activation analysis on both the vision and language modules of an MLLM across diverse translation tasks to identify neurons that are language-agnostic (shared across languages) and language-specific (specialized for certain languages). This involves measuring neuron activation patterns and importance scores under different translation instructions. Based on this analysis, they select layers and neuron subsets that are most relevant to the target translation task. Fine-tuning is then done selectively: only parameters associated with the identified language-specific and language-agnostic neurons in the chosen layers are updated, while all other neurons and layers are frozen to preserve general pre-trained knowledge and reduce redundancy.
AI.result: Across multiple image translation benchmarks, MNAFT substantially outperforms cascaded systems (e.g., OCR + text-only LLM translation), traditional full-parameter fine-tuning of MLLMs, and mainstream parameter-efficient tuning methods. Quantitatively, it yields better translation accuracy and robustness on images containing rich or challenging text. Qualitatively and analytically, neuron activation visualizations and clustering show clear differentiation between language-agnostic and language-specific neurons, supporting the claim that these groups play distinct roles in cross-modal understanding and translation.
AI.conclusion: MNAFT offers an effective and efficient strategy for adapting MLLMs to image translation by exploiting neuron-level modality and language specialization. By updating only carefully selected language-specific and language-agnostic neurons, it preserves general knowledge, reduces parameter redundancy, and improves generalization.
