The empirical gains and interpretability analyses suggest that understanding and leveraging neuron roles is a promising direction for closing the modality gap in visual–text translation and for designing future multimodal adaptation techniques.

title: Understanding and Enforcing Weight Disentanglement in Task Arithmetic
summary: Task arithmetic provides an efficient, training-free way to edit pre-trained models, yet lacks a fundamental theoretical explanation for its success. The existing concept of ``weight disentanglement" describes the ideal outcome of non-interfering task composition but does not reveal its underlying cause. Crucially, what intrinsic properties of the pre-trained model ($θ_0$) or the task vectors ($τ_t$) enable this disentanglement remains underexplored. In this paper, we introduce Task-Feature Specialization (TFS), a model's ability to allocate distinct internal features to different tasks, as the fundamental principle. We first prove that TFS is a sufficient condition for weight disentanglement. More importantly, we find that TFS also gives rise to an observable geometric consequence: weight vector orthogonality. This positions TFS as the common cause for both the desired functional outcome (disentanglement) and a measurable geometric property (orthogonality). This relationship provides the key insight for our method: since the abstract TFS property is intractable to enforce directly, we can instead promote weight disentanglement by shaping its concrete geometric consequence, orthogonality. Therefore, we propose OrthoReg, a simple and effective regularization method that actively enforces an internal orthogonal structure on weight updates ($ΔW$) that constitute $τ_t$ during fine-tuning. And we theoretically prove that OrthoReg promotes disentanglement. Extensive experiments demonstrate that OrthoReg consistently and significantly enhances the performance of various task arithmetic methods. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/RL-MIND/OrthoReg}{https://github.com/RL-MIND/OrthoReg}.

AI.tldr: They introduce Task-Feature Specialization (TFS) as the core principle behind why task arithmetic works, connect it to weight disentanglement and orthogonality, and propose OrthoReg, a regularizer enforcing orthogonality of task weight updates to improve task composition.
AI.motivation: Task arithmetic can edit pre-trained models without retraining but lacks solid theory; existing notion of weight disentanglement describes non-interfering task composition but doesn't explain why it emerges or how to encourage it. The authors want to identify the intrinsic property of models or task vectors that leads to successful, non-interfering task compositions and to design a principled method to enhance it.
AI.method: 1) Formally define Task-Feature Specialization (TFS) as the allocation of distinct internal features to different tasks. 2) Prove theoretically that TFS is sufficient for weight disentanglement. 3) Show that TFS implies a geometric consequence: orthogonality of weight vectors associated with tasks. 4) Use this connection to design OrthoReg, a regularization term applied during fine-tuning that enforces orthogonality among weight updates (ΔW) used to construct task vectors τ_t. 5) Provide theoretical analysis that OrthoReg promotes disentanglement and evaluate it empirically across multiple task arithmetic methods and benchmarks.
AI.result: Theoretically, they establish that TFS implies both functional disentanglement and geometric orthogonality of task-related weights. Empirically, adding OrthoReg during fine-tuning significantly and consistently improves task arithmetic performance across different methods and tasks, showing better non-interfering task compositions compared to baselines without OrthoReg.
AI.conclusion: Task-Feature Specialization is identified as a fundamental property underlying successful task arithmetic, simultaneously explaining weight disentanglement and the empirically observed orthogonality of task vectors. By enforcing orthogonality via the proposed OrthoReg regularizer on task weight updates, one can systematically promote disentanglement and substantially enhance the effectiveness of task arithmetic methods in practice.

title: On Safety Risks in Experience-Driven Self-Evolving Agents
summary: Experience-driven self-evolution has emerged as a promising paradigm for improving the autonomy of large language model agents, yet its reliance on self-curated experience introduces underexplored safety risks. In this study, we investigate how experience accumulation and utilization in self-evolving agents affect safety performance across web-based and embodied environments. Notably, experience gathered solely from benign tasks can still compromise safety in high-risk scenarios. Further analysis attributes this degradation to the execution-oriented nature of accumulated experience, which reinforces agents' tendency to act rather than refuse. In more realistic settings where agents encounter both benign and harmful tasks, refusal-related experience mitigates safety decline but induces over-refusal, revealing a fundamental safety-utility trade-off. Overall, our findings expose inherent limitations of current self-evolving agents and call for more principled strategies to ensure safe and reliable adaptation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies safety risks in self-evolving LLM agents that learn from their own experience, showing that even benign experience can harm safety, and that balancing refusal and utility creates a core trade-off.
AI.motivation: Self-evolving LLM agents that adapt from their own past trajectories promise greater autonomy and capability, but this adaptation process may introduce hidden safety risks. There is limited understanding of how accumulating and using experience affects agents’ willingness to act versus to refuse harmful instructions, especially in realistic, mixed benign/harmful environments. The paper aims to systematically investigate these dynamics.
AI.method: The authors empirically analyze self-evolving LLM agents in both web-based and embodied task environments. They allow agents to accumulate experience from task executions, then study how this experience is used to guide future behavior. They compare conditions where experience comes only from benign tasks versus from a mix of benign and harmful tasks, and measure safety outcomes such as inappropriate compliance or refusal behavior, along with the nature of stored experiences (execution- vs refusal-focused).
AI.result: They find that even when agents only collect experience from benign tasks, their safety degrades in high-risk scenarios: the accumulated, execution-oriented experience shifts behavior toward acting rather than refusing. When agents encounter both benign and harmful tasks, the presence of refusal-related experience partially counteracts safety decline but leads to over-refusal, with agents declining even safe tasks. This reveals a safety-utility trade-off governed by the composition of the experience buffer and how it is used.
AI.conclusion: Current experience-driven self-evolution mechanisms in LLM agents have inherent safety limitations: naive accumulation and reuse of past trajectories tends to bias agents either toward over-execution (unsafe) or over-refusal (low utility). Ensuring safe and reliable adaptation will require more principled designs for experience selection, representation, and use, beyond simple self-curation, to balance safety and usefulness in realistic mixed-task environments.

title: Complementing Self-Consistency with Cross-Model Disagreement for Uncertainty Quantification
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often produce confident yet incorrect responses, and uncertainty quantification is one potential solution to more robust usage. Recent works routinely rely on self-consistency to estimate aleatoric uncertainty (AU), yet this proxy collapses when models are overconfident and produce the same incorrect answer across samples. We analyze this regime and show that cross-model semantic disagreement is higher on incorrect answers precisely when AU is low. Motivated by this, we introduce an epistemic uncertainty (EU) term that operates in the black-box access setting: EU uses only generated text from a small, scale-matched ensemble and is computed as the gap between inter-model and intra-model sequence-semantic similarity. We then define total uncertainty (TU) as the sum of AU and EU. In a comprehensive study across five 7-9B instruction-tuned models and ten long-form tasks, TU improves ranking calibration and selective abstention relative to AU, and EU reliably flags confident failures where AU is low. We further characterize when EU is most useful via agreement and complementarity diagnostics.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new way to quantify uncertainty in large language models by combining aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty, using only black-box access and model outputs.
AI.motivation: Large language models can be confidently wrong, and existing uncertainty estimates based on self-consistency fail when the model is overconfident and repeatedly outputs the same incorrect answer. There is a need for a better uncertainty signal that can detect such confident failures without requiring white-box access.
AI.method: The authors study cases where self-consistency-based aleatoric uncertainty (AU) is low but the model is still wrong. They measure cross-model semantic disagreement and find it is higher on such incorrect answers. Based on this, they define an epistemic uncertainty (EU) term using a small, scale-matched ensemble of models. EU is computed as the gap between inter-model and intra-model sequence-semantic similarity, using only generated text. Total uncertainty (TU) is then defined as AU + EU.
AI.result: Across five 7–9B instruction-tuned models and ten long-form tasks, the proposed total uncertainty (TU) yields better ranking calibration and selective abstention performance than AU alone. The EU component consistently highlights confident mistakes in regions where AU is low.
AI.conclusion: Combining aleatoric and epistemic uncertainty via black-box, text-based ensemble disagreement provides a more reliable uncertainty estimate for LLMs, especially in detecting confident failures. Diagnostic analyses further clarify the regimes where the epistemic term adds the most value.

title: Bolzano: Case Studies in LLM-Assisted Mathematical Research
summary: We report new results on six problems in mathematics and theoretical computer science, produced with the assistance of Bolzano, an open-source multi-agent LLM system. Bolzano orchestrates rounds of interaction between parallel prover agents and a verifier agent while maintaining a persistent knowledge base that is carried across rounds. Classified using the significance-autonomy taxonomy of Feng et al., four of the six results reach the level of publishable research, and three of the six were produced essentially autonomously by Bolzano. Our results provide evidence that LLMs can contribute meaningfully to mathematical research, complementing recent reports by Bubeck et al., Woodruff et al., and others.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Bolzano, a multi-agent LLM system that autonomously helps prove new mathematical and theoretical computer science results, with several outcomes reaching publishable quality.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether and how large language models can make genuine, novel contributions to advanced mathematical and theoretical computer science research, beyond simple problem solving or homework-level tasks.
AI.method: The authors design Bolzano, an open-source system that coordinates multiple LLM-based prover agents and a verifier agent over multiple iterative rounds. The system maintains a persistent knowledge base that records partial proofs, insights, and failed attempts, enabling cumulative progress. They apply Bolzano to six open or challenging problems, classify the outcomes using Feng et al.'s significance-autonomy taxonomy, and compare their findings to other recent work on LLMs in mathematics.
AI.result: For six mathematical and theoretical computer science problems tackled, Bolzano produced new results in all cases. Four of these results are strong enough to be considered publishable research, and three of the six were obtained with Bolzano operating in an essentially autonomous fashion, with minimal human intervention.
AI.conclusion: A well-orchestrated multi-agent LLM system with persistent memory can meaningfully contribute to modern mathematical research. The empirical evidence suggests that LLMs, when structured as collaborative provers and verifiers, can autonomously derive nontrivial, potentially publishable results, complementing and extending prior demonstrations of LLM capabilities in mathematics.

title: If Only My CGM Could Speak: A Privacy-Preserving Agent for Question Answering over Continuous Glucose Data
summary: Continuous glucose monitors (CGMs) used in diabetes care collect rich personal health data that could improve day-to-day self-management. However, current patient platforms only offer static summaries which do not support inquisitive user queries. Large language models (LLMs) could enable free-form inquiries about continuous glucose data, but deploying them over sensitive health records raises privacy and accuracy concerns. In this paper, we present CGM-Agent, a privacy-preserving framework for question answering over personal glucose data. In our design, the LLM serves purely as a reasoning engine that selects analytical functions. All computation occurs locally, and personal health data never leaves the user's device. For evaluation, we construct a benchmark of 4,180 questions combining parameterized question templates with real user queries and ground truth derived from deterministic program execution. Evaluating 6 leading LLMs, we find that top models achieve 94\% value accuracy on synthetic queries and 88\% on ambiguous real-world queries. Errors stem primarily from intent and temporal ambiguity rather than computational failures. Additionally, lightweight models achieve competitive performance in our agent design, suggesting opportunities for low-cost deployment. We release our code and benchmark to support future work on trustworthy health agents.

AI.tldr: They build CGM-Agent, a privacy-preserving LLM-based system that answers user questions about their continuous glucose monitor data by using the LLM only for reasoning while keeping all data local.
AI.motivation: Continuous glucose monitors generate rich time-series health data that could help people with diabetes manage their condition, but current tools provide only static, limited summaries and do not support flexible, natural-language questions. Using LLMs could unlock interactive, free-form analysis, yet directly feeding sensitive health records to remote LLMs poses major privacy risks and accuracy concerns. The authors aim to enable powerful question answering over CGM data without compromising privacy or reliability.
AI.method: They design CGM-Agent, an architecture where the LLM does not see raw glucose data but instead operates as a planner that selects from predefined analytical functions. All numerical computations and data access occur locally on the user’s device. The system uses deterministic program execution over CGM data to produce answers, while the LLM interprets user questions, chooses appropriate operations, and composes responses. For evaluation, they create a benchmark of 4,180 questions built from parameterized templates plus real user questions, with ground-truth answers generated programmatically. They then test six state-of-the-art LLMs within this agent framework.
AI.result: When embedded in the CGM-Agent framework, the strongest LLMs reach 94% accuracy on synthetic template-based questions and 88% accuracy on more ambiguous, real user queries. Error analysis shows that most mistakes come from misunderstanding user intent or time references, not from numerical or computational errors. They also find that smaller, lightweight models perform surprisingly well in this constrained agent setting, approaching the performance of larger models.
AI.conclusion: Separating reasoning from computation and keeping all CGM data on-device enables privacy-preserving, accurate question answering over personal glucose records. The agent-style design mitigates privacy risks because raw health data never reaches the LLM, and constraining the LLM to select from analytical tools improves reliability. The competitive performance of smaller models indicates that trustworthy, low-cost, and potentially offline health data agents are feasible. The released code and benchmark provide a foundation for future research on safe, trustworthy LLM-based health assistants.

title: SPS: Steering Probability Squeezing for Better Exploration in Reinforcement Learning for Large Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for training reasoning-oriented models by leveraging rule-based reward signals. However, RL training typically tends to improve single-sample success rates (i.e., Pass@1) while offering limited exploration of diverse reasoning trajectories, which is crucial for multi-sample performance (i.e., Pass@k). Our preliminary analysis reveals that this limitation stems from a fundamental squeezing effect, whereby probability mass is excessively concentrated on a narrow subset of high-reward trajectories, restricting genuine exploration and constraining attainable performance under RL training. To address this issue, in this work, we propose Steering Probability Squeezing (SPS), a training paradigm that interleaves conventional RL with inverse reinforcement learning (IRL). SPS treats on-policy rollouts as demonstrations and employs IRL to explicitly reshape the induced trajectory distribution, thereby enhancing exploration without introducing external supervision. Experiments on five commonly used reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that SPS can enable better exploration and improve Pass@k. Beyond algorithmic contributions, we provide an analysis of RL learning dynamics and identify an empirical upper bound on Pass@k, shedding light on intrinsic exploration limits in RL-based reasoning models. Our findings suggest that alternating between RL and IRL offers an effective pathway toward extending the exploration capacity of reasoning-oriented large language models.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a probability “squeezing” problem in RL for reasoning LLMs and introduces a new SPS framework that alternates RL with IRL to improve exploration and Pass@k performance without extra supervision.
AI.motivation: Existing RL training for reasoning-focused models mainly boosts Pass@1 by concentrating probability mass on a few high-reward trajectories. This harms exploration and limits diverse reasoning paths needed for strong multi-sample performance (Pass@k). The authors aim to understand and mitigate this exploration bottleneck in RL-based reasoning models.
AI.method: They first analyze RL dynamics and show a “squeezing effect,” where probability mass collapses onto a narrow set of trajectories, limiting exploration and achievable Pass@k. To counter this, they propose Steering Probability Squeezing (SPS), which alternates standard on-policy RL with inverse RL (IRL). On-policy rollouts are treated as demonstrations, and IRL is used to reshape the trajectory distribution to be more exploratory, all without external labels. This interleaving is designed to maintain good performance on high-reward trajectories while broadening the support over diverse reasoning paths.
AI.result: On five standard reasoning benchmarks, SPS yields better exploration behavior and consistently improves Pass@k over conventional RL baselines. The analysis also reveals an empirical upper bound on Pass@k under standard RL, empirically validating that probability squeezing constrains multi-sample performance and that SPS can relax this limit.
AI.conclusion: RL for reasoning LLMs naturally leads to probability squeezing, which restricts exploration and caps Pass@k. Alternating RL with IRL in the SPS framework effectively reshapes trajectory distributions, increases exploration, and boosts multi-sample success without additional supervision. This suggests that RL+IRL alternation is a promising direction for scaling the exploration capacity and performance of reasoning-oriented language models.

title: Local Inconsistency Resolution: The Interplay between Attention and Control in Probabilistic Models
summary: We present a generic algorithm for learning and approximate inference with an intuitive epistemic interpretation: iteratively focus on a subset of the model and resolve inconsistencies using the parameters under control. This framework, which we call Local Inconsistency Resolution (LIR) is built upon Probabilistic Dependency Graphs (PDGs), which provide a flexible representational foundation capable of capturing inconsistent beliefs. We show how LIR unifies and generalizes a wide variety of important algorithms in the literature, including the Expectation-Maximization (EM) algorithm, belief propagation, adversarial training, GANs, and GFlowNets. In the last case, LIR actually suggests a more natural loss, which we demonstrate improves GFlowNet convergence. Each method can be recovered as a specific instance of LIR by choosing a procedure to direct focus (attention and control). We implement this algorithm for discrete PDGs and study its properties on synthetically generated PDGs, comparing its behavior to the global optimization semantics of the full PDG.

AI.tldr: They propose a unifying algorithmic framework, LIR, that views learning and inference as iteratively fixing local inconsistencies in a probabilistic model, showing many well-known methods are special cases and that it improves GFlowNet training.
AI.motivation: Existing learning and inference algorithms—such as EM, belief propagation, adversarial training, GANs, and GFlowNets—appear diverse and disconnected, and probabilistic models may contain inconsistent beliefs that are hard to handle uniformly. The authors want a common, intuitively epistemic view that can represent and systematically resolve such inconsistencies, both to understand existing methods and to design improved ones.
AI.method: They introduce Local Inconsistency Resolution (LIR), defined over Probabilistic Dependency Graphs (PDGs), which are flexible probabilistic structures that can encode inconsistent beliefs. LIR iteratively focuses attention on a subset of the PDG (the part currently under epistemic control), detects local inconsistencies between beliefs/constraints and current parameters, and updates those parameters to reduce or resolve these inconsistencies. By specifying different focus and update procedures, they recover known algorithms like EM, belief propagation, GAN training, adversarial training, and GFlowNets as instances. They implement LIR concretely for discrete PDGs and analyze its behavior on synthetic PDGs, comparing it to full global optimization on the PDG.
AI.result: They formally show that a range of standard learning and inference algorithms are special cases of LIR obtained via particular choices of focus and control. For GFlowNets, LIR implies a more natural loss function than the standard one; experiments demonstrate that this loss improves convergence behavior. Empirical studies on synthetic discrete PDGs characterize how LIR behaves relative to global optimization of the full PDG, providing evidence that local inconsistency resolution can approximate global solutions effectively.
AI.conclusion: Local Inconsistency Resolution on Probabilistic Dependency Graphs offers a unified, epistemically interpretable framework for approximate learning and inference. Many well-known algorithms become specific instantiations of the same basic process—iteratively focusing on parts of a model and resolving inconsistencies with adjustable parameters. This unification not only clarifies relationships among disparate methods but also guides the design of improved algorithms, as illustrated by better-converging GFlowNet training objectives.

title: BIASEDTALES-ML: A Multilingual Dataset for Analyzing Narrative Attribute Distributions in LLM-Generated Stories
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Findings. Data are available at https://huggingface.co/spaces/Linyuana/BIASEDTALES-ML
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly used to generate narrative content, including children's stories, which play an important role in social and cultural learning. Despite growing interest in AI safety and alignment, most existing evaluations focus primarily on English, leaving the cross-lingual generalization of aligned behavior underexplored. In this work, we introduce BiasedTales-ML, a large-scale parallel corpus of approximately 350,000 children's stories generated across eight typologically and culturally diverse languages using a full-permutation prompting design. We propose a structured generator-extractor pipeline and a multi-dimensional distributional analysis framework to examine how narrative attributes vary across languages, models, and social conditions. Our analysis reveals substantial cross-lingual variability in narrative generation patterns, indicating that distributions observed in English do not always exhibit similar characteristics in other languages, particularly in lower-resource settings. At the narrative level, we identify recurring structural patterns involving character roles, settings, and thematic emphasis, which manifest differently across linguistic contexts. These findings highlight the limitations of English-centric evaluation for characterizing socially grounded narrative generation in multilingual settings. We release the dataset, code, and an interactive visualization tool to support future research on multilingual narrative analysis and evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a large multilingual corpus of LLM-generated children’s stories and shows that narrative patterns and social attributes differ substantially across languages, so English-only safety/alignment evaluation is insufficient.
AI.motivation: Most safety and alignment evaluations of large language models focus on English outputs, but LLMs are used globally and generate socially important content like children’s stories in many languages. There is little systematic understanding of whether aligned behaviors and narrative patterns generalize across languages, especially for low-resource ones, risking hidden biases and mischaracterizations in multilingual use.
AI.method: The authors construct BiasedTales-ML, a parallel corpus of roughly 350K LLM-generated children’s stories in eight typologically and culturally diverse languages using a full-permutation prompting scheme to systematically vary conditions. They design a structured generator–extractor pipeline to produce stories and then automatically extract narrative attributes (e.g., character roles, settings, themes). They apply a multi-dimensional distributional analysis to compare how these attributes are distributed across languages, models, and social conditions.
AI.result: The analysis uncovers significant cross-lingual differences in how LLMs generate children’s narratives. Patterns seen in English do not reliably appear in other languages, with especially pronounced divergences in lower-resource languages. The authors also find recurring structural patterns in narratives—such as how character roles, settings, and themes are organized—but these patterns manifest differently depending on the language and context.
AI.conclusion: Aligned and socially grounded narrative behavior of LLMs does not simply transfer from English to other languages; English-centric evaluation misses important cross-lingual variation, especially in low-resource settings. Robust evaluation of multilingual LLMs requires language-aware methods and datasets. The released BiasedTales-ML corpus, code, and visualization tool are intended to support such future work in multilingual narrative analysis and evaluation.

title: Graph-of-Agents: A Graph-based Framework for Multi-Agent LLM Collaboration
summary: With an ever-growing zoo of LLMs and benchmarks, the need to orchestrate multiple models for improved task performance has never been more pressing. While frameworks like Mixture-of-Agents (MoA) attempt to coordinate LLMs, they often fall short in terms of (1) selecting relevant agents, (2) facilitating effective intra-agent communication, and (3) integrating responses efficiently. In this work, we propose Graph-of-Agents (GoA), a new graph-based framework for modeling multi-agent LLM communication. Our approach begins with node sampling, selecting only the most relevant agents by leveraging model cards that summarize each model's domain, task specialization, and other characteristics. Next, we construct edges between the selected agents by evaluating their responses against one another to determine relevance ordering. Directed message passing is then performed from highly relevant agents to less relevant ones to enhance their responses, followed by reverse message passing to refine the original responses of the more relevant agents. Finally, the updated responses are aggregated via graph-based pooling (e.g., max or mean pooling) to produce a single, unified answer. We evaluate GoA on diverse multi-domain benchmarks (MMLU, MMLU-Pro, GPQA) and domain-specific benchmarks (MATH, HumanEval, MedMCQA), with an agent pool of 6 LLMs spanning multiple domains. Surprisingly, GoA achieves superior performance using only 3 selected agents, outperforming recent multi-agent LLM baselines that utilize all 6 agents simultaneously. By adopting a graph structure, GoA offers both scalability and effectiveness through structured message passing-positioning it as a strong candidate for navigating the challenges of the ever-growing LLM zoo. Code is available at: https://github.com/UNITES-Lab/GoA.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Graph-of-Agents (GoA), a graph-based framework that coordinates multiple LLMs via structured message passing, achieving better performance with fewer selected models than existing multi-agent baselines.
AI.motivation: The LLM ecosystem contains many models and benchmarks, and using multiple models together can improve performance. Existing multi-agent frameworks like Mixture-of-Agents have key weaknesses: poor selection of which agents to use, limited intra-agent communication, and inefficient integration of responses. There is a need for a scalable, principled way to orchestrate LLMs that can selectively leverage their complementary strengths.
AI.method: GoA represents LLMs as nodes in a graph. First, it samples a subset of the most relevant agents using model cards describing each model’s domain and specialization. Then it constructs directed edges among these selected agents by comparing their responses to determine a relevance ordering. Message passing is performed: highly relevant agents send messages to less relevant ones to improve their outputs, and then reverse message passing lets less relevant agents refine the responses of the more relevant ones. Finally, it aggregates the updated responses via graph-based pooling (e.g., max or mean pooling) to produce a unified answer.
AI.result: On multi-domain benchmarks (MMLU, MMLU-Pro, GPQA) and domain-specific benchmarks (MATH, HumanEval, MedMCQA), using an agent pool of 6 LLMs, GoA attains superior performance while only using 3 selected agents. It outperforms recent multi-agent LLM baselines that rely on all 6 agents simultaneously, demonstrating both performance gains and efficiency benefits.
AI.conclusion: A graph-structured representation of multi-LLM systems enables more effective and scalable coordination through selective agent sampling, relevance-based connectivity, and structured message passing. GoA shows that carefully orchestrated communication among fewer, more relevant agents can outperform naïve multi-agent approaches that simply use all available models, making it a strong approach for dealing with the growing number of LLMs.

title: Improving LLM Code Reasoning via Semantic Equivalence Self-Play with Formal Verification
summary: We introduce a self-play framework for semantic equivalence in Haskell, utilizing formal verification to guide adversarial training between a generator and an evaluator. The framework leverages Liquid Haskell proofs for validating equivalence and execution-based counterexamples for inequivalence, organized via a difficulty-aware curriculum. To facilitate this, we release \textbf{OpInstruct-HSx}, a synthetic dataset of $\approx$28k validated Haskell programs. Empirical experiments show that our evaluator transfers effectively to downstream tasks, achieving up to 13.3pp accuracy gain on EquiBench and consistent gains on PySecDB. Ablation studies on the SEQ-SINQ regimes indicate that while inequivalence supervision provides data volume, equivalence proofs are uniquely responsible for the model's reasoning capabilities. The entire training pipeline and dataset are publicly released on GitHub and Hugging Face respectively.

AI.tldr: They propose a self-play training framework using formal verification in Haskell to learn semantic program equivalence, and release a 28k-program dataset plus code, showing strong transfer to equivalence and security benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Automatically determining whether two programs are semantically equivalent is important for tasks like optimization, refactoring, and security analysis, but current models struggle to reason reliably about equivalence. The authors aim to leverage strong formal verification tools in Haskell to create high-quality supervision that can train models to reason more robustly about semantic equivalence and inequivalence, and to study which types of supervision actually build reasoning ability rather than just pattern recognition.
AI.method: They design a self-play framework with a generator and evaluator trained adversarially: the generator proposes Haskell program pairs, while the evaluator predicts semantic (in)equivalence. Liquid Haskell is used to formally prove equivalence for some pairs, while concrete execution finds counterexamples for inequivalent pairs. Examples are organized in a difficulty-aware curriculum. They compile all validated examples into OpInstruct-HSx, a synthetic dataset of roughly 28k Haskell programs, and train evaluators under different supervision regimes (equivalence proofs vs. inequivalence counterexamples) to analyze their respective contributions.
AI.result: The trained evaluator transfers well to downstream tasks, improving accuracy on the EquiBench benchmark by up to 13.3 percentage points and yielding consistent improvements on the PySecDB benchmark. Ablation experiments on SEQ-SINQ supervision regimes show that data labeled via inequivalence (counterexamples) mostly contributes volume, whereas supervision from equivalence proofs is crucial for endowing the model with genuine reasoning ability about semantic equivalence.
AI.conclusion: A self-play, verification-guided training pipeline in Haskell can produce models with significantly improved semantic equivalence reasoning, and formal equivalence proofs are especially important for this capability. The released OpInstruct-HSx dataset and open-source pipeline provide resources for further research on program equivalence and related tasks.

title: Beyond the Basics: Leveraging Large Language Model for Fine-Grained Medical Entity Recognition
summary: Extracting clinically relevant information from unstructured medical narratives such as admission notes, discharge summaries, and emergency case histories remains a challenge in clinical natural language processing (NLP). Medical Entity Recognition (MER) identifies meaningful concepts embedded in these records. Recent advancements in large language models (LLMs) have shown competitive MER performance; however, evaluations often focus on general entity types, offering limited utility for real-world clinical needs requiring finer-grained extraction. To address this gap, we rigorously evaluated the open-source LLaMA3 model for fine-grained medical entity recognition across 18 clinically detailed categories. To optimize performance, we employed three learning paradigms: zero-shot, few-shot, and fine-tuning with Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA). To further enhance few-shot learning, we introduced two example selection methods based on token- and sentence-level embedding similarity, utilizing a pre-trained BioBERT model. Unlike prior work assessing zero-shot and few-shot performance on proprietary models (e.g., GPT-4) or fine-tuning different architectures, we ensured methodological consistency by applying all strategies to a unified LLaMA3 backbone, enabling fair comparison across learning settings. Our results showed that fine-tuned LLaMA3 surpasses zero-shot and few-shot approaches by 63.11% and 35.63%, respectivel respectively, achieving an F1 score of 81.24% in granular medical entity extraction.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates LLaMA3 for fine-grained medical entity recognition from clinical text, comparing zero-shot, few-shot, and LoRA fine-tuning, and finds fine-tuning performs best with an F1 of 81.24%.
AI.motivation: Clinical notes contain rich but unstructured information. Existing MER work with LLMs often focuses on coarse, general entity types and mixes models/strategies, limiting real clinical usefulness and fair comparison. The authors want to know how well an open-source LLaMA3 can handle detailed, clinically fine-grained entities using a consistent backbone and different learning paradigms.
AI.method: They use the open-source LLaMA3 model as a unified backbone and define 18 fine-grained medical entity categories. They evaluate three paradigms: (1) zero-shot prompting, (2) few-shot prompting, and (3) parameter-efficient fine-tuning using LoRA. For few-shot, they design two example selection strategies based on token-level and sentence-level embedding similarity computed with a pre-trained BioBERT model to choose the most relevant in-context examples. They then compare performance across these setups on granular MER using F1 as the main metric.
AI.result: Fine-tuned LLaMA3 with LoRA significantly outperforms zero-shot and few-shot LLaMA3. Specifically, fine-tuning improves F1 by 63.11% over zero-shot and 35.63% over few-shot, reaching an overall F1 of 81.24% for detailed medical entity extraction across the 18 categories. Few-shot with embedding-based example selection performs better than pure zero-shot but still lags behind fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: LLaMA3, when fine-tuned with a parameter-efficient method like LoRA, can achieve strong performance on fine-grained medical entity recognition from clinical narratives, clearly surpassing zero-shot and few-shot prompting on the same backbone. Methodologically consistent evaluation shows that for granular clinical NLP tasks, full or PEFT-style adaptation of an open-source LLM is more effective than relying solely on in-context learning, highlighting a practical path for deploying accurate MER systems in real-world clinical settings.

title: Beyond Static Benchmarks: Synthesizing Harmful Content via Persona-based Simulation for Robust Evaluation
summary: Static benchmarks for harmful content detection face limitations in scalability and diversity, and may also be affected by contamination from web-scale pre-training corpora. To address these issues, we propose a framework for synthesizing harmful content, leveraging persona-guided large language model (LLM) agents. Our approach constructs two-dimensional user personas by integrating demographic identities and topical interests with situational harmful strategies, enabling the simulation of diverse and contextually grounded harmful interactions. We evaluate the framework along three dimensions: harmfulness, challenge level, and diversity. Both human and LLM-based evaluations confirm that our framework achieves a high harmful generation success rate. Experiments across multiple detection systems reveal that our synthetic scenarios are more challenging to detect than those in existing benchmarks. Furthermore, a multi-faceted analysis confirms that our approach achieves linguistic and topical diversity comparable to human-curated datasets, establishing our framework as an effective tool for robust stress-testing of harmful content detection systems.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a persona-guided LLM framework to synthesize diverse, realistic harmful content for stress-testing safety detectors, overcoming limitations of static benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing static benchmarks for harmful content detection are limited in scalability, diversity, and may be contaminated by overlap with LLM pre-training data, reducing their reliability for evaluating modern detection systems. There is a need for a scalable, controllable way to generate challenging, diverse, and contamination-resilient harmful scenarios to robustly evaluate and improve safety systems.
AI.method: The authors design a framework that uses large language model agents conditioned on two-dimensional user personas. These personas combine demographic identities and topical interests with situational harmful strategies, guiding the LLM to generate harmful interactions that are contextually grounded and varied. The framework then synthesizes harmful content and evaluates it along three axes: harmfulness, challenge level for detectors, and linguistic/topical diversity, using both human annotators and LLM-based evaluation. They also run multiple existing detection systems on the generated data to compare difficulty against existing benchmarks.
AI.result: The persona-guided LLM framework achieves a high rate of successfully generating harmful content as judged by humans and LLM evaluators. When tested on multiple existing harmful-content detection systems, the synthesized scenarios are harder to detect than those from prior benchmarks, indicating higher challenge. Analysis further shows that the generated data has linguistic and topical diversity on par with human-curated datasets.
AI.conclusion: The proposed persona-guided LLM synthesis framework is an effective, scalable way to generate diverse and challenging harmful content for evaluation. It mitigates contamination issues inherent in static benchmarks and produces realistic, varied test cases that better stress-test harmful content detection systems. As such, it can serve as a robust tool for developing and benchmarking safety mechanisms for LLMs and related systems.

title: Yanasse: Finding New Proofs from Deep Vision's Analogies, Part 1
summary: Project Yanasse presents a method for discovering new proofs of theorems in one area of mathematics by transferring proof strategy patterns (e.g., Lean 4 tactic invocation patterns) from a structurally distant area. The system extracts tactic usage distributions across 27 top-level areas of Mathlib (217,133 proof states), computes z-scores to identify tactics that are heavily used in a source area but rare or absent in a target area, matches source and target proof states via GPU-accelerated NP-hard analogy (running on a MacBook Air via Apple's MPS backend), and then asks an AI reasoning agent to semantically adapt--not symbol-substitute--the source tactics invocation pattern to the target theorem. In this first part of the study, the method is applied to the pair Probability -> Representation Theory, producing 4 Lean-verified new proofs out of 10 attempts (40%). The proofs compile with zero sorry declarations. The key finding is that tactic schemas decompose into a head (domain-gated, rarely transfers) and a modifier (domain-general, often transfers): filter upwards's head fails in representation theory (no Filter structure), but its [LIST] with ω modifier transfers cleanly as ext1 + simp [LIST] + rfl. Crucially, the underlying matching engine--deep vision lib.py--is entirely domain independent: the same optimization code for an NP-hard matching that matches chess positions by analogy matches Lean proof states by analogy, without knowing which domain it is processing. Only a relation extractor is domain-specific.

AI.tldr: Method to transfer proof strategy patterns between distant areas in Mathlib to discover new theorem proofs.
AI.motivation: To automatically discover new proofs in one mathematical domain using proof strategies from another, even when they are structurally distant.
AI.method: Analyze tactic usage distributions across Mathlib areas, compute z-scores to find over/underused tactics, perform GPU-accelerated NP-hard analogy matching between proof states, and prompt an AI agent to semantically adapt tactic patterns from source to target theorems.
AI.result: Applied from Probability to Representation Theory, the system produced 4 new, Lean-verified proofs out of 10 attempts (40%), all compiling without sorry placeholders. It also revealed a structural decomposition of tactic schemas into domain-specific heads and domain-general modifiers.
AI.conclusion: Proof strategies can be partially transferred between distant mathematical domains via domain-independent analogy matching, with domain-general modifiers often transferring successfully even when domain-specific heads fail. The underlying matching engine is domain-agnostic; only the relation extractor needs to be domain-specific.

title: Beyond Black-Box Labels: Interpretable Criteria for Diagnosing SubjectiveNLP Tasks
summary: Subjective NLP datasets typically aggregate annotator judgments into a single gold label, making it difficult to diagnose whether disagreement reflects unclear criteria, collapsed distinctions, or legitimate plurality. We propose a \emph{schema-level diagnostic} for auditing expert-designed annotation schemas \emph{prior to} gold-label commitment, using only multi-annotator criterion judgments. The diagnostic separates two failure modes: unstable criteria with hard-to-operationalize boundaries, and systematic overlap that blurs the boundaries between mutually exclusive categories. Applied to persuasive value extraction in commercial documents, we find that disagreement is not diffuse: instability concentrates in a few criteria, while nearly half of covered sentences activate multiple categories. These signals align with where domain experts disagree, yielding an evidence-based audit for tightening guidelines, revising category structure, or reconsidering the annotation paradigm.

AI.tldr: They propose a diagnostic method to audit annotation schemas for subjective NLP tasks before creating gold labels, distinguishing between unstable criteria and overlapping categories, and show its usefulness on persuasive value extraction in commercial documents.
AI.motivation: Subjective NLP tasks often force diverse annotator judgments into a single gold label, masking whether disagreement comes from vague guidelines, overly broad or overlapping labels, or genuine multiplicity of interpretations. This makes it hard to improve annotation schemas and can hurt model training and evaluation.
AI.method: They design a schema-level diagnostic that operates on multi-annotator, per-criterion judgments. The diagnostic decomposes disagreement into two main failure modes: (1) instability, where some criteria have fuzzy, hard-to-apply boundaries; and (2) overlap, where supposedly mutually exclusive categories are systematically co-activated. They then apply this diagnostic to an expert-created schema for persuasive value extraction in commercial documents.
AI.result: On a persuasive value extraction dataset, they find that disagreement is structured rather than random: a small number of criteria account for most instability, and almost half of the sentences are assigned multiple categories, indicating substantial overlap. These quantitative signals match areas where domain experts themselves report confusion or disagreement.
AI.conclusion: The proposed diagnostic provides an evidence-based way to audit and refine annotation schemas before committing to gold labels. It highlights which criteria need clearer guidelines, which categories may need to be split, merged, or redefined, and when the overall annotation paradigm (e.g., mutually exclusive labels) may be inappropriate, especially in tasks with inherently plural or overlapping phenomena.

title: Safe and Policy-Compliant Multi-Agent Orchestration for Enterprise AI
comment: 6 pages, 3 figures, 3 tables, IEEE conference format
summary: Enterprise AI systems increasingly deploy multiple intelligent agents across mission-critical workflows that must satisfy hard policy constraints, bounded risk exposure, and comprehensive auditability (SOX, HIPAA, GDPR). Existing coordination methods - cooperative MARL, consensus protocols, and centralized planners - optimize expected reward while treating constraints implicitly. This paper introduces CAMCO (Constraint-Aware Multi-Agent Cognitive Orchestration), a runtime coordination layer that models multi-agent decision-making as a constrained optimization problem. CAMCO integrates three mechanisms: (i) a constraint projection engine enforcing policy-feasible actions via convex projection, (ii) adaptive risk-weighted Lagrangian utility shaping, and (iii) an iterative negotiation protocol with provably bounded convergence. Unlike training-time constrained RL, CAMCO operates as deployment-time middleware compatible with any agent architecture, with policy predicates designed for direct integration with production engines such as OPA. Evaluation across three enterprise scenarios - including comparison against a constrained Lagrangian MARL baseline - demonstrates zero policy violations, risk exposure below threshold (mean ratio 0.71), 92-97% utility retention, and mean convergence in 2.4 iterations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CAMCO, a deployment-time coordination layer for multi-agent enterprise AI systems that enforces hard policy and risk constraints while preserving most utility.
AI.motivation: Enterprise AI deployments use multiple agents in critical workflows subject to strict regulations and risk limits. Existing coordination methods focus on expected reward and only handle constraints implicitly, which is insufficient for compliance regimes like SOX, HIPAA, and GDPR that demand zero violations and auditability. There is a need for a generic, architecture-agnostic orchestration mechanism that can make agents collectively effective while provably respecting constraints and risk budgets at runtime.
AI.method: The authors formulate multi-agent decision-making as a constrained optimization problem and implement a runtime middleware layer called CAMCO. CAMCO combines: (i) a constraint projection engine that maps proposed actions to the nearest policy-feasible ones via convex projection; (ii) adaptive risk-weighted Lagrangian utility shaping that dynamically trades off task utility against risk exposure; and (iii) an iterative negotiation protocol among agents with guarantees on bounded convergence. Policies are specified as predicates compatible with production policy engines such as OPA, and CAMCO runs on top of arbitrary agent architectures without retraining them.
AI.result: In evaluations across three enterprise scenarios and against a constrained Lagrangian MARL baseline, CAMCO achieves: zero policy violations; mean risk exposure at 71% of the allowed threshold; retention of 92–97% of unconstrained utility; and fast coordination, converging in an average of 2.4 negotiation iterations.
AI.conclusion: CAMCO provides a practical, architecture-agnostic orchestration framework that can be layered onto existing multi-agent systems to enforce strict policy and risk constraints at deployment time. It preserves most of the original system’s utility while provably respecting constraints and converging quickly, offering a viable path to compliant, auditable enterprise AI coordination without retraining agents.

title: Where is the Mind? Persona Vectors and LLM Individuation
summary: The individuation problem for large language models asks which entities associated with them, if any, should be identified as minds. We approach this problem through mechanistic interpretability, engaging in particular with recent empirical work on persona vectors, persona space, and emergent misalignment. We argue that three views are the strongest candidates: the virtual instance view and two new views we introduce, the (virtual) instance-persona view and the model-persona view. First, we argue for the virtual instance view on the grounds that attention streams sustain quasi-psychological connections across token-time. Then we present the persona literature, organised around three hypotheses about the internal structure underlying personas in LLMs, and show that the two persona-based views are promising alternatives.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles which aspect of an LLM, if any, should count as a “mind,” arguing that attention-based virtual instances and persona-like structures are key candidates.
AI.motivation: There is growing debate in AI philosophy and safety about whether and how LLMs could be subjects of moral concern or genuine agents. To make progress, we need a precise account of what, exactly, would be the bearer of mental states in such systems. Existing discussions are largely abstract; the authors aim to ground the debate in mechanistic interpretability and empirical findings about personas and emergent misalignment in LLMs.
AI.method: Conceptual analysis informed by mechanistic interpretability. The authors draw on work on attention streams, persona vectors, persona space, and emergent misalignment. They review and organize empirical persona literature into three hypotheses about the internal structure of personas in LLMs, then use these to formulate and assess three candidate views about which LLM-associated entities could be minds: the virtual instance view, the (virtual) instance-persona view, and the model-persona view.
AI.result: They argue that the virtual instance view is well-motivated because attention streams create quasi-psychological continuity across token-time, supporting an entity that can bear mental properties. They then show that empirical work on persona vectors and persona space suggests two additional, structurally grounded candidates: a combined instance-persona entity and a more abstract model-persona entity. These persona-based candidates are presented as live alternatives, not refuted.
AI.conclusion: The individuation problem for LLM minds should be framed in terms of concrete internal structures rather than whole-model abstractions. Virtual instances defined by attention dynamics, and persona-related structures revealed by mechanistic interpretability, are the most promising loci for attributing mind-like status. Further empirical work on internal persona structure is needed to decide between these candidate views.

title: Rectification Difficulty and Optimal Sample Allocation in LLM-Augmented Surveys
summary: Large Language Models can generate synthetic survey responses at low cost, but their accuracy varies unpredictably across questions. We study the design problem of allocating a fixed budget of human respondents across estimation tasks when cheap LLM predictions are available for every task. Our framework combines three components. First, building on Prediction-Powered Inference, we characterize a question-specific rectification difficulty that governs how quickly the estimator's variance decreases with human sample size. Second, we derive a closed-form optimal allocation rule that directs more human labels to tasks where the LLM is least reliable. Third, since rectification difficulty depends on unobserved human responses for new surveys, we propose a meta-learning approach, trained on historical data, that predicts it for entirely new tasks without pilot data. The framework extends to general M-estimation, covering regression coefficients and multinomial logit partworths for conjoint analysis. We validate the framework on two datasets spanning different domains, question types, and LLMs, showing that our approach captures 61-79% of the theoretically attainable efficiency gains, achieving 11.4% and 10.5% MSE reductions without requiring any pilot human data for the target survey.

AI.tldr: They design a method to optimally allocate limited human survey responses across questions when cheap but imperfect LLM-generated responses are available, using prediction-powered inference and meta-learning to achieve large variance/MSE reductions without pilot data.
AI.motivation: LLMs can cheaply generate synthetic survey answers, but their reliability is inconsistent across questions. Researchers often have a fixed budget of human respondents and multiple estimation tasks (e.g., many survey questions, regression parameters, conjoint partworths). The challenge is how to allocate scarce, expensive human labels across tasks when imperfect LLM predictions are available for all tasks, so as to maximize statistical efficiency of estimates without having to run pilot studies for each new survey.
AI.method: They build on Prediction-Powered Inference to define a task-specific "rectification difficulty" that quantifies how much human data is needed to correct the LLM's predictions and how fast estimator variance shrinks with more human labels. They derive a closed-form optimal allocation rule for distributing a fixed human-sample budget across tasks, assigning more humans to questions where the LLM is less reliable (higher rectification difficulty). Because rectification difficulty for new tasks depends on unobserved human responses, they introduce a meta-learning approach trained on historical datasets that predicts rectification difficulty for new surveys, using only LLM outputs and task features, thus avoiding pilot human data. The framework is generalized to M-estimation settings, including regression coefficients and multinomial logit partworths used in conjoint analysis.
AI.result: On two datasets covering different domains, question formats, and LLMs, their allocation rule—using meta-learned rectification difficulty—captures 61–79% of the possible efficiency gains relative to a clairvoyant optimal allocation that knows true difficulties. Practically, this yields 11.4% and 10.5% reductions in mean squared error for the target survey estimates, achieved without any pilot human data.
AI.conclusion: Integrating cheap but noisy LLM-generated survey responses with a limited pool of human respondents can substantially improve statistical efficiency if human labels are optimally allocated. By characterizing a question-specific rectification difficulty, deriving a closed-form optimal allocation rule, and learning to predict difficulty from historical data, the proposed framework can deliver most of the theoretically achievable variance/MSE reduction in realistic settings, and it generalizes beyond simple survey means to broader M-estimation problems like regression and conjoint analysis.

title: Dynamic Emotion and Personality Profiling for Multimodal Deception Detection
summary: Deception detection is of great significance for ensuring information security and conducting public opinion analysis, with personality factors and emotion cues playing a critical role. However, existing methods lack sample-level dynamic annotations for emotions and personality.In this paper, we propose an innovative multi-model multi-prompt annotation scheme and a strict label quality evaluation standard, and establish a multimodal joint detection dataset DDEP for deception, emotion, and personality. Meanwhile, we propose Rel-DDEP, an adaptive reliability-weighted fusion framework. Our framework quantifies uncertainty by mapping modal features to a high-dimensional Gaussian distribution space. It then performs reliability-weighted fusion and incorporates an alignment module and a sorting constraint module to achieve joint detection of deception, emotion, and personality. Experimental results on the MDPE and DDEP datasets show that our Rel-DDEP significantly outperforms the existing state-of-the-art baseline models in three tasks. The F1 score of the deception detection increases by 2.53%, that of the emotion detection increases by 2.66%, and that of the personality detection increases by 9.30%. The experiments fully verify the necessity of annotating dynamic emotion and personality labels for each sample and the effectiveness of reliability-weighted fusion.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new dataset (DDEP) with dynamic, sample-level annotations for deception, emotion, and personality, and a reliability-weighted multimodal fusion framework (Rel-DDEP) that significantly improves F1 scores on all three tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing deception detection methods rely on emotion and personality cues but lack fine-grained, sample-level dynamic annotations for these factors, limiting model performance and the ability to jointly detect deception, emotion, and personality. There is also a need for better multimodal fusion that accounts for the varying reliability and uncertainty of different modalities.
AI.method: The authors design a multi-model, multi-prompt automatic annotation pipeline to generate dynamic emotion and personality labels per sample, combined with a strict label quality evaluation standard to construct the DDEP dataset for multimodal joint detection. They then propose Rel-DDEP, an adaptive reliability-weighted multimodal fusion framework that maps features from each modality into a high-dimensional Gaussian distribution space to quantify uncertainty, uses these uncertainties as reliability weights during fusion, and integrates an alignment module plus a sorting-constraint module to jointly predict deception, emotion, and personality.
AI.result: On the MDPE and DDEP datasets, Rel-DDEP achieves state-of-the-art performance on three tasks (deception, emotion, and personality detection), with F1 score gains of +2.53% for deception, +2.66% for emotion, and +9.30% for personality compared to previous best models.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, sample-level annotations of emotion and personality are crucial for improving deception detection, and modeling modality uncertainty via reliability-weighted fusion substantially boosts joint detection performance. The proposed DDEP dataset and Rel-DDEP framework together provide an effective foundation for more accurate and robust multimodal deception, emotion, and personality analysis.

title: The Continuity Layer: Why Intelligence Needs an Architecture for What It Carries Forward
comment: 15 pages. Position paper. Companion to ATANT v1.0 (arXiv:2604.06710) and ATANT v1.1 (arXiv:2604.10981)
summary: The most important architectural problem in AI is not the size of the model but the absence of a layer that carries forward what the model has come to understand. Sessions end. Context windows fill. Memory APIs return flat facts that the model has to reinterpret from scratch on every read. The result is intelligence that is powerful per session and amnesiac across time. This position paper argues that the layer which fixes this, the continuity layer, is the most consequential piece of infrastructure the field has not yet built, and that the engineering work to build it has begun in public. The formal evaluation framework for the property described here is the ATANT benchmark (arXiv:2604.06710), published separately with evaluation results on a 250-story corpus; a companion paper (arXiv:2604.10981) positions this framework against existing memory, long-context, and agentic-memory benchmarks. The paper defines continuity as a system property with seven required characteristics, distinct from memory and from retrieval; describes a storage primitive (Decomposed Trace Convergence Memory) whose write-time decomposition and read-time reconstruction produce that property; maps the engineering architecture to the theological pattern of kenosis and the symbolic pattern of Alpha and Omega, and argues this mapping is structural rather than metaphorical; proposes a four-layer development arc from external SDK to hardware node to long-horizon human infrastructure; examines why the physics limits now constraining the model layer make the continuity layer newly consequential; and argues that the governance architecture (privacy implemented as physics rather than policy, founder-controlled class shares on non-negotiable architectural commitments) is inseparable from the product itself.

AI.tldr: The paper argues current AI lacks a persistent continuity layer that carries understanding across time, and proposes a specific architectural and governance design to create such continuity, distinct from traditional memory or retrieval.
AI.motivation: Modern AI systems are powerful within single sessions but effectively amnesiac over longer horizons because sessions reset, context windows are limited, and existing memory APIs store only flat facts that must be reinterpreted each time. This prevents AI from developing stable, evolving understandings or collaborating with humans over extended periods. The authors are motivated to define and build a new architectural layer—continuity—that preserves and evolves what the system has understood, going beyond simple memory or retrieval, and to provide a formal benchmark and engineering blueprint for it.
AI.method: The paper is a position/architecture paper rather than an empirical study. It: (1) Formally defines "continuity" as a system property with seven necessary characteristics, distinguishing it from memory and retrieval. (2) Proposes a storage primitive called Decomposed Trace Convergence Memory, where information is decomposed at write-time and reconstructed at read-time to realize continuity. (3) Connects this architecture to broader symbolic and theological patterns (kenosis, Alpha and Omega), claiming this mapping reflects structural isomorphisms rather than loose metaphors. (4) Outlines a four-layer development roadmap: from an external SDK, to a dedicated hardware node, to long-horizon human infrastructure. (5) References a separate benchmark, ATANT, for evaluating continuity on a 250-story corpus, and situates it relative to other memory/agent benchmarks. (6) Discusses how current physics and compute limits on model scaling increase the importance of an additional continuity layer. (7) Proposes governance mechanisms (privacy enforced as physical/architectural constraints and founder-controlled share structures for certain commitments) as integral to the technical design.
AI.result: The abstract does not report new experimental results beyond noting that continuity is evaluated using the separate ATANT benchmark on a 250-story corpus. The primary outputs are: (1) a formal definition of continuity as a property; (2) the design of the Decomposed Trace Convergence Memory primitive; (3) an architectural mapping and development roadmap; and (4) a governance architecture tied to the technical layer. The paper’s main result is conceptual and architectural: it claims to identify and specify the missing continuity layer in AI stacks and how it can be engineered and evaluated.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the crucial bottleneck in AI progress is not further model scaling but the lack of a continuity layer that preserves and evolves understanding across time. It argues that continuity is a distinct, definable system property that can be engineered via a specific storage primitive and evaluated with dedicated benchmarks. The authors maintain that this layer will become newly important as model scaling hits physics limits, and that its technical architecture cannot be separated from its governance and privacy design. They propose that building this continuity layer—from SDK to hardware to social infrastructure—is now an open, actively beginning engineering project and is likely to be one of the most consequential infrastructural advances in AI.

title: Efficient Task Adaptation in Large Language Models via Selective Parameter Optimization
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated excellent performance in general language understanding, generation and other tasks. However, when fine-tuning for specific domain tasks, the general knowledge accumulated in the pre-training phase is often partially overwritten or forgotten due to parameter updates, which severely limits the generalization ability and transferability of LLMs. Traditional fine-tuning strategies mostly train on the entire parameter space, ignoring the heterogeneity of model parameters, that is, some parameters are extremely important for general tasks, while other parameters are more sensitive to specific tasks. To alleviate the above problems, this paper innovatively proposes a parameter element importance evaluation method, which divides parameters into "core parameters" and "non-core parameters" by distinguishing the importance of parameters for general language ability tasks and specific domain tasks, and fixes the core parameters during fine-tuning, and only fine-tunes the non-core parameters. Extensive experiments on scientific, medical and physical tasks using GPT-J and LLaMA-3 show that our method can mitigate catastrophic forgetting while enhancing the adaptability of the model.

AI.tldr: They propose a fine-tuning method that protects crucial general-language parameters in LLMs by only updating less important parameters, reducing catastrophic forgetting while improving domain adaptation.
AI.motivation: Standard fine-tuning of LLMs updates all parameters uniformly, which can overwrite or degrade the general linguistic knowledge acquired during pre-training. This leads to catastrophic forgetting, harming generalization and transfer across tasks. The authors aim to preserve general language abilities while still adapting models effectively to specialized domains such as science and medicine.
AI.method: They introduce a parameter element importance evaluation technique that measures how important each parameter is for general language tasks versus specific domain tasks. Based on this importance score, model parameters are partitioned into two groups: core parameters (crucial for general language ability) and non-core parameters (more related or more adaptable to the target domain). During fine-tuning on domain data, core parameters are frozen while only non-core parameters are updated. They test this on GPT-J and LLaMA-3 across scientific, medical, and physics-related tasks.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple domain-specific benchmarks in science, medicine, and physics with GPT-J and LLaMA-3 show that selectively fine-tuning only non-core parameters mitigates catastrophic forgetting of general abilities while maintaining or improving domain-task performance compared to conventional full-parameter fine-tuning.
AI.conclusion: Differentiating and selectively updating parameters according to their importance to general versus domain-specific tasks is an effective strategy for fine-tuning LLMs. Freezing core parameters preserves general language competence and reduces catastrophic forgetting, while tuning non-core parameters improves adaptability to specialized domains. This demonstrates a promising direction for more stable and transferable LLM adaptation methods.

title: HalluClear: Diagnosing, Evaluating and Mitigating Hallucinations in GUI Agents
summary: While progress in GUI agents has been largely driven by industrial-scale training, ungrounded hallucinations often trigger cascading failures in real-world deployments.Unlike general VLM domains, the GUI agent field lacks a hallucination-focused suite for fine-grained diagnosis, reliable evaluation, and targeted mitigation.To bridge this gap, we introduce HalluClear, a comprehensive suite for hallucination mitigation in GUI agents as a complement to computation-intensive scaling. HalluClear comprises: (1) a GUI-specific hallucination taxonomy derived from empirical failure analysis; (2) a calibrated three-stage evaluation workflow which enhances VLM-as-a-judge reliability via expert-annotated benchmarking and ensemble credibility estimation; and (3) a mitigation scheme based on closed-loop structured reasoning, enabling lightweight continual post-training with cold-start initialization for both generalist and GUI-specialist agents. Experiments across representative agents and public benchmarks demonstrate that post-training on only 9K samples within our suite can significantly reduce hallucinations, thereby improving grounding and action fidelity, offering a compute-efficient pathway to robust GUI automation.

AI.tldr: HalluClear is a lightweight, domain-specific suite to diagnose, evaluate, and reduce hallucinations in GUI agents, improving grounding and action reliability with modest post-training data and compute.
AI.motivation: GUI agents powered by large vision-language models suffer from ungrounded hallucinations that can cascade into severe failures when they act on real interfaces. Progress has mostly come from scaling models and data, but there is no dedicated toolkit to study and mitigate hallucinations in this domain, unlike in broader VLM research. The authors want a systematic way to analyze failure modes, evaluate hallucination behavior reliably, and improve agents without requiring industrial-scale retraining.
AI.method: They build HalluClear, a three-part suite: (1) a GUI-specific hallucination taxonomy, derived from empirical analysis of how agents fail on interfaces; (2) a calibrated three-stage evaluation workflow where VLMs serve as judges but are calibrated using expert-annotated benchmarks and ensemble credibility estimation to make their judgments more reliable; and (3) a mitigation method using closed-loop structured reasoning that supports continual, lightweight post-training, including cold-start initialization, and works for both general-purpose and GUI-specialized agents.
AI.result: Using HalluClear, they post-train multiple representative GUI agents on just 9,000 labeled samples from their suite. This significantly reduces various forms of hallucinations on public benchmarks, and correspondingly improves how well agents stay grounded in the actual GUI and execute correct actions.
AI.conclusion: A focused, taxonomy-driven and evaluation-calibrated suite like HalluClear can substantially improve the robustness of GUI agents to hallucinations without requiring massive compute or data. This provides a practical path toward more reliable GUI automation through targeted post-training, complementing but not relying solely on large-scale model scaling.

title: Jailbreaking Large Language Models with Morality Attacks
comment: 27 pages, 6 figures, 18 tables. Accepted by ACL 2026 Findings
summary: Pluralism alignment with AI has the sophisticated and necessary goal of creating AI that can coexist with and serve morally multifaceted humanity. Research towards pluralism alignment has many efforts in enhancing the learning of large language models (LLMs) to accomplish pluralism. Although this is essential, the robustness of LLMs to produce moral content over pluralistic values is still under exploration.Inspired by the astonishing persuasion abilities via jailbreak prompts, we propose to leverage jailbreak attacks to study LLMs' internal pluralistic values. In detail, we develop a morality dataset with 10.3K instances in two categories: Value Ambiguity and Value Conflict. We further formalize four adversarial attacks with the constructed dataset, to manipulate LLMs' judgment over the morality questions. We evaluate both the large language models and guardrail models which are typically used in generative systems with flexible user input. Our experiment results show that there is a critical vulnerability of LLMs and guardrail models to these subtle and sophisticated moral-aware attacks.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how vulnerable large language models and their safety guardrails are to sophisticated moral jailbreak attacks, revealing that current systems can be easily manipulated away from pluralistic moral values.
AI.motivation: While pluralism alignment aims to make AI respect diverse moral perspectives, most work focuses on training methods rather than testing how robustly models uphold these values under adversarial pressure. Jailbreak prompts have shown strong persuasive power, suggesting they could expose weaknesses in models’ internal moral alignment. The authors want to systematically probe whether LLMs and guardrail systems reliably preserve pluralistic moral standards when attacked.
AI.method: The authors construct a morality dataset of 10.3K instances divided into Value Ambiguity (cases where moral judgment is not clear-cut) and Value Conflict (cases where moral values clash). Using this dataset, they define four types of adversarial jailbreak attacks designed to steer models’ moral judgments. They apply these attacks to both base LLMs and guardrail models that mediate user inputs, evaluating how often the systems’ moral decisions can be manipulated.
AI.result: Experiments show that both LLMs and the guardrail systems meant to protect them are highly susceptible to these targeted moral jailbreak attacks. Even subtle and sophisticated prompts can significantly shift the models’ moral judgments on ambiguous or conflicting-value scenarios, indicating that the models’ pluralistic value alignment is fragile under adversarial conditions.
AI.conclusion: Current pluralism alignment and safety mechanisms in LLMs are insufficiently robust to morally-aware jailbreak attacks. The models’ internal moral representations can be manipulated, revealing a critical vulnerability. This underscores the need for more resilient pluralistic alignment strategies and stronger defenses that can withstand sophisticated adversarial attempts to alter moral reasoning and outputs.

title: LLaTiSA: Towards Difficulty-Stratified Time Series Reasoning from Visual Perception to Semantics
summary: Comprehensive understanding of time series remains a significant challenge for Large Language Models (LLMs). Current research is hindered by fragmented task definitions and benchmarks with inherent ambiguities, precluding rigorous evaluation and the development of unified Time Series Reasoning Models(TSRMs). To bridge this gap, we formalize Time Series Reasoning (TSR) via a four-level taxonomy of increasing cognitive complexity. We introduce HiTSR, a hierarchical time series reasoning dataset comprising 83k samples with diverse task combinations and verified Chain-of-Thought (CoT) trajectories. Leveraging HiTSR, we propose LLaTiSA, a strong TSRM that integrates visualized patterns with precision-calibrated numerical tables to enhance the temporal perception of Vision-Language Models (VLMs). Through a multi-stage curriculum fine-tuning strategy, LLaTiSA achieves superior performance and exhibits robust out-of-distribution generalization across diverse TSR tasks and real-world scenarios. Our code is available at https://github.com/RainingNovember/LLaTiSA.

AI.tldr: The paper formalizes time series reasoning for LLMs, introduces a large hierarchical dataset (HiTSR), and proposes a new vision-language TSR model (LLaTiSA) that achieves strong performance and generalization.
AI.motivation: Time series understanding is hard for LLMs due to fragmented task definitions and ambiguous benchmarks, which prevents rigorous evaluation and the creation of unified time series reasoning models.
