The authors aim to establish a clear taxonomy, standardized benchmarks, and stronger models for time series reasoning.
AI.method: 1) Define a four-level taxonomy for Time Series Reasoning (TSR) based on increasing cognitive complexity. 2) Construct HiTSR, an 83k-sample hierarchical dataset with diverse combinations of TSR tasks and verified Chain-of-Thought annotations. 3) Design LLaTiSA, a Time Series Reasoning Model that feeds vision-language models with both visualized time series patterns and precision-calibrated numerical tables to improve temporal perception. 4) Apply a multi-stage curriculum fine-tuning strategy using HiTSR to train LLaTiSA.
AI.result: LLaTiSA, trained on HiTSR with curriculum fine-tuning, outperforms baselines on various time series reasoning tasks and demonstrates strong out-of-distribution generalization to different TSR tasks and real-world scenarios.
AI.conclusion: By formalizing TSR with a clear taxonomy, providing a large hierarchical dataset with CoT supervision, and introducing LLaTiSA that combines visual and tabular representations, the paper advances time series reasoning for LLMs and VLMs, enabling more accurate and generalizable temporal understanding.

title: Stability-Weighted Decoding for Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion large language models (dLLMs) enable parallel text generation by iteratively denoising a fully masked sequence, unmasking a subset of masked tokens at each step. Existing decoding strategies rely on static confidence metrics computed at a single denoising step, ignoring temporal history and often leading to premature unmasking of unstable tokens. In this work, we theoretically establish that a token's temporal instability, quantified by the KL divergence between consecutive prediction distributions, provides a strict lower bound on its mutual information with the remaining masked context, indicating that temporally unstable tokens are inherently unsafe to unmask. Based on this insight, we propose Stability-Weighted Decoding (SWD), a training-free, plug-and-play strategy that incorporates temporal stability into token scoring and acts as a universal modulator for arbitrary score-based decoding policies. Experiments on code generation and mathematical reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that SWD consistently improves generation accuracy across representative scoring metrics and selection policies, and exhibits exceptional robustness, maintaining a significant performance lead over standard baselines across varying acceleration ratios.

AI.tldr: The paper improves decoding for diffusion-based large language models by using temporal stability of token predictions to avoid prematurely unmasking unstable tokens, leading to more accurate and robust parallel text generation.
AI.motivation: Existing decoding strategies for diffusion LLMs rely on static confidence estimates from a single denoising step, which ignore the temporal evolution of token predictions and can cause unstable tokens to be unmasked too early, harming generation quality. There is a need for a principled way to use temporal information to guide which tokens should be fixed at each step.
AI.method: The authors theoretically analyze the relationship between a token’s temporal instability—measured as KL divergence between its prediction distributions at consecutive denoising steps—and its mutual information with the still-masked context. They prove that this temporal instability lower-bounds the mutual information, implying that unstable tokens are still strongly coupled to unresolved context. Using this, they design Stability-Weighted Decoding (SWD), a training-free, plug-and-play scheme that modulates existing token scoring and selection policies by downweighting unstable tokens. SWD integrates temporal stability signals into any score-based decoding rule in diffusion LLMs.
AI.result: On code generation and math reasoning benchmarks, SWD consistently boosts generation accuracy when combined with various existing scoring metrics and selection strategies. It shows strong robustness: even when the number of denoising steps is reduced to accelerate inference (higher acceleration ratios), SWD preserves a sizable performance advantage over standard baselines that lack stability weighting.
AI.conclusion: Temporal stability of token predictions is a reliable indicator of how safe it is to fix tokens during diffusion LLM decoding. By using KL-based instability as a proxy for mutual information with masked context, the proposed SWD method provides a principled, training-free way to improve decoding for diffusion LLMs, yielding more accurate and robust parallel text generation across tasks and decoding configurations.

title: Efficient Test-Time Scaling via Temporal Reasoning Aggregation
comment: Accepted to Findings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Test-time scaling improves the reasoning performance of large language models but often results in token-inefficient overthinking, where models continue reasoning beyond what is necessary for a correct answer. Existing dynamic early-exit methods typically rely on single-step confidence signals, which are often unreliable for detecting reasoning convergence in multi-step settings. To mitigate this limitation, we propose TRACE, a training-free framework for efficient test-time scaling that determines when to terminate reasoning based on temporal aggregation of multi-step evidence rather than instantaneous signals. TRACE detects reasoning convergence over time by aggregating two complementary signals across recent reasoning steps: answer consistency, capturing the persistence of predicted answers, and confidence trajectory, modeling the temporal evolution of model confidence. Benefiting from these two factors, TRACE can accurately determine whether the reasoning process has converged, thereby promptly halting inference and effectively avoiding redundant reasoning steps. Extensive experiments on multiple challenging benchmarks show that TRACE reduces reasoning token usage by 25-30% on average while maintaining accuracy within 1-2% of full-length reasoning, consistently outperforming existing dynamic reasoning methods.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TRACE, a training-free framework that makes test-time reasoning of large language models more efficient by detecting when the reasoning has converged, cutting 25–30% of reasoning tokens with almost no accuracy loss.
AI.motivation: Test-time scaling improves reasoning by allowing LLMs to generate longer chain-of-thought, but this often leads to overthinking and wasted tokens. Existing dynamic early-exit methods rely on single-step confidence signals, which are unreliable for multi-step reasoning and can either stop too early (hurting accuracy) or too late (wasting tokens). A better way is needed to detect true convergence of the reasoning process over time.
AI.method: TRACE is a training-free, dynamic test-time framework that decides when to halt reasoning by aggregating information across multiple recent reasoning steps. It uses two temporal signals: (1) answer consistency, which measures how stable the predicted answer is across steps, and (2) confidence trajectory, which models how the model’s confidence evolves over time. By jointly tracking these aggregated temporal patterns rather than instantaneous confidence, TRACE infers when the reasoning process has converged and triggers early stopping.
AI.result: On multiple challenging reasoning benchmarks, TRACE reduces the number of reasoning tokens used by about 25–30% on average while keeping accuracy within 1–2% of full-length chain-of-thought inference. It consistently outperforms prior dynamic reasoning / early-exit methods in the trade-off between accuracy and token efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Temporal aggregation of multi-step evidence—via answer consistency and confidence trajectory—provides a more reliable signal of reasoning convergence than single-step confidence. TRACE leverages this to offer a practical, training-free way to make test-time scaling more token-efficient, substantially cutting redundant reasoning while nearly preserving full-length performance and beating existing dynamic early-exit approaches.

title: Abstain-R1: Calibrated Abstention and Post-Refusal Clarification via Verifiable RL
summary: Reinforcement fine-tuning improves the reasoning ability of large language models, but it can also encourage them to answer unanswerable queries by guessing or hallucinating missing information. Existing abstention methods either train models to produce generic refusals or encourage follow-up clarifications without verifying whether those clarifications identify the key missing information. We study queries that are clear in meaning but cannot be reliably resolved from the given information, and argue that a reliable model should not only abstain, but also explain what is missing. We propose a clarification-aware RLVR reward that, while rewarding correct answers on answerable queries, jointly optimizes explicit abstention and semantically aligned post-refusal clarification on unanswerable queries. Using this reward, we train Abstain-R1, a 3B model that improves abstention and clarification on unanswerable queries while preserving strong performance on answerable ones. Experiments on Abstain-Test, Abstain-QA, and SelfAware show that Abstain-R1 substantially improves over its base model and achieves unanswerable-query behavior competitive with larger systems including DeepSeek-R1, suggesting that calibrated abstention and clarification can be learned through verifiable rewards rather than emerging from scale alone.

AI.tldr: They propose a new RL-based reward scheme that teaches small LLMs not just to abstain on unanswerable questions, but also to explain what information is missing, achieving behavior competitive with much larger models.
AI.motivation: Reinforcement fine-tuning improves LLM reasoning but also makes models more likely to guess or hallucinate when a question is actually unanswerable. Existing abstention methods lead to generic refusals or clarifications that are not grounded in the true missing information. There is a need for models that can recognize truly unanswerable queries, abstain reliably, and explicitly identify what key information is missing, all while maintaining strong performance on answerable questions.
AI.method: They define a new reward function called clarification-aware RLVR. This reward simultaneously: (1) rewards correct answers on answerable queries, and (2) on unanswerable queries, rewards the model for explicitly abstaining and for providing a clarification that is semantically aligned with the actually missing information. They then use this reward to reinforcement-train a 3B-parameter model called Abstain-R1. Training data are partitioned into answerable and unanswerable queries, with verifiable signals used to assess correctness, abstention, and the semantic quality of post-refusal clarifications.
AI.result: The trained Abstain-R1 3B model shows improved behavior on unanswerable queries: it abstains more appropriately and its clarifications better pinpoint the missing information, compared to its base model and other baselines. On benchmarks including Abstain-Test, Abstain-QA, and SelfAware, Abstain-R1 matches or exceeds larger models such as DeepSeek-R1 specifically in calibrated abstention and clarification, while maintaining strong performance on answerable questions.
AI.conclusion: Clarification-aware RLVR can effectively teach even relatively small LLMs to exhibit calibrated abstention: recognizing when questions are unanswerable, refusing to guess, and explaining what information is missing. This behavior, often assumed to arise mainly from model scale, can instead be learned via carefully designed, verifiable rewards. The approach yields a compact model (Abstain-R1, 3B) whose unanswerable-query behavior is competitive with much larger systems, suggesting a practical path toward safer and more transparent LLM deployments.

title: SkillFlow:Benchmarking Lifelong Skill Discovery and Evolution for Autonomous Agents
summary: As the capability frontier of autonomous agents continues to expand, they are increasingly able to complete specialized tasks through plug-and-play external skills. Yet current benchmarks mostly test whether models can use provided skills, leaving open whether they can discover skills from experience, repair them after failure, and maintain a coherent library over time. We introduce SkillFlow, a benchmark of 166 tasks across 20 families in which task construction within each family follows a Domain-Agnostic Execution Flow (DAEF) that defines an agent workflow framework, allowing these tasks to share a consistent workflow. Agents are evaluated under an Agentic Lifelong Learning protocol in which they begin without skills, solve tasks sequentially within each family, externalize lessons through trajectory- and rubric-driven skill patches, and carry the updated library forward. Experiments reveal a substantial capability gap. For Claude Opus 4.6, lifelong skill evolution improves task success from 62.65% to 71.08% (+8.43 points). However, high skill usage does not necessarily imply high utility: Kimi K2.5 gains only +0.60 points despite 66.87% skill usage, while Qwen-Coder-Next reaches only a 44.58% task completion rate and still regresses relative to the vanilla setting. SkillFlow contributes a structured testbed for this direction and an in-depth empirical analysis of skill discovery, patching, transfer, and their failure modes under lifelong evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper presents SkillFlow, a benchmark to test whether autonomous agents can discover, refine, and maintain reusable skills over time across related tasks, revealing a significant gap between current model capabilities and robust lifelong skill management.
AI.motivation: Existing benchmarks largely assess if models can use pre-given tools or skills on isolated tasks, but they do not evaluate whether agents can autonomously discover skills from experience, repair them after failures, and manage a consistent, evolving skill library across a sequence of related tasks. As autonomous agents grow more capable and are expected to operate in dynamic environments, assessing lifelong skill evolution becomes essential for building reliable and scalable systems.
AI.method: The authors design SkillFlow, a benchmark with 166 tasks grouped into 20 families. Within each family, tasks are generated according to a Domain-Agnostic Execution Flow (DAEF), which specifies a general workflow that different tasks in that family instantiate. Agents are evaluated using an Agentic Lifelong Learning protocol: they start with no external skills, solve tasks sequentially within a family, and after each task they can externalize what they learned into skill patches guided by trajectories and rubrics. This evolving skill library is then reused and updated on later tasks in the same family, enabling measurement of skill discovery, patching, transfer, and retention.
AI.result: In experiments on several state-of-the-art models, the authors find a large capability gap between current systems and robust lifelong skill management. For Claude Opus 4.6, enabling lifelong skill evolution increases task success from 62.65% to 71.08% (+8.43 percentage points), illustrating that skill patches can help. However, other models exhibit weaker or even negative returns: Kimi K2.5 shows high skill usage (66.87%) but only a small improvement of +0.60 points, and Qwen-Coder-Next achieves only 44.58% task completion and performs worse than in the vanilla, no-skill-evolution setting. These results indicate that simply using many skills does not guarantee utility, and that current models struggle with skill quality, repair, and transfer.
AI.conclusion: SkillFlow provides a structured, domain-agnostic benchmark for evaluating lifelong skill evolution in autonomous agents, going beyond simple tool-use tests. The study demonstrates that while some models can benefit meaningfully from self-discovered and patched skills, overall performance reveals serious weaknesses in discovering high-quality skills, repairing them after failure, and maintaining a coherent, useful skill library over time. The benchmark and analyses aim to guide future research on agents that learn, refine, and organize skills across their operational lifetime.

title: Auditing Support Strategies in LLMs through Grounded Multi-Turn Social Simulation
summary: When users seek social support from chatbots, they disclose their situation gradually, yet most evaluations of supportive LLMs rely on single-turn, fully specified prompts. We introduce a multi-turn simulation framework that closes this gap. Support-seeking narratives from five Reddit communities are decomposed into ordered fragments and revealed turn by turn to a language model. Each response is coded with the Social Support Behavior Code (SSBC), an established multi-label taxonomy that captures the composition of support, rather than a single quality score. To ask whether support choices track the model's own construal of user distress, we use linear probes on hidden representations to estimate this internal signal without altering the generation context. Across two mid-scale models (Llama-3.1-8B, OLMo-3-7B) and more than 6,200 turns, support composition shifts systematically with estimated distress: teaching declines as estimated distress rises, a finding that replicates across architectures, while increases in affective and esteem-oriented strategies (such as validation) are suggestive but model-specific and rest on noisier annotations. Community context independently shapes behavior, tracking topic and discourse norms rather than demographic categories. These trajectory-level dynamics, invisible to single-turn evaluation, motivate multi-turn auditing frameworks for socially sensitive applications.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how chatbots provide social support over multi-turn conversations, showing that support strategies systematically change as the model infers more user distress, and arguing that single-turn evaluations miss these dynamics.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations of supportive LLMs typically use single-turn, fully specified prompts, whereas real users seeking social support reveal their situation gradually over multiple turns. This mismatch hides how support strategies evolve with the model’s evolving understanding of user distress and ignores the nuanced composition of support behaviors. The paper aims to close this gap and to understand how internal distress signals in models relate to their shifting support strategies in realistic, multi-turn settings.
AI.method: The authors build a multi-turn simulation framework using support-seeking posts from five Reddit communities. They decompose each post into ordered fragments that are revealed sequentially to a language model (Llama-3.1-8B and OLMo-3-7B). At each turn, the model responds, and each response is annotated using the Social Support Behavior Code (SSBC), a multi-label taxonomy capturing different types of social support (e.g., informational, emotional, esteem). To probe the model’s internal perception of user distress, they train linear probes on hidden representations to estimate an internal distress signal, without modifying the generation context. They then analyze how the composition of support (SSBC labels) changes as estimated distress and community context vary across more than 6,200 turns.
AI.result: They find systematic changes in support composition as the model’s estimated distress signal increases. Specifically, teaching/instructional support declines reliably at higher estimated distress levels across both architectures (Llama and OLMo). Shifts toward affective and esteem-oriented strategies (such as validation) appear, but these effects are model-dependent and rely on noisier annotations, so they are less robust. Community context exerts an independent effect: models adjust their support styles to fit topic and discourse norms of each subreddit, rather than to demographic categories. These dynamics are only visible when examining full multi-turn trajectories, not isolated single turns.
AI.conclusion: LLMs’ social support behavior is dynamic across multi-turn interactions and is modulated by their internal construal of user distress and by community-specific norms. Single-turn, fully specified prompt evaluations fail to capture these evolving support trajectories and can thus misrepresent how models behave in realistic support-seeking scenarios. The findings underscore the need for multi-turn, trajectory-level auditing frameworks, especially in socially sensitive applications like mental health or emotional support, to ensure that support strategies appropriately track user distress and context.

title: Knows: Agent-Native Structured Research Representations
comment: This paper serves as a technical report/white paper for the Knows.Academy project (https://knows.academy)
summary:
Research artifacts are distributed primarily as reader-oriented documents like PDFs. This creates a bottleneck for increasingly agent-assisted and agent-native research workflows, in which LLM agents need to infer fine-grained, task-relevant information from lengthy full documents, a process that is expensive, repetitive, and unstable at scale.
  We introduce Knows, a lightweight companion specification that binds structured claims, evidence, provenance, and verifiable relations to existing research artifacts in a form LLM agents can consume directly. Knows addresses the gap with a thin YAML sidecar (KnowsRecord) that coexists with the original PDF, requiring no changes to the publication itself, and validated by a deterministic schema linter. We evaluate Knows on 140 comprehension questions across 20 papers spanning 14 academic disciplines, comparing PDF-only, sidecar-only, and hybrid conditions across six LLM agents of varying capacity. Weak models (0.8B--2B parameters) improve from 19--25\% to 47--67\% accuracy (+29 to +42 percentage points) when reading sidecar instead of PDF, while consuming 29--86\% fewer input tokens; an LLM-as-judge re-scoring confirms that weak-model sidecar accuracy (75--77\%) approaches stronger-model PDF accuracy (78--83\%). Beyond this controlled evaluation, a community sidecar hub at https://knows.academy/ has already indexed over ten thousand publications and continues to grow daily, providing independent evidence that the format is adoption-ready at scale.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Knows, a lightweight YAML sidecar format that attaches structured, agent-readable claims, evidence, and provenance to research PDFs to make them easier and cheaper for LLM agents to consume.
AI.motivation: Current research artifacts are mainly distributed as PDFs aimed at human readers. LLM-based research agents must repeatedly parse long, unstructured documents to extract fine-grained information, which is costly, slow, and unreliable at scale. There is a need for a standard, machine-friendly representation of key research claims and evidence that can coexist with traditional PDFs without changing publication workflows.
AI.method: The authors design Knows, a companion specification represented as a thin YAML "sidecar" file (KnowsRecord) that attaches to an existing PDF and encodes structured claims, evidence, provenance, and verifiable relations. The format is validated via a deterministic schema linter. They then evaluate Knows with 140 comprehension questions over 20 papers from 14 academic fields, testing six LLM agents of various sizes under three conditions: reading only PDFs, only sidecars, or both (hybrid). Accuracy and token consumption are measured, and an LLM-as-judge is used for re-scoring to assess answer quality.
AI.result: Weak LLMs (0.8B–2B parameters) show large gains when using the sidecar instead of the PDF, improving from 19–25% to 47–67% accuracy, an increase of 29–42 percentage points, while using 29–86% fewer input tokens. An LLM-as-judge re-evaluation finds that weak-model accuracy on sidecars (75–77%) nearly matches that of stronger models reading PDFs (78–83%). Additionally, a public community hub (https://knows.academy/) has indexed over 10,000 publications with Knows sidecars, demonstrating early large-scale adoption.
AI.conclusion: Knows provides an adoption-ready, lightweight, and publication-compatible sidecar format that enables LLM agents to access structured research knowledge more accurately and efficiently than parsing PDFs alone. It substantially boosts weak-model performance while reducing token costs and shows signs of scalable, real-world uptake via a growing public index of Knows-annotated papers.

title: Comparing Human and Large Language Model Interpretation of Implicit Information
summary: The interpretation of implicit meanings is an integral aspect of human communication. However, this framework may not transfer to interactions with Large Language Models (LLMs). To investigate this, we introduce the task of Implicit Information Extraction (IIE) and propose an LLM-based IIE pipeline that builds a structured knowledge graph from a context sentence by extracting relational triplets, validating implicit inferences, and analyzing temporal relations. We evaluate two LLMs against crowdsourced human judgments on two datasets. We find that humans agree with most model triplets yet consistently propose many additions, indicating limited coverage in current LLM-based IIE. Moreover, in our experiments, models appear to be more conservative about implicit inferences than humans in socially rich contexts, whereas humans become more conservative in shorter, fact-oriented contexts. Our code is available at https://github.com/Antonio-Dee/IIE_from_LLM.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces and studies Implicit Information Extraction (IIE), showing that current LLMs can extract many correct implicit relational facts from text but with limited coverage and different conservativeness patterns compared to humans, especially across social vs factual contexts.
AI.motivation: Human communication relies heavily on implicit meanings and inferences, but it is unclear whether current LLMs can reliably recover such implicit information from text. Existing information extraction and knowledge graph construction methods mainly target explicit statements, leaving a gap in understanding and modeling the implicit layer of meaning that humans readily infer. The authors aim to systematically define and evaluate how well LLMs can extract implicit relational knowledge and how this compares to human judgments, in order to better understand LLMs’ capabilities and limitations for deeper, human-like text understanding.
AI.method: The authors define the task of Implicit Information Extraction (IIE), where the goal is to build a structured knowledge graph from a single context sentence, including both explicit and implicit relational triplets. They propose an LLM-based pipeline with three stages: (1) extract relational triplets from the context sentence, (2) validate which of these triplets correspond to valid implicit inferences, and (3) analyze temporal relations among the extracted facts. They apply this pipeline using two different LLMs and evaluate their outputs against crowdsourced human judgments on two datasets designed to contain both socially rich and fact-oriented contexts. The analysis focuses on agreement rates, coverage (how many human-proposed triplets are missed by models), and comparative conservativeness in making implicit inferences across context types.
AI.result: The LLMs generate relational triplets that humans judge as mostly correct, indicating that the models are capable of capturing many explicit and some implicit relations. However, human annotators consistently suggest numerous additional triplets that the models fail to extract, revealing substantial coverage gaps in LLM-based IIE. The study also uncovers a systematic difference in inference behavior: in socially rich contexts, LLMs are more conservative than humans, omitting many plausible implicit inferences, whereas in short, fact-oriented contexts, humans become more conservative and the models relatively more willing to infer, leading to different patterns of over- and under-inference depending on context type.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that while current LLMs can support Implicit Information Extraction and align reasonably well with human judgments on the triplets they do extract, they still fall short in coverage and exhibit context-dependent conservativeness that diverges from human inference patterns. This highlights that LLMs do not yet fully mirror human handling of implicit meaning, especially in socially nuanced scenarios. The authors’ IIE task and pipeline, along with the released code, establish a framework and benchmark for future work on improving LLMs’ ability to recover and structure implicit relational knowledge from text.

title: AutoSearch: Adaptive Search Depth for Efficient Agentic RAG via Reinforcement Learning
summary: Agentic retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) systems enable large language models (LLMs) to solve complex tasks through multi-step interaction with external retrieval tools. However, such multi-step interaction often involves redundant search steps, incurring substantial computational cost and latency. Prior work limits search depth (i.e., the number of search steps) to reduce cost, but this often leads to underexploration of complex questions. To address this, we first investigate how search depth affects accuracy and find a minimal sufficient search depth that defines an accuracy-efficiency trade-off, jointly determined by question complexity and the agent's capability. Furthermore, we propose AutoSearch, a reinforcement learning (RL) framework that evaluates each search step via self-generated intermediate answers. By a self-answering mechanism, AutoSearch identifies the minimal sufficient search depth and promotes efficient search by rewarding its attainment while penalizing over-searching. In addition, reward mechanisms are introduced to stabilize search behavior and improve answer quality on complex questions. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks show that AutoSearch achieves a superior accuracy-efficiency trade-off, alleviating over-searching while preserving search quality.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AutoSearch, an RL-based framework that adaptively decides how many retrieval steps an agentic RAG system should take, achieving better accuracy-efficiency trade-offs by avoiding unnecessary searches while maintaining performance on complex questions.
AI.motivation: Agentic RAG systems use multi-step retrieval to let LLMs solve complex tasks, but this often introduces redundant search steps, leading to high computational cost and latency. Existing approaches use fixed limits on search depth, which can underexplore complex questions and harm accuracy. The authors are motivated to find a principled way to adapt search depth per question, balancing efficiency and accuracy based on question complexity and agent capability.
AI.method: The authors first empirically study how search depth impacts accuracy and identify the concept of a minimal sufficient search depth that captures the best accuracy-efficiency trade-off for a given question and agent. They then introduce AutoSearch, a reinforcement learning framework where the agent evaluates each retrieval step using self-generated intermediate answers. A self-answering mechanism estimates when the current information is sufficient, and the RL policy is trained with rewards that encourage stopping at the minimal sufficient depth and penalize unnecessary additional searches. Additional reward shaping and stabilization mechanisms are designed to regularize search behavior and enhance answer quality on more complex questions.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks, AutoSearch reduces the number of retrieval steps compared to baselines that use fixed or naive depth controls, thereby lowering computational cost and latency. At the same time, it maintains or improves answer accuracy, particularly on complex questions. The experiments show that AutoSearch yields a better accuracy-efficiency trade-off and mitigates the over-searching behavior of standard agentic RAG systems.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive control of search depth is crucial for efficient agentic RAG. By learning when to stop searching through self-answering and reinforcement learning, AutoSearch can automatically discover the minimal sufficient search depth per query, reducing redundant computation without sacrificing performance. This demonstrates that principled RL-based control over retrieval steps can significantly improve the practicality of multi-step RAG systems.

title: GenericAgent: A Token-Efficient Self-Evolving LLM Agent via Contextual Information Density Maximization (V1.0)
summary: Long-horizon large language model (LLM) agents are fundamentally limited by context. As interactions become longer, tool descriptions, retrieved memories, and raw environmental feedback accumulate and push out the information needed for decision-making. At the same time, useful experience gained from tasks is often lost across episodes. We argue that long-horizon performance is determined not by context length, but by how much decision-relevant information is maintained within a finite context budget. We present GenericAgent (GA), a general-purpose, self-evolving LLM agent system built around a single principle: context information density maximization. GA implements this through four closely connected components: a minimal atomic tool set that keeps the interface simple, a hierarchical on-demand memory that only shows a small high-level view by default, a self-evolution mechanism that turns verified past trajectories into reusable SOPs and executable code, and a context truncation and compression layer that maintains information density during long executions. Across task completion, tool use efficiency, memory effectiveness, self-evolution, and web browsing, GA consistently outperforms leading agent systems while using significantly fewer tokens and interactions, and it continues to evolve over time. Project: https://github.com/lsdefine/GenericAgent

AI.tldr: Introduces GenericAgent, a self-evolving LLM agent focused on maximizing information density in limited context for better long-horizon performance.
AI.motivation: LLM agents struggle on long-horizon tasks because context windows get filled with tool specs, memories, and environment logs, pushing out the information actually needed for current decisions. Additionally, valuable experience from past episodes is not effectively retained or reused, meaning agents repeatedly solve similar problems from scratch. The authors aim to overcome these limitations by shifting focus from sheer context length to how efficiently decision-relevant information is maintained within a fixed context budget.
AI.method: They design GenericAgent (GA), a general-purpose agent framework that maximizes context information density via four components: (1) a minimal atomic tool set to keep the action interface compact and simple; (2) a hierarchical, on-demand memory system that shows only a coarse high-level summary by default and reveals details only when required; (3) a self-evolution mechanism that converts verified successful trajectories into reusable standard operating procedures (SOPs) and executable code; and (4) a context truncation and compression layer that selectively prunes and compresses information to maintain high information density during extended interactions.
AI.result: In evaluations across multiple aspects—task completion rates, efficiency of tool use, quality and utility of memory, the effectiveness of self-evolution, and web browsing performance—GenericAgent achieves better performance than leading existing LLM agent systems. It does so while consuming significantly fewer tokens and requiring fewer interaction steps, and it continues to improve as it accumulates and evolves from experience.
AI.conclusion: Long-horizon agent performance is better improved by maximizing the density of decision-relevant information in a constrained context than by simply expanding context length. GenericAgent operationalizes this idea with a compact tool interface, hierarchical memory, self-evolving procedural knowledge, and context compression, thereby yielding more efficient, scalable, and continually improving LLM agents for complex long-running tasks.

title: Formal Foundations of Agentic Business Process Management
summary: Just like traditional BPM systems, agentic BPM systems are built around a specification of the process under consideration. Their distinguishing feature, however, is that the execution of the process is driven by multiple autonomous decision-makers, referred to as agents. Since such agents cannot be fully controlled, the process specification is augmented with explicit objectives, or goals, assigned to the participating agents. Agents then pursue these goals, at least to the best of their efforts, under suitable assumptions on the behavior of others, by adopting appropriate strategies. Centrally, the organization enacting the process can use these specifications to provide guardrails on the decision-making capabilities of agents at the strategy level. This paper sets up the mathematical foundations of such systems in three key settings and analyzes four foundational problems of agentic BPM.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a formal, mathematical foundation for Business Process Management (BPM) systems where multiple autonomous agents drive process execution under goal-based specifications and strategic guardrails.
AI.motivation: Traditional BPM assumes centrally controlled execution, which does not fit settings with autonomous, semi-controllable actors (e.g., humans, software agents, or organizations) whose decisions affect process outcomes. There is a need for a rigorous framework that can model, analyze, and constrain such agent-driven processes while respecting their autonomy.
AI.method: The authors formalize "agentic BPM" by augmenting standard process specifications with explicit goals for each agent and modeling agent behavior in terms of strategies under assumptions about others. They define three key mathematical settings for such systems and frame four foundational decision/analysis problems over these formal models, likely using techniques from game theory, logic, and formal methods.
AI.result: The paper provides formal definitions of agentic BPM systems in three core settings and characterizes four central problems (such as goal satisfaction, strategy existence, compliance with organizational guardrails, and interaction effects among agents). It establishes precise mathematical properties and conditions under which these problems can be analyzed or solved.
AI.conclusion: Agentic BPM can be rigorously captured by augmenting process models with agent goals and strategy-level constraints. This foundational framework enables organizations to impose guardrails on autonomous decision-makers while still leveraging their autonomy, and it opens the way to systematic analysis of goal satisfaction and strategic behavior in BPM contexts.

title: SOCIA-EVO: Automated Simulator Construction via Dual-Anchored Bi-Level Optimization
comment: This paper has been accepted to the ACL 2026 Main Conference
summary: Automated simulator construction requires distributional fidelity, distinguishing it from generic code generation. We identify two failure modes in long-horizon LLM agents: contextual drift and optimization instability arising from conflating structural and parametric errors. We propose SOCIA-EVO, a dual-anchored evolutionary framework. SOCIA-EVO introduces: (1) a static blueprint to enforce empirical constraints; (2) a bi-level optimization to decouple structural refinement from parameter calibration; and (3) a self-curating Strategy Playbook that manages remedial hypotheses via Bayesian-weighted retrieval. By falsifying ineffective strategies through execution feedback, SOCIA-EVO achieves robust convergence, generating simulators that are statistically consistent with observational data. The code and data of SOCIA-EVO are available here: https://github.com/cruiseresearchgroup/SOCIA/tree/evo.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SOCIA-EVO, an evolutionary framework that uses a static blueprint, bi-level optimization, and a Bayesian-weighted strategy playbook to build data-faithful simulators while avoiding common LLM agent failure modes.
AI.motivation: Existing automated simulator construction with LLMs struggles with distributional fidelity and long-horizon reliability, often failing due to contextual drift and mixing up structural versus parametric errors. There is a need for a principled framework that can reliably produce simulators whose behavior matches real-world observational data.
AI.method: The authors design SOCIA-EVO, a "dual-anchored" evolutionary optimization framework. It uses (1) a static blueprint as a hard anchor enforcing empirical constraints during generation; (2) a bi-level optimization process that separately optimizes structural aspects of the simulator (e.g., rules, mechanisms) and parametric aspects (e.g., numerical values); and (3) a self-curating Strategy Playbook that stores, retrieves, and updates candidate remedial strategies using Bayesian weighting based on execution feedback. Strategies that underperform are falsified and down-weighted or removed over time, steering the evolution toward effective configurations.
AI.result: Using execution feedback to iteratively refine both structure and parameters while enforcing a blueprint, SOCIA-EVO converges more robustly than naive LLM-based code generation approaches. The resulting simulators match target data distributions (statistical consistency with observational data), demonstrating improved distributional fidelity and stability across long horizons.
AI.conclusion: SOCIA-EVO effectively addresses contextual drift and optimization instability in long-horizon LLM agents for simulator construction. By anchoring generation to a static empirical blueprint, decoupling structural and parametric optimization, and managing strategies through Bayesian, feedback-driven evolution, the framework produces robust, data-faithful simulators. This approach offers a structured pathway toward reliable automated simulator construction with LLMs.

title: Beyond Word Boundaries: A Hebrew Coreference Benchmark and an Evaluation Protocol for Morphologically Complex Text
summary: Coreference Resolution (CR) is a fundamental NLP task critical for long-form tasks as information extraction, summarization, and many business applications. However, CR methods originally designed for English struggle with Morphologically Rich Languages (MRLs), where mention boundaries do not necessarily align with word boundaries, and a single token may consist of multiple anaphors. CR modeling and evaluation protocols standardly assume that, as in English, words and mentions mostly align. However, this assumption breaks down in MRLs, particularly in the context of LLMs' raw-text processing and end-to-end tasks. To assess and address this challenge, we introduce {\em KibutzR}, the first comprehensive CR dataset for Modern Hebrew, an MRL rich with complex words and pronominal clitics. We deliver an annotated dataset that identifies mentions at word, sub-word and multi-word levels, and propose an evaluation protocol that directly addresses word/morpheme boundary discrepancies. Our experiments show that contemporary LLMs perform significantly worse on Hebrew than on English, and that performance degrades on raw unsegmented text. Crucially, we show an inverse performance-trend in Hebrew relative to English, where smaller encoders perform far better than contemporary decoder models, leaving ample space for investigation and improvement. We deliver a new benchmark for Hebrew coreference resolution and a segmentation-aware evaluation protocol to inform future work on other MRLs.

AI.tldr: The paper presents KibutzR, the first comprehensive Hebrew coreference resolution dataset, with a segmentation-aware evaluation that exposes unique challenges of morphologically rich languages (MRLs) and shows current LLMs underperform, especially on raw text.
AI.motivation: Coreference resolution systems are mostly built and evaluated on English, implicitly assuming that mentions align with word boundaries. This assumption fails for morphologically rich languages like Modern Hebrew, where single tokens can contain multiple anaphors and complex clitics, especially problematic for LLMs that process raw text. There is a lack of suitable datasets and evaluation protocols to study and improve CR in such languages.
AI.method: The authors construct KibutzR, a coreference resolution dataset for Modern Hebrew that annotates mentions at multiple granularities: word, sub-word (morpheme), and multi-word expressions. They then design a segmentation-aware evaluation protocol that accounts for mismatches between words and morpheme-based mentions. Using this dataset, they benchmark a range of contemporary encoder and decoder LLM architectures on both segmented and raw unsegmented Hebrew text, and compare with English performance trends.
AI.result: The experiments reveal that current large decoder-based LLMs perform substantially worse on Hebrew coreference resolution than on English, with further degradation when applied to raw unsegmented Hebrew text. Contrary to English, smaller encoder models outperform contemporary decoder LLMs on Hebrew CR under the proposed evaluation. The evaluation protocol highlights how segmentation errors and word/morpheme boundary mismatches significantly impact performance in MRLs.
AI.conclusion: KibutzR establishes a new benchmark for Hebrew coreference resolution and demonstrates that English-centric modeling and evaluation assumptions break down in morphologically rich languages. The segmentation-aware protocol provides a more realistic and fair way to evaluate CR in such settings. The findings, especially the reversed performance hierarchy between encoders and decoders, suggest substantial open space for new architectures and methods tailored to MRLs, with implications beyond Hebrew to other morphologically rich languages.

title: Hive: A Multi-Agent Infrastructure for Algorithm- and Task-Level Scaling
summary:
Large language models are increasingly deployed as complex agentic systems that scale with task complexity. While prior work has extensively explored model- and system-level scaling, algorithm- and task-level scaling remain largely unaddressed, constraining the full potential of agentic systems. At the algorithm level, allocating additional inference-time computation can enhance workflow capacity but introduces cross-path redundancy: overlapping computations across multiple reasoning branches. At the task level, complex tasks can be decomposed into subproblems and delegated across multiple agents for improved scalability and parallelism. However, existing infrastructures' scheduling is unaware of the existence of multiple agents, missing opportunities to optimize resource allocation.
  We propose Hive, a multi-agent infrastructure that enables algorithm- and task-level scaling. Hive features a description frontend that captures per-agent behavior and supports test-time scaling algorithms. Leveraging this specification, our backend introduces two key mechanisms: Logits Cache that reuses intermediate logits across redundant sampling paths to mitigate cross-path redundancy at the algorithm level, and Agent-Aware Scheduling that efficiently allocates compute and KV-cache resources according to agent contributions at the task level. Experiments show that Logits Cache achieves an average speedup of $1.11\times$-$1.76\times$ for re-sampling, and Agent-Aware Scheduling reduces the hotspot miss rate by $33\%$-$51\%$.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Hive, a multi-agent infrastructure for large language models that scales both algorithms and tasks by reducing redundant computation and improving resource scheduling.
AI.motivation: Existing work focuses mainly on model- and system-level scaling of large language models, but not on how to better scale inference-time algorithms and complex multi-agent tasks. Current approaches suffer from redundant reasoning across multiple sampling paths and from schedulers that are unaware of multiple agents, leading to inefficient resource allocation and limited scalability for complex, decomposed tasks.
AI.method: The authors design Hive, which consists of a description frontend to specify per-agent behavior and enable test-time scaling algorithms, and a backend with two mechanisms: (1) Logits Cache, which caches and reuses intermediate logits across overlapping sampling paths to eliminate cross-path redundancy at the algorithm level; and (2) Agent-Aware Scheduling, which uses knowledge of different agents’ roles and contributions to more efficiently allocate compute and KV-cache resources at the task level.
AI.result: Experiments with Hive demonstrate that the Logits Cache provides an average speedup of 1.11×–1.76× on re-sampling workloads, and Agent-Aware Scheduling lowers KV-cache hotspot miss rates by 33%–51%, indicating both faster inference and more efficient resource use in multi-agent LLM systems.
AI.conclusion: Hive effectively enables algorithm- and task-level scaling for multi-agent LLM systems. By caching reusable logits and scheduling resources based on agent contributions, it reduces redundancy and improves throughput, making complex multi-agent workflows more efficient and scalable.

title: The Provenance Gap in Clinical AI: Evidence-Traceable Temporal Knowledge Graphs for Rare Disease Reasoning
comment: 32 pages, 9 figures, 7 tables. Will submit to npj Digital Medicine. Supplementary materials included
summary: Frontier large language models generate clinically accurate outputs, but their citations are often fabricated. We term this the Provenance Gap. We tested five frontier LLMs across 36 clinician-validated scenarios for three rare neuromuscular disease pairs. No model produced a clinically relevant PubMed identifier without prompting. When explicitly asked to cite, the best model achieved 15.3% relevant PMIDs; the majority resolved to real publications in unrelated fields. We present HEG-TKG (Hierarchical Evidence-Grounded Temporal Knowledge Graphs), a system that grounds clinical claims in temporal knowledge graphs built from 4,512 PubMed records and curated sources with quality-tier stratification and 1,280 disease-trajectory milestones. In a controlled three-arm comparison using the same synthesis model, HEG-TKG matches baseline clinical feature coverage while achieving 100% evidence verifiability with 203 inline citations. Guideline-RAG, given overlapping source documents as raw text, produces zero verifiable citations. LLM judges cannot distinguish fabricated from verified citations without PubMed audit data. Independent clinician evaluation confirms the verifiability advantage (Cohen's d = 1.81, p < 0.001) with no degradation on safety or completeness. A counterfactual experiment shows 80% resistance to injected clinical errors with 100% detectability via citation trace. The system deploys on-premise via open-source models so patient data never leaves institutional infrastructure.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies that current frontier LLMs often fabricate clinical citations (the “Provenance Gap”), and proposes HEG-TKG, a temporal, evidence-grounded knowledge graph system that allows LLMs to generate clinically accurate, fully verifiable, and resilient outputs with high-quality inline citations, without sacrificing safety or completeness.
AI.motivation: Although state-of-the-art LLMs can generate clinically accurate content, their supporting citations are frequently fabricated, irrelevant, or unverifiable, posing significant risks in medical settings where provenance of evidence is critical. Existing retrieval-augmented approaches still fail to ensure trustworthy citations or resistance to erroneous source information. There is a need for a method that ties clinical claims to verifiable, time-aware, quality-stratified evidence while maintaining clinical coverage and safety.
AI.method: The authors evaluate five frontier LLMs on 36 clinician-validated scenarios spanning three rare neuromuscular disease pairs, measuring their ability to provide relevant PubMed identifiers both without and with explicit citation prompting. They then build HEG-TKG, which constructs hierarchical, temporal knowledge graphs from 4,512 PubMed records and curated clinical sources, stratified by evidence quality and encoded as 1,280 disease-trajectory milestones. Using the same synthesis model in a controlled three-arm comparison (baseline, HEG-TKG, and Guideline-RAG with overlapping documents as raw text), they assess citation verifiability, clinical feature coverage, and safety, including evaluation by LLM judges and independent clinicians, plus a counterfactual experiment injecting clinical errors to test robustness and detectability via citation traces. The system is implemented on-premise with open-source models.
AI.result: Unprompted, no tested LLM produced clinically relevant PubMed IDs. When prompted for citations, the best-performing model produced only 15.3% relevant PMIDs, with most citations pointing to real but clinically irrelevant papers. In the three-arm comparison, HEG-TKG matched the baseline in clinical feature coverage while yielding 100% verifiable evidence and 203 inline citations; in contrast, Guideline-RAG produced zero verifiable citations from similar sources. LLM judges were unable to reliably distinguish fabricated from verified citations without access to PubMed audit data. Independent clinician assessments showed a large verifiability advantage for HEG-TKG (Cohen’s d = 1.81, p < 0.001), with no observed loss in safety or completeness. The counterfactual experiment showed that HEG-TKG outputs resisted 80% of injected clinical errors and allowed 100% detection via citation tracing.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates a substantial Provenance Gap in current frontier LLMs applied to clinical tasks: they can be clinically accurate yet provide untrustworthy citations. HEG-TKG closes this gap by grounding clinical reasoning in temporal, quality-stratified knowledge graphs, achieving fully verifiable citations without compromising coverage or safety, and offering robustness to erroneous information. Guideline-style RAG over raw text is insufficient for citation reliability. Deploying HEG-TKG with open-source models on-premise enables trustworthy, auditable, and privacy-preserving clinical decision support, where every claim can be traced back to specific, time-aware, and quality-tiered evidence.

title: T-DuMpRa: Teacher-guided Dual-path Multi-prototype Retrieval Augmented framework for fine-grained medical image classification
summary: Fine-grained medical image classification is challenged by subtle inter-class variations and visually ambiguous cases, where confidence estimates often exhibit uncertainty rather than being overconfident. In such scenarios, purely discriminative classifiers may achieve high overall accuracy yet still fail to distinguish between highly similar categories, leading to miscalibrated predictions. We propose T-DuMpRa, a teacher-guided dual-path multi-prototype retrieval-augmented framework, where discriminative classification and multi-prototype retrieval jointly drive both training and prediction. During training, we jointly optimize cross-entropy and supervised contrastive objectives to learn a cosine-compatible embedding geometry for reliable prototype matching. We further employ an exponential moving average (EMA) teacher to obtain smoother representations and build a multi-prototype memory bank by clustering teacher embeddings in the teacher embedding space. Our framework is plug-and-play: it can be easily integrated into existing classification models by constructing a compact prototype bank, thereby improving performance on visually ambiguous cases. At inference, we combine the classifier's predicted distribution with a similarity-based distribution computed via cosine matching to prototypes, and apply a conservative confidence-gated fusion that activates retrieval only when the classifier's prediction is uncertain and the retrieval evidence is decisive and conflicting, otherwise keeping confident predictions unchanged. On HAM10000 and ISIC2019, our method yields 0.68%-0.21% and 0.44%-2.69% improvements on 5 different backbones. And visualization analysis proves our model can enhance the model's ability to handle visually ambiguous cases.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes T-DuMpRa, a teacher-guided dual-path multi-prototype retrieval-augmented framework to improve fine-grained medical image classification, particularly for visually ambiguous cases, by combining discriminative classification with prototype-based retrieval and confidence-gated fusion.
AI.motivation: Fine-grained medical image classification suffers from subtle inter-class differences and ambiguous images, where standard discriminative classifiers can be accurate overall but miscalibrated on hard cases, leading to confusion between highly similar categories. The authors aim to address this by augmenting classifiers with a retrieval mechanism that can provide more reliable decisions in uncertain, visually confusing scenarios without sacrificing performance on confident cases.
AI.method: The authors introduce T-DuMpRa, which jointly trains a classifier with cross-entropy loss and supervised contrastive loss to learn a cosine-compatible embedding space. An EMA teacher network produces smoother embeddings, which are clustered to build a multi-prototype memory bank in the teacher space. The framework is plug-and-play, attaching this compact prototype bank to existing backbones. At inference, the system computes the classifier’s prediction and a similarity-based distribution via cosine matching to prototypes, then uses a conservative confidence-gated fusion that only leverages retrieval when the classifier is uncertain and retrieval evidence is strong and conflicting; otherwise it leaves confident classifier predictions unchanged.
AI.result: On two skin-lesion datasets, HAM10000 and ISIC2019, T-DuMpRa improves classification performance across five different backbones, with gains of 0.68%–0.21% and 0.44%–2.69%, respectively. Visualization analyses indicate that the method particularly improves handling of visually ambiguous cases, suggesting better calibration and discrimination between similar lesion categories.
AI.conclusion: T-DuMpRa effectively enhances fine-grained medical image classification by integrating prototype-based retrieval into standard discriminative models using a teacher-guided, dual-path, multi-prototype framework. The approach is easily pluggable into existing architectures, yields modest but consistent performance gains across datasets and backbones, and improves robustness on visually ambiguous cases via a confidence-gated fusion of classifier and retrieval-based predictions.

title: Please refuse to answer me! Mitigating Over-Refusal in Large Language Models via Adaptive Contrastive Decoding
summary: Safety-aligned large language models (LLMs) often generate refusal responses to harmless queries due to the over-refusal problem. However, existing methods for mitigating over-refusal cannot maintain a low refusal ratio for harmless queries while keeping a high refusal ratio for malicious ones. In this paper, we analyze how system prompts with varying safety levels affect LLM refusal behaviors when facing over-refusal queries. A key observation is that, when LLMs suffer from the over-refusal issue, non-refusal tokens remain present in the next-token candidate list, but the model systematically fails to select them, despite the generation of refusal tokens. Based on this observation, we propose a training-free and model-agnostic approach, Adaptive Contrastive Decoding (AdaCD), to mitigate over-refusal while maintaining LLM safety. First, AdaCD compares the output distributions of the LLM with or without an extreme safety system prompt to refine the refusal token distribution. Second, we introduce an adaptive contrastive decoding strategy that dynamically incorporates or removes the refusal token distribution, adaptively boosting the probability of selecting refusal or non-refusal tokens. Experimental results on five benchmark datasets show that, on average, AdaCD reduces the refusal ratio for over-refusal queries by 10.35%, yet still increases the refusal ratio for malicious queries by 0.13%. Code is available at https://github.com/OutdoorManofML/AdaCD.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Adaptive Contrastive Decoding (AdaCD), a training-free, model-agnostic decoding method to fix over-refusal in safety-aligned LLMs while preserving strong safety on malicious queries.
AI.motivation: Safety-tuned LLMs often refuse to answer even harmless queries (over-refusal). Existing mitigation methods either lower safety on malicious queries or fail to sufficiently reduce refusals on benign ones. The authors aim to understand and control how different safety system prompts affect refusal behavior, and to design a decoding-time solution that selectively avoids unnecessary refusals without retraining the model.
AI.method:
1) Empirically analyze the impact of system prompts with different safety strengths on LLM refusal behavior.
2) Observe that for over-refusal cases, non-refusal tokens remain in the next-token candidate list but are systematically disfavored compared to refusal tokens.
3) Propose AdaCD, a training-free, model-agnostic decoding method:
   - Use two forward passes: one with an extreme safety system prompt and one without, to obtain two output distributions.
   - Refine the refusal token distribution by comparing these distributions and identifying tokens associated with refusal behavior.
   - Apply an adaptive contrastive decoding rule that dynamically up-weights or down-weights refusal-related tokens, effectively boosting refusal tokens for malicious queries and boosting non-refusal tokens for benign queries.
4) Integrate AdaCD into standard decoding pipelines and evaluate across multiple benchmarks.
AI.result: Across five benchmark datasets, AdaCD reduces the refusal ratio on over-refusal (harmless) queries by 10.35% on average, while still slightly improving safety by increasing the refusal ratio on malicious queries by 0.13%. This demonstrates improved alignment: fewer unnecessary refusals without sacrificing, and even marginally improving, safety against harmful queries.
AI.conclusion: Over-refusal in safety-aligned LLMs is not due to the absence of viable non-refusal continuations, but to a systematic bias in token selection. By contrasting the model’s behavior under extreme safety prompting and normal prompting, AdaCD can identify and control refusal-related tokens at decoding time. This training-free, model-agnostic approach effectively reduces over-refusal while maintaining or slightly enhancing safety on malicious inputs, making it a practical plug-in solution for deployed LLMs.

title: LLM-Guided Strategy Synthesis for Scalable Equality Saturation
summary:
Equality saturation (EqSat) is a powerful optimization paradigm that compactly represents many equivalent programs in an e-graph and delays commitment until extraction selects a lowest-cost program. Making EqSat effective, therefore, requires not only domain-specific rewrite rules but also domain-specific strategies. Today, much of this strategy design is still manual, making it a major obstacle to automating e-graph-based compilers. Recent rule-synthesis frameworks can automatically infer large rewrite vocabularies from semantic specifications, but they also enlarge the rewrite space and further exacerbate e-graph explosion. Although large language models (LLMs) make automated strategy synthesis plausible, directly evolving backend code remains ineffective in practice. The search lacks reusable strategy abstractions and actionable feedback, and can easily trigger e-graph explosion or converge to poor designs.
  We present EggMind, an LLM-guided, end-to-end framework for synthesizing reusable EqSat strategies. At its core, EggMind introduces a domain-specific language, EqSatL, to represent EqSat strategies as explicit and inspectable artifacts. It then proposes an LLM-guided agentic workflow, equipped with novel techniques including proof-derived rewrite motif caching and tractability guidance, to search efficiently for high-quality strategies while keeping synthesis stable under e-graph growth. Evaluation shows that EggMind substantially improves the resource-quality trade-off on vectorization benchmarks, reducing final cost by 45.1% and peak RAM by 69.1% relative to full EqSat. We further show that the same methodology transfers effectively to an XLA-based tensor compiler, and demonstrate its practical potential in a logic-synthesis case study with augmented rewrite spaces.

AI.tldr: EggMind is an LLM-guided framework that automatically synthesizes reusable equality-saturation (EqSat) optimization strategies, achieving better performance and lower resource usage than hand-tuned or brute-force EqSat.
AI.motivation: Equality saturation compilers can encode many equivalent programs but need good, domain-specific strategies to avoid e-graph explosion and obtain low-cost programs. These strategies are currently designed manually, which is a bottleneck, and recent automatic rule-synthesis makes the search space even larger and more unstable. Existing attempts to use LLMs to design strategies directly on backend code perform poorly due to lack of abstractions and feedback, often causing e-graph blowups or weak optimizations.
AI.method: EggMind introduces EqSatL, a domain-specific language for representing EqSat strategies as explicit, reusable programs, and wraps it in an LLM-guided, agentic search workflow. The workflow uses techniques such as proof-derived rewrite motif caching and tractability guidance to constrain exploration and keep e-graph growth manageable while iteratively proposing and refining strategies.
AI.result: On vectorization benchmarks, EggMind improves the trade-off between optimization quality and resources compared with full EqSat, cutting final program cost by 45.1% and peak memory usage by 69.1%. The same approach transfers successfully to an XLA-based tensor compiler and shows benefits in a logic-synthesis case study with larger rewrite spaces.
AI.conclusion: Encoding EqSat strategies in a DSL and searching over them with an LLM-guided, feedback-driven workflow enables automatic synthesis of high-quality, reusable EqSat strategies that scale better than manual or naïve EqSat, both improving optimization results and controlling resource consumption across multiple compiler domains.

title: RoIt-XMASA: Multi-Domain Multilingual Sentiment Analysis Dataset for Romanian and Italian
comment: Accepted at the International AAAI Conference on Web and Social Media (ICWSM 2026)
summary: We present RoIt-XMASA, a multilingual dataset that extends the Cross-lingual Multi-domain Amazon Sentiment Analysis to Italian and Romanian, comprising 36,000 labeled reviews across three domains (books, movies, and music) and 202,141 unlabeled samples. To address cross-lingual and cross-domain challenges, we propose a multi-target adversarial training framework that employs loss reversal with meta-learned coefficients to dynamically balance sentiment discrimination with domain and language invariance. XLM-R achieves an F1-score of 66.23% with our approach, outperforming the baseline by 4.64%. Few-shot evaluation shows that Llama-3.1-8B achieves 58.43% F1-score, revealing a meaningful trade-off between the efficiency of prompting-based approaches and the higher performance of task-specific fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RoIt-XMASA, a new multilingual sentiment analysis dataset (Italian and Romanian) and a multi-target adversarial training method that improves cross-lingual, cross-domain performance over strong baselines.
AI.motivation: Existing cross-lingual sentiment resources and methods are limited in language coverage and struggle with both cross-lingual and cross-domain generalization. Italian and Romanian are underrepresented, and there is a need to better balance learning sentiment information while remaining invariant to domain and language shifts.
AI.method: They build RoIt-XMASA, extending the Cross-lingual Multi-domain Amazon Sentiment Analysis dataset with labeled and unlabeled Italian and Romanian reviews in three domains. Methodologically, they propose a multi-target adversarial training framework that simultaneously learns sentiment prediction while enforcing invariance to both language and domain. This is done via adversarial loss reversal where meta-learned coefficients dynamically weigh the trade-off between sentiment discrimination and invariance objectives, applied on top of XLM-R and also evaluated in a few-shot setting with Llama-3.1-8B using prompting.
AI.result: With the proposed adversarial framework, XLM-R reaches an F1-score of 66.23%, improving the baseline by 4.64 percentage points on the new dataset. Few-shot prompting with Llama-3.1-8B yields a 58.43% F1-score, which is lower than fine-tuned XLM-R but attained with much less task-specific training, highlighting efficiency–performance trade-offs.
AI.conclusion: RoIt-XMASA provides a valuable resource for multilingual, cross-domain sentiment analysis in Italian and Romanian. The multi-target adversarial training strategy effectively improves cross-lingual and cross-domain robustness for models like XLM-R. Large language models used in a few-shot prompting regime can approach these results with less training, illustrating complementary strengths of fine-tuning and prompting-based approaches.

title: Beyond Meta-Reasoning: Metacognitive Consolidation for Self-Improving LLM Reasoning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong reasoning capabilities, and as existing approaches for enhancing LLM reasoning continue to mature, increasing attention has shifted toward meta-reasoning as a promising direction for further improvement. However, most existing meta-reasoning methods remain episodic: they focus on executing complex meta-reasoning routines within individual instances, but ignore the accumulation of reusable meta-reasoning skills across instances, leading to recurring failure modes and repeatedly high metacognitive effort. In this paper, we introduce Metacognitive Consolidation, a novel framework in which a model consolidates metacognitive experience from past reasoning episodes into reusable knowledge that improves future meta-reasoning. We instantiate this framework by structuring instance-level problem solving into distinct roles for reasoning, monitoring, and control to generate rich, attributable meta-level traces. These traces are then consolidated through a hierarchical, multi-timescale update mechanism that gradually forms evolving meta-knowledge. Experimental results demonstrate consistent performance gains across benchmarks and backbone models, and show that performance improves as metacognitive experience accumulates over time.

AI.tldr: They propose a framework that lets an LLM learn from its own past meta-reasoning processes so that its future reasoning oversight improves over time.
AI.motivation: Existing meta-reasoning methods for LLMs are mostly episodic: they plan, monitor, and adjust reasoning within a single problem but don’t accumulate reusable meta-level skills across problems, causing repeated failures and high metacognitive cost each time.
AI.method: Define a Metacognitive Consolidation framework where, during problem solving, the model is separated into roles for reasoning, monitoring, and control, producing detailed meta-level traces. These traces are then processed by a hierarchical, multi-timescale update mechanism that consolidates them into evolving meta-knowledge that can be reused in later tasks.
AI.result: Across multiple reasoning benchmarks and different backbone LLMs, using metacognitive consolidation consistently improves performance, and the gains grow as more metacognitive experience (traces) is accumulated over time.
AI.conclusion: Turning episodic meta-reasoning into a learnable, persistent process via metacognitive consolidation allows LLMs to build and reuse meta-knowledge, leading to systematic and compounding improvements in reasoning performance.

title: The Consensus Trap: Rescuing Multi-Agent LLMs from Adversarial Majorities via Token-Level Collaboration
summary: Multi-agent large language model (LLM) architectures increasingly rely on response-level aggregation, such as Majority Voting (MAJ), to raise reasoning ceilings. However, in open environments, agents are highly susceptible to stealthy contextual corruption, such as targeted prompt injections. We reveal a critical structural vulnerability in current multi-agent systems: response-level aggregation collapses when corrupted agents form a local majority. Because voting aggregates fully-formed conclusions, it is blind to flawed intermediate logic. To overcome this systematic limitation, we propose the Token-Level Round-Robin (RR) Collaboration, where agents sequentially interleave generation within a shared auto-regressive context. We formalize this process as a discrete-time dynamical system, proving that token-level interleaving transitions aggregation from a brittle counting of final votes (a linear sum) to a dynamic, interwoven chain of logic (a non-linear operator product). Through this theoretical lens, we prove that the honest model's restorative pull can overpower adversarial corruptions, even when corrupted agents form a majority. We conduct an exhaustive empirical evaluation across diverse reasoning benchmarks and demonstrate that while MAJ collapses when corrupted agents reach a majority, RR maintains robust accuracy well beyond this critical threshold.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a vulnerability in multi-agent LLM systems that rely on majority voting and proposes a token-level, round-robin collaboration mechanism that is provably and empirically more robust to corrupted agents, even when they are in the majority.
AI.motivation: Multi-agent LLM systems commonly aggregate agents’ outputs using response-level majority voting to improve reasoning. However, in open settings agents can be corrupted by prompt injections or contextual attacks. When enough agents are corrupted, majority voting fails catastrophically, because it only sees final answers and cannot inspect or correct flawed intermediate reasoning. There is a need for an aggregation scheme that remains robust even when corrupted agents are numerous or form a local majority.
AI.method: The authors introduce Token-Level Round-Robin (RR) Collaboration. Instead of each agent independently generating a full response that is then aggregated, agents take turns generating tokens in a shared autoregressive context: they interleave their contributions token by token. The process is formalized as a discrete-time dynamical system. Mathematically, this replaces a simple linear sum over final votes with a non-linear operator product over token-level updates. Theoretical analysis shows how the honest model’s influence can dominate and correct adversarial perturbations within this dynamic, shared-context process, even when corrupted agents outnumber honest ones.
AI.result: Theoretical results show that, under the RR scheme, the dynamic interaction of agents’ token-level contributions allows the honest model’s corrective influence to outweigh the effects of adversarially corrupted agents, in contrast to majority voting which fails once corruption reaches a majority. Empirically, across multiple reasoning benchmarks and varying corruption levels, majority voting’s accuracy drops sharply when corrupted agents become the majority, while RR collaboration preserves high accuracy well beyond this majority-corruption point.
AI.conclusion: Response-level majority voting has a structural fragility in adversarial or corrupted multi-agent LLM settings because it aggregates only final answers. Token-Level Round-Robin Collaboration, which interleaves agents’ token-level reasoning in a shared context and is formalized as a non-linear dynamical system, offers substantial robustness: it allows honest agents to correct or overpower corrupted logic even when they are in the minority, as confirmed by both theory and experiments.

title: SciImpact: A Multi-Dimensional, Multi-Field Benchmark for Scientific Impact Prediction
summary: The rapid growth of scientific literature calls for automated methods to assess and predict research impact. Prior work has largely focused on citation-based metrics, leaving limited evaluation of models' capability to reason about other impact dimensions. To this end, we introduce SciImpact, a large-scale, multi-dimensional benchmark for scientific impact prediction spanning 19 fields. SciImpact captures various forms of scientific influence, ranging from citation counts to award recognition, media attention, patent reference, and artifact adoption, by integrating heterogeneous data sources and targeted web crawling. It comprises 215,928 contrastive paper pairs reflecting meaningful impact differences in both short-term (e.g., Best Paper Award) and long-term settings (e.g., Nobel Prize). We evaluate 11 widely used large language models (LLMs) on SciImpact. Results show that off-the-shelf models exhibit substantial variability across dimensions and fields, while multi-task supervised fine-tuning consistently enables smaller LLMs (e.g., 4B) to markedly outperform much larger models (e.g., 30B) and surpass powerful closed-source LLMs (e.g., o4-mini). These results establish SciImpact as a challenging benchmark and demonstrate its value for multi-dimensional, multi-field scientific impact prediction. Our project homepage is https://flypig23.github.io/sciimpact-homepage/

AI.tldr: SciImpact introduces a large-scale benchmark to evaluate how well models can predict diverse dimensions of scientific impact, beyond just citations, and shows that properly fine-tuned small LLMs can beat much larger or even closed-source models on this task.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to measuring and predicting scientific impact mostly use citation-based metrics, which miss many other important dimensions of influence (awards, media attention, patents, artifact use). Moreover, there is little systematic evaluation of whether LLMs can reason about these broader impact signals across different fields. A standardized, multi-dimensional benchmark is needed to study and improve models’ capability to understand and forecast scientific impact.
AI.method: The authors construct SciImpact, a multi-dimensional benchmark dataset covering 19 scientific fields. They integrate heterogeneous sources—such as citation databases, award records, media coverage, patent references, and indicators of artifact adoption—augmented with targeted web crawling.
