By integrating GRPO with semantic grouping and a composite reward, CoSearch overcomes the retrieval bottleneck that limits existing approaches, achieving stronger QA performance and highlighting coordinated agent–retriever learning as a crucial direction for future search agents.

title: REZE: Representation Regularization for Domain-adaptive Text Embedding Pre-finetuning
summary: Recent text embedding models are often adapted to specialized domains via contrastive pre-finetuning (PFT) on a naive collection of scattered, heterogeneous tasks. However, this approach often introduces task-induced bias alongside domain knowledge, leading to uncontrolled representation shifts that distort the pretrained embedding geometry and cause substantial performance degradation. To address this issue, we propose REZE}, a representation regularization framework that explicitly controls representation shift during embedding pre-finetuning. REZE operates on the relations of anchor-positive pairs and decomposes them in an eigenspace. It then measures task-wise dispersion along each eigencomponent to identify task-variant directions and applies adaptive soft-shrinkage to suppress task-induced noise while preserving task-invariant semantic structure, without inference-time overhead. Experiments across multiple embedding backbones and specialized benchmarks show that REZE outperforms standard pre-finetuning and isotropy-oriented post-hoc regularization in most settings, remaining stable where existing PFT variants collapse. Embedding space analyses further confirm that REZE induces controlled shifts aligned with the original embedding manifold, underscoring representation shift control as a key principle for robust embedding pre-finetuning under heterogeneous supervision.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces REZE, a framework to regularize and control representation shift during contrastive pre-finetuning of text embedding models, reducing task-induced noise while preserving semantic structure and improving robustness across domains.
AI.motivation: Existing contrastive pre-finetuning methods adapt general-purpose text embeddings to specialized domains by training on many heterogeneous tasks. This often unintentionally mixes domain knowledge with task-specific biases, causing uncontrolled shifts in the embedding space that distort the pretrained geometry and hurt downstream performance, especially under heterogeneous supervision. The authors aim to separate beneficial domain adaptation from harmful task-induced bias and provide a principled way to control representation shifts during pre-finetuning.
AI.method: The authors propose REZE, a representation regularization framework that works at training time. REZE analyzes anchor-positive relations in contrastive learning by decomposing them in the eigenspace of the embedding manifold. It computes task-wise dispersion along each eigencomponent to detect which directions are highly variant across tasks (task-variant directions) versus more stable (task-invariant). It then applies adaptive soft-shrinkage only along the task-variant directions to suppress task-induced noise while preserving the original semantic structure along task-invariant directions. This is integrated into pre-finetuning without adding inference-time overhead.
AI.result: Across multiple backbone embedding models and several specialized benchmarks, REZE consistently outperforms standard contrastive pre-finetuning and post-hoc isotropy-based regularization methods in most evaluated settings. It is notably more stable in challenging heterogeneous supervision setups where other pre-finetuning variants sometimes collapse or degrade substantially. Quantitative results show better task performance, and qualitative/analytic results indicate more controlled and aligned representation shifts.
AI.conclusion: REZE demonstrates that explicitly controlling representation shift during embedding pre-finetuning leads to more robust domain adaptation of text embeddings. By selectively shrinking task-variant directions and preserving task-invariant structure, it mitigates task-induced bias without extra inference cost. Embedding space analyses support that REZE maintains alignment with the original embedding manifold, suggesting that principled shift control should be a core design principle for future pre-finetuning strategies under heterogeneous supervision.

title: SafeAgent: A Runtime Protection Architecture for Agentic Systems
summary: Large language model (LLM) agents are vulnerable to prompt-injection attacks that propagate through multi-step workflows, tool interactions, and persistent context, making input-output filtering alone insufficient for reliable protection. This paper presents SafeAgent, a runtime security architecture that treats agent safety as a stateful decision problem over evolving interaction trajectories. The proposed design separates execution governance from semantic risk reasoning through two coordinated components: a runtime controller that mediates actions around the agent loop and a context-aware decision core that operates over persistent session state. The core is formalized as a context-aware advanced machine intelligence and instantiated through operators for risk encoding, utility-cost evaluation, consequence modeling, policy arbitration, and state synchronization. Experiments on Agent Security Bench (ASB) and InjecAgent show that SafeAgent consistently improves robustness over baseline and text-level guardrail methods while maintaining competitive benign-task performance. Ablation studies further show that recovery confidence and policy weighting determine distinct safety-utility operating points.

AI.tldr: SafeAgent is a runtime security architecture that treats LLM-agent safety as a stateful control problem over interaction trajectories, outperforming standard guardrails on agent-security benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM safety methods largely focus on single-turn input-output filtering and cannot reliably protect multi-step LLM agents that interact with tools, external systems, and long-lived context, leaving them vulnerable to prompt-injection attacks that propagate across steps and state. There is a need for a principled, runtime-aware framework that governs agent behavior over time rather than just screening individual messages.
AI.method: The paper proposes SafeAgent, a runtime security architecture for LLM agents. It splits safety into (1) a runtime controller that sits around the agent loop and mediates actions and (2) a context-aware decision core that reasons over persistent session state. The decision core is formalized as a context-aware advanced machine intelligence and instantiated via operators for (a) encoding risks from trajectories, (b) evaluating utilities and costs, (c) modeling consequences of potential actions, (d) arbitrating among policies, and (e) synchronizing safety-relevant state across steps. SafeAgent is evaluated on Agent Security Bench (ASB) and InjecAgent, with ablation studies on internal components such as recovery confidence and policy weighting.
AI.result: On ASB and InjecAgent benchmarks, SafeAgent yields higher robustness to prompt-injection and related agent attacks than baseline systems and standard text-level guardrail approaches, while retaining competitive performance on benign tasks. Ablation experiments reveal how varying recovery confidence and policy weighting allows SafeAgent to trade off between stronger safety and higher utility, leading to different operating points on the safety-utility curve.
AI.conclusion: Treating LLM-agent safety as a stateful decision problem over interaction trajectories enables more reliable defense against prompt-injection attacks than stateless input-output filtering. By decoupling execution governance from semantic risk reasoning and introducing a context-aware decision core with explicit operators for risk, utility, consequences, and policy arbitration, SafeAgent provides a flexible runtime architecture that can improve robustness without severely degrading benign-task performance. Its tunable components support different safety-utility trade-offs, suggesting a generalizable path forward for secure LLM-agent design.

title: Rethinking Meeting Effectiveness: A Benchmark and Framework for Temporal Fine-grained Automatic Meeting Effectiveness Evaluation
summary: Evaluating meeting effectiveness is crucial for improving organizational productivity. Current approaches rely on post-hoc surveys that yield a single coarse-grained score for an entire meeting. The reliance on manual assessment is inherently limited in scalability, cost, and reproducibility. Moreover, a single score fails to capture the dynamic nature of collaborative discussions. We propose a new paradigm for evaluating meeting effectiveness centered on novel criteria and temporal fine-grained approach. We define effectiveness as the rate of objective achievement over time and assess it for individual topical segments within a meeting. To support this task, we introduce the AMI Meeting Effectiveness (AMI-ME) dataset, a new meta-evaluation dataset containing 2,459 human-annotated segments from 130 AMI Corpus meetings. We also develop an automatic effectiveness evaluation framework that uses a Large Language Model (LLM) as a judge to score each segment's effectiveness relative to the overall meeting objectives. Through substantial experiments, we establish a comprehensive benchmark for this new task and evaluate the framework's generalizability across distinct meeting types, ranging from business scenarios to unstructured discussions. Furthermore, we benchmark end-to-end performance starting from raw speech to measure the capabilities of a complete system. Our results validate the framework's effectiveness and provide strong baselines to facilitate future research in meeting analysis and multi-party dialogue. Our dataset and code will be publicly available. The AMI-ME dataset and the Automatic Evaluation Framework are available at: this URL.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new, fine-grained way to evaluate meeting effectiveness over time using a new annotated dataset (AMI-ME) and an LLM-based automatic judging framework.
AI.motivation: Existing meeting effectiveness evaluations rely on manual, post-hoc surveys that provide only a single coarse score per meeting. This is not scalable, is costly, lacks reproducibility, and fails to capture how effectiveness changes throughout the meeting. There is a need for an automatic, temporally fine-grained, and objective-oriented evaluation method.
AI.method: The authors redefine meeting effectiveness as the rate of objective achievement over time and operationalize it at the level of topical segments within meetings. They construct the AMI-ME dataset by annotating 2,459 segments from 130 AMI Corpus meetings with human effectiveness labels. They then develop an automatic evaluation framework where a Large Language Model serves as a judge, scoring each segment’s effectiveness relative to global meeting objectives. They run extensive experiments, including from raw speech input, to benchmark and analyze performance and generalizability across different meeting types.
AI.result: They create the AMI-ME dataset and show that their LLM-based evaluation framework can effectively assess segment-level meeting effectiveness aligned with human judgments. Experiments demonstrate that the framework generalizes across various meeting genres (structured business meetings and unstructured discussions) and functions even in an end-to-end setting starting from raw audio. They provide quantitative benchmarks that can serve as baselines for future work.
AI.conclusion: Fine-grained, objective-oriented evaluation of meeting effectiveness is feasible and useful. The newly released AMI-ME dataset and LLM-based automatic evaluation framework offer a strong baseline and standardized benchmark for future research on meeting analysis and multi-party dialogue. Making data and code public aims to catalyze further work on understanding and improving meeting effectiveness.

title: Beyond Static Snapshots: A Grounded Evaluation Framework for Language Models at the Agentic Frontier
summary: We argue that current evaluation frameworks for large language models (LLMs) suffer from four systematic failures that make them structurally inadequate for assessing deployed, agentic systems: distributional invalidity (evaluation inputs do not reflect real interaction distributions), temporal invalidity (evaluations are post-hoc rather than training-integrated), scope invalidity (evaluations measure single-turn outputs rather than long-horizon trajectories), and process invalidity (evaluations assess outputs rather than reasoning). These failures compound critically in RLHF, where reward models are evaluated under conditions that do not hold during RL training, making reward hacking a predictable consequence of evaluation design rather than a training pathology. We propose the Grounded Continuous Evaluation (GCE) framework and present ISOPro, a simulation-based fine-tuning and evaluation system. ISOPro replaces the learned reward model with a deterministic ground-truth verifier, eliminating reward hacking by construction in verifiable-reward domains, and operates on LoRA adapter weights updatable on CPU, reducing the hardware barrier by an order of magnitude. We validate ISOPro on a resource-constrained scheduling domain with six difficulty tiers, demonstrating capability emergence visible only through continuous evaluation, an implicit curriculum that forms without researcher curation, and a 3x accuracy improvement over zero-shot baselines, all on consumer hardware with 0.216% trainable parameters.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies four systemic flaws in current LLM evaluation and proposes a new simulation-based framework (GCE/ISOPro) that uses ground-truth verifiers and continuous, training-integrated evaluation to avoid reward hacking and measure long-horizon, agentic behavior efficiently on modest hardware.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM evaluation practices are misaligned with how deployed, agentic LLM systems actually operate. Evaluations use static, non-representative benchmarks, occur only after training, focus on single-turn outputs, and assess final answers instead of reasoning processes. These issues become critical in RLHF, where reward models are evaluated under unrealistic conditions, leading to reward hacking and misleading assessments of model capabilities. The authors are motivated to design an evaluation and training framework that better mirrors real interaction distributions, captures long-horizon behavior, and structurally prevents reward hacking in verifiable domains, while remaining accessible on consumer hardware.
AI.method: The authors introduce the Grounded Continuous Evaluation (GCE) framework and implement it as ISOPro, a simulation-based fine-tuning and evaluation system. Instead of relying on a learned reward model, ISOPro uses a deterministic ground-truth verifier in domains where rewards are verifiable. This verifier provides exact feedback for training, removing the possibility of reward hacking by construction. ISOPro is designed to work via LoRA adapter weights that can be updated on CPU, drastically reducing hardware requirements. The system is evaluated in a controlled, resource-constrained scheduling simulation with multiple difficulty levels, where the model interacts repeatedly, enabling continuous, training-integrated evaluation of its behavior over long horizons.
AI.result: In the scheduling domain with six difficulty tiers, ISOPro exhibits several notable outcomes: (1) capability emergence that becomes apparent only through continuous, integrated evaluation rather than static benchmarks; (2) an implicit curriculum effect, where the model progressively tackles harder instances without manual curation by researchers; and (3) a threefold accuracy improvement over zero-shot baselines, all achieved on consumer-grade hardware by updating only 0.216% of the model parameters via LoRA adapters.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that standard LLM evaluation frameworks are structurally inadequate for assessing agentic, deployed systems due to distributional, temporal, scope, and process invalidities, and that these flaws help explain reward hacking in RLHF as an artifact of evaluation design. The proposed GCE framework and ISOPro implementation demonstrate that using deterministic ground-truth verifiers and continuous, training-integrated evaluation can eliminate reward hacking in verifiable domains, reveal emergent capabilities, and enable effective fine-tuning on modest hardware. This suggests a more robust path forward for evaluating and training agentic LLMs, especially in domains where ground-truth verification is feasible.

title: HopRank: Self-Supervised LLM Preference-Tuning on Graphs for Few-Shot Node Classification
summary: Node classification on text-attributed graphs (TAGs) is a fundamental task with broad applications in citation analysis, social networks, and recommendation systems. Current GNN-based approaches suffer from shallow text encoding and heavy dependence on labeled data, limiting their effectiveness in label-scarce settings. While large language models (LLMs) naturally address the text understanding gap with deep semantic reasoning, existing LLM-for-graph methods either still require abundant labels during training or fail to exploit the rich structural signals freely available in graph topology. Our key observation is that, in many real-world TAGs, edges predominantly connect similar nodes under the homophily principle, meaning graph topology inherently encodes class structure without any labels. Building on this insight, we reformulate node classification as a link prediction task and present HopRank, a fully self-supervised LLM-tuning framework for TAGs. HopRank constructs preference data via hierarchical hop-based sampling and employs adaptive preference learning to prioritize informative training signals without any class labels. At inference, nodes are classified by predicting their connection preferences to labeled anchors, with an adaptive early-exit voting scheme to improve efficiency. Experiments on three TAG benchmarks show that HopRank matches fully-supervised GNNs and substantially outperforms prior graph-LLM methods, despite using zero labeled training data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes HopRank, a self-supervised LLM-tuning framework that reformulates node classification on text-attributed graphs as a link prediction problem using graph homophily and hop-based sampling, achieving performance comparable to fully supervised GNNs without labeled training data.
AI.motivation: Existing GNN-based methods for node classification on text-attributed graphs rely on shallow text encoders and many labeled examples, making them ineffective in label-scarce scenarios. While LLMs can better understand text, current graph-LLM approaches either still need many labels or underuse structural information from graph connectivity. The paper is motivated by the need for a method that leverages LLMs' semantic power and the inherent class information encoded in graph topology, without relying on labeled data.
AI.method: The paper introduces HopRank, a fully self-supervised framework that tunes LLMs for text-attributed graphs by reframing node classification as link prediction under the homophily assumption. It constructs preference data via hierarchical hop-based sampling, where nodes’ relationships across different hop distances are sampled to generate pairwise preferences. An adaptive preference learning mechanism selects and emphasizes informative training signals, all without any class labels. During inference, the model classifies nodes by predicting their connection preferences to a set of labeled anchor nodes, using an adaptive early-exit voting scheme to reduce computation while maintaining accuracy.
AI.result: On three text-attributed graph benchmarks, HopRank achieves performance on par with fully supervised GNN models and significantly surpasses existing graph-LLM methods, all while using zero labeled data during training. This demonstrates that the method effectively exploits graph homophily and LLM semantic capabilities for label-efficient node classification.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that node classification on text-attributed graphs can be successfully reframed as a self-supervised link prediction task that leverages homophily in graph topology, enabling effective LLM tuning without labeled data. HopRank’s hop-based sampling, adaptive preference learning, and anchor-based inference collectively allow it to match or exceed supervised baselines, highlighting a promising direction for label-free graph learning with LLMs.

title: DIRCR: Dual-Inference Rule-Contrastive Reasoning for Solving RAVENs
summary: Abstract visual reasoning remains challenging as existing methods often prioritize either global context or local row-wise relations, failing to integrate both, and lack intermediate feature constraints, leading to incomplete rule capture and entangled representations. To address these issues, we propose the Dual-Inference Rule-Contrastive Reasoning (DIRCR) model. Its core component, the Dual-Inference Reasoning Module, combines a local path for row-wise analogical reasoning and a global path for holistic inference, integrated via a gated attention mechanism. Additionally, a Rule-Contrastive Learning Module introduces pseudo-labels to construct positive and negative rule samples, applying contrastive learning to enhance feature separability and promote abstract, transferable rule learning. Experimental results on three RAVEN datasets demonstrate that DIRCR significantly enhances reasoning robustness and generalization. Codes are available at https://github.com/csZack-Zhang/DIRCR.

AI.tldr: DIRCR is a visual reasoning model that jointly captures global and local patterns and uses contrastive learning on rule representations to improve robustness and generalization on RAVEN benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Abstract visual reasoning tasks, such as those in RAVEN, require models to infer underlying rules from matrix-like image patterns. Existing approaches tend to focus either on global holistic patterns or on local row-wise relations, but rarely both, leading to incomplete rule modeling. Furthermore, they often lack explicit constraints on intermediate features, causing entangled representations that do not cleanly separate different reasoning rules and thus generalize poorly. The paper aims to design a model that can jointly exploit local and global reasoning paths and enforce more structured, rule-aware feature spaces.
AI.method: The authors propose the Dual-Inference Rule-Contrastive Reasoning (DIRCR) model. Its central component, the Dual-Inference Reasoning Module, has two parallel reasoning paths: a local path that performs row-wise analogical reasoning over the visual matrices, and a global path that performs holistic inference across the entire pattern. These two paths are fused using a gated attention mechanism that adaptively balances their contributions. On top of this, a Rule-Contrastive Learning Module generates pseudo rule labels and constructs positive and negative pairs of rule instances. Contrastive learning is then applied to these rule-level features, encouraging samples sharing the same rule to be close in feature space and different rules to be separated, thereby promoting more abstract and transferable rule representations.
AI.result: DIRCR is evaluated on three variants of the RAVEN abstract visual reasoning datasets. Across these benchmarks, it achieves superior performance compared to prior methods, particularly in terms of robustness to distribution shifts and the ability to generalize learned rules to new settings. The experiments demonstrate that combining dual-path inference with rule-level contrastive learning leads to more reliable and accurate visual reasoning.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that integrating local and global reasoning within a unified module, together with rule-aware contrastive learning on intermediate representations, yields significant gains in abstract visual reasoning. DIRCR effectively disentangles rule representations, enhancing robustness and generalization on RAVEN benchmarks. The authors also release their implementation to support further research and extension of dual-path, rule-contrastive approaches in visual reasoning.

title: MedPRMBench: A Fine-grained Benchmark for Process Reward Models in Medical Reasoning
summary: Process-Level Reward Models (PRMs) are essential for guiding complex reasoning in large language models, yet existing PRM benchmarks cover only general domains such as mathematics, failing to address medical reasoning -- which is uniquely characterized by safety criticality, knowledge intensity, and diverse error patterns. Without a reliable medical PRM evaluation framework, we cannot quantify models' error detection capabilities in clinical reasoning, leaving their safety in real-world healthcare applications unverified. We propose MedPRMBench, the first process-level reward model benchmark for the medical domain. Built through a three-phase pipeline based on Clinical Reasoning Blueprints (CRBs), MedPRMBench systematically generates high-quality evaluation data from seven medical QA sources, covering 14 fine-grained error types across three categories (Simplicity, Soundness, and Sensitivity) with the first 4-level severity grading system to quantify clinical impact. The benchmark comprises 6{,}500 questions with 13{,}000 reasoning chains and 113{,}910 step-level labels, plus 6{,}879 questions for training. Our medical PRM baseline achieves an 87.1\% overall PRMScore -- substantially surpassing all baselines -- and serves as a plug-and-play verifier that improves downstream medical QA accuracy by 3.2--6.7 percentage points. Systematic evaluation spanning proprietary frontier models, open-source reasoning models, and medical-specialized models reveals critical weaknesses in current models' medical reasoning error detection capabilities, providing clear directions for future PRM improvement.

AI.tldr: Introduces MedPRMBench, the first process-level reward model benchmark tailored to medical reasoning, enabling systematic evaluation of models’ ability to detect clinical reasoning errors and their severity.
AI.motivation: Existing process-level reward model (PRM) benchmarks focus on general domains like math and ignore medical reasoning, which is safety-critical, knowledge-intensive, and has complex error patterns. Without a domain-specific medical PRM benchmark, we cannot reliably measure or improve models’ ability to detect errors in clinical reasoning, leaving their deployment in healthcare unsafe and unverified.
AI.method: Design a medical PRM benchmark (MedPRMBench) using a three-phase pipeline grounded in Clinical Reasoning Blueprints (CRBs). Collect and structure data from seven medical QA sources, generate multiple reasoning chains per question, and annotate them at the step level for 14 fine-grained error types grouped into Simplicity, Soundness, and Sensitivity. Introduce a 4-level severity grading system to quantify clinical impact. The final benchmark includes 6,500 questions, 13,000 reasoning chains, and 113,910 step-level labels, plus an additional 6,879 questions for training a medical PRM baseline model.
AI.result: Constructed a large-scale medical PRM benchmark and trained a medical PRM baseline that attains 87.1% overall PRMScore, outperforming all comparison methods. When used as a plug-and-play verifier, this PRM improves the accuracy of downstream medical QA tasks by 3.2–6.7 percentage points. The benchmark is used to evaluate proprietary frontier models, open-source reasoning models, and medical-specialized models, exposing notable deficits in their ability to detect medical reasoning errors.
AI.conclusion: MedPRMBench provides the first systematic, process-level evaluation framework for medical reasoning, enabling fine-grained assessment of error types and clinical impact severity. The strong baseline PRM both outperforms alternatives and improves end-task QA performance, while evaluations across diverse LLMs reveal substantial gaps in current models’ medical error detection capabilities. The benchmark offers a concrete foundation and clear directions for advancing safer, more reliable medical reasoning in LLMs.

title: Characterizing Model-Native Skills
comment: We argue that when the goal is to intervene on model behavior, skill characterization should be *model-native*: grounded in the model's own representations rather than imposed through external ontologies
summary: Skills are a natural unit for describing what a language model can do and how its behavior can be changed. However, existing characterizations rely on human-written taxonomies, textual descriptions, or manual profiling pipelines--all external hypotheses about what matters that need not align with the model's internal representations. We argue that when the goal is to intervene on model behavior, skill characterization should be *model-native*: grounded in the model's own representations rather than imposed through external ontologies. We instantiate this view by recovering a compact orthogonal basis from sequence-level activations. The resulting basis is semantically interpretable but need not correspond to any predefined human ontology; instead, it captures axes of behavioral variation that the model itself organizes around. We validate this characterization on reasoning post-training, using the recovered basis for both SFT data selection and inference-time steering. We develop lightweight proxy interventions to identify which directions are most useful for a given model. Across Llama3-8B and Qwen2.5-3B, selecting data along those directions improves Pass@1 by up to 20% on MATH and 41% on AMC, outperforming data selection based on human-characterized skills. Because the basis lives in activation space, the same directions also serve as steering vectors at inference time, improving Pass@8 by up to 4.8% on MATH--an intervention that human-characterized skills cannot support. We further validate the characterization on safety alignment, where selecting adversarial training data for model-native skill coverage rather than textual diversity yields more sample-efficient learning. These results suggest that recovering skills from the model's own representations, rather than imposing them externally, provides a more effective foundation for intervening on model behavior. Codes are open-sourced.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a model-native way to define and use “skills” in language models by extracting an orthogonal basis from internal activations, then using these directions for data selection and inference-time steering, improving reasoning and safety alignment performance over human-defined skill taxonomies.
AI.motivation: Existing ways to describe or intervene on language model behavior depend on human-designed taxonomies, textual labels, or manual profiling, which may not match how models internally represent capabilities. This mismatch can limit the effectiveness and generality of interventions (e.g., for reasoning enhancement or safety alignment). The authors want a principled, scalable, and model-grounded way to characterize skills that directly reflects the structure of the model’s own representations so that interventions (training data selection, steering) target what the model actually uses to organize its behavior.
AI.method: They derive a compact, approximately orthogonal basis from sequence-level activation patterns of the model. Each basis direction corresponds to a “model-native skill”: an axis of behavioral variation implied by the model’s internal representations, without assuming any human ontology or pre-labeled skills. They analyze and interpret these directions semantically, then use them in two ways: (1) selecting supervised fine-tuning (SFT) or adversarial training data aligned with particular directions, and (2) using the same directions as steering vectors in activation space at inference time. They also build lightweight proxy interventions to efficiently identify which directions are most beneficial for a given target task and model.
AI.result: On reasoning post-training with Llama3-8B and Qwen2.5-3B, selecting SFT data along model-native skill directions yields substantial gains: up to 20% Pass@1 improvement on MATH and 41% on AMC, exceeding gains from data selected using human-characterized skills. Because their skills live directly in activation space, the learned directions can also be used for inference-time steering, improving Pass@8 on MATH by up to 4.8%, which is not possible with purely text-defined skills. In safety alignment experiments, training on adversarial data chosen for model-native skill coverage produces more sample-efficient improvements than selecting data based on textual diversity. Code is released to support reproduction and extension.
AI.conclusion: Defining skills as directions in a model’s own activation space—rather than via external human ontologies—yields a more faithful and manipulable representation of model capabilities. These model-native skills are semantically interpretable, support both effective data selection and inference-time steering, and lead to measurable gains in reasoning benchmarks and safety alignment efficiency. The findings suggest that grounding skill characterization in internal representations is a promising and more powerful basis for understanding and intervening on language model behavior than traditional human-designed taxonomies, and the approach is practical enough to be deployed on real models with open-sourced implementations.

title: HorizonBench: Long-Horizon Personalization with Evolving Preferences
summary: User preferences evolve across months of interaction, and tracking them requires inferring when a stated preference has been changed by a subsequent life event. We define this problem as long-horizon personalization and observe that progress on it is limited by data availability and measurement, with no existing resource providing both naturalistic long-horizon interactions and the ground-truth provenance needed to diagnose why models fail. We introduce a data generator that produces conversations from a structured mental state graph, yielding ground-truth provenance for every preference change across 6-month timelines, and from it construct HorizonBench, a benchmark of 4,245 items from 360 simulated users with 6-month conversation histories averaging ~4,300 turns and ~163K tokens. HorizonBench provides a testbed for long-context modeling, memory-augmented architectures, theory-of-mind reasoning, and user modeling. Across 25 frontier models, the best model reaches 52.8% and most score at or below the 20% chance baseline. When these models err on evolved preferences, over a third of the time they select the user's originally stated value without tracking the updated user state. This belief-update failure persists across context lengths and expression explicitness levels, identifying state-tracking capability as the primary bottleneck for long-horizon personalization.

AI.tldr: They build HorizonBench, a synthetic but realistic benchmark to test whether models can track and update user preferences over many months of conversation, and show that current models largely fail at this long-horizon personalization task.
AI.motivation: User preferences change over time due to life events, but current personalization systems and LLMs struggle to recognize and adapt to these evolving preferences over long periods. There is no existing dataset that simultaneously provides long, naturalistic user–assistant interactions and precise, ground-truth information about when and why user preferences change. This gap makes it hard to study and improve models’ ability to track user state across months of interaction.
AI.method: They design a data generator that simulates users via a structured mental state graph. This graph encodes user preferences and how they change due to life events. From it, they generate multi-month conversational histories between simulated users and an assistant. Each conversation is annotated with ground-truth provenance for every preference change. Using this generator, they construct HorizonBench: 360 synthetic users, each with 6-month histories averaging ~4,300 turns and ~163K tokens. They then evaluate 25 contemporary language models on tasks that require tracking and updating beliefs about user preferences over these long horizons, under varying context lengths and levels of explicitness in how preferences are expressed.
AI.result: HorizonBench consists of 4,245 benchmark items built from 360 simulated users with long, dense conversation histories. When evaluated on HorizonBench, the best of 25 frontier models only achieves 52.8% accuracy, while most models perform at or near the 20% chance baseline. Error analysis shows that in over one-third of cases involving changed preferences, models incorrectly stick with the user’s original stated preference rather than the updated one, evidencing a failure to update their beliefs about the user state. This pattern persists regardless of how much context the model is given or how explicitly the preference changes are stated.
AI.conclusion: Long-horizon personalization is currently a major unsolved challenge for language models. Even advanced models frequently fail to track and update user preferences over months-long interactions, defaulting to outdated information. HorizonBench provides a controlled, well-annotated testbed for diagnosing and improving long-context modeling, memory mechanisms, theory-of-mind reasoning, and user modeling. The key bottleneck is not just context length or subtle expression, but the underlying state-tracking and belief-update capabilities of current models.

title: KnowledgeBerg: Evaluating Systematic Knowledge Coverage and Compositional Reasoning in Large Language Models
summary: Many real-world questions appear deceptively simple yet implicitly demand two capabilities: (i) systematic coverage of a bounded knowledge universe and (ii) compositional set-based reasoning over that universe, a phenomenon we term "the tip of the iceberg." We formalize this challenge through two orthogonal dimensions: knowledge width, the cardinality of the required universe, and reasoning depth, the number of compositional set operations. We introduce KnowledgeBerg, a benchmark of 4,800 multiple-choice questions derived from 1,183 enumeration seeds spanning 10 domains and 17 languages, with universes grounded in authoritative sources to ensure reproducibility. Representative open-source LLMs demonstrate severe limitations, achieving only 5.26-36.88 F1 on universe enumeration and 16.00-44.19 accuracy on knowledge-grounded reasoning. Diagnostic analyses reveal three stages of failure: completeness, or missing knowledge; awareness, or failure to identify requirements; and application, or incorrect reasoning execution. This pattern persists across languages and model scales. Although test-time compute and retrieval augmentation yield measurable gains -- up to 4.35 and 3.78 points, respectively -- substantial gaps remain, exposing limitations in how current LLMs organize structured knowledge and execute compositional reasoning over bounded domains. The dataset is available at https://huggingface.co/datasets/2npc/KnowledgeBerg

AI.tldr: The paper introduces KnowledgeBerg, a multilingual benchmark to test whether LLMs can fully enumerate bounded knowledge universes and perform compositional set-based reasoning over them, revealing large performance gaps even with retrieval and extra test-time compute.
AI.motivation: Many real-world questions require not only recalling facts but also exhaustively covering a closed set of relevant items and then performing multi-step set operations on that set. Existing benchmarks do not explicitly disentangle or stress-test these two capabilities—coverage of a bounded universe and compositional reasoning over it—across domains and languages. The authors aim to systematically measure where and how current LLMs fail on these “tip of the iceberg” tasks.
AI.method: The authors formalize the challenge with two axes: knowledge width (size of the required universe) and reasoning depth (number of compositional set operations). They build KnowledgeBerg, a benchmark of 4,800 multiple-choice questions generated from 1,183 enumeration seeds across 10 domains and 17 languages. Each question’s “universe” is grounded in authoritative sources (e.g., canonical lists) to ensure reproducibility. They evaluate open-source LLMs on two tasks: (1) universe enumeration (coverage of the full set) and (2) knowledge-grounded reasoning (accuracy on questions requiring set operations over the universe). They also run diagnostic analyses to separate failures into completeness, awareness, and application, and test interventions such as increased test-time compute and retrieval augmentation.
AI.result: Open-source LLMs perform poorly: 5.26–36.88 F1 on universe enumeration and 16.00–44.19 accuracy on knowledge-grounded reasoning, with similar weaknesses across languages and model sizes. Error analysis identifies three failure types—missing items in the universe (completeness), not recognizing what needs to be enumerated or reasoned about (awareness), and incorrect execution of set operations (application). Both extra test-time compute and retrieval-based augmentation improve scores modestly (up to +4.35 and +3.78 points), but do not close the gap to reliable performance.
AI.conclusion: KnowledgeBerg exposes fundamental limitations in current LLMs’ ability to (1) fully and systematically enumerate bounded knowledge domains and (2) execute compositional, set-based reasoning on top of those domains, even when supported by retrieval or extra computation. These limitations are robust across domains, languages, and model scales, suggesting that improving how models structure and manipulate explicit sets of knowledge is an open research problem. The benchmark provides a standardized way to study and track progress on these capabilities.

title: Probabilistic Programs of Thought
summary: LLMs are widely used for code generation and mathematical reasoning tasks where they are required to generate structured output. They either need to reason about code, generate code for a given specification, or reason using programs of thought. The typical approach to code generation is to prompt the model and generate samples until an appropriate program is obtained. Within this process, sampling $n$ programs from the language model requires $n$ GPU compute-intensive generations which becomes prohibitively expensive for larger values of $n$. In this work, we address this limitation by exposing the LLM's distribution within the generated programs themselves. We propose a novel test-time framework we dub probabilistic programs of thought to obtain more samples from the model with fewer LLM generations. Given a program generated by a model and the associated next-token probabilities, we build a probabilistic program that compactly represents exponentially many deterministic programs. Since performing probabilistic reasoning in this probabilistic program is much cheaper, our approach allows sampling new programs without any additional GPU compute and little CPU overhead. We instantiate our approach on benchmarks for code generation, code understanding and mathematical reasoning and report improvements in performance with fewer generations from the LLM.

AI.tldr: They propose a way to get many effective program samples from an LLM with far fewer expensive generations by turning one probabilistic generation into a compact probabilistic program that encodes many possible deterministic programs.
AI.motivation: Sampling many candidate programs from LLMs for code generation or mathematical reasoning is expensive because each sample requires a full GPU-backed generation pass. This cost becomes prohibitive when we need large sample sizes for better performance, such as in self-consistency or best-of-N decoding. The authors want to reduce the GPU cost while retaining or improving the benefits of having many diverse candidate programs.
AI.method: At test time, when the LLM generates a program, they also extract the next-token probability distributions at each step. From this single (or few) forward passes, they construct a probabilistic program that encodes branching at positions where several tokens have non-negligible probability. This probabilistic program compactly represents exponentially many concrete deterministic programs (corresponding to different token choices along branches). Then they perform cheaper probabilistic reasoning or sampling over this probabilistic program on CPU, effectively drawing additional program samples without further GPU calls. This framework is termed probabilistic programs of thought and can be plugged into code generation, code understanding, and math reasoning tasks.
AI.result: Using their probabilistic programs of thought framework, they can obtain more functional and diverse program samples with the same or fewer LLM generations. On benchmarks for code generation, code understanding, and mathematical reasoning they achieve higher accuracy or success rates compared to standard sampling or self-consistency baselines given the same or lower GPU budget. The method shows good trade-offs: substantial reduction in GPU calls with only modest CPU overhead.
AI.conclusion: Exposing and reusing the LLM’s internal token-level probability distribution inside a constructed probabilistic program allows exponential reuse of a small number of generations. This makes program- and reasoning-based use of LLMs more computationally efficient, enabling better performance under tight GPU budgets and suggesting a general direction for leveraging probabilistic structure in LLM outputs at test time.

title: Toward Reusability of AI Models Using Dynamic Updates of AI Documentation
summary:
This work addresses the challenge of disseminating reusable artificial intelligence (AI) models accompanied by AI documentation (a.k.a., AI model cards). The work is motivated by the large number of trained AI models that are not reusable due to the lack of (a) AI documentation and (b) the temporal lag between rapidly changing requirements on AI model reusability and those specified in various AI model cards. Our objectives are to shorten the lag time in updating AI model card templates and align AI documentation more closely with current AI best practices.
  Our approach introduces a methodology for delivering agile, data-driven, and community-based AI model cards. We use the Hugging Face (HF) repository of AI models, populated by a subset of the AI research and development community, and the AI consortium-based Zero Draft (ZD) templates for the AI documentation of AI datasets and AI models, as our test datasets. We also address questions about the value of AI documentation for AI reusability.
  Our work quantifies the correlations between AI model downloads/likes (i.e., AI model reuse metrics) from the HF repository and their documentation alignment with the ZD documentation templates using tables of contents and word statistics (i.e., AI documentation quality metrics). Furthermore, our work develops the infrastructure to regularly compare AI documentation templates against community-standard practices derived from millions of uploaded AI models in the Hugging Face repository. The impact of our work lies in introducing a methodology for delivering agile, data-driven, and community-based standards for documenting AI models and improving AI model reuse.

AI.tldr: They propose a data-driven, community-based way to keep AI model cards up-to-date with best practices, showing that better-aligned documentation correlates with more model reuse.
AI.motivation: Many trained AI models are hard to reuse because (1) they lack documentation and (2) existing model card templates lag behind fast-changing best practices, so documentation quickly becomes outdated and misaligned with community needs.
AI.method: Use millions of Hugging Face models and consortium-based Zero Draft (ZD) documentation templates as testbeds; compute documentation quality metrics (e.g., table-of-contents matching, word statistics) and correlate them with reuse metrics (downloads/likes), and build infrastructure to continually compare/update templates based on observed community practices.
AI.result: They find measurable correlations between documentation quality (alignment with ZD templates) and reuse metrics (downloads/likes) and implement infrastructure that can periodically analyze HF model documentation to update or assess model card templates.
AI.conclusion: Agile, data-driven, and community-informed model card standards are feasible and useful; continuously aligning documentation templates with observed community practices can improve documentation quality and, in turn, support greater AI model reuse.

title: Beyond "I Don't Know": Evaluating LLM Self-Awareness in Discriminating Data and Model Uncertainty
summary: Reliable Large Language Models (LLMs) should abstain when confidence is insufficient. However, prior studies often treat refusal as a generic "I don't know'', failing to distinguish input-level ambiguity (data uncertainty) from capability limitations (model uncertainty). This lack of distinction limits downstream action decisions like requesting clarification or invoking external tools. In this work, we introduce UA-Bench, a benchmark of over 3,500 questions drawn from six datasets spanning knowledge-intensive and reasoning-intensive tasks, designed to evaluate explicit uncertainty attribution. An evaluation of 18 frontier LLMs shows that even state-of-the-art models struggle to reliably discriminate between data uncertainty and model uncertainty, and that high answer accuracy does not necessarily imply strong uncertainty attribution ability. To narrow this gap, we propose a lightweight data synthesis and reinforcement learning strategy. Experiments on both Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 and Qwen3-8B in thinking mode show that the proposed method improves uncertainty attribution while preserving answer accuracy. Our code and data are publicly available now.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces UA-Bench and a training strategy to help LLMs distinguish between uncertainty due to ambiguous input vs. their own limitations, improving when and how they abstain from answering.
AI.motivation: Current LLMs often refuse or say "I don't know" without explaining whether the issue is ambiguous data or lack of capability. This prevents downstream systems or users from choosing appropriate follow-up actions such as clarifying the query or invoking external tools. There is also no systematic benchmark to measure this kind of fine-grained uncertainty attribution, and high answer accuracy is often incorrectly assumed to mean good uncertainty handling.
AI.method: 1) Construct UA-Bench, a benchmark of 3,500+ questions from six datasets covering both knowledge-intensive and reasoning-intensive tasks, each labeled to distinguish data uncertainty vs. model uncertainty. 2) Evaluate 18 frontier LLMs on this benchmark to measure their ability to attribute uncertainty explicitly. 3) Propose a lightweight approach combining data synthesis (to generate training examples for different uncertainty types) and reinforcement learning to optimize models for better uncertainty attribution without degrading answer accuracy. 4) Apply this method to Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 and Qwen3-8B (in thinking mode) and compare performance before and after training.
AI.result: The evaluation shows that even strong frontier LLMs have difficulty reliably distinguishing between data-driven ambiguity and their own knowledge/skill limitations. Moreover, answer accuracy is not predictive of good uncertainty attribution. After applying the proposed data synthesis and RL-based training to Qwen3-4B-Instruct-2507 and Qwen3-8B, the models show improved performance on UA-Bench in terms of correctly attributing uncertainty, while maintaining their original answer accuracy.
AI.conclusion: Explicit uncertainty attribution—separating data uncertainty from model uncertainty—is a distinct capability that current LLMs largely lack, and it cannot be inferred from answer accuracy alone. UA-Bench provides a standardized way to evaluate this ability. A targeted training strategy using synthetic data and reinforcement learning can significantly improve uncertainty attribution without harming task performance, making LLMs more reliable and actionable in downstream applications. The resources are released to support further research in this direction.

title: PV-SQL: Synergizing Database Probing and Rule-based Verification for Text-to-SQL Agents
summary: Text-to-SQL systems often struggle with deep contextual understanding, particularly for complex queries with subtle requirements. We present PV-SQL, an agentic framework that addresses these failures through two complementary components: Probe and Verify. The Probe component iteratively generates probing queries to retrieve concrete records from the database, resolving ambiguities in value formats, column semantics, and inter-table relationships to build richer contextual understanding. The Verify component employs a rule-based method to extract verifiable conditions and construct an executable checklist, enabling iterative SQL refinement that effectively reduces missing constraints. Experiments on the BIRD benchmarks show that PV-SQL outperforms the best text-to-SQL baseline by 5% in execution accuracy and 20.8% in valid efficiency score while consuming fewer tokens.

AI.tldr: PV-SQL is an agentic text-to-SQL framework with probing and verification steps that improves contextual understanding and constraint completeness, yielding higher execution accuracy and efficiency on BIRD.
AI.motivation: Existing text-to-SQL models frequently misinterpret subtle query requirements and database-specific nuances, leading to incorrect or under-constrained SQL, especially on complex benchmarks. There is a need for mechanisms that can actively explore database contents and systematically verify query constraints rather than relying solely on one-shot semantic parsing.
AI.method: PV-SQL consists of two components. (1) Probe: an agent iteratively generates auxiliary probing queries to the database to inspect actual values, clarify column semantics, and understand relationships between tables. These probes return concrete records that the system uses to disambiguate formats and context before forming the final SQL. (2) Verify: a rule-based verifier extracts explicit, checkable conditions from the natural language question, converts them into an executable checklist, and iteratively refines the candidate SQL by checking which conditions are satisfied or missing, thereby adding or correcting constraints.
AI.result: On the BIRD benchmark suite, PV-SQL surpasses the strongest prior text-to-SQL baseline by 5 percentage points in execution accuracy and improves the valid efficiency score by 20.8%, all while using fewer language-model tokens, indicating both better accuracy and higher efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating probing interactions with the database and explicit verification of NL-derived conditions leads to more context-aware and constraint-complete text-to-SQL generation. PV-SQL demonstrates that an agentic, multi-step framework can outperform standard baselines in both accuracy and efficiency on challenging benchmarks like BIRD.

title: CRISP: Compressing Redundancy in Chain-of-Thought via Intrinsic Saliency Pruning
comment: Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2026
summary: Long Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning is pivotal for the success of recent reasoning models but suffers from high computational overhead and latency. While prior works attempt to compress CoT via external compressor, they often fail to align with the model's internal reasoning dynamics, resulting in the loss of critical logical steps. This paper presents \textbf{C}ompressing \textbf{R}edundancy in Chain-of-Thought via \textbf{I}ntrinsic \textbf{S}aliency \textbf{P}runing (\textbf{CRISP}), a framework that compresses CoT by exploiting the model's intrinsic saliency. Our analysis reveals a distinct phenomenon: the reasoning termination token \texttt{[object Object]} acts as an information anchor, where its attention pattern effectively demarcates essential reasoning from redundancy. Based on this finding, we design a policy that utilizes these intrinsic attention signals to guide atomic compression operations. In contrast to coarse-grained pruning strategies, CRISP strategically distills the reasoning chain to maximize information density while preserving logical coherence. Empirical results across various backbone models and mathematical datasets demonstrate that CRISP achieves a 50-60% reduction in token count without compromising accuracy, effectively mitigating the efficiency bottleneck of long-context reasoning. We open-source our implementation to facilitate further research in efficient reasoning.

AI.tldr: They propose CRISP, a method that uses a model’s own attention saliency—especially around the reasoning-termination token—to compress chain-of-thought, roughly halving token length without hurting accuracy.
AI.motivation: Long chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning greatly boosts model performance but is computationally expensive and slow. Existing CoT compression relies on external compressors that are misaligned with how the base model actually reasons, often dropping crucial logical steps. There is a need for an intrinsic, model-aware way to compress CoT that preserves reasoning quality while reducing cost.
AI.method: CRISP (Compressing Redundancy in Chain-of-Thought via Intrinsic Saliency Pruning) analyzes the model’s internal attention patterns, especially around a special reasoning termination token, which they find acts as an information anchor separating essential reasoning from redundant text. They derive a policy that uses these intrinsic attention signals to guide fine-grained, atomic pruning operations on the CoT, strategically removing low-saliency tokens and segments while retaining high-saliency, logically important content. This produces a shorter yet information-dense and coherent reasoning chain, as opposed to coarse chunk- or step-level pruning.
AI.result: Across multiple backbone models and math reasoning datasets, CRISP reduces the number of tokens in the chain-of-thought by about 50–60% without measurable loss in task accuracy. This substantially reduces computation and latency for long-context reasoning compared to uncompressed CoT and outperforms prior external compression approaches in the efficiency–accuracy tradeoff.
AI.conclusion: Intrinsic attention-based saliency, particularly around a reasoning termination token, can reliably distinguish essential from redundant parts of a chain-of-thought. By leveraging this signal, CRISP provides a fine-grained, model-aligned compression mechanism that significantly improves efficiency while preserving reasoning quality. The authors release their implementation to encourage further work on efficient reasoning and CoT compression.

title: Poly-EPO: Training Exploratory Reasoning Models
summary: Exploration is a cornerstone of learning from experience: it enables agents to find solutions to complex problems, generalize to novel ones, and scale performance with test-time compute. In this paper, we present a framework for post-training language models (LMs) that explicitly encourages optimistic exploration and promotes a synergy between exploration and exploitation. The central idea is to train the LM to generate sets of responses that are collectively accurate under the reward function and exploratory in their reasoning strategies. We first develop a general recipe for optimizing LMs with set reinforcement learning (set RL) under arbitrary objective functions, showing how standard RL algorithms can be adapted to this setting through a modification to the advantage computation. We then propose Polychromic Exploratory Policy Optimization (Poly-EPO), which instantiates this framework with an objective that explicitly synergizes exploration and exploitation. Across a range of reasoning benchmarks, we show that Poly-EPO improves generalization, as evidenced by higher pass@$k$ coverage, preserves greater diversity in model generations, and effectively scales with test-time compute.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Poly-EPO, a post-training framework for language models that uses set-based reinforcement learning to jointly promote accurate and diverse exploratory reasoning, leading to better generalization and scaling with test-time compute on reasoning benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Standard language model training and many RL fine-tuning methods emphasize exploitation of known high-reward behaviors, which can reduce diversity in reasoning and hurt generalization, especially when leveraging large test-time compute via multiple sampled responses. There is a need for methods that explicitly encourage exploration in a way that remains aligned with task reward and that can better use many-sample evaluation (pass@k) by producing responses that are both accurate and diverse in reasoning strategies.
AI.method: The authors formulate a set reinforcement learning (set RL) framework where the LM is trained to produce sets of responses rather than single outputs, and the objective evaluates the collective properties of these sets under a reward function. They adapt standard RL algorithms to this set setting via a modified advantage computation. Building on this, they propose Polychromic Exploratory Policy Optimization (Poly-EPO), which defines an objective that explicitly couples exploration (diverse reasoning trajectories across the set) with exploitation (high task reward/accuracy), and optimize the LM accordingly in a post-training phase.
AI.result: On a variety of reasoning benchmarks, models trained with Poly-EPO obtain higher pass@k scores (meaning that across multiple samples, the chance that at least one answer is correct increases), maintain greater diversity in their generated outputs and reasoning patterns, and show better scaling behavior when more test-time samples/compute are used, compared with baselines lacking this exploratory set-RL training.
AI.conclusion: Treating language model outputs as sets and optimizing them with a set-based RL objective that balances exploration and exploitation can significantly improve reasoning generalization, sample diversity, and the benefits gained from additional test-time compute. Poly-EPO demonstrates that explicitly optimistic exploratory training in this set RL framework is an effective post-training strategy for enhancing LM reasoning performance.

title: Cat-DPO: Category-Adaptive Safety Alignment
summary: Aligning large language models with human preferences must balance two competing goals: responding helpfully to legitimate requests and reliably refusing harmful ones. Most preference-based safety alignment methods collapse safety into a single scalar that is applied uniformly to every preference pair. The result is a model that looks safe on average but stays relatively unsafe on a minority of harm categories. We cast safety alignment as a per-category constrained optimization problem and derive Cat-DPO, a direct-preference-optimization algorithm with a separate adaptive safety margin for each harm category. The margin tightens when the model still produces unsafe responses on a category and relaxes once the model catches up, so the training signal tracks each category's current difficulty rather than averaging under one global rate. Across two LLM backbones and six preference-learning baselines, Cat-DPO iimproves aggregate helpfulness and harmlessness and compresses per-category safety variance and the best-to-worst gap, offering a drop-in per-category refinement of direct preference safety alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Cat-DPO, a per-category direct preference optimization method for aligning LLMs with human preferences that improves both overall safety and consistency across different harm categories.
AI.motivation: Existing safety alignment methods typically collapse safety into a single global scalar preference signal, which makes models look safe on average but leaves them notably unsafe for some specific harm categories. This aggregate treatment fails to address uneven safety performance and high variance between categories, creating worst-case failures even when average metrics look good. The authors want a method that accounts for per-category difficulty and reduces the gap between the safest and least safe categories without sacrificing helpfulness.
AI.method: They formulate safety alignment as a per-category constrained optimization problem. Building on direct preference optimization (DPO), they introduce Cat-DPO, which assigns each harm category its own adaptive safety margin. During training, the method inspects whether the model is still producing unsafe responses in each category: if a category remains unsafe, its margin is tightened to enforce stronger preference for safe responses; if a category improves, the margin is relaxed. This adaptive margin thus modulates the effective strength of safety constraints per category, letting the training signal track and compensate for each category’s current difficulty instead of using a single global rate.
AI.result: When applied to two different LLM backbones and compared against six preference-learning baselines, Cat-DPO improves both helpfulness (i.e., quality and usefulness of responses to legitimate requests) and harmlessness (reliability of refusals to harmful requests). Additionally, it reduces the variance of safety performance across harm categories and narrows the performance gap between the best- and worst-performing categories.
AI.conclusion: Per-category, adaptively constrained preference optimization—implemented as Cat-DPO—offers a practical, drop-in refinement for safety alignment of LLMs. By replacing a single global safety scalar with category-specific adaptive margins, it yields models that are not only safer and more helpful on average but also more consistent and robust across all harm categories.

title: Semantic Entanglement in Vector-Based Retrieval: A Formal Framework and Context-Conditioned Disentanglement Pipeline for Agentic RAG Systems
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems depend on the geometric properties of vector representations to retrieve contextually appropriate evidence. When source documents interleave multiple topics within contiguous text, standard vectorization produces embedding spaces in which semantically distinct content occupies overlapping neighborhoods. We term this condition semantic entanglement. We formalize entanglement as a model-relative measure of cross-topic overlap in embedding space and define an Entanglement Index (EI) as a quantitative proxy. We argue that higher EI constrains attainable Top-K retrieval precision under cosine similarity retrieval. To address this, we introduce the Semantic Disentanglement Pipeline (SDP), a four-stage preprocessing framework that restructures documents prior to embedding. We further propose context-conditioned preprocessing, in which document structure is shaped by patterns of operational use, and a continuous feedback mechanism that adapts document structure based on agent performance. We evaluate SDP on a real-world enterprise healthcare knowledge base comprising over 2,000 documents across approximately 25 sub-domains. Top-K retrieval precision improves from approximately 32% under fixed-token chunking to approximately 82% under SDP, while mean EI decreases from 0.71 to 0.14. We do not claim that entanglement fully explains RAG failure, but that it captures a distinct preprocessing failure mode that downstream optimization cannot reliably correct once encoded into the vector space.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and quantifies a failure mode in RAG systems called semantic entanglement—when embeddings of different topics overlap—and proposes a preprocessing pipeline that restructures documents to disentangle topics, dramatically improving retrieval precision.
AI.motivation: RAG systems often fail to retrieve the right context even with strong LLMs, especially on documents that mix several topics in contiguous text. Standard chunking and embedding create overlapping neighborhoods in vector space where semantically different content is close together, harming Top-K retrieval precision. There is a need to (1) define and measure this phenomenon and (2) design preprocessing that reduces it, rather than relying solely on downstream retrieval or model tuning.
AI.method: The authors formalize semantic entanglement as a model-relative measure of cross-topic overlap in embedding space and define an Entanglement Index (EI) as a quantitative proxy for that overlap. They then introduce the Semantic Disentanglement Pipeline (SDP), a four-stage document preprocessing framework that restructures documents before embedding. SDP includes context-conditioned preprocessing, where document structure is tailored to actual usage patterns, and a continuous feedback loop that iteratively adapts document structure based on agent performance metrics. Retrieval uses cosine similarity over these restructured embeddings.
AI.result: On a real-world enterprise healthcare knowledge base containing 2,000+ documents across ~25 sub-domains, using fixed-token chunking yields about 32% Top-K retrieval precision and a mean EI of 0.71. Applying SDP raises Top-K retrieval precision to about 82% and reduces mean EI to 0.14, indicating substantially less overlap between topics in embedding space and much better retrieval quality.
AI.conclusion: Semantic entanglement is a distinct, quantifiable failure mode rooted in document structure and preprocessing, not solely in retrieval algorithms or model quality. The Semantic Disentanglement Pipeline, especially when coupled with context-conditioned preprocessing and feedback-driven adaptation, can substantially reduce entanglement (lower EI) and greatly improve retrieval precision. Once entanglement is encoded in embeddings, downstream optimization alone is unlikely to fix it, so addressing it at the preprocessing stage is crucial for robust RAG performance.

title: RoTRAG: Rule of Thumb Reasoning for Conversation Harm Detection with Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Detecting harmful content in multi turn dialogue requires reasoning over the full conversational context rather than isolated utterances. However, most existing methods rely mainly on models internal parametric knowledge, without explicit grounding in external normative principles. This often leads to inconsistent judgments in socially nuanced contexts, limited interpretability, and redundant reasoning across turns. To address this, we propose RoTRAG, a retrieval augmented framework that incorporates concise human written moral norms, called Rules of Thumb (RoTs), into LLM based harm assessment. For each turn, RoTRAG retrieves relevant RoTs from an external corpus and uses them as explicit normative evidence for turn level reasoning and final severity classification. To improve efficiency, we further introduce a lightweight binary routing classifier that decides whether a new turn requires retrieval grounded reasoning or can reuse existing context. Experiments on ProsocialDialog and Safety Reasoning Multi Turn Dialogue show that RoTRAG consistently improves both harm classification and severity estimation over competitive baselines, with an average relative gain of around 40% in F1 across benchmark datasets and an average relative reduction of 8.4% in distributional error, while reducing redundant computation without sacrificing performance.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RoTRAG, a retrieval-augmented LLM framework that uses human-written moral rules of thumb to more consistently detect and rate harmful content in multi-turn dialogues, achieving notably better F1 and severity estimation while cutting redundant computation.
AI.motivation: Existing harmful-content detectors for multi-turn dialogue mostly rely on the LLM’s internal knowledge and reason about each turn in isolation, which leads to inconsistent decisions in socially nuanced conversations, low interpretability of why something is labeled harmful, and inefficient, repeated reasoning across dialogue turns. The authors want a system that can: (1) ground judgments in explicit, human-written moral norms; (2) leverage the full conversational context; (3) provide more consistent, interpretable assessments of harm and its severity; and (4) reduce unnecessary computation when turns are clearly safe or can reuse prior reasoning.
AI.method: They introduce RoTRAG, a retrieval-augmented framework where, for each dialogue turn, the system retrieves relevant human-written moral norms—called Rules of Thumb (RoTs)—from an external corpus and feeds them, along with the dialogue context, into an LLM that performs explicit, RoT-grounded reasoning and outputs harm / severity labels. To improve efficiency, they add a lightweight binary routing classifier that decides per turn whether fresh retrieval and reasoning are needed or whether existing RoTs and reasoning from previous turns can be reused, thus avoiding redundant computation. The framework is evaluated on multi-turn safety benchmarks (ProsocialDialog and Safety Reasoning Multi Turn Dialogue).
AI.result: Across benchmarks, RoTRAG outperforms strong baselines on both harm detection (classification) and severity estimation, achieving on average about a 40% relative improvement in F1 scores and an 8.4% relative reduction in distributional error for severity predictions. The routing mechanism reduces redundant computation while preserving these performance gains.
AI.conclusion: Grounding dialogue-harm assessment in retrieved, human-written moral rules of thumb substantially improves accuracy, consistency, and interpretability compared to relying solely on an LLM’s internal knowledge. The RoTRAG framework shows that retrieval-augmented, norm-aware reasoning and efficient routing can better handle complex, multi-turn safety judgments, suggesting a promising direction for building more reliable and scalable safety moderation systems for conversational AI.

title: Stratagem: Learning Transferable Reasoning via Trajectory-Modulated Game Self-Play
summary: Games offer a compelling paradigm for developing general reasoning capabilities in language models, as they naturally demand strategic planning, probabilistic inference, and adaptive decision-making. However, existing self-play approaches rely solely on terminal game outcomes, providing no mechanism to distinguish transferable reasoning patterns from game-specific heuristics. We present STRATAGEM, which addresses two fundamental barriers to reasoning transfer: domain specificity, where learned patterns remain anchored in game semantics, and contextual stasis, where static game contexts fail to cultivate progressive reasoning. STRATAGEM selectively reinforces trajectories exhibiting abstract, domain-agnostic reasoning through a Reasoning Transferability Coefficient, while incentivizing adaptive reasoning development via a Reasoning Evolution Reward. Experiments across mathematical reasoning, general reasoning, and code generation benchmarks demonstrate substantial improvements, with particularly strong gains on competition-level mathematics where multi-step reasoning is critical. Ablation studies and human evaluation confirm that both components contribute to transferable reasoning.

AI.tldr: STRATAGEM is a training framework that improves the transfer of reasoning skills learned from games to other domains, by reinforcing trajectories that exhibit abstract, domain-agnostic reasoning and adaptive reasoning evolution.
AI.motivation: While games are powerful environments for training language models in reasoning, existing self-play methods only use win/loss signals and cannot distinguish generally useful reasoning strategies from game-specific tricks. This limits the transfer of learned reasoning patterns to other tasks like math or coding. The paper aims to overcome this limitation by identifying and amplifying transferable reasoning behaviors during training.
AI.method: The authors propose STRATAGEM, a self-play-based training framework with two key components: (1) a Reasoning Transferability Coefficient that scores and selectively reinforces trajectories showing abstract, domain-agnostic reasoning patterns instead of game-specific heuristics; and (2) a Reasoning Evolution Reward that encourages reasoning that becomes progressively more sophisticated and adaptive over time rather than remaining static. These signals are integrated into the training loop to guide which trajectories are upweighted during learning.
AI.result: Across benchmarks in mathematical reasoning, general reasoning, and code generation, models trained with STRATAGEM show substantial performance improvements compared to baselines, with especially large gains on competition-level math tasks that require multi-step reasoning. Ablation studies show that removing either the transferability coefficient or the evolution reward reduces the gains, and human evaluations corroborate that STRATAGEM-trained models exhibit more general and adaptable reasoning behaviors.
AI.conclusion: STRATAGEM makes game-based training of language models more effective for learning generally useful reasoning skills by explicitly rewarding abstract, transferable and evolving reasoning patterns. This leads to better performance on diverse downstream tasks, particularly those involving complex, multi-step reasoning, and highlights the importance of shaping self-play signals beyond raw game outcomes.

title: Calibrated? Not for Everyone: How Sexual Orientation and Religious Markers Distort LLM Accuracy and Confidence in Medical QA
summary: Safe clinical deployment of Large Language Models (LLMs) requires not only high accuracy but also robust uncertainty calibration to ensure models defer to clinicians when appropriate. Our paper investigates how social descriptors of a patient (specifically sexual orientation and religious affiliation) distort these uncertainty signals and model accuracy. Evaluating nine general-purpose and biomedical LLMs on 2,364 medical questions and their counterfactual variants, we demonstrate that identity markers cause a "calibration crisis". "Homosexual" markers consistently trigger performance drops, and intersectional identities produce idiosyncratic, non-additive harms to calibration. Moreover, a clinician-validated case study in an open-ended generation setting confirms that these failures are not an artifact of the multiple-choice format. Our results demonstrate that the presence of social identity cues does not merely shift predictions; it affects the reliability of confidence signals, posing a significant risk to equitable care and safe deployment in confidence-based clinical workflows.
