Within this controlled environment, an RL algorithm trains a small LLM-based search agent to iteratively search, gather, and reason over information. The virtual world is constructed so that the skills learned there transfer to real-world deep research tasks, allowing continuous improvement without constant access to expensive, noisy web search APIs.
AI.result: Using LiteResearcher, the authors train a 4B-parameter model (LiteResearcher-4B) that achieves state-of-the-art performance among open-source models on deep research benchmarks. On GAIA it scores 71.3%, and on Xbench it scores 78.0%. These results reportedly surpass both large-scale open-source systems and commercial research agents such as Tongyi DeepResearch and Claude-4.5 Sonnet.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed virtual search environment can make agentic RL for deep research scalable, stable, and cost-effective. Even relatively small models, when trained with LiteResearcher’s RL recipe, can surpass much larger commercial and open-source agents on demanding research benchmarks. This suggests that scalable RL training—rather than simply scaling model size—is a key ingredient for building strong deep research agents.

title: DuConTE: Dual-Granularity Text Encoder with Topology-Constrained Attention for Text-attributed Graphs
summary: Text-attributed graphs integrate semantic information of node texts with topological structure, offering significant value in various applications such as document classification and information extraction. Existing approaches typically encode textual content using language models (LMs), followed by graph neural networks (GNNs) to process structural information. However, during the LM-based text encoding phase, most methods not only perform semantic interaction solely at the word-token granularity, but also neglect the structural dependencies among texts from different nodes. In this work, we propose DuConTE, a dual-granularity text encoder with topology-constrained attention. The model employs a cascaded architecture of two pretrained LMs, encoding semantics first at the word-token granularity and then at the node granularity. During the self-attention computation in each LM, we dynamically adjust the attention mask matrix based on node connectivity, guiding the model to learn semantic correlations informed by the graph structure. Furthermore, when composing node representations from word-token embeddings, we separately evaluate the importance of tokens under the center-node context and the neighborhood context, enabling the capture of more contextually relevant semantic information. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmark datasets demonstrate that DuConTE achieves state-of-the-art performance on the majority of them.

AI.tldr: DuConTE is a dual-granularity text encoder for text-attributed graphs that injects graph topology into attention masks of two cascaded language models and reweights tokens under center-node and neighborhood contexts, achieving state-of-the-art results on several benchmarks.
AI.motivation: In text-attributed graphs, nodes contain rich textual content alongside graph structure. Existing methods usually encode node texts with a language model and then pass node embeddings to a GNN. However, these approaches: (1) only model semantic interaction at word-token level within each text, ignoring higher-level node-to-node semantic relations, and (2) fail to incorporate the graph’s structural dependencies into the text encoding process itself. Consequently, they underutilize the synergy between textual semantics and graph topology. The paper aims to more tightly couple text encoding with graph structure and to better exploit multi-granularity semantics for downstream tasks like document classification and information extraction.
AI.method: The authors propose DuConTE, a dual-granularity text encoder with topology-constrained attention. The method stacks two pretrained language models in a cascaded manner. The first LM encodes semantics at the word-token level; its outputs are then aggregated and fed into a second LM that operates at node granularity. In both LMs, the self-attention mechanism is modified by dynamically adjusting the attention mask matrix according to node connectivity in the underlying graph, so that attention is guided by structural relations. When aggregating word-token embeddings into node-level representations, DuConTE explicitly distinguishes and scores token importance under two contexts: (a) the center-node context (information local to the node) and (b) the neighborhood context (information relevant to neighboring nodes). These components jointly produce node embeddings that are semantically rich and structure-aware.
AI.result: On several standard text-attributed graph benchmarks, DuConTE is empirically shown to outperform existing baselines. The experiments demonstrate consistent performance gains over prior LM+GNN pipelines and other advanced graph text encoders, with DuConTE achieving state-of-the-art results on most datasets evaluated. Ablation studies indicate that both the topology-constrained attention and the dual-context token importance mechanism contribute substantially to the performance improvements.
AI.conclusion: DuConTE effectively strengthens the integration of textual semantics and graph structure for text-attributed graphs. By employing dual-granularity encoding and topology-aware attention, along with separate modeling of token importance under center-node and neighborhood contexts, the model learns more informative, structure-guided node representations. The empirical results suggest that making the text encoder itself graph-aware is a powerful design choice, yielding state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks and providing a promising direction for future research on text-attributed graph learning.

title: ContraPrompt: Contrastive Prompt Optimization via Dyadic Reasoning Trace Analysis
summary: Prompt optimization methods either analyze individual failures in isolation or compare prompt variants across examples, operating on single execution traces with no access to the reasoning process distinguishing success from failure on the same input. We introduce ContraPrompt, built on the observation that when a model fails but succeeds on a retry with feedback, the difference between its two chain-of-thought traces constitutes an optimization signal not captured by prior methods. Unlike prior contrastive methods, we compare complete intermediate reasoning processes: the two traces share model, input, and base prompt, so remaining differences reflect reasoning strategy and appended error feedback -- we call this dyadic reasoning trace analysis. The multi-attempt solving phase is an instrumented agentic retry loop that generates contrastive data automatically without human annotation. Extracted rules are organized into an input-aware decision tree routing instructions by observable input characteristics. On four reasoning and compliance benchmarks, ContraPrompt outperforms GEPA (Agrawal et al., 2026) on all four, with absolute gains of +8.29 pp on HotPotQA (+20.8% rel.), +2.21 pp on GDPR-Bench (+18.2% rel.), +7.14 pp on GPQA Diamond (+10.6% rel.), and +0.74 pp on BBH (+0.85% rel.). Ablations confirm dyadic trace contrastivity is the critical component, with a -16% relative average drop upon its removal. On 53 EvalSet black-box optimization problems, ContraPrompt beats GEPA on 11, ties on 41, and loses on 1 at equal budget. On FiNER-139 financial named entity recognition (Loukas et al., 2022), ContraPrompt achieves +7.77 pp over the unoptimized baseline (+11.6% rel.) and +1.94 pp over GEPA (+2.66% rel.), with branch conditions aligning with standard US GAAP financial-instrument categories.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ContraPrompt, a prompt optimization method that leverages contrastive pairs of chain-of-thought traces from failed and subsequently successful retries on the same input to automatically learn input-aware prompt routing rules, outperforming prior methods such as GEPA on multiple reasoning, compliance, black-box optimization, and financial NER benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing prompt optimization methods either inspect single failures or compare different prompts across different examples, but they lack access to contrastive reasoning traces that show why a model fails on an input yet succeeds on a retry with feedback. This misses a rich optimization signal contained in the difference between failed and successful chains-of-thought for the same input. The paper aims to exploit this overlooked signal to build more effective, automatically learned, and input-aware prompting strategies without heavy human annotation.
AI.method: ContraPrompt runs an instrumented multi-attempt, agentic retry loop for each input: the model first attempts a solution with a base prompt, receives feedback when it fails, and retries. The resulting pairs of failed and successful chain-of-thought traces share the same model, input, and base prompt, differing mainly in reasoning strategy and appended error feedback—a setup termed dyadic reasoning trace analysis. From these contrastive trace pairs, the system automatically extracts rules capturing which reasoning patterns and feedback-driven modifications correlate with success. These rules are then organized into an input-aware decision tree that routes future inputs to appropriate prompt variants or prompting strategies based on observable input characteristics. The entire contrastive data generation is automated and does not rely on human annotation.
AI.result: On four benchmarks—HotPotQA, GDPR-Bench, GPQA Diamond, and BBH—ContraPrompt consistently outperforms GEPA, with absolute gains of +8.29 pp, +2.21 pp, +7.14 pp, and +0.74 pp respectively, corresponding to sizable relative improvements (up to +20.8%). Ablation experiments show that removing dyadic trace contrastivity causes a 16% relative average performance drop, indicating this component is central to the method’s effectiveness. On 53 EvalSet black-box optimization tasks, ContraPrompt beats GEPA on 11, ties on 41, and loses on only 1 under equal budget. On the FiNER-139 financial NER dataset, it improves over the unoptimized baseline by +7.77 pp and over GEPA by +1.94 pp, with learned decision-tree branches that correspond well to standard US GAAP financial instrument categories.
AI.conclusion: Using contrastive analysis between failed and successful chain-of-thought traces on the same input—dyadic reasoning trace analysis—provides a powerful optimization signal for prompt design. ContraPrompt leverages this to automatically construct input-aware decision trees that route instructions to effective prompting strategies, offering consistent gains over a strong baseline (GEPA) across reasoning, compliance, black-box optimization, and financial NER tasks. The key driver of these improvements is the use of dyadic contrastive traces rather than conventional single-trace or cross-example prompt comparisons.

title: Jupiter-N Technical Report
summary: We present Jupiter-N, a hybrid reasoning model post-trained from Nemotron 3 Super, a fully open-source 120 billion parameter LLM. We target three objectives: (1) agentic capability via uncertainty-curated trajectories; (2) UK cultural alignment via synthetic data grounded in cultural norms; and (3) Welsh language support via parallel corpora and LLM-translated Welsh conversations. Our data curation strategy carefully preserves the base model's capabilities: using our Forget-Me-Not framework, we mix on-policy synthetic replay with off-policy task data to mitigate catastrophic forgetting, and include a mixture of reasoning and non-reasoning traces to maintain Nemotron's hybrid reasoning ability. Jupiter-N achieves standout gains over Nemotron in Welsh (+18 on ARC-Easy, +5.25 on MMLU-Lite), terminal-use (+9.1 on Terminal Bench 2) and instruction following (+4.4 on IFBench), while retaining the base model capabilities. We frame this work as a reproducible template for sovereign post-training: substituting cultural knowledge, institutional corpora, and target languages produces an equivalent pipeline for any country. All model weights and all post-training datasets are publicly released under open licences.

AI.tldr: Jupiter-N is a 120B-parameter hybrid reasoning LLM post-trained from Nemotron 3 Super to improve agentic tools use, UK cultural alignment, and Welsh language support while preserving base capabilities.
AI.motivation: Large open LLMs often lack strong agentic behaviors, fine-grained local cultural alignment, and robust support for minority languages, and naive post-training can cause catastrophic forgetting of base capabilities. The authors aim to create a reproducible, open template for "sovereign" post-training that tailors a general LLM to a specific country’s culture, institutions, and languages without degrading its original strengths.
AI.method: Starting from Nemotron 3 Super (120B), the authors perform post-training focused on three goals: (1) agentic capability via uncertainty-curated trajectories and terminal/tool-use data, (2) UK cultural alignment using synthetic datasets grounded in UK norms, and (3) Welsh support using parallel corpora plus LLM-translated Welsh conversations. They propose a Forget-Me-Not framework that mixes on-policy synthetic replay with off-policy task data and a balanced blend of reasoning and non-reasoning traces to maintain hybrid reasoning. All data and weights are released under open licenses to enable reproducibility and adaptation to other countries.
AI.result: Relative to the Nemotron base, Jupiter-N shows large Welsh performance gains (+18 on ARC-Easy, +5.25 on MMLU-Lite), better terminal-use (+9.1 on Terminal Bench 2), and stronger instruction following (+4.4 on IFBench), while retaining the base model’s general capabilities. The empirical results indicate that their curated post-training pipeline effectively improves targeted abilities without catastrophic forgetting.
AI.conclusion: Jupiter-N demonstrates that careful post-training with the Forget-Me-Not framework can localize a large open LLM to specific cultural, institutional, and linguistic contexts, enhance agentic behavior, and preserve original strengths. The authors propose this as a general, reproducible template for sovereign post-training: by swapping in other countries’ cultural knowledge, institutional corpora, and target languages, practitioners can build similarly specialized yet capable national models, supported by the fully open weights and datasets.

title: CADMAS-CTX: Contextual Capability Calibration for Multi-Agent Delegation
summary: We revisit multi-agent delegation under a stronger and more realistic assumption: an agent's capability is not fixed at the skill level, but depends on task context. A coding agent may excel at short standalone edits yet fail on long-horizon debugging; a planner may perform well on shallow tasks yet degrade on chained dependencies. Static skill-level capability profiles therefore average over heterogeneous situations and can induce systematic misdelegation. We propose CADMAS-CTX, a framework for contextual capability calibration. For each agent, skill, and coarse context bucket, CADMAS-CTX maintains a Beta posterior that captures stable experience in that part of the task space. Delegation is then made by a risk-aware score that combines the posterior mean with an uncertainty penalty, so that agents delegate only when a peer appears better and that assessment is sufficiently well supported by evidence. This paper makes three contributions. First, a hierarchical contextual capability profile replaces static skill-level confidence with context-conditioned posteriors. Second, based on contextual bandit theory, we formally prove context-aware routing achieves lower cumulative regret than static routing under sufficient context heterogeneity, formalizing the bias-variance tradeoff. Third, we empirically validate our method on GAIA and SWE-bench benchmarks. On GAIA with GPT-4o agents, CADMAS-CTX achieves 0.442 accuracy, outperforming static baseline 0.381 and AutoGen 0.354 with non-overlapping 95% confidence intervals. On SWE-bench Lite, it improves resolve rate from 22.3% to 31.4%. Ablations show the uncertainty penalty improves robustness against context tagging noise. Our results demonstrate contextual calibration and risk-aware delegation significantly improve multi-agent teamwork compared with static global skill assignments.

AI.tldr: They introduce CADMAS-CTX, a contextual, risk-aware routing framework that calibrates agent capabilities by task context instead of static skills, theoretically and empirically showing better multi-agent delegation.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent delegation assumes each agent has a fixed skill-level capability, but in practice agents perform differently across task contexts (e.g., short edits vs. long debugging). Static, global capability profiles average over heterogeneous situations, causing systematic misdelegation and suboptimal teamwork. The authors aim to model and exploit contextual variation in capabilities to improve how tasks are delegated across AI agents.
AI.method: They design CADMAS-CTX, which builds a hierarchical contextual capability profile for each agent. For every (agent, skill, context-bucket) triple, the system maintains a Beta posterior representing success probability given historical performance in that context. Delegation decisions use a risk-aware score: posterior mean minus an uncertainty penalty, so agents only delegate when there is sufficient evidence that a peer is better. The framework is grounded in contextual bandit theory, and they compare CADMAS-CTX to static routing baselines and AutoGen on GAIA and SWE-bench Lite benchmarks, along with ablations on uncertainty penalty and context tagging noise.
AI.result: Theoretically, they prove that context-aware routing has lower cumulative regret than static routing when there is enough context heterogeneity, formalizing a bias-variance tradeoff between global and contextual models. Empirically, on GAIA with GPT-4o agents, CADMAS-CTX reaches 0.442 accuracy versus 0.381 for a static baseline and 0.354 for AutoGen, with non-overlapping 95% confidence intervals. On SWE-bench Lite, it improves bug-resolve rate from 22.3% to 31.4%. Ablations show that including the uncertainty penalty makes the system more robust to noise in context tagging.
AI.conclusion: Contextual capability calibration and risk-aware delegation lead to noticeably better multi-agent coordination than static global skill assignments. Modeling agent performance as context-dependent, with statistically principled uncertainty handling, reduces misdelegation and improves overall task performance, both in theory and in practice on challenging benchmarks.

title: Self-Consistency from Only Two Samples: CoT-PoT Ensembling for Efficient LLM Reasoning
comment: 9 pages, 3 figures; accepted to Findings of ACL 2026
summary: Self-consistency (SC) is a popular technique for improving the reasoning accuracy of large language models by aggregating multiple sampled outputs, but it comes at a high computational cost due to extensive sampling. We introduce a hybrid ensembling approach that leverages the complementary strengths of two distinct modes of reasoning: Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program-of-Thought (PoT). We describe a general framework for combining these two forms of reasoning in self-consistency, as well as particular strategies for both full sampling and early-stopping. We show that CoT-PoT ensembling not only improves overall accuracy, but also drastically reduces the number of samples required for SC by a factor of 9.3x. In particular, the majority of tasks (78.6%) can be addressed with only two samples, which has not been possible with any prior SC methods.

AI.tldr: Hybrid ensembling of Chain-of-Thought and Program-of-Thought reasoning reduces the sampling cost of self-consistency while improving accuracy.
AI.motivation: Self-consistency improves reasoning in large language models by aggregating multiple sampled outputs, but it is computationally expensive because it requires many samples. There is a need for methods that retain or improve accuracy while drastically lowering the number of required samples.
AI.method: The paper proposes a hybrid ensembling approach that combines two complementary reasoning modes: Chain-of-Thought (natural language step-by-step explanations) and Program-of-Thought (code-based or programmatic reasoning). It defines a general framework for integrating these two reasoning types within self-consistency and presents specific strategies for both full sampling and early-stopping, where sampling can be halted once sufficient agreement is reached among samples.
AI.result: The CoT-PoT ensembling method increases reasoning accuracy compared to standard self-consistency while reducing the number of samples needed by a factor of about 9.3x. Most tasks (78.6%) can be solved using only two samples under this framework, which is significantly more efficient than prior self-consistency methods.
AI.conclusion: Hybrid CoT-PoT ensembling is a more sample-efficient and accurate form of self-consistency, demonstrating that combining different reasoning modalities allows large language models to reach high performance with far fewer sampled outputs than traditional approaches.

title: TPS-CalcBench: A Benchmark and Diagnostic Evaluation Framework for LLM Analytical Calculation Competence in Hypersonic Thermal Protection System Engineering
summary: Deploying LLMs as reasoning assistants in safety-critical aerospace engineering requires stricter evaluation criteria than general scientific benchmarks. In hypersonic thermal protection system (TPS) design, inaccurate stagnation-point heat flux or boundary-layer calculations may cause catastrophic design margin violations. Models with numerically reasonable but physically invalid answers are more dangerous than those declining to respond. Current scientific benchmarks only test abstract math and basic physics, evaluate final answers solely, ignore engineering reasoning processes, and cannot detect such critical failures. We propose TPS-CalcBench, the first diagnostic benchmark for closed-form analytical calculations in hypersonic aerodynamics and high-temperature gas dynamics that experienced TPS engineers conduct without simulations. Our contributions include domain-oriented task taxonomy with 4 difficulty levels and 8 categories from Anderson's textbook, dual-track evaluation measuring result accuracy and reasoning quality via an 8-dimension rubric and calibrated judge with human audit to identify right answer wrong reasoning issues, human-AI data pipeline producing 420 high-confidence core items and 810 noise-controlled pre-gating items from 4560 raw data, noise-sensitivity analysis measuring data quality impacts on model ranking, and three diagnostic intervention methods: DFA-TPS fine-tuning, RAG-EQ retrieval grounding and PA-CoT process-aware prompting. Tests on 13 models from 7 groups show wide performance differences (KPI 12.6-87.9), hidden formula selection defects, data-driven rank changes and effective intervention improvements, establishing a complete diagnose-evaluate-intervene framework for safety-critical engineering LLM deployment assessment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TPS-CalcBench, a specialized benchmark to rigorously evaluate LLMs’ analytical reasoning for hypersonic thermal protection system design, focusing on both numerical accuracy and physical reasoning quality, and demonstrates diagnostic and intervention methods to improve LLM reliability in safety-critical aerospace engineering.
AI.motivation: LLMs are being considered as reasoning assistants in safety-critical aerospace engineering, such as hypersonic thermal protection system (TPS) design, where errors in calculations like stagnation-point heat flux or boundary-layer properties can cause catastrophic design failures. Existing scientific benchmarks focus on abstract math and basic physics, evaluate only final answers, and ignore the detailed engineering reasoning process, so they cannot detect cases where models give numerically plausible but physically invalid solutions. There is a need for a domain-specific, process-aware benchmark that reflects real analytical tasks performed by experienced TPS engineers to ensure LLM deployment is safe and trustworthy in this high-risk context.
AI.method: The authors design TPS-CalcBench, a domain-oriented benchmark for closed-form analytical calculations in hypersonic aerodynamics and high-temperature gas dynamics, derived from Anderson’s textbook. They build a task taxonomy with 4 difficulty levels and 8 categories, and implement a dual-track evaluation: (1) quantitative result accuracy and (2) qualitative reasoning quality using an 8-dimension rubric, supported by a calibrated LLM judge and human audit to detect correct-answer-but-wrong-reasoning cases. They construct a human–AI data pipeline that filters 4560 raw problems into 420 high-confidence core items and 810 noise-controlled pre-gating items, and perform noise-sensitivity analysis to study how data quality affects model ranking. They further test three intervention strategies—domain-specific fine-tuning (DFA-TPS), retrieval-augmented equation grounding (RAG-EQ), and process-aware chain-of-thought prompting (PA-CoT)—on 13 LLMs from 7 provider groups.
AI.result: Across 13 models, the benchmark reveals large performance variation, with overall key performance indicators ranging from 12.6 to 87.9, and uncovers hidden weaknesses such as incorrect formula selection that are not evident from final-answer accuracy alone. Noise-sensitivity analysis shows that data quality can significantly shift model rankings. The proposed intervention techniques—DFA-TPS, RAG-EQ, and PA-CoT—demonstrate measurable improvements in both numerical accuracy and reasoning quality under the TPS-CalcBench evaluation framework.
AI.conclusion: TPS-CalcBench provides a comprehensive diagnose–evaluate–intervene framework for assessing and improving LLMs in safety-critical aerospace engineering calculations. By combining domain-structured tasks, dual-track evaluation of both answers and reasoning, controlled data curation, and targeted intervention strategies, the benchmark can expose subtle reasoning failures and guide the safe deployment and enhancement of LLM-based assistants in hypersonic TPS design and similar high-stakes engineering domains.

title: MoVE: Translating Laughter and Tears via Mixture of Vocalization Experts in Speech-to-Speech Translation
comment: Submitted to Interspeech. Audio Demo and Dataset: https://47zzz.github.io/MoVE/
summary: Recent Speech-to-Speech Translation (S2ST) systems achieve strong semantic accuracy yet consistently strip away non-verbal vocalizations (NVs), such as laughter and crying that convey pragmatic intent, which severely limits real-world utility. We address this via three contributions. First, we propose a synthesis pipeline for building scalable expressive datasets to overcome the data scarcity limitation. Second, we propose MoVE, a Mixture-of-LoRA-Experts architecture with expressive-specialized adapters and a soft-weighting router that blends experts for capturing hybrid expressive states. Third, we show pretrained AudioLLMs enable striking data efficiency: 30 minutes of curated data is enough for strong performance. On English-Chinese S2ST, while comparing with strong baselines, MoVE reproduces target NVs in 76% of cases and achieves the highest human-rated naturalness and emotional fidelity among all compared systems, where existing S2ST systems preserve at most 14% of NVs.

AI.tldr: They improve speech-to-speech translation so it can preserve non-verbal vocalizations (like laughter/crying) using a new data pipeline and a Mixture-of-LoRA-Experts model, achieving far better non-verbal preservation and naturalness.
AI.motivation: Current S2ST systems focus on semantic accuracy but drop non-verbal vocalizations (NVs) such as laughter or crying that convey important pragmatic and emotional information, limiting real-world usefulness. This is partly because expressive data is scarce and models aren’t designed to handle mixed emotional or expressive states.
AI.method: (1) Build a scalable synthesis pipeline to generate expressive datasets containing NVs, mitigating data scarcity. (2) Introduce MoVE, a Mixture-of-LoRA-Experts architecture where specialized LoRA adapters focus on different expressive aspects, and a soft-weighting router dynamically blends these experts to represent hybrid expressive states. (3) Leverage pretrained AudioLLMs to get strong expressive S2ST performance from as little as 30 minutes of curated expressive data.
AI.result: On English-Chinese S2ST, MoVE reproduces target non-verbal vocalizations in 76% of cases, compared to at most 14% for existing S2ST baselines. Human evaluations also rate MoVE highest in naturalness and emotional fidelity among all compared systems, demonstrating both quantitative and qualitative improvements.
AI.conclusion: Expressive S2ST with preserved NVs is feasible and data-efficient: combining a synthetic expressive data pipeline, a Mixture-of-LoRA-Experts design, and pretrained AudioLLMs yields large gains in NV preservation, naturalness, and emotional fidelity, even with only 30 minutes of curated data.

title: A Sugeno Integral View of Binarized Neural Network Inference
summary: In this article, we establish a precise connection between binarized neural networks (BNNs) and Sugeno integrals. The advantage of the Sugeno integral is that it provides a framework for representing the importance of inputs and their interactions, while being equivalent to a set of if-then rules. For a hidden BNN neuron at inference time, we show that the activation threshold test can be written as a Sugeno integral on binary inputs. This yields an explicit set-function representation of each neuron decision, and an associated rule-based representation. We also provide a Sugeno-integral expression for the last-layer score. Finally, we discuss how the same framework can be adapted to support richer input interactions and how it can be extended beyond the binary case induced by binarized neural networks.

AI.tldr: The paper connects binarized neural networks (BNNs) with Sugeno integrals, showing that BNN neuron decisions and final scores can be expressed as Sugeno integrals, enabling interpretable rule-based representations and extensions to richer interactions and non-binary settings.
AI.motivation: BNNs are efficient but hard to interpret. Sugeno integrals, coming from fuzzy measure theory, provide a way to model the importance of inputs and their interactions and are equivalent to sets of if-then rules. The authors want to bridge these two to make BNNs more interpretable and to leverage the structured nature of Sugeno integrals for understanding and extending BNN models.
AI.method: The authors analyze the inference-time behavior of hidden neurons in BNNs. They show mathematically that the activation threshold test for a hidden BNN neuron with binary inputs can be re-expressed as a Sugeno integral with respect to an appropriate set function. This gives each neuron an explicit set-function and rule-based representation. They then extend this analysis to show that the last-layer output score of the BNN can also be written as a Sugeno integral. Finally, they discuss generalizations of this framework to model richer interactions between inputs and to move beyond strictly binary inputs.
AI.result: They derive a formal equivalence between BNN hidden neuron threshold tests and Sugeno integrals on binary inputs, yielding an explicit set-function for each neuron and a corresponding rule-based representation. They also provide a Sugeno-integral formulation for the final-layer score of the BNN. Conceptually, they show that the BNN architecture can be viewed through the lens of Sugeno integrals, grounding it in a well-studied, interpretable framework and indicating a path toward more expressive, interaction-aware models.
AI.conclusion: BNNs can be precisely characterized using Sugeno integrals, which allows every neuron and the final-layer score to be interpreted as a set of if-then rules defined by a set function capturing input importance and interactions. This connection enhances interpretability and provides a structured framework to extend BNNs to more complex interactions and to non-binary settings, suggesting new directions for model design that combine the efficiency of BNNs with the expressiveness and transparency of Sugeno integrals.

title: Answer Only as Precisely as Justified: Calibrated Claim-Level Specificity Control for Agentic Systems
summary: Agentic systems often fail not by being entirely wrong, but by being too precise: a response may be generally useful while particular claims exceed what the evidence supports. We study this failure mode as overcommitment control and introduce compositional selective specificity (CSS), a post-generation layer that decomposes an answer into claims, proposes coarser backoffs, and emits each claim at the most specific calibrated level that appears admissible. The method is designed to express uncertainty as a local semantic backoff rather than as a whole-answer refusal. Across a full LongFact run and HotpotQA pilots, calibrated CSS improves the risk-utility trade-off of fixed drafts. On the full LongFact run, it raises overcommitment-aware utility from 0.846 to 0.913 relative to the no-CSS output while achieving 0.938 specificity retention. These results suggest that claim-level specificity control is a useful uncertainty interface for agentic systems and a target for future distribution-free validity layers.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a technique called compositional selective specificity (CSS) to reduce overconfident, overly specific claims in agentic system outputs while preserving as much useful detail as possible.
AI.motivation: Agentic AI systems often fail by making some claims that are too specific or not fully supported by evidence, even when their overall answer is mostly correct and useful. Current safety approaches often respond by refusing to answer or broadly hedging, which can sacrifice utility. The authors want a way to manage uncertainty at the level of individual claims, letting systems back off locally in specificity instead of discarding or heavily censoring whole answers.
AI.method: They introduce a post-generation control layer called compositional selective specificity (CSS). CSS decomposes a model’s drafted answer into separate claims, then for each claim proposes coarser, less specific alternative versions (backoffs). It then selects and emits each claim at the most specific level that appears well-calibrated and supported by evidence, effectively tuning specificity per-claim instead of for the answer as a whole. The approach is evaluated on LongFact and HotpotQA settings, comparing different calibration and backoff choices.
AI.result: On a full LongFact benchmark run, calibrated CSS substantially improves the balance between risk (overcommitment) and utility of fixed model drafts. It increases an overcommitment-aware utility score from 0.846 to 0.913 relative to answers without CSS, while keeping 93.8% of the original answer’s specificity. Pilot experiments on HotpotQA show similarly promising patterns, indicating that CSS can reduce harmful overcommitment with minimal loss of detail.
AI.conclusion: Claim-level specificity control via CSS is an effective way to express uncertainty through local semantic backoffs instead of whole-answer refusals. It improves the risk-utility trade-off of agentic system outputs while retaining most of their informational richness. The authors argue that such fine-grained specificity and uncertainty interfaces are a valuable direction for building future distribution-free validity layers and safer, more reliable agentic systems.

title: From Fallback to Frontline: When Can LLMs be Superior Annotators of Human Perspectives?
summary: Although large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used as annotators at scale, they are typically treated as a pragmatic fallback rather than a faithful estimator of human perspectives. This work challenges that presumption. By framing perspective-taking as the estimation of a latent group-level judgment, we characterize the conditions under which modern LLMs can outperform human annotators, including in-group humans, when predicting aggregate subgroup opinions on subjective tasks, and show that these conditions are common in practice. This advantage arises from structural properties of LLMs as estimators, including low variance and reduced coupling between representation and processing biases, rather than any claim of lived experience. Our analysis identifies clear regimes where LLMs act as statistically superior frontline estimators, as well as principled limits where human judgment remains essential. These findings reposition LLMs from a cost-saving compromise to a principled tool for estimating collective human perspectives.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that modern large language models (LLMs) can, under common real-world conditions, estimate aggregate human group opinions on subjective tasks more accurately than human annotators, and identifies when this works and when human judgment is still needed.
AI.motivation: LLMs are widely used as cheap, scalable annotators, but they are usually viewed as an imperfect substitute for human judgments, especially on subjective or perspective-based tasks. There is a lack of principled understanding of whether, when, and why LLMs can validly stand in for human groups in estimating collective perspectives, beyond cost and convenience considerations. The authors want to challenge the assumption that humans are always the gold standard and instead rigorously compare LLMs and humans as estimators of group-level latent opinions.
AI.method: The authors formalize perspective-taking as estimating a latent group-level judgment (e.g., the aggregate opinion of a demographic subgroup). They analyze LLMs and human annotators within a statistical estimation framework, focusing on variance, bias, and how representation biases interact with processing. They then empirically study conditions under which LLMs can better predict aggregate subgroup opinions than both out-group and in-group human annotators on subjective labeling tasks, and delineate the structural properties of LLMs that yield these advantages.
AI.result: They find that modern LLMs often outperform individual human annotators, including members of the target subgroup, at predicting the aggregate opinions of that subgroup on subjective tasks. This superiority arises not from the model having lived experience but from desirable estimator properties: comparatively low variance and weaker coupling between who/what is represented in the data and how the model processes information. They characterize practical regimes where this advantage is robust and highlight cases where it breaks down.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that LLMs should not just be seen as a low-cost replacement for human annotation, but as statistically principled tools for estimating collective human perspectives. It specifies regimes where LLMs can serve as superior frontline estimators of group-level judgments and clarifies the principled boundaries where human involvement is still crucial, thus providing guidance on how to responsibly integrate LLM-based and human-based annotation in practice.

title: AIT Academy: Cultivating the Complete Agent with a Confucian Three-Domain Curriculum
summary:
What does it mean to give an AI agent a complete education? Current agent development produces specialists systems optimized for a single capability dimension, whether tool use, code generation, or security awareness that exhibit predictable deficits wherever they were not trained. We argue this pattern reflects a structural absence: there is no curriculum theory for agents, no principled account of what a fully developed agent should know, be, and be able to do across the full scope of intelligent behavior.
  This paper introduces the AIT Academy (Agents Institute of Technology Academy), a curriculum framework for cultivating AI agents across the tripartite structure of human knowledge. Grounded in Kagan's Three Cultures and UNESCO ISCED-F 2013, AIT organizes agent capability development into three domains: Natural Science and Technical Reasoning (Domain I), Humanities and Creative Expression (Domain II), and Social Science and Ethical Reasoning (Domain III). The Confucian Six Arts (liuyi) a 2,500-year-old holistic education system are reinterpreted as behavioral archetypes that map directly onto trainable agent capabilities within each domain.
  Three representative training grounds instantiate the framework across multiple backbone LLMs: the ClawdGO Security Dojo (Domain I), Athen's Academy (Domain II), and the Alt Mirage Stage (Domain III). Experiments demonstrate a 15.9-point improvement in security capability scores under weakest-first curriculum scheduling, and a 7-percentage-point gain in social reasoning performance under principled attribution modeling. A cross-domain finding Security Awareness Calibration Pathology (SACP), in which over-trained Domain I agents fail on out-of-distribution evaluation illustrates the diagnostic value of a multi-domain perspective unavailable to any single-domain framework.

AI.tldr: Proposes a structured, multi-domain curriculum framework (AIT Academy) for training more ‘fully educated’ AI agents, inspired by human education systems, and shows it improves capabilities and reveals cross-domain pathologies.
AI.motivation: Current AI agents are usually trained as narrow specialists (e.g., for tool use, coding, or security) and perform poorly outside their training focus. There is no general curriculum theory specifying what a well-rounded agent should know or how its capabilities should be systematically developed across the full range of intelligent behavior. The authors aim to fill this gap by defining a principled, comprehensive educational framework for agents.
AI.method: They design the AIT Academy curriculum framework, structuring agent education into three domains of human knowledge based on Kagan’s Three Cultures and UNESCO ISCED-F 2013: (I) Natural Science and Technical Reasoning, (II) Humanities and Creative Expression, and (III) Social Science and Ethical Reasoning. They reinterpret the Confucian Six Arts as behavioral archetypes mapping to concrete, trainable capabilities inside each domain. They implement three training grounds tied to these domains (ClawdGO Security Dojo, Athen’s Academy, Alt Mirage Stage) and train multiple backbone LLM-based agents using different curriculum strategies, including weakest-first scheduling and principled attribution modeling. They then empirically evaluate domain-specific and cross-domain performance, including out-of-distribution tests.
AI.result: Using the AIT framework, they observe a 15.9-point improvement in security capability scores when applying a weakest-first curriculum schedule in Domain I, and a 7-percentage-point improvement in social reasoning tasks with principled attribution modeling in Domain III. They also identify a cross-domain phenomenon they call Security Awareness Calibration Pathology (SACP), where agents over-trained in security (Domain I) perform poorly on out-of-distribution evaluations, revealing miscalibration and the dangers of over-specialization.
AI.conclusion: A structured, multi-domain curriculum framework inspired by human education theory can systematically cultivate more well-rounded AI agents, improve specific capabilities (security and social reasoning), and expose important cross-domain pathologies like SACP that single-domain training would miss. The work suggests that future agent development should be organized around principled, holistic curricula rather than ad hoc, domain-isolated specialization.

title: SELF-EMO: Emotional Self-Evolution from Recognition to Consistent Expression
summary: Emotion Recognition in Conversation (ERC) has become a fundamental capability for large language models (LLMs) in human-centric interaction. Beyond accurate recognition, coherent emotional expression is also crucial, yet both are limited by the scarcity and static nature of high-quality annotated data. In this work, we propose SELF-EMO, a self-evolution framework grounded in the hypothesis that better emotion prediction leads to more consistent emotional responses. We introduce two auxiliary tasks, emotional understanding and emotional expression, and design a role-based self-play paradigm where the model acts as both an emotion recognizer and a dialogue responder. Through iterative interactions, the model generates diverse conversational trajectories, enabling scalable data generation. To ensure quality, we adopt a data flywheel mechanism that filters candidate predictions and responses using a smoothed IoU-based reward and feeds selected samples back for continuous self-improvement without external supervision. We further develop SELF-GRPO, a reinforcement learning algorithm that stabilizes optimization with multi-label alignment rewards and group-level consistency signals. Experiments on IEMOCAP, MELD, and EmoryNLP show that SELF-EMO achieves state-of-the-art performance, improving accuracy by +6.33% on Qwen3-4B and +8.54% on Qwen3-8B, demonstrating strong effectiveness and generalization.

AI.tldr: SELF-EMO is a self-evolution framework that uses role-based self-play, auxiliary emotional understanding/expression tasks, and a custom RL algorithm (SELF-GRPO) to improve emotion recognition in conversation for LLMs, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Emotion recognition in conversation is important for human-centric LLM interactions, but progress is limited by scarce, static annotated datasets and by the need not only to recognize but also to express emotions coherently. The paper seeks a way to scale high-quality supervision and improve both recognition accuracy and emotional response quality without heavy manual labeling.
AI.method: The authors propose SELF-EMO, where an LLM simultaneously plays two roles: emotion recognizer and dialogue responder, in a role-based self-play setup. They define two auxiliary tasks (emotional understanding and emotional expression) and let the model iteratively generate diverse conversational trajectories as synthetic training data. A data flywheel mechanism filters generated samples via a smoothed IoU-based reward, keeping only high-quality predictions/responses which are then reused for further training. To optimize the model, they introduce SELF-GRPO, a reinforcement learning algorithm that uses multi-label alignment rewards and group-level consistency signals to stabilize training and align emotion predictions with responses.
AI.result: On three ERC benchmarks (IEMOCAP, MELD, EmoryNLP), SELF-EMO delivers state-of-the-art results, with notable accuracy gains of +6.33% on Qwen3-4B and +8.54% on Qwen3-8B models, indicating effective and generalizable improvements in conversational emotion recognition.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that self-evolution via role-based self-play and carefully designed RL signals can substantially enhance both emotion recognition and emotional response generation in LLMs without relying on additional external annotations, offering a scalable path toward more emotionally intelligent conversational agents.

title: OPSDL: On-Policy Self-Distillation for Long-Context Language Models
summary: Extending the effective context length of large language models (LLMs) remains a central challenge for real-world applications. While recent post-training methods have made progress in long-context scaling, they either rely on high-quality supervision data or sparse sequence-level rewards, leading to unstable and inefficient optimization. We propose OPSDL, an On-Policy Self-Distillation method for enhancing the Long-context capabilities of LLMs. Unlike other recent self-distillation methods that inject privileged information and rely on the model's in-context learning ability to act as a teacher, OPSDL leverages the model's own inherently strong short-context capability as a self-teacher to supervise its own generation in long-context scenarios. The model first generates responses conditioned on the full long-context, then the self-teacher provides per-token supervision signals via point-wise reverse KL divergence under the relevant extracted short-context. This dense token-level signal encourages faithful use of relevant evidence and mitigates hallucinations induced by irrelevant context. We evaluate OPSDL on long-context benchmarks across a range of models from 7B to 32B parameters. Results show consistent and substantial improvements across varying context lengths, outperforming standard post-training approaches such as SFT and DPO with higher sample efficiency. Notably, these gains are achieved without degrading general short-context performance. These findings highlight the effectiveness of OPSDL as a scalable and stable approach for long-context learning.

AI.tldr: OPSDL is an on-policy self-distillation method that uses a model’s own strong short-context abilities to supervise and improve its performance on long-context tasks via dense token-level signals.
AI.motivation: Large language models struggle to effectively use very long contexts, and existing long-context post-training methods either need expensive high-quality supervision data or rely on sparse sequence-level rewards that cause unstable, inefficient training. A more data- and sample-efficient, stable method is needed to scale long-context capabilities without hurting short-context performance.
AI.method: OPSDL (On-Policy Self-Distillation for Long-context) trains an LLM to better handle long contexts by using the same model as a self-teacher based on its short-context strengths. For a given long context, the model first generates a response conditioned on the full context (on-policy generation). Then, relevant short subcontexts are extracted, and the model re-processes them to provide per-token supervision: the student’s token distribution under the long context is aligned with the teacher’s distribution under the short, relevant context using point-wise reverse KL divergence. This creates dense token-level learning signals that guide the model to rely on relevant evidence and avoid being distracted by irrelevant portions of the long context.
AI.result: Across multiple long-context benchmarks and models ranging from 7B to 32B parameters, OPSDL yields consistent, substantial gains in long-context performance. It also shows better sample efficiency than standard post-training baselines such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and direct preference optimization (DPO). Importantly, these improvements in long-context ability do not degrade the models’ performance on standard short-context tasks.
AI.conclusion: OPSDL provides a scalable, stable, and sample-efficient way to extend the effective context length of LLMs by leveraging their own short-context competence as supervision. Dense token-level reverse-KL self-distillation encourages faithful use of relevant information in long documents and mitigates hallucinations from irrelevant context, improving long-context capabilities without sacrificing short-context performance.

title: The Topological Dual of a Dataset: A Logic-to-Topology Encoding for AlphaGeometry-Style Data
summary: AlphaGeometry represents a milestone in neuro-symbolic reasoning, yet its architecture faces a log-linear scaling bottleneck within its symbolic deduction engine that limits its efficiency as problem complexity increases. Recent technical reports suggest that current domain-specific languages may be isomorphic as input representations to natural language, interchanging them acts as a performance-invariant transformation, implying that current neural guidance relies on superficial encodings rather than structural understanding. This paper addresses this representation bottleneck by proposing a logic-to-topology encoding designed to reveal the structural invariants of a model's latent space under a transformation of its input space. By leveraging the Logic of Observation, we utilize the duality between provability in observable theories and topologies to propose a logic-to-topology encoder for the input space. We introduce the concept of the "topological dual of a dataset", a transformation that bridges formal logic, topology, and neural processing. This framework serves as a Rosetta Stone for neuro-symbolic AI, providing a principled pathway for the mechanistic interpretability of how models navigate complex discovery paths.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new way to encode logical information as topological structures to overcome representation and scaling bottlenecks in neuro-symbolic systems like AlphaGeometry, enabling better structural understanding and interpretability.
AI.motivation: Neuro-symbolic systems such as AlphaGeometry are powerful but face a log-linear scaling bottleneck in their symbolic deduction engines, making them inefficient for complex problems. Additionally, evidence suggests that common domain-specific languages used as input are effectively equivalent to natural language for current neural models, meaning that switching between them doesn’t change performance. This hints that current neural guidance depends on superficial token-level encodings rather than genuine structural understanding of the underlying logic. The paper is motivated by the need to escape this representation bottleneck and expose structural invariants that models exploit when reasoning, to improve both performance and mechanistic interpretability.
AI.method: The paper proposes a logic-to-topology encoding framework grounded in the Logic of Observation. It exploits the duality between provability in observable theories and topological spaces to construct an encoder that maps logical input representations to topological structures. The key construct is the "topological dual of a dataset," which transforms datasets expressed in formal logic into topological spaces that reflect the structure of proofs and observables. This encoding is then intended to be used as the input space for neural components in neuro-symbolic models, so that the model’s latent space geometry aligns with meaningful logical/topological invariants.
AI.result: Conceptually, the paper introduces and formalizes the notion of a "topological dual of a dataset" and describes a logic-to-topology encoder leveraging the Logic of Observation. It shows how this encoder can, in principle, reveal structural invariants in the model’s latent space under transformations of the input representation, thereby decoupling performance from purely syntactic encodings like DSLs or natural language. While the abstract does not detail empirical benchmarks, the main result is a theoretical framework and encoding scheme that connects formal logic, topology, and neural processing for neuro-symbolic reasoning tasks such as those in AlphaGeometry.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that encoding logical information as topological structures via the Logic of Observation offers a principled way to overcome representation bottlenecks in neuro-symbolic AI. By providing a logic-to-topology encoder and the notion of a dataset’s topological dual, it positions this framework as a kind of Rosetta Stone that unifies formal logic, topology, and neural network representations. This unification aims to enable mechanistic interpretability of how models traverse complex reasoning and discovery paths, and to move beyond superficial input encodings toward deeper structural understanding.

title: PoliLegalLM: A Technical Report on a Large Language Model for Political and Legal Affairs
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved remarkable success in general-domain tasks, yet their direct application to the legal domain remains challenging due to hallucinated legal citations, incomplete knowledge coverage, and weak structured reasoning. To address these issues, we propose PoliLegalLM, a domain-specific large language model tailored for political and legal applications. Our approach adopts a unified training framework that integrates continued pretraining, progressive supervised fine-tuning, and preference-based reinforcement learning to jointly enhance legal knowledge grounding, task alignment, and reasoning capability. We construct a large-scale, high-quality legal corpus and design a structured post-training pipeline, enabling the model to effectively learn domain-specific knowledge and adapt to diverse legal tasks. We evaluate PoliLegalLM on three representative benchmarks, including LawBench, LexEval, and a real-world dataset, PoliLegal. Experimental results demonstrate that PoliLegalLM achieves strong and consistent performance, outperforming competitive models of similar scale and remaining highly competitive with significantly larger models, while achieving the best results on real-world legal scenarios. These results highlight the effectiveness of our training paradigm and the practical value of domain-specific LLMs for real-world legal applications.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PoliLegalLM, a political‑legal domain large language model that combines continued pretraining, supervised fine‑tuning, and preference-based RL on a curated legal corpus, achieving state-of-the-art performance on several legal benchmarks and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLMs perform poorly on legal tasks because they hallucinate citations, lack comprehensive legal knowledge, and struggle with structured legal reasoning. The authors are motivated to build a domain-specialized model that is more accurate, grounded, and reliable for practical political and legal applications.
AI.method: They construct a large-scale, high-quality legal corpus and adopt a unified training framework: (1) continued pretraining on legal text to inject domain knowledge, (2) progressive supervised fine-tuning on diverse legal tasks to align the model with task formats and reasoning patterns, and (3) preference-based reinforcement learning to further refine task alignment and reasoning quality. They then evaluate the resulting model, PoliLegalLM, on legal benchmarks and a real-world dataset.
AI.result: PoliLegalLM demonstrates strong and consistent performance on three benchmarks: LawBench, LexEval, and PoliLegal (a real-world dataset). It outperforms competitive models of a similar parameter scale and remains competitive with much larger models, achieving the best results on the real-world PoliLegal tasks.
AI.conclusion: The unified training paradigm—continued domain pretraining plus structured post-training with SFT and preference-based RL—effectively improves legal grounding and reasoning. This shows that domain-specific LLMs like PoliLegalLM can deliver high practical value and state-of-the-art performance for real-world legal applications, supporting the case for specialized models over purely general-purpose ones.

title: Understanding Human Actions through the Lens of Executable Models
summary: Human-centred systems require an understanding of human actions in the physical world. Temporally extended sequences of actions are intentional and structured, yet existing methods for recognising what actions are performed often do not attempt to capture their structure, particularly how the actions are executed. This, however, is crucial for assessing the quality of the action's execution and its differences from other actions. To capture the internal mechanics of actions, we introduce a domain-specific language EXACT that represents human motions as underspecified motion programs, interpreted as reward-generating functions for zero-shot policy inference using forward-backwards representations. By leveraging the compositional nature of EXACT motion programs, we combine individual policies into an executable neuro-symbolic model that uses program structure for compositional modelling. We evaluate the utility of the proposed pipeline for creating executable action models by analysing motion-capture data to understand human actions, for the tasks of human action segmentation and action anomaly detection. Our results show that the use of executable action models improves data efficiency and captures intuitive relationships between actions compared with monolithic, task-specific approaches.

AI.tldr: They propose a domain-specific language (EXACT) to model human motions as composable motion programs for better understanding, segmenting, and detecting anomalies in human action sequences.
AI.motivation: Existing human action recognition methods often classify what action is performed but fail to capture how it is executed, i.e., the internal structure and quality of motion, which is important for tasks like assessing execution quality, distinguishing similar actions, and understanding relationships between actions.
AI.method: Introduce a domain-specific language called EXACT that encodes human motions as underspecified, compositional motion programs. These programs are interpreted as reward-generating functions enabling zero-shot policy inference via forward-backwards representations. By composing individual policies derived from these programs, they build an executable neuro-symbolic model of actions and then apply this pipeline to motion-capture data.
AI.result: On tasks of human action segmentation and action anomaly detection with motion-capture data, the executable action models created using EXACT improve data efficiency and better capture intuitive inter-action relationships compared to standard monolithic, task-specific baselines.
AI.conclusion: Executable, compositional action models expressed in the EXACT language allow human actions to be represented as motion programs whose structure can be exploited for more data-efficient, interpretable, and generalisable understanding of human motion, outperforming monolithic approaches for segmentation and anomaly detection.

title: MAPLE: A Meta-learning Framework for Cross-Prompt Essay Scoring
summary: Automated Essay Scoring (AES) faces significant challenges in cross-prompt settings, where models must generalize to unseen writing prompts. To address this limitation, we propose MAPLE, a meta-learning framework that leverages prototypical networks to learn transferable representations across different writing prompts. Across three diverse datasets (ELLIPSE and ASAP (English), and LAILA (Arabic)), MAPLE achieves state-of-the-art performance on ELLIPSE and LAILA, outperforming strong baselines by 8.5 and 3 points in QWK, respectively. On ASAP, where prompts exhibit heterogeneous score ranges, MAPLE yields improvements on several traits, highlighting the strengths of our approach in unified scoring settings. Overall, our results demonstrate the potential of meta-learning for building robust cross-prompt AES systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MAPLE, a meta-learning framework using prototypical networks to improve cross-prompt automated essay scoring, achieving state-of-the-art results on multiple English and Arabic datasets.
AI.motivation: Existing Automated Essay Scoring models struggle to generalize to new, unseen prompts, which is crucial for scalable, practical deployment, especially across different languages and scoring schemes. The authors aim to overcome poor cross-prompt transfer and build more robust and unified scoring systems.
AI.method: They propose MAPLE, a meta-learning framework that employs prototypical networks to learn prompt-invariant, transferable representations of essays. By treating different prompts as separate tasks within a meta-learning setup, MAPLE learns prototypes for scoring and adapts to unseen prompts more effectively than standard supervised baselines.
AI.result: On three datasets—ELLIPSE and ASAP (English) and LAILA (Arabic)—MAPLE attains state-of-the-art performance on ELLIPSE and LAILA, outperforming strong baselines by 8.5 and 3 QWK points, respectively. On ASAP, with its heterogeneous score ranges, MAPLE improves performance on multiple traits, though not uniformly across all of them.
AI.conclusion: Meta-learning with prototypical networks is a promising strategy for cross-prompt AES, enabling better generalization to unseen prompts, competitive or superior performance across languages, and stronger performance in unified scoring scenarios, thereby advancing robust, practical AES systems.

title: Architectural Design Decisions in AI Agent Harnesses
summary: AI agent systems increasingly rely on reusable non-LLM engineering infrastructure that packages tool mediation, context handling, delegation, safety control, and orchestration. Yet the architectural design decisions in this surrounding infrastructure remain understudied. This paper presents a protocol-guided, source-grounded empirical study of 70 publicly available agent-system projects, addressing three questions: which design-decision dimensions recur across projects, which co-occurrences structure those decisions, and which typical architectural patterns emerge. Methodologically, we contribute a transparent investigation procedure for analyzing heterogeneous agent-system corpora through source-code and technical-material reading. Empirically, we identify five recurring design dimensions (subagent architecture, context management, tool systems, safety mechanisms, and orchestration) and find that the corpus favors file-persistent, hybrid, and hierarchical context strategies; registry-oriented tool systems remain dominant while MCP- and plugin-oriented extensions are emerging; and intermediate isolation is common but high-assurance audit is rare. Cross-project co-occurrence analysis reveals that deeper coordination pairs with more explicit context services, stronger execution environments with more structured governance, and formalized tool-registration boundaries with broader ecosystem ambitions. We synthesize five recurring architectural patterns spanning lightweight tools, balanced CLI frameworks, multi-agent orchestrators, enterprise systems, and scenario-verticalized projects. The result provides an evidence-based account of architectural regularities in agent-system engineering, with grounded guidance for framework designers, selectors, and researchers.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically studies 70 open-source AI agent system projects to identify common architectural design dimensions and recurring patterns in their non-LLM infrastructure.
AI.motivation: AI agent systems increasingly use reusable infrastructure around LLMs—handling tools, context, orchestration, delegation, and safety—but the architectural choices in this layer are poorly understood and undocumented. There is a need for systematic, evidence-based insight to guide framework design, selection, and research.
AI.method: The authors conduct a protocol-guided, source-grounded empirical analysis of 70 publicly available agent-system projects. They read and code source code and technical materials to extract design decisions, then analyze recurring dimensions, co-occurrence structures, and emergent architectural patterns across the corpus.
AI.result: They identify five key recurring design dimensions: (1) subagent architecture, (2) context management, (3) tool systems, (4) safety mechanisms, and (5) orchestration. They find that file-persistent, hybrid, and hierarchical context strategies are common; registry-oriented tool systems dominate while MCP- and plugin-based approaches are emerging; intermediate isolation is typical but high-assurance safety audits are rare. Co-occurrence analysis shows, for example, that deeper coordination correlates with more explicit context services, stronger execution environments correlate with more structured governance, and formalized tool-registration boundaries correlate with broader ecosystem aims. They also synthesize five recurring architectural patterns ranging from lightweight tool wrappers to enterprise and scenario-specialized systems.
AI.conclusion: Non-LLM infrastructure in agent systems exhibits clear architectural regularities along five design dimensions, with characteristic co-occurrence structures and patterns. These findings offer grounded, empirically based guidance for designers and users of agent frameworks and suggest a methodological template for analyzing heterogeneous agent-system corpora in future research.

title: Beyond Fine-Tuning: In-Context Learning and Chain-of-Thought for Reasoned Distractor Generation
summary: Distractor generation (DG) remains a labor-intensive task that still significantly depends on domain experts. The task focuses on generating plausible yet incorrect options, known as distractors, for multiple-choice questions. A reliable distractor must be contextually relevant to the question and able to mislead examinees through implicit reasoning when identifying the correct answer. While a recent method integrates fine-tuning pre-trained encoder-decoder models with contrastive learning to generate semantically relevant distractors for a given question-answer, it often fails to capture the underlying reasoning process that experts utilize when selecting distractors in benchmarks. In this paper, we explore large language models (LLMs) reasoning for DG through in-context learning with unsupervised semantic retrieval for selecting few-shot examples. We design a rationale-augmented DG framework that jointly generates distractors and their rationales for a given question-answer. Extensive experiments on six benchmarks, with varying average distractor lengths and domains, demonstrate that prompting LLMs with few-shot examples substantially improves the performance compared to recent DG models. It outperforms recent approaches and achieves state-of-the-art results in generating reasoned distractors that align with human-labeled benchmarks.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a rationale-augmented large language model framework for generating high-quality, reasoning-based distractors for multiple-choice questions via in-context learning and semantic retrieval, achieving state-of-the-art results across six benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Distractor generation for multiple-choice questions is still largely manual and expert-dependent. Existing neural methods, even those using contrastive learning and encoder-decoder models, tend to produce semantically related but shallow distractors that do not reflect the deeper reasoning processes human experts use. There is a need for an automated method that can generate contextually relevant, misleading, and reasoning-aware distractors that match or surpass expert-created benchmarks across domains and varying distractor lengths.
AI.method: The authors leverage large language models with in-context learning, combined with an unsupervised semantic retrieval module to select the most relevant few-shot examples for each input question-answer pair. They propose a rationale-augmented distractor generation framework in which the LLM is prompted not only to generate distractor options but also to output explicit rationales explaining why each distractor is plausible yet incorrect. This joint generation of distractors and their rationales is guided by carefully designed prompts containing retrieved, semantically similar QA-distractor-rationale examples.
AI.result: Across six distractor generation benchmarks that differ in domain and typical distractor length, the proposed framework significantly improves over prior DG models, including contrastive-learning-based encoder-decoders. The few-shot LLM prompting setting yields higher-quality, more contextually appropriate, and more misleading distractors whose rationales better align with human reasoning, leading to state-of-the-art performance on automatic metrics and human evaluations against human-labeled benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Reasoning-aware distractor generation can be effectively achieved by prompting large language models with semantically retrieved few-shot examples and asking them to jointly produce distractors and their rationales. This approach reduces dependence on domain experts, better captures the implicit reasoning used in human-created distractors, and establishes new state-of-the-art performance across multiple DG benchmarks, suggesting that LLM-based, rationale-augmented frameworks are a promising direction for automated assessment item generation.

title: DSAINet: An Efficient Dual-Scale Attentive Interaction Network for General EEG Decoding
summary: In real-world applications of noninvasive electroencephalography (EEG), specialized decoders often show limited generalizability across diverse tasks under subject-independent settings. One central challenge is that task-relevant EEG signals often follow different temporal organization patterns across tasks, while many existing methods rely on task-tailored architectural designs that introduce task-specific temporal inductive biases. This mismatch makes it difficult to adapt temporal modeling across tasks without changing the model configuration. To address these challenges, we propose DSAINet, an efficient dual-scale attentive interaction network for general EEG decoding. Specifically, DSAINet constructs shared spatiotemporal token representations from raw EEG signals and models diverse temporal dynamics through parallel convolutional branches at fine and coarse scales. The resulting representations are then adaptively refined by intra-branch attention to emphasize salient scale-specific patterns and by inter-branch attention to integrate task-relevant features across scales, followed by adaptive token aggregation to yield a compact representation for prediction. Extensive experiments on five downstream EEG decoding tasks across ten public datasets show that DSAINet consistently outperforms 13 representative baselines under strict subject-independent evaluation. Notably, this performance is achieved using the same architecture hyperparameters across datasets. Moreover, DSAINet achieves a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off with only about 77K trainable parameters and provides interpretable neurophysiological insights. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/zy0929/DSAINet.

AI.tldr: They propose DSAINet, a compact dual‑scale attention network that generalizes well across many EEG tasks and subjects using a single architecture.
AI.motivation: Existing EEG decoders are usually tailored to specific tasks and rely on task-specific temporal inductive biases. EEG signals, however, show different temporal organization patterns across tasks, so architectures that are optimized for one task generalize poorly to others, especially in subject-independent settings. There is a need for a single, efficient, interpretable EEG model that can flexibly capture diverse temporal dynamics across multiple tasks and datasets without changing its configuration.
AI.method: They design DSAINet, a dual-scale attentive interaction network. First, it constructs shared spatiotemporal token representations directly from raw EEG signals. Then it passes these tokens through parallel convolutional branches that operate at fine and coarse temporal scales to model diverse temporal dynamics. Within each branch, intra-branch attention refines representations by emphasizing salient, scale-specific patterns. Between branches, inter-branch attention fuses task-relevant information across temporal scales. Finally, an adaptive token aggregation module compresses the refined tokens into a compact representation used for prediction. The network is lightweight, with around 77K trainable parameters, and is trained and evaluated under strict subject-independent protocols.
AI.result: On five different EEG decoding tasks spanning ten public datasets, DSAINet consistently outperforms 13 strong baseline methods under strict subject-independent evaluation. It maintains the same architecture and hyperparameters across all datasets, yet still achieves superior accuracy. The model is also computationally efficient, achieving a favorable accuracy-efficiency trade-off with only about 77K parameters, and produces interpretable patterns that align with known neurophysiological findings.
AI.conclusion: A single, compact dual-scale attention architecture (DSAINet) can effectively generalize across diverse EEG decoding tasks and subjects without task-specific reconfiguration. By combining multi-scale temporal convolutions with intra- and inter-branch attentions plus adaptive token aggregation, the method flexibly captures heterogeneous temporal dynamics, improves subject-independent performance over many baselines, and remains efficient and interpretable. The public release of code facilitates adoption and further research on general EEG decoders.

title: Agents Explore but Agents Ignore: LLMs Lack Environmental Curiosity
summary: LLM-based agents are assumed to integrate environmental observations into their reasoning: discovering highly relevant but unexpected information should naturally lead to a model exploiting its own discoveries. We show that this assumption is false for current LLM-based agents, which struggle to reflect or react to unexpected information. Across three benchmarks (Terminal-Bench, SWE-Bench, AppWorld), we inject complete task solutions into the agent environments to deliberately expose a task's solution to a model. While agents discover these solutions on Terminal-Bench in 79-81% of runs, they interact, or exploit, them in only 37-50% of cases. This gap is starkest in AppWorld: agents see documentation stating that a command "returns the complete solution to this task" in over 90% of attempts but exploit this in fewer than 7% of trials. We show that agents lack what we call environmental curiosity: the capability to recognize and investigate unexpected but relevant observations in response to environmental stimuli. We identify three main factors influencing environmental curiosity: available tools in the agent scaffold, test-time compute, and training data distribution. Our findings identify configurations that maximize curiosity also achieve the best performance on the unmodified benchmarks. Yet even jointly optimized agents still ignore discovered solutions in the majority of trials: current agents use the environment to fetch expected information, but not to revise their strategy or maximally exploit useful stimuli.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that current LLM-based agents often fail to exploit complete task solutions that are explicitly exposed in their environment, revealing a lack of what the authors call 'environmental curiosity.'
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents are widely assumed to naturally integrate new, unexpected but relevant information from their environment into their reasoning, adjusting plans as they discover helpful clues. However, this assumption had not been stress-tested: it was unclear whether agents would actually recognize and exploit such unexpectedly provided solutions instead of rigidly following initial plans. This gap in understanding agent behavior in dynamic environments motivates the paper.
