AI.method: The authors design interventions on three established benchmarks (Terminal-Bench, SWE-Bench, AppWorld) where they deliberately inject complete task solutions or highly informative hints directly into the agent’s environment. They then measure two stages: (1) discovery—whether the agent ever encounters the injected solution; and (2) exploitation—whether it uses this discovered information to solve the task. They also manipulate three factors to study their effect on 'environmental curiosity': the toolset available in the agent scaffold, the amount of test-time compute (e.g., steps, calls), and aspects related to the training data distribution. They compare different agent configurations and quantify how often solutions are noticed and used.
AI.result: On Terminal-Bench, agents discover injected solutions in 79–81% of runs but exploit them in only 37–50% of cases, showing a large drop between seeing and using the information. On AppWorld, the discrepancy is even more dramatic: agents encounter documentation that explicitly states a command “returns the complete solution to this task” in over 90% of runs but actually exploit this in fewer than 7% of trials. Similar patterns appear on SWE-Bench. The authors find that tool availability, greater test-time compute, and more favorable training distributions all increase environmental curiosity, and that agent setups with higher curiosity also achieve better scores on the original, unmodified benchmarks. Nonetheless, even under jointly optimized settings, agents still ignore discovered solutions in most runs.
AI.conclusion: Current LLM-based agents tend to use their environments primarily to retrieve information that fits their initial expectations, rather than to revise strategies or leverage surprising but useful discoveries. They lack robust 'environmental curiosity'—the ability to recognize, investigate, and fully exploit unexpected but relevant observations. Although modifying scaffolds, compute, and training can improve curiosity and overall performance, these changes are insufficient to close the gap: agents frequently discover but fail to act on complete solutions. Future agent designs must directly target mechanisms for noticing and re-planning around unexpected information, rather than assuming such behavior emerges automatically from large language modeling alone.

title: Stability Implies Redundancy: Delta Attention Selective Halting for Efficient Long-Context Prefilling
summary: Prefilling computational costs pose a significant bottleneck for Large Language Models (LLMs) and Large Multimodal Models (LMMs) in long-context settings. While token pruning reduces sequence length, prior methods rely on heuristics that break compatibility with hardware-efficient kernels like FlashAttention. In this work, we observe that tokens evolve toward \textit{semantic fixing points}, making further processing redundant. To this end, we introduce Delta Attention Selective Halting (DASH), a training-free policy that monitors the layer-wise update dynamics of the self-attention mechanism to selectively halt stabilized tokens. Extensive evaluation confirms that DASH generalizes across language and vision benchmarks, delivering significant prefill speedups while preserving model accuracy and hardware efficiency. Code will be released at https://github.com/verach3n/DASH.git.

AI.tldr: They propose DASH, a method to stop processing tokens that have converged, speeding up LLM/LMM prefilling without retraining or breaking FlashAttention compatibility.
AI.motivation: Long-context LLMs and LMMs are computationally expensive in the prefill phase, and existing token pruning methods either require training changes or are incompatible with optimized attention kernels like FlashAttention.
AI.method: Observe that token representations tend to converge to semantic fixed points across layers. Design Delta Attention Selective Halting (DASH), a training-free policy that tracks layer-wise changes in self-attention outputs and halts computation for tokens whose updates fall below a stability threshold, in a way that preserves hardware-efficient attention kernels.
AI.result: Across various language and vision benchmarks, DASH produces substantial prefill speedups while maintaining accuracy and compatibility with FlashAttention-style kernels.
AI.conclusion: Monitoring self-attention dynamics to selectively halt stabilized tokens is an effective, training-free approach to reduce prefill costs for long-context LLMs/LMMs without sacrificing accuracy or hardware efficiency.

title: Does Welsh media need a review? Detecting bias in Nation.Cymru's political reporting
summary: Wales' political landscape has been marked by growing accusations of bias in Welsh media. This paper takes the first computational step toward testing those claims by examining Nation.Cymru, a prominent Welsh political news outlet. I use a two-stage natural language processing (NLP) pipeline: (1) a robustly optimized BERT approach (RoBERTa) bias detector for efficient bias discovery and (2) a large language model (LLM) for target-attributed sentiment classification of bias labels from (1). A primary analysis of 15,583 party mentions across 2022-2026 news articles finds that Reform UK attracts biased framing at twice the rate of Plaid Cymru and over three times as negative in mean sentiment (p<0.001). A secondary analysis across four parties across both news and opinion articles shows that Plaid Cymru is the outlier, receiving markedly more favourable framing than any other party. These findings provide evidence of measurable differential framing in a single Welsh political media outlet, supporting calls for a broader review of Welsh media coverage. Furthermore, the two-stage pipeline offers a low-cost, replicable framework for extending this analysis to other Welsh outlets, as well as media ecosystems outside of Wales.

AI.tldr: The paper computationally analyzes political bias in the Welsh news outlet Nation.Cymru and finds measurable, differential framing of parties, with Reform UK framed more negatively and Plaid Cymru more favorably, using a two-stage NLP pipeline that can be replicated for other media.
AI.motivation: There are growing accusations that Welsh media exhibit political bias, but these claims lack systematic, computational evidence. The author aims to rigorously test such claims for a key outlet, Nation.Cymru, and to provide a scalable methodology that can be applied to other news sources and contexts.
AI.method: The study uses a two-stage NLP pipeline. First, it applies a RoBERTa-based bias detector to identify biased framing in text efficiently. Second, it employs a large language model to perform target-attributed sentiment classification on the segments labeled as biased, attributing sentiment to specific political parties. The analysis covers 15,583 party mentions in Nation.Cymru articles from 2022 to 2026, including both news and opinion pieces, and applies statistical tests (e.g., significance at p<0.001) to compare bias and sentiment across parties.
AI.result: In the main analysis, mentions of Reform UK are found to receive biased framing at twice the rate of Plaid Cymru and more than three times as negative mean sentiment, with high statistical significance (p<0.001). In a secondary analysis across four parties and across both news and opinion genres, Plaid Cymru emerges as an outlier, receiving substantially more favorable framing than any other party. These quantitative findings indicate consistent, differential treatment of parties in Nation.Cymru coverage.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that Nation.Cymru exhibits measurable and systematic differential framing of political parties, consistent with claims of bias in Welsh media. It argues that these results justify broader, systematic reviews of Welsh media coverage. Additionally, it presents the two-stage RoBERTa+LLM pipeline as a low-cost, replicable framework suitable for extending this type of bias analysis to other Welsh outlets and to media ecosystems beyond Wales.

title: Training LLM Agents for Spontaneous, Reward-Free Self-Evolution via World Knowledge Exploration
summary:
Most agents today ``self-evolve'' by following rewards and rules defined by humans. However, this process remains fundamentally dependent on external supervision; without human guidance, the evolution stops. In this work, we train agents to possess an intrinsic meta-evolution capability to spontaneously learn about unseen environments prior to task execution.
  To instill this ability, we design an outcome-based reward mechanism that measures how much an agent's self-generated world knowledge improves its success rate on downstream tasks. This reward signal is used exclusively during the training phase to teach the model how to explore and summarize effectively. At inference time, the agent requires no external rewards or human instructions. It spontaneously performs native self-evolution to adapt to unknown environments using its internal parameters.
  When applied to Qwen3-30B and Seed-OSS-36B, this shift to native evolution yields a 20% performance increase on WebVoyager and WebWalker. Most strikingly, the generated world knowledge even enables a compact 14B Qwen3 model to outperform the unassisted Gemini-2.5-Flash, establishing a new paradigm for truly evolving agents.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes intrinsically self-evolving agents that can autonomously explore and build world knowledge before tasks, improving performance without external rewards at inference.
AI.motivation: Current agents rely on human-defined rewards and supervision; their learning halts without external guidance. The authors aim to create agents that can continue adapting on their own, especially to unseen environments, by developing an internal mechanism for meta-evolution and self-directed exploration.
AI.method: They introduce an outcome-based reward during training that evaluates how much an agent’s self-generated world knowledge improves performance on downstream tasks. This reward is only used in training to teach the model how to explore, gather, and summarize environmental information effectively. At inference, the agent uses learned internal mechanisms—without external rewards or instructions—to perform a native self-evolution phase that adapts it to new environments before executing tasks.
AI.result: Integrating this native evolution capability into Qwen3-30B and Seed-OSS-36B boosts performance by about 20% on web-based benchmarks WebVoyager and WebWalker. Additionally, a smaller 14B Qwen3 model equipped with this mechanism surpasses the performance of an unassisted Gemini-2.5-Flash model, showing that good self-evolution can compensate for smaller model size.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that agents can be trained to autonomously adapt to new environments via intrinsically driven meta-evolution, guided only by outcome-based rewards during training. This establishes a practical path toward agents that continue evolving and improving at inference time without ongoing human supervision, hinting at a new paradigm for more truly self-improving AI agents.

title: Copy First, Translate Later: Interpreting Translation Dynamics in Multilingual Pretraining
summary: Large language models exhibit impressive cross-lingual capabilities. However, prior work analyzes this phenomenon through isolated factors and at sparse points during training, limiting our understanding of how cross-lingual generalization emerges--particularly in the early phases of learning. To study the early trajectory of linguistic and translation capabilities, we pretrain a multilingual 1.7B model on nine diverse languages, capturing checkpoints at a much finer granularity. We further introduce a novel word-level translation dataset and trace how translation develops over training through behavioral analyses, model-component analysis, and parameter-based ablations. We find that the model quickly acquires basic linguistic capabilities in parallel with token-level copying, while translation develops in two distinct phases: an initial phase dominated by copying and surface-level similarities, and a second phase in which more generalizing translation mechanisms are developed while copying is refined. Together, these findings provide a fine-grained view of how cross-lingual generalization develops during multilingual pretraining.

AI.tldr: Study of how cross-lingual and translation abilities emerge early in training of a multilingual 1.7B LLM using fine-grained checkpoints and a new word-level translation dataset.
AI.motivation: Existing analyses of cross-lingual generalization in large language models look at isolated factors and only at a few training checkpoints, so we lack a detailed picture of how cross-lingual and translation abilities actually emerge over time, especially early in training. Understanding this trajectory can clarify what mechanisms underlie multilingual generalization and copying vs. genuine translation behaviors.
AI.method: Pretrain a 1.7B parameter multilingual language model on nine typologically diverse languages while saving checkpoints at very fine-grained intervals in early training. Construct a novel word-level translation dataset. Track the evolution of translation and linguistic capabilities using: (1) behavioral analyses on the dataset, (2) component-level analyses within the model, and (3) parameter-based ablations to see which parameters and mechanisms drive translation versus copying at different stages.
AI.result: The model acquires basic linguistic skills early, in parallel with a strong token-level copying behavior. Translation ability emerges in two phases: first, a phase where outputs are dominated by copying and superficial cross-lingual similarities; second, a later phase where more general, abstract translation mechanisms appear and copying behavior becomes more targeted and refined. The checkpoints and analyses map out this developmental trajectory in detail.
AI.conclusion: Cross-lingual generalization and translation in multilingual LLMs do not emerge as a single, monolithic capability. Instead, they develop in stages: early shared linguistic structure and copying, followed by more systematic, generalizing translation mechanisms. Fine-grained checkpointing plus targeted behavioral, structural, and ablation analyses provide a clearer mechanistic picture of how multilingual pretraining gives rise to translation abilities.

title: Multi-Agent Systems: From Classical Paradigms to Large Foundation Model-Enabled Futures
comment: Accepted by IEEE/CAA Journal of Automatica Sinica
summary: With the rapid advancement of artificial intelligence, multi-agent systems (MASs) are evolving from classical paradigms toward architectures built upon large foundation models (LFMs). This survey provides a systematic review and comparative analysis of classical MASs (CMASs) and LFM-based MASs (LMASs). First, within a closed-loop coordination framework, CMASs are reviewed across four fundamental dimensions: perception, communication, decision-making, and control. Beyond this framework, LMASs integrate LFMs to lift collaboration from low-level state exchanges to semantic-level reasoning, enabling more flexible coordination and improved adaptability across diverse scenarios. Then, a comparative analysis is conducted to contrast CMASs and LMASs across architecture, operating mechanism, adaptability, and application. Finally, future perspectives on MASs are presented, summarizing open challenges and potential research opportunities.

AI.tldr: This survey compares traditional multi-agent systems (CMASs) with new multi-agent architectures built on large foundation models (LMASs), describing their components, differences, and future directions.
AI.motivation: Classical MASs are powerful but mainly operate with low-level, task-specific designs that can be rigid and hard to generalize across varied environments. With the emergence of large foundation models, there is a need to understand how MASs are changing when these models are used to enable higher-level semantic reasoning, more flexible coordination, and broader applicability. The paper aims to systematize this shift and clarify the advantages, limitations, and open problems of LFM-based MASs relative to classical approaches.
AI.method: The authors adopt a survey and comparative analysis methodology. They first classify and review classical MASs using a closed-loop coordination framework divided into four core dimensions: perception, communication, decision-making, and control. Next, they examine LMASs, focusing on how LFMs are integrated to move from low-level state interactions to semantic-level reasoning in multi-agent coordination. They then perform a structured comparison of CMASs and LMASs along several axes: system architecture, operating mechanisms, adaptability to different tasks and environments, and real-world applications. Finally, they synthesize trends to outline open challenges and future research directions.
AI.result: The survey identifies how CMASs and LMASs differ structurally and functionally. CMASs are found to be more specialized, tightly engineered systems, while LMASs exploit LFMs to support richer semantic understanding and more flexible coordination strategies. LMASs demonstrate stronger adaptability and potential for general-purpose use across heterogeneous scenarios, though they also introduce new challenges related to reliability, control, and integration. The paper organizes the literature into a coherent taxonomy and highlights characteristic patterns of design and performance for each MAS paradigm.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that integrating large foundation models fundamentally shifts multi-agent systems from low-level signal exchange toward high-level semantic collaboration, offering improved flexibility and adaptability. However, LMASs are still in an early stage, with significant challenges in safety, robustness, efficiency, and systematic design. The authors argue that future research should focus on principled architectures that combine the strengths of CMASs and LMASs, better evaluation benchmarks, and methods to harness LFMs in a controllable and trustworthy way for multi-agent coordination.

title: ThreadSumm: Summarization of Nested Discourse Threads Using Tree of Thoughts
summary: Summarizing deeply nested discussion threads requires handling interleaved replies, quotes, and overlapping topics, which standard LLM summarizers struggle to capture reliably. We introduce ThreadSumm, a multi-stage LLM framework that treats thread summarization as a hierarchical reasoning problem over explicit aspect and content unit representations. Our method first performs content planning via LLM-based extraction of discourse aspects and Atomic Content Units, then applies sentence ordering to construct thread-aware sequences that surface multiple viewpoints rather than a single linear strand. On top of these interpretable units, ThreadSumm employs a Tree of Thoughts search that generates and scores multiple paragraph candidates, jointly optimizing coherence and coverage within a unified search space. With this multi-proposal and iterative refinement design, we show improved performance in generating logically structured summaries compared to existing baselines, while achieving higher aspect retention and opinion coverage in nested discussions.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes ThreadSumm, a hierarchical, multi-stage LLM framework for summarizing deeply nested discussion threads with better structure, aspect retention, and opinion coverage.
AI.motivation: Standard LLM summarizers struggle with deeply nested, interleaved discussion threads that contain quotes, overlapping topics, and multiple viewpoints. These models often collapse conversations into a single linear narrative, missing important aspects, viewpoints, and the thread structure. The authors aim to create a summarization method that can better capture the hierarchical structure of discussions, keep track of discourse aspects, and preserve diverse opinions.
AI.method: ThreadSumm treats thread summarization as a hierarchical reasoning problem. First, it performs content planning by using an LLM to extract discourse aspects and Atomic Content Units (fine-grained information units). Then it orders sentences to create thread-aware sequences that represent multiple intertwined viewpoints rather than forcing a single linear flow. On top of these interpretable units, it applies a Tree of Thoughts (ToT) search that generates multiple candidate summary paragraphs and scores them within a unified search space. The search jointly optimizes coherence and coverage through multi-proposal generation and iterative refinement.
AI.result: ThreadSumm achieves improved performance relative to existing baselines in generating logically structured summaries of nested discussions. Empirically, it shows higher aspect retention and better coverage of different opinions and viewpoints in the threads, indicating that its hierarchical planning and ToT-based search yield more complete and balanced summaries.
AI.conclusion: Treating thread summarization as hierarchical reasoning over explicit aspect and content unit representations, combined with Tree of Thoughts search over multiple candidate summaries, leads to more coherent and comprehensive summaries of complex discussion threads. The approach better preserves multiple aspects and viewpoints than standard LLM summarizers, suggesting that explicit content planning plus structured search is an effective strategy for complex conversational summarization tasks.

title: State Transfer Reveals Reuse in Controlled Routing
summary: Prompt-based interventions can change model behavior, but trained success alone does not identify where the behaviorally relevant state is represented. We study this question in controlled routing tasks using interfaces chosen on support data, held-out query evaluation, and matched necessity, sufficiency, and wrong-interface controls. On GPT-2 triop, an early interface supports exact transfer under these tests. On GPT-2 add/sub, zero-retrain compiled transfer at the fixed interface recovers most of donor routing accuracy, while trainable prompt slots can relearn the same behavior at several other positions only after additional support examples and optimization. These results distinguish fixed-interface reuse from prompt relocation in a setting where the two can be tested directly. Qwen routing provides a cross-architecture consistency check for the same matched-interface pattern at the operator token, although donor-specific identity on the local V-path remains unresolved. Generation and reasoning branches are used to map scope: they show broader transport or weaker controller identifiability once control depends on longer trajectories or harder selection. In controlled routing, fixed-interface transfer is therefore stronger evidence of reuse than trained prompt success alone.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how and where prompt-based interventions affect model behavior in controlled routing tasks, finding that fixed interfaces enabling zero-retrain transfer are stronger evidence of mechanism reuse than merely achieving behavioral success with trained prompts.
AI.motivation: Prompt engineering can successfully change model behavior, but success on its own does not reveal which internal representations or interfaces are actually responsible for the behavior, nor whether the same mechanisms are being reused or simply re-learned elsewhere. The authors want a controlled setting to distinguish true reuse of a specific internal interface from flexible relocation of prompts that achieve similar behavior through different internal mechanisms.
AI.method: They design controlled routing tasks and define specific internal interfaces (positions/tokens/paths) hypothesized to control routing. Interfaces are selected using support data, and then evaluated on held-out queries. They test necessity, sufficiency, and use wrong-interface controls. On GPT-2 triop and add/sub tasks, they compare zero-retrain compiled transfer at a fixed interface against training prompt slots at various positions, measuring how well routing behavior transfers and how much additional data/optimization is needed. They replicate the matched-interface pattern on Qwen at the operator token, and use separate generation and reasoning branches to probe the scope and identifiability of controllers along longer trajectories and more complex selection tasks.
AI.result: For GPT-2 triop, an early interface enables exact behavioral transfer that passes necessity, sufficiency, and wrong-interface controls, indicating a well-localized controller. For GPT-2 add/sub, fixing an interface and compiling transfer without retraining recovers most donor routing performance, while movable, trainable prompt slots can only relearn similar behavior at various positions given extra support data and optimization. This shows a clear difference between fixed-interface reuse and prompt relocation. In Qwen routing, they observe a similar matched-interface effect at the operator token, offering cross-architecture consistency, though the detailed identity of the donor mechanism along the value path is still unclear. Generation and reasoning branches suggest that as control depends on longer trajectories or more complex selection, behavior is more broadly distributed and controller identity becomes weaker or less pinpointable.
AI.conclusion: In controlled routing tasks, being able to transfer behavior through a fixed internal interface—especially with zero or minimal retraining and passing necessity/sufficiency controls—is stronger evidence that a model is reusing a specific internal mechanism than simply getting behavioral success through trained prompts. Prompt success alone can reflect flexible relocation or re-learning of control at different positions, so careful interface-based experiments are needed to identify where behaviorally relevant states are actually represented.

title: Measuring Distribution Shift in User Prompts and Its Effects on LLM Performance
comment: Proceedings of the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers)
summary: LLMs are increasingly deployed in dynamic, real-world settings, where the distribution of user prompts can shift substantially over time as new tasks, prompts, and users are introduced to a deployed model. Such natural prompt distribution shift poses a major challenge to LLM reliability, particularly for specialized models designed for narrow domains or user populations. Despite attention to out-of-distribution robustness, there is very limited exploration of measuring natural prompt distribution shift in prior work, and its impact on deployed LLMs remains poorly understood. We introduce the LLM Evaluation under Natural prompt Shift (LENS) framework: a data-centric approach for quantifying natural prompt distribution shift and evaluating its effect on the performance of deployed LLMs. We perform a large-scale evaluation using 192 real-world post-deployment prompt shift settings over time, user group, and geographic axes, training a total of 81 models on 4.68M training prompts, and evaluating on 57.6k prompts. We find that even moderate shifts in user prompt behavior correspond with large performance drops (73% average loss) in deployed LLMs. This performance degradation is particularly prevalent when users from different latent groups and geographic regions interact with models and is correlated with natural prompt distribution shift over time. We systematically characterize how LLM instruction following ability degrades over time and between user groups. Our findings highlight the critical need for data-driven monitoring to ensure LLM performance remains stable across diverse and evolving user populations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces LENS, a framework to quantify natural prompt distribution shift in real LLM deployments and measure its impact on model performance, finding large reliability drops under realistic shifts.
AI.motivation: Deployed LLMs face changing, real-world prompt distributions as tasks, users, and usage patterns evolve over time. Existing robustness work largely focuses on synthetic or benchmark-style out-of-distribution shifts, offering little understanding of how natural, real-world prompt shifts affect specialized or deployed LLMs. There is a need for systematic, data-driven methods to measure such shifts and their impact on reliability so that models can be monitored and maintained across evolving user populations.
AI.method: The authors propose LENS (LLM Evaluation under Natural prompt Shift), a data-centric evaluation framework. It (1) collects real-world prompt logs reflecting deployment scenarios; (2) quantitatively measures distribution shifts across time, user groups, and geographic regions; and (3) evaluates models trained on one prompt distribution against shifted distributions. They run a large-scale study with 192 real-world post-deployment shift settings, training 81 models on 4.68M prompts and evaluating on 57.6k prompts to analyze performance under varying natural shifts.
AI.result: Across the 192 natural prompt shift settings, even moderate changes in user prompt behavior lead to substantial performance drops in deployed LLMs, with an average loss of 73%. Degradation is especially severe when new latent user groups or users from different geographic regions interact with the models. These performance drops correlate strongly with measured degrees of natural prompt distribution shift over time and between user populations.
AI.conclusion: Natural prompt distribution shift is both common and highly damaging to the reliability of deployed LLMs, especially specialized ones. Instruction-following abilities degrade significantly over time and across user groups as prompt distributions evolve. Therefore, maintaining LLM performance in real-world deployments requires continuous, data-driven monitoring and adaptation based on observed prompt distributions, rather than relying solely on static, pre-deployment robustness evaluations.

title: QuantumQA: Enhancing Scientific Reasoning via Physics-Consistent Dataset and Verification-Aware Reinforcement Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) show strong capabilities in general reasoning but typically lack reliability in scientific domains like quantum mechanics, which demand strict adherence to physical constraints. This limitation arises from the scarcity of verifiable training resources and the inadequacy of coarse feedback signals in standard alignment paradigms. To address the data challenge, we introduce QuantumQA, a large-scale dataset constructed via a task-adaptive strategy and a hybrid verification protocol that combines deterministic solvers with semantic auditing to guarantee scientific rigor. Building on this foundation, we propose the verification-aware reward model (VRM) tailored for Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), which employs an adaptive reward fusion (ARF) mechanism to dynamically integrate deterministic signals from a scientific execution suite (SES) with multidimensional semantic evaluations for precise supervision. Experimental results demonstrate that our method consistently outperforms baselines and general-purpose preference models. Notably, our optimized 8B model achieves performance competitive with proprietary models, validating that incorporating verifiable, rule-based feedback into the reinforcement learning loop offers a parameter-efficient alternative to pure scaling.

AI.tldr: They build a quantum mechanics Q&A dataset and a verification-aware RL framework that fuses symbolic solvers and semantic rewards, enabling an 8B LLM to reach near–proprietary performance in quantum reasoning.
AI.motivation: LLMs are good at general reasoning but unreliable for quantum mechanics, where answers must strictly obey physical laws. Existing alignment methods rely on scarce verified data and coarse, often noisy preference signals, which are insufficient for domains needing mathematically correct, verifiable outputs. The authors aim to create both better training data and a fine-grained reward mechanism that enforces scientific correctness, not just linguistic plausibility.
AI.method: 1) Data: They construct QuantumQA with a task-adaptive strategy, likely generating diverse quantum problems and solutions and then filtering/augmenting them. They use a hybrid verification protocol: deterministic solvers (e.g., symbolic/numeric quantum tools) check hard constraints, while semantic auditing (LLM or expert-like checks) ensures conceptual and linguistic correctness. 2) Training: They design a Verification-aware Reward Model (VRM) for RL with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). VRM uses an Adaptive Reward Fusion (ARF) mechanism to dynamically weight and combine deterministic scores from a Scientific Execution Suite (SES)—code or tools executing quantum calculations—with multidimensional semantic evaluations (e.g., coherence, completeness, consistency). This fused reward supervises policy optimization of an 8B LLM. They compare against standard preference models and baseline RL setups.
AI.result: Across quantum QA and reasoning benchmarks, their RLVR-trained models surpass baselines and general-purpose preference-aligned LLMs. The 8B parameter model trained with verifiable rewards performs on par with much larger or proprietary models on quantum tasks, indicating that structured, verifiable feedback substantially boosts domain reliability without massive scale.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating domain-specific, verifiable and rule-based feedback into the RL alignment loop significantly improves LLM reliability in quantum mechanics. Carefully constructed datasets like QuantumQA and hybrid reward models that fuse tool-based verification with semantic assessment provide a parameter-efficient path to scientific reasoning, suggesting that future domain-expert LLMs should integrate formal verification signals rather than relying only on generic preference optimization and model scaling.

title: Semantic Density Effect (SDE): Maximizing Information Per Token Improves LLM Accuracy
summary: We introduce the Semantic Density Effect (SDE): the empirical finding that prompts carrying higher semantic information per token consistently produce more accurate, focused, and less hallucinated outputs across all major LLM families. SDE is defined as the ratio of semantically loaded tokens to total prompt tokens, adjusted for redundancy and concreteness. Unlike prior prompt optimization techniques that add tokens (Chain of Thought), duplicate the prompt (Prompt Repetition), or reorder components (Instruction Placement Effect), SDE improves performance by removing or replacing low-information tokens while preserving or sharpening the semantic signal. Evaluated across five frontier models and seven benchmarks, ultra-dense prompts (SDE > 0.80) outperform diluted counterparts by an average of +8.4 percentage points with 0 additional tokens and 0 latency overhead. Combined with Instruction Placement Effect (IPE), the gain reaches +11.7 percentage points

AI.tldr: The paper identifies a 'Semantic Density Effect' showing that prompts with more semantic information per token yield better LLM performance without increasing token count or latency.
AI.motivation: To understand and systematically exploit how the information content of prompt tokens (rather than just length or structure) affects LLM accuracy, focus, and hallucination rates, and to go beyond existing prompt optimization methods that mainly add or rearrange tokens.
AI.method: Define a quantitative metric for Semantic Density (ratio of semantically loaded tokens to total tokens, adjusted for redundancy and concreteness). Construct prompts with varying SDE levels and compare model performance across five frontier models and seven benchmarks, including combinations with other techniques like the Instruction Placement Effect.
AI.result: Prompts with high semantic density (SDE > 0.80, called ultra-dense prompts) consistently outperform lower-density prompts by ~+8.4 percentage points on average across evaluated models and tasks, with no increase in token count or latency. When combined with the Instruction Placement Effect, the performance gain reaches ~+11.7 percentage points.
AI.conclusion: Carefully compressing and sharpening the semantic content of prompts—removing or replacing low-information tokens while keeping the core signal—systematically improves LLM outputs. Semantic density is a key, model-agnostic dimension of prompt quality, complementary to prior techniques like Chain-of-Thought or prompt reordering, and can be exploited for better performance without extra cost.

title: A Control Architecture for Training-Free Memory Use
summary: Prompt-injected memory can improve reasoning without updating model weights, but it also creates a control problem: retrieved content helps only when it is applied in the right state. We study this problem in a strict training-free setting and formulate it as applicability control: when to trigger a memory-assisted second pass, when to trust it, and how to maintain the memory bank over time. Our method combines uncertainty-based routing, confidence-based selective acceptance, bank selection across rule and exemplar memory, and evidence-based governance of the memory bank over time. Under a locked training-free protocol with compute-matched controls, it improves two core arithmetic benchmarks by +7.0 points on SVAMP and +7.67 points on ASDiv over baseline. The same architecture also transfers to QA and agent benchmarks with smaller positive effects and shows the same positive direction on a second checkpoint for the main arithmetic tasks. On arithmetic, the main empirical pattern is that the control architecture, rather than raw memory exposure, drives the improvements on SVAMP and ASDiv. Mechanistically, confidence separates helpful from harmful rule-bank interventions, and under fixed retrieval the repair-versus-corrupt difference localizes to rows whose retrieved set actually contains the edited entries.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a training-free control architecture for using prompt-injected memories that improves reasoning on arithmetic benchmarks by selectively deciding when and how to use retrieved memories.
AI.motivation: Prompt-injected, retrieval-based memory can enhance reasoning accuracy without updating model weights, but it introduces a control problem: retrieved information only helps if used in appropriate contexts and can otherwise degrade performance. There is a need for methods that, without additional training, can decide when to invoke memory, when to trust it, and how to maintain and update the memory bank over time.
AI.method: The authors formulate the challenge as an applicability control problem and design a training-free control architecture. The method integrates: (1) uncertainty-based routing to decide when to trigger a second, memory-assisted pass; (2) confidence-based selective acceptance to decide whether to trust the memory-assisted output; (3) bank selection mechanisms that choose between rule-based memory and exemplar-based memory; and (4) evidence-based governance procedures to maintain and update the memory bank over time. All experiments follow a locked, training-free protocol and use compute-matched baselines for fair comparison.
AI.result: On two core arithmetic reasoning benchmarks, SVAMP and ASDiv, the method improves performance by +7.0 and +7.67 points respectively over a baseline under equal compute and no training of model weights. The same architecture transfers to question answering and agent benchmarks, yielding smaller but consistently positive gains, and similar positive trends on another model checkpoint. Analyses show that performance gains are mainly due to the control architecture rather than simple exposure to more memory content. Mechanistic inspection reveals that confidence metrics effectively separate beneficial from harmful rule-bank interventions, and that, under fixed retrieval, the difference between examples that are repaired versus corrupted localizes to cases where retrieved sets actually contain edited entries.
AI.conclusion: A carefully designed, training-free control framework for applicability of prompt-injected memories can substantially improve arithmetic reasoning by determining when and how to use retrieved information and how to govern the memory bank. The improvements stem from controlling applicability, not just adding more memory, and the approach generalizes, with smaller positive effects, to other tasks and checkpoints. Confidence and retrieval structure are key to separating helpful from harmful memory interventions.

title: Peerispect: Claim Verification in Scientific Peer Reviews
summary: Peer review is central to scientific publishing, yet reviewers frequently include claims that are subjective, rhetorical, or misaligned with the submitted work. Assessing whether review statements are factual and verifiable is crucial for fairness and accountability. At the scale of modern conferences and journals, manually inspecting the grounding of such claims is infeasible. We present Peerispect, an interactive system that operationalizes claim-level verification in peer reviews by extracting check-worthy claims from peer reviews, retrieving relevant evidence from the manuscript, and verifying the claims through natural language inference. Results are presented through a visual interface that highlights evidence directly in the paper, enabling rapid inspection and interpretation. Peerispect is designed as a modular Information Retrieval (IR) pipeline, supporting alternative retrievers, rerankers, and verifiers, and is intended for use by reviewers, authors, and program committees. We demonstrate Peerispect through a live, publicly available demo (https://app.reviewer.ly/app/peerispect) and API services (https://github.com/Reviewerly-Inc/Peerispect), accompanied by a video tutorial (https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pc9RkvkUh14).

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Peerispect, an interactive system to automatically check and visualize whether peer-review claims are grounded in the reviewed manuscript.
AI.motivation: Peer reviews often contain subjective or poorly grounded claims, and at the scale of modern conferences it is infeasible to manually verify every claim against the manuscript. There is a need for automated, fine-grained tools that improve fairness and accountability by checking how well review statements are supported by the paper.
AI.method: The authors build Peerispect, a modular IR-based pipeline that: (1) extracts check-worthy claims from review text, (2) retrieves potentially relevant passages from the manuscript, and (3) applies natural language inference models to verify each claim against the retrieved evidence. The system provides a visual interface that highlights supporting or contradicting evidence in the paper and is designed to flexibly plug in different retrievers, rerankers, and verifiers. They provide a live demo, APIs, and a video tutorial.
AI.result: Peerispect successfully implements end-to-end claim-level verification of peer-review statements, presenting the results in an interactive UI. The system can highlight relevant manuscript evidence for each review claim and is available as a working demo and API, demonstrating its practical viability for reviewers, authors, and program chairs.
AI.conclusion: Claim-level verification in peer review can be operationalized via a modular IR and NLI pipeline, and Peerispect shows that such a system can be made usable through an interactive visual interface. This opens the door to more accountable, transparent, and scalable review processes, and the released demo and APIs support broader adoption and future extensions.

title: TacticGen: Grounding Adaptable and Scalable Generation of Football Tactics
summary: Success in association football relies on both individual skill and coordinated tactics. While recent advancements in spatio-temporal data and deep learning have enabled predictive analyses like trajectory forecasting, the development of tactical design remains limited. Bridging this gap is essential, as prediction reveals what is likely to occur, whereas tactic generation determines what should occur to achieve strategic objectives. In this work, we present TacticGen, a generative model for adaptable and scalable tactic generation. TacticGen formulates tactics as sequences of multi-agent movements and interactions conditioned on the game context. It employs a multi-agent diffusion transformer with agent-wise self-attention and context-aware cross-attention to capture cooperative and competitive dynamics among players and the ball. Trained with over 3.3 million events and 100 million tracking frames from top-tier leagues, TacticGen achieves state-of-the-art precision in predicting player trajectories. Building on it, TacticGen enables adaptable tactic generation tailored to diverse inference-time objectives through classifier guidance mechanism, specified via rules, natural language, or neural models. Its modeling performance is also inherently scalable. A case study with football experts confirms that TacticGen generates realistic, strategically valuable tactics, demonstrating its practical utility for tactical planning in professional football. The project page is available at: https://shengxu.net/TacticGen/.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TacticGen, a generative model that designs realistic, strategically useful football tactics by modeling multi-agent player and ball movements, achieving state-of-the-art trajectory prediction and enabling controllable tactic generation via flexible, objective-guided conditioning.
AI.motivation: Existing work in football analytics focuses mainly on prediction, such as forecasting player trajectories from spatio-temporal data. However, coaches and analysts also need tools that propose new tactics—what should happen to achieve strategic goals—not just what is likely to happen. There is a lack of scalable, data-driven generative methods that can synthesize coordinated multi-player tactics under different game contexts and objectives. The paper aims to bridge this gap by turning rich tracking data into a generative engine for tactic design, usable in professional settings.
AI.method: The authors model football tactics as sequences of multi-agent trajectories and interactions (players plus ball) conditioned on game context. They design a multi-agent diffusion transformer that combines: (1) agent-wise self-attention to capture intra- and inter-team coordination and competition; and (2) context-aware cross-attention to condition on match state and other contextual signals. The model is trained on a large dataset comprising over 3.3 million events and 100 million tracking frames from elite leagues. At inference, they employ classifier guidance to steer the generative process toward chosen objectives (e.g., certain outcomes, styles, or patterns), where the guidance can be specified by rules, natural-language descriptions, or learned neural classifiers.
AI.result: TacticGen achieves state-of-the-art precision in predicting future player trajectories compared to existing baselines, validating its underlying spatio-temporal modeling quality. More importantly, it supports controllable generation of tactics aligned with diverse objectives specified at inference time, demonstrating adaptability and scalability. Through a case study with professional football experts, the generated tactics were judged realistic and strategically meaningful, indicating that the model’s outputs are not only statistically sound but also practically valuable for real-world tactical planning.
AI.conclusion: TacticGen shows that a multi-agent diffusion transformer trained on large-scale tracking data can both accurately predict player movements and generate realistic, goal-directed football tactics. The model’s classifier-guided conditioning allows flexible control using rules, natural language, or neural objectives, making it a versatile tool for tactical exploration. Expert evaluation supports its practical utility, suggesting that such generative systems can augment human tactical design in professional football and potentially extend to other multi-agent sports and domains.

title: Towards Intelligent Legal Document Analysis: CNN-Driven Classification of Case Law Texts
summary: Legal practitioners and judicial institutions face an ever-growing volume of case-law documents characterised by formalised language, lengthy sentence structures, and highly specialised terminology, making manual triage both time-consuming and error-prone. This work presents a lightweight yet high-accuracy framework for citation-treatment classification that pairs lemmatisation-based preprocessing with subword-aware FastText embeddings and a multi-kernel one-dimensional Convolutional Neural Network (CNN). Evaluated on a publicly available corpus of 25,000 annotated legal documents with a 75/25 training-test partition, the proposed system achieves 97.26% classification accuracy and a macro F1-score of 96.82%, surpassing established baselines including fine-tuned BERT, Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM) with FastText, CNN with random embeddings, and a Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) k-Nearest Neighbour (KNN) classifier. The model also attains the highest Area Under the Receiver Operating Characteristic (AUC-ROC) curve of 97.83% among all compared systems while operating with only 5.1 million parameters and an inference latency of 0.31 ms per document - more than 13 times faster than BERT. Ablation experiments confirm the individual contribution of each pipeline component, and the confusion matrix reveals that residual errors are confined to semantically adjacent citation categories. These findings indicate that carefully designed convolutional architectures represent a scalable, resource-efficient alternative to heavyweight transformers for intelligent legal document analysis.

AI.tldr: A lightweight CNN-based model using FastText embeddings is proposed for classifying how legal citations are treated in case-law documents, achieving very high accuracy and efficiency, and outperforming heavier baselines like BERT.
AI.motivation: Legal professionals must quickly understand how prior cases are treated (e.g., followed, distinguished, overruled) within new judgments, but the huge volume, complexity, and technical language of legal texts make manual triage slow and error-prone. Existing high-performing NLP models like transformers (e.g., BERT) are accurate but computationally heavy and slow, limiting their practical deployment in large-scale or resource-constrained legal settings. There is a need for a simpler, faster, yet still highly accurate approach tailored to citation-treatment classification in legal documents.
AI.method: The paper proposes a framework combining: (1) lemmatization-based preprocessing of legal text; (2) subword-aware FastText embeddings to capture word and subword information; and (3) a multi-kernel 1D CNN architecture for classification. The model is evaluated on a corpus of 25,000 annotated legal documents (75/25 train-test split). Baselines include fine-tuned BERT, LSTM with FastText, CNN with random embeddings, and a TF-IDF + KNN classifier. Ablation studies remove or modify components (e.g., lemmatization, subword embeddings, multi-kernel design) to measure their individual contributions. Confusion-matrix analysis is used to inspect error patterns across citation-treatment categories.
AI.result: On the 25,000-document legal corpus, the proposed model achieves 97.26% accuracy, macro F1-score of 96.82%, and an AUC-ROC of 97.83%, outperforming all baselines including fine-tuned BERT, LSTM+FastText, CNN with random embeddings, and TF-IDF+KNN. Despite this strong performance, the model is lightweight, with only 5.1M parameters, and yields an average inference latency of 0.31 ms per document—over 13x faster than BERT. Ablation experiments show that each pipeline component (lemmatization, subword-aware FastText, multi-kernel CNN) contributes measurably to performance, and confusion-matrix analysis indicates that the remaining misclassifications mostly occur between semantically close citation-treatment categories.
AI.conclusion: Carefully designed convolutional architectures, when combined with appropriate preprocessing and subword-aware embeddings, can match or surpass the performance of heavy transformer-based models like BERT on legal citation-treatment classification, while being far more efficient in terms of parameters and inference speed. This makes them a practical, scalable solution for intelligent legal document analysis in real-world environments where computational resources and latency are critical constraints.

title: AJ-Bench: Benchmarking Agent-as-a-Judge for Environment-Aware Evaluation
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Findings. 43 pages total, 5 figures
summary:
As reinforcement learning continues to scale the training of large language model-based agents, reliably verifying agent behaviors in complex environments has become increasingly challenging. Existing approaches rely on rule-based verifiers or LLM-as-a-Judge models, which struggle to generalize beyond narrow domains. Agent-as-a-Judge addresses this limitation by actively interacting with environments and tools to acquire verifiable evidence, yet its capabilities remain underexplored.
  We introduce a benchmark AJ-Bench to systematically evaluate Agent-as-a-Judge across three domains-search, data systems, and graphical user interfaces-comprising 155 tasks and 516 annotated trajectories. The benchmark comprehensively assesses judge agents' abilities in information acquisition, state verification, and process verification. Experiments demonstrate consistent performance gains over LLM-as-a-Judge baselines, while also revealing substantial open challenges in agent-based verification. Our data and code are available at https://aj-bench.github.io/.

AI.tldr: They propose AJ-Bench, a benchmark to evaluate "Agent-as-a-Judge" systems that actively interact with environments to verify other agents, showing they outperform standard LLM-as-a-Judge but still face challenges.
AI.motivation: As RL-trained LLM agents grow more complex, it becomes hard to reliably verify their behavior in realistic environments. Existing verification methods—rule-based checkers or static LLM-as-a-Judge models—don't generalize well beyond narrow domains and cannot interact with tools/environments to gather evidence. Agent-as-a-Judge, which actively operates in environments to verify other agents, is promising but underexplored and lacks systematic evaluation.
AI.method: They design AJ-Bench, a benchmark specifically for evaluating Agent-as-a-Judge systems. It spans three domains (web search, data systems, graphical user interfaces) and contains 155 tasks with 516 human-annotated trajectories. The benchmark is structured to test three core judge capabilities: (1) information acquisition (actively seeking evidence), (2) state verification (checking environment states or results), and (3) process verification (evaluating whether an agent followed a correct procedure). They then test various judge agents on AJ-Bench and compare them with standard LLM-as-a-Judge baselines.
AI.result: Agent-as-a-Judge systems evaluated on AJ-Bench show consistent performance improvements over conventional LLM-as-a-Judge baselines, indicating the value of active interaction and tool use for verification. However, performance is still far from perfect, and the experiments surface multiple failure modes and open challenges in using agents for robust verification in complex environments.
AI.conclusion: AJ-Bench offers a systematic way to study and compare Agent-as-a-Judge systems across multiple domains and verification skills. Empirical results validate that interactive judge agents are more effective than passive LLM judges, but also highlight that agent-based verification is still an open research area with substantial room for improvement. The released data and code aim to catalyze further work on reliable verification of LLM agents.

title: Before You Interpret the Profile: Validity Scaling for LLM Metacognitive Self-Report
summary: Clinical personality assessment screens response validity before interpreting substantive scales. LLM evaluation does not. We apply the validity scaling framework from the PAI and MMPI-3 to metacognitive probe data from 20 frontier models across 524 items. Six validity indices are operationalised: L (maintaining confidence on errors), K (betting on errors), F (withdrawing consensus-endorsed items), Fp (withdrawing correct answers), RBS (inverted monitoring), and TRIN (fixed responding). A tiered classification system identifies four models as construct-level invalid and two as elevated. Valid-profile models produce item-sensitive confidence (mean r = .18, 14 of 16 significant). Invalid-profile models do not (mean r = -.20, d = 2.17, p = .001). Chain-of-thought training produces two opposite response distortions. Two latent dimensions account for 94.6% of index variance. Companion papers extract a portable screening protocol (Cacioli, 2026e) and validate it against selective prediction (Cacioli, 2026f). All data and code: https://github.com/synthiumjp/validity-scaling-llm

AI.tldr: The paper adapts clinical personality test validity scales to evaluate the response validity of large language models (LLMs), finding that some models show systematic metacognitive distortions that undermine their confidence judgments.
AI.motivation: In clinical personality assessment, results are not interpreted until response validity is checked with specialized scales (e.g., PAI, MMPI-3). LLM evaluations, especially those using confidence or metacognitive probes, lack an analogous validity check, risking misinterpretation of performance and calibration. The authors aim to fill this gap by bringing established psychological validity-scaling frameworks to LLM evaluation.
AI.method: The authors map six classic clinical validity indices (L, K, F, Fp, RBS, TRIN) onto metacognitive probe behavior of 20 frontier LLMs across 524 items. They operationalize each index using patterns in confidence and betting behavior on correct vs. incorrect items, withdrawal from consensus-endorsed items, inverted monitoring, and fixed responding. A tiered classification system is used to label model profiles as valid, elevated, or construct-level invalid. They further examine item-wise confidence–accuracy correlations, analyze the impact of chain-of-thought training on distortions, and use latent variable analysis to identify underlying dimensions of validity variance.
AI.result: Four models are classified as construct-level invalid, two as elevated, and the rest as valid, based on the adapted validity indices. Models with valid profiles show positive, item-sensitive confidence–accuracy relations (mean r = .18; 14 of 16 significant). Models with invalid profiles show the opposite pattern, with negative confidence–accuracy relations (mean r = -.20) and a large effect size difference (d = 2.17, p = .001). Chain-of-thought training is associated with two distinct, opposite distortions in validity profiles. Factor analysis reveals two latent dimensions that explain 94.6% of the variance across the six indices. Companion papers describe a portable screening protocol and its validation using selective prediction, and all data and code are released publicly.
AI.conclusion: Clinical-style validity scaling can be successfully transferred to LLM metacognitive evaluation and reveals that some frontier models generate systematically distorted confidence patterns that make their metacognitive outputs construct-invalid. Validity screening should precede the use of LLM confidence or betting behavior in downstream tasks, especially selective prediction. The work offers a principled framework and tools to identify and filter out invalid profiles, and highlights that training choices such as chain-of-thought can introduce characteristic distortions in metacognitive behavior.

title: LeGo-Code: Can Modular Curriculum Learning Advance Complex Code Generation? Insights from Text-to-SQL
summary: Recently, code-oriented large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in translating natural language into executable code. Text-to-SQL is a significant application of this ability, enabling non-technical users to interact with relational databases using natural language. However, state-of-the-art models continue to struggle with highly complex logic, particularly deeply nested statements involving multiple joins and conditions, as well as with real-world database schemas that are noisy or poorly structured. In this paper, we investigate whether curriculum learning can improve the performance of code-based LLMs on Text-to-SQL tasks. Employing benchmarks including Spider and BIRD, we fine-tune models under different curriculum strategies. Our experiments show that naive curriculum, simply ordering training samples by complexity in a single epoch, fails to surpass standard fine-tuning due to catastrophic forgetting. To overcome this, we propose a Modular Adapter Composition (MAC) strategy. By sequentially training tier-specific adapters on incremental complexity levels (Easy to Extra-Hard), we create a scaffolded learning environment that improves performance on complex queries. Our approach not only produces measurable performance gains on the Spider and BIRD benchmarks but also provides a flexible, "Lego-like" architecture, allowing models to be composed and deployed based on specific schema difficulty requirements. These findings demonstrate that structured, modular learning is a superior alternative to monolithic fine-tuning for mastering the syntax and logic of complex code generation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether curriculum learning can improve Text-to-SQL performance of code LLMs and proposes a modular adapter composition strategy that outperforms naive curriculum and standard fine-tuning, especially on complex queries.
AI.motivation: Code LLMs can translate natural language to SQL, but they struggle with highly complex, deeply nested queries and noisy real-world schemas. Existing fine-tuning approaches do not effectively address these challenges, motivating the exploration of curriculum learning and better training architectures.
AI.method: The authors experiment with curriculum learning strategies on benchmarks like Spider and BIRD by fine-tuning code-oriented LLMs. They first test a naive curriculum that orders training examples by complexity in a single pass, then introduce Modular Adapter Composition (MAC), where they sequentially train separate adapters on different complexity tiers (Easy to Extra-Hard) and compose these adapters for inference.
AI.result: Naive curriculum learning, where data is simply ordered by complexity in one epoch, does not improve over standard fine-tuning due to catastrophic forgetting. In contrast, the MAC strategy yields measurable gains, particularly on complex Text-to-SQL queries, on Spider and BIRD benchmarks. It also enables modular deployment, where different adapters can be combined based on schema difficulty.
AI.conclusion: Structured, modular curriculum learning via MAC is more effective than monolithic fine-tuning for Text-to-SQL with code LLMs. The adapter-based, composable architecture improves performance on complex queries and offers practical flexibility in deploying models tailored to different schema difficulties.

title: DeInfer: Efficient Parallel Inferencing for Decomposed Large Language Models
summary: Existing works on large language model (LLM) decomposition mainly focus on improving performance on downstream tasks, but they ignore the poor parallel inference performance when trying to scale up the model size. To mitigate this important performance issue, this paper introduces DeInfer, a high-performance inference system dedicated to parallel inference of decomposed LLMs. It consists of multiple optimizations to maximize performance and be compatible with state-of-the-art optimization techniques. Extensive experiments are carried out to evaluate DeInfer's performance, where the results demonstrate its superiority, suggesting it can greatly facilitate the parallel inference of decomposed LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper presents DeInfer, a high-performance system for parallel inference on decomposed large language models, significantly improving efficiency while remaining compatible with existing optimizations.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM decomposition approaches improve downstream task performance but suffer from poor parallel inference efficiency when scaling to larger models, creating a bottleneck that limits their practical deployment.
AI.method: The authors design DeInfer, an inference system tailored for decomposed LLMs, incorporating multiple system-level and algorithmic optimizations to better exploit parallelism and integrate with current state-of-the-art inference and hardware optimization techniques.
AI.result: Through extensive experiments, DeInfer is shown to outperform existing solutions in parallel inference performance for decomposed LLMs, demonstrating clear superiority on a range of benchmarks and setups.
AI.conclusion: DeInfer effectively addresses the parallel inference bottleneck of decomposed LLMs, substantially improving performance while maintaining compatibility with modern optimizations, thereby making decomposed LLMs more practical for real-world large-scale deployment.

title: Enhancing Tabular Anomaly Detection via Pseudo-Label-Guided Generation
summary: Identifying anomalous instances in tabular data is essential for improving data reliability and maintaining system stability. Due to the scarcity of ground-truth anomaly labels, existing methods mainly rely on unsupervised anomaly detection models, or exploit a small number of labeled anomalies to facilitate detection via sample generation or contrastive learning. However, unsupervised methods lack sufficient anomaly awareness, while current generation and contrastive approaches tend to compute anomalies globally, overlooking the localized anomaly patterns of tabular features, resulting in suboptimal detection performance. To address these limitations, we propose PLAG, a pseudo-label-guided anomaly generation method designed to enhance tabular anomaly detection. Specifically, by utilizing pseudo-anomalies as guidance signals and decoupling the overall anomaly quantification of a sample into an accumulation of feature-level abnormalities, PLAG not only effectively obviates the need for scarce ground-truth labels but also provides a novel perspective for the model to comprehend localized anomalous signals at a fine-grained level. Furthermore, a two-stage data selection strategy is proposed, integrating format verification and uncertainty estimation to rigorously filter candidate samples, thereby ensuring the fidelity and diversity of the synthetic anomalies. Ultimately, these filtered synthetic anomalies serve as robust discriminative guidance, empowering the model to better separate normal and anomalous instances. Extensive experiments demonstrate that PLAG achieves state-of-the-art performance against eight representative baselines. Moreover, as a flexible framework, it integrates seamlessly with existing unsupervised detectors, consistently boosting F1-scores by 0.08 to 0.21.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes PLAG, a pseudo-label-guided anomaly generation framework that enhances tabular anomaly detection by modeling feature-level (localized) anomalies and synthesizing high-fidelity anomalous samples, achieving state-of-the-art performance and boosting existing detectors’ F1-scores by 0.08–0.21.
AI.motivation: Existing tabular anomaly detection methods struggle due to scarce ground-truth anomaly labels. Purely unsupervised methods lack explicit anomaly awareness, and methods using generation or contrastive learning often treat anomalies globally, ignoring localized, feature-level anomaly patterns. This leads to suboptimal detection performance. The authors are motivated to design a method that (1) works with few or no real anomaly labels, (2) captures fine-grained, feature-wise anomaly signals, and (3) supplies better synthetic anomalies to guide detectors.
AI.method: They introduce PLAG, a pseudo-label-guided anomaly generation framework for tabular data. PLAG uses pseudo-anomalies (derived without needing many true labels) as guidance and decomposes a sample’s overall anomaly score into an aggregation of feature-level abnormality scores. This lets the model learn localized anomaly patterns in individual features. PLAG also employs a two-stage data selection pipeline: first using format verification to discard clearly invalid generations, then using uncertainty estimation to filter low-confidence or redundant samples. The resulting high-fidelity, diverse synthetic anomalies are then used to train or guide anomaly detectors, and PLAG is designed to plug into existing unsupervised detection methods as an enhancement layer.
AI.result: Across extensive experiments and comparisons with eight representative baseline methods, PLAG attains state-of-the-art performance on tabular anomaly detection tasks. When combined with existing unsupervised detectors, PLAG consistently improves F1-scores by about 0.08 to 0.21, demonstrating both effectiveness and compatibility with other models.
AI.conclusion: PLAG effectively addresses the label scarcity and global-anomaly limitations of current tabular anomaly detection approaches. By guiding synthetic anomaly generation with pseudo-labels and explicitly modeling feature-level abnormality, it provides high-quality anomalous samples that significantly enhance detection performance. Its flexible, plug-and-play design allows it to serve as a generic performance booster for a wide range of unsupervised tabular anomaly detectors.

title: Screen Before You Interpret: A Portable Validity Protocol for Benchmark-Based LLM Confidence Signals
comment: 25 pages, 6 figures, 8 tables, 2 appendices. Companion to arXiv:2604.15702
summary: LLM confidence signals are used for abstention, routing, and safety-critical decisions. No standard practice exists for checking whether a confidence signal carries item-level information before building on it. We transfer the validity screening principle from clinical personality assessment (PAI, MMPI-3) as a portable protocol for benchmark-based LLM confidence data. The protocol specifies three core indices (L, Fp, RBS), a structural indicator (TRIN), and an item-sensitivity statistic, computed from a single 2x2 contingency table. A three-tier classification system (Invalid, Indeterminate, Valid) draws on four clinical traditions. Validated on 20 frontier LLMs across 524 items, four models are classified Invalid, two Indeterminate. Valid-profile models show mean r = .18 (15/16 significant). Invalid-profile models show mean r = -.20 (d = 2.48). Cross-benchmark validation on 18 models using MMLU with verbalized confidence and on external data from Yang et al. (2024) confirms the screen transfers across benchmarks and probe formats. All data and code: https://github.com/synthiumjp/validity-scaling-llm

AI.tldr: The paper proposes and validates a standardized “validity screen” for LLM confidence signals, adapting methods from clinical personality assessment to determine whether confidence scores contain reliable item-level information before using them for downstream decisions.
AI.motivation: LLM confidence scores are increasingly used for abstention, routing, and high-stakes decisions, but there is no standard way to test whether these scores meaningfully correspond to item-level correctness. Without such screening, systems may rely on confidence signals that are systematically invalid or even misleading. The authors borrow the well-established concept of validity scales from clinical personality assessment (e.g., PAI, MMPI-3) to create a portable, domain-agnostic protocol for evaluating whether LLM confidence data are trustworthy at the item level.
AI.method: The authors transfer validity screening principles from clinical personality inventories to LLM benchmarks. They define three core indices (L, Fp, RBS), one structural indicator (TRIN), and an item-sensitivity statistic, all derivable from a single 2×2 contingency table relating confidence and correctness on benchmark items. Using these indices they construct a three-tier classification of confidence profiles as Invalid, Indeterminate, or Valid, grounded in four clinical traditions. They then apply this protocol to benchmark-based confidence data from 20 frontier LLMs across 524 items, and run cross-benchmark validation using MMLU with verbalized confidence and external data from Yang et al. (2024).
AI.result: Applying the protocol to 20 frontier LLMs, four models were classified as having Invalid confidence profiles, two as Indeterminate, and the remainder as Valid. Models with Valid profiles showed a small but positive mean correlation between confidence and correctness (r ≈ .18, significant for 15 of 16 cases). In contrast, Invalid-profile models exhibited a negative mean correlation (r ≈ −.20) with a large effect size (d = 2.48), indicating that their confidence signals are systematically misaligned with correctness. Cross-benchmark analyses on 18 models with MMLU verbalized confidence data and on external data from Yang et al. (2024) demonstrated that the screening method generalizes across benchmarks and confidence elicitation formats.
AI.conclusion: The paper introduces a simple, portable validity-screening protocol for LLM confidence signals, inspired by clinical personality assessment, that can be implemented from basic contingency tables. The results show that some frontier LLMs produce confidence signals that are not just uninformative but inversely related to correctness, underscoring the need to screen confidence before deploying it in downstream applications. The protocol appears robust across benchmarks and confidence formats, and the authors release data and code to facilitate adoption and further study.

title: Agent-World: Scaling Real-World Environment Synthesis for Evolving General Agent Intelligence
summary: Large language models are increasingly expected to serve as general-purpose agents that interact with external, stateful tool environments. The Model Context Protocol (MCP) and broader agent skills offer a unified interface for connecting agents with scalable real-world services, but training robust agents remains limited by the lack of realistic environments and principled mechanisms for life-long learning. In this paper, we present \textbf{Agent-World}, a self-evolving training arena for advancing general agent intelligence through scalable environments. Agent-World has two main components: (1) Agentic Environment-Task Discovery, which autonomously explores topic-aligned databases and executable tool ecosystems from thousands of real-world environment themes and synthesizes verifiable tasks with controllable difficulty; and (2) Continuous Self-Evolving Agent Training, which combines multi-environment reinforcement learning with a self-evolving agent arena that automatically identifies capability gaps through dynamic task synthesis and drives targeted learning, enabling the co-evolution of agent policies and environments. Across 23 challenging agent benchmarks, Agent-World-8B and 14B consistently outperforms strong proprietary models and environment scaling baselines. Further analyses reveal scaling trends in relation to environment diversity and self-evolution rounds, offering insights for building general agent intelligence.

AI.tldr: Agent-World is a self-evolving training arena that automatically discovers realistic tool-based environments and tasks, then continuously trains LLM agents with reinforcement learning, leading to stronger general-purpose tool-using agents.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents are expected to operate as general-purpose tool users in realistic, stateful environments via interfaces like MCP, but current training is hampered by a lack of scalable, realistic environments and structured life-long learning setups. The paper aims to provide a principled and scalable way to generate such environments and support continual improvement of agent capabilities.
AI.method: The authors build Agent-World, consisting of (1) Agentic Environment-Task Discovery, which automatically explores many topic-aligned databases and tool ecosystems to synthesize verifiable tasks of controllable difficulty from thousands of real-world environment themes; and (2) Continuous Self-Evolving Agent Training, which uses multi-environment reinforcement learning plus a self-evolving arena that dynamically generates tasks targeting identified capability gaps, enabling co-evolution of agents and environments over multiple self-evolution rounds.
AI.result: Using Agent-World, they train 8B and 14B parameter agents (Agent-World-8B/14B) that, across 23 demanding agent benchmarks, consistently outperform strong proprietary models and baselines that only scale environments without self-evolution mechanisms. Analyses show clear scaling behaviors as environment diversity and the number of self-evolution rounds increase.
AI.conclusion: A self-evolving, automatically generated environment-task ecosystem coupled with continuous RL-based training can significantly advance general agent intelligence for tool-using LLMs. The observed scaling trends suggest that systematically increasing environment diversity and iterative self-evolution is a promising path toward more general and robust AI agents.

title: Concurrent Criterion Validation of a Validity Screen for LLM Confidence Signals via Selective Prediction
comment: 11 pages, 4 figures, 2 tables. Companion to arXiv:2604.15702
summary: The validity screen (Cacioli, 2026d, 2026e) classifies LLM confidence signals as Valid, Indeterminate, or Invalid. We test whether these classifications predict selective prediction performance. Twenty frontier LLMs from seven families were evaluated on 524 items across six cognitive tracks. Valid models show mean Type 2 AUROC = .624 (SD = .048). Invalid models show mean AUROC = .357 (SD = .231). Cohen's d = 2.81, p = .002. The tiers order monotonically: Invalid (.357) < Indeterminate (.554) < Valid (.624). Split-half cross-validation yields median d = 1.77, P(d > 0) = 1.0 across 1,000 splits. The three-tier classification accounts for 47% of the variance in AUROC. DeepSeek-R1 drops from 85.3% accuracy at full coverage to 11.3% at 10% coverage. The screen predicts the criterion. For selective prediction, the screen matters.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether a validity screen for LLM confidence signals (classifying models as Valid, Indeterminate, or Invalid) predicts performance in selective prediction tasks, finding that the classification strongly correlates with Type 2 AUROC and thus is useful for identifying models that will perform well when allowed to abstain.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used in settings where they can choose when to answer or abstain (selective prediction), reliable confidence estimation becomes crucial.
