However, not all models’ internal confidence signals are well calibrated or trustworthy. The author previously proposed a "validity screen" to categorize models’ confidence signals, but it needs empirical validation: does this simple screen actually predict which models will perform well in selective prediction scenarios?
AI.method: The authors apply the previously defined validity screen to 20 frontier LLMs from seven different model families, classifying each model’s confidence signals as Valid, Indeterminate, or Invalid. They then evaluate the models on 524 items across six cognitive tracks, measuring selective prediction performance via Type 2 AUROC (the ability of confidence to discriminate correct from incorrect answers). They compare AUROC across the three validity tiers, perform statistical tests (Cohen’s d, p-values), and use split-half cross-validation (1,000 random splits) to assess robustness. They also examine coverage–accuracy tradeoffs for specific models (e.g., DeepSeek-R1).
AI.result: Models classified as Valid by the screen achieve substantially higher Type 2 AUROC (mean = .624, SD = .048) than Invalid models (mean = .357, SD = .231), with a very large effect size (Cohen’s d = 2.81, p = .002). Performance orders monotonically across tiers: Invalid (.357) < Indeterminate (.554) < Valid (.624). Split-half cross-validation yields a median effect size d = 1.77 with P(d > 0) = 1.0 across 1,000 splits, indicating stable predictive value of the screen. The three-tier classification explains 47% of variance in AUROC. A case study shows that DeepSeek-R1’s accuracy collapses from 85.3% at full coverage to 11.3% at 10% coverage, illustrating the importance of trustworthy confidence for selective prediction.
AI.conclusion: The validity screen is a strong predictor of selective prediction performance across frontier LLMs: models whose confidence signals pass the screen tend to have much better metacognitive discrimination (Type 2 AUROC) than those that fail it, and the three-tier classification captures nearly half the variance in AUROC. This supports using the screen as a practical, model-agnostic tool for deciding which LLMs are suitable for selective prediction deployments, where accurate self-knowledge about correctness is essential. In such contexts, passing the validity screen "matters" in a substantial and quantifiable way.

title: Do LLMs Use Cultural Knowledge Without Being Told? A Multilingual Evaluation of Implicit Pragmatic Adaptation
summary: Many benchmarks show that large language models can answer direct questions about culture. We study a different question: do they also change how they speak when culture is only implied by the situation? We evaluate 60 culturally grounded conversational scenarios across five languages in three conditions: a neutral baseline (Prompt A), an explicit cultural instruction (Prompt B), and implicit situational cueing (Prompt C). We score responses on 12 pragmatic features covering deference to authority, individual-versus-group framing, and uncertainty management. We define Pragmatic Context Sensitivity (PCS) as the fraction of the Prompt A->B shift that reappears under Prompt A->C. Across four deployed LLMs and five languages (English, German, Hindi, Nepali, Urdu), the primary stable-only PCS mean is 0.196 (SD = 0.113), indicating that the models recover only about one-fifth of the pragmatic shift they can produce when instructed explicitly. Transfer is strongest for authority-related cues (0.299) and weakest for individual-versus-group framing (0.120). Uncertainty-related behaviour is mixed: hedging density exhibits negative explicit gaps in all five languages, suggesting that alignment training actively suppresses the target behaviour. Because Hindi and Urdu share core grammar yet index distinct cultural communities, we use them as a natural control; a paired analysis finds no reliable baseline difference (t = 0.96, p = 0.339, dz = 0.06), suggesting that models respond primarily to linguistic structure rather than to the cultural associations a language carries. We argue that multilingual cultural pragmatics is an explicit-versus-implicit deployment problem, not only a factual knowledge problem.

AI.tldr: The paper studies whether large language models (LLMs) adapt their conversational style to implied (implicit) cultural context, not just explicit cultural instructions, and finds they only partially do so.
AI.motivation: While many evaluations show that LLMs can answer direct questions about cultural facts or norms, real-world interactions often rely on subtle, implicit cultural cues. It is unclear whether LLMs can pick up such cues and adjust their pragmatic behavior (e.g., politeness, deference, collectivist vs. individualist framing, and hedging). This gap matters for trustworthy, culturally competent deployment across languages and communities.
AI.method: The authors construct 60 conversational scenarios that embed culturally grounded expectations across five languages (English, German, Hindi, Nepali, Urdu). Each scenario is tested under three prompt conditions: (A) a neutral baseline with no culture cue, (B) an explicit instruction to follow a target culture’s norms, and (C) an implicit situational cue suggesting that culture. Responses from four deployed LLMs are coded on 12 pragmatic features related to authority, individual-vs-group framing, and uncertainty management. They define Pragmatic Context Sensitivity (PCS) as the fraction of the style shift from A to B that is recovered in A to C. They also perform a paired comparison of Hindi vs. Urdu responses to disentangle linguistic vs. cultural effects.
AI.result: Across models and languages, the mean PCS is about 0.196 (SD = 0.113), meaning that implicit cues trigger only about one-fifth of the pragmatic adaptation observed under explicit cultural instructions. Authority-related cues transfer best (PCS ≈ 0.299), individual-vs-group framing worst (≈ 0.120), and uncertainty-related behavior is inconsistent. Hedging density shows negative explicit gaps, implying alignment training suppresses hedging even when culturally expected. The Hindi–Urdu comparison shows no reliable baseline difference (t = 0.96, p = 0.339, dz = 0.06), despite the languages indexing different cultural communities, suggesting that models are driven more by linguistic form than by sociocultural associations of the language.
AI.conclusion: LLMs do not robustly infer and adapt to implicit cultural context in conversation; they mainly adjust pragmatics when cultures are named explicitly. Their behavior appears governed more by surface linguistic structure and generic alignment objectives than by rich, community-specific cultural pragmatics. Consequently, multilingual cultural competence in LLMs should be viewed as an explicit-versus-implicit deployment and conditioning challenge, not merely as a deficit in stored cultural knowledge.

title: RePrompT: Recurrent Prompt Tuning for Integrating Structured EHR Encoders with Large Language Models
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have shown strong promise for mining Electronic Health Records (EHRs) by reasoning over longitudinal clinical information to capture context-rich patient trajectories. However, leveraging LLMs for structured EHRs (e.g., standardized diagnosis and medication codes) presents two key challenges. First, translating time-stamped EHR sequences into plain text can obscure both temporal structure and code identities, weakening the ability to capture code co-occurrence and longitudinal regularities. Second, unlike cohort-trained predictive models that learn a shared, task-aligned representation space across patients, LLMs are often applied in a case-isolated inference setting where each patient is processed independently without leveraging population-level patterns. To address these challenges, we introduce RePrompT, a time-aware LLM framework that integrates structured EHR encoders through prompt tuning, without modifying underlying architectures. Specifically, RePrompT recurrently incorporates latent states from prior visits to preserve longitudinal information, and injects population-level information through trainable prompt tokens derived from a cohort-trained, task-aligned EHR encoder. Experiments on MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV demonstrate that RePrompT consistently outperforms both EHR-based and LLM-based baselines across multiple clinical prediction tasks.

AI.tldr: RePrompT is a time-aware framework that augments LLMs with structured EHR encoders via prompt tuning to better model longitudinal and population-level patterns in clinical prediction.
AI.motivation: Using LLMs on structured, time-stamped EHR codes is difficult because converting them to plain text loses temporal and code-identity information, and standard LLM use ignores population-level patterns that cohort-trained models exploit. There is a need to combine LLMs’ reasoning strengths with representations that respect EHR temporal structure and cohort information.
AI.method: RePrompT connects a task-aligned EHR encoder to an LLM via prompt tuning, without changing the LLM architecture. It recurrently feeds latent states from previous visits into the LLM to preserve longitudinal information and includes trainable prompt tokens derived from the cohort-trained encoder to inject population-level patterns into each patient’s input.
AI.result: On MIMIC-III and MIMIC-IV datasets, RePrompT yields consistently better performance than both traditional EHR-based models and plain LLM-based approaches across several clinical prediction tasks, indicating improved use of temporal and cohort information.
AI.conclusion: Coupling LLMs with time-aware, cohort-trained EHR encoders through prompt tuning enables better modeling of structured EHR data, overcoming limitations of naive text conversion and case-isolated inference, and yields superior clinical prediction performance.

title: One Pass for All: A Discrete Diffusion Model for Knowledge Graph Triple Set Prediction
summary: Knowledge Graphs (KGs) are composed of triples, and the goal of Knowledge Graph Completion (KGC) is to infer the missing factual triples. Traditional KGC tasks predict missing elements in a triple given one or two of its elements. As a more realistic task, the Triple Set Prediction (TSP) task aims to infer the set of missing triples conditioned only on the observed knowledge graph, without assuming any partial information about the missing triples. Existing TSP methods predict the set of missing triples in a triple-by-triple manner, falling short in capturing the dependencies among the predicted triples to ensure consistency. To address this issue, we propose a novel discrete diffusion model termed DiffTSP that treats TSP as a generative task. DiffTSP progressively adds noise to the KG through a discrete diffusion process, achieved by masking relational edges. The reverse process then gradually recovers the complete KG conditioned on the incomplete graph. To this end, we design a structure-aware denoising network that integrates a relational context encoder with a relational graph diffusion transformer for knowledge graph generation. DiffTSP can generate the complete set of triples in a one-pass manner while ensuring the dependencies among the predicted triples. Our approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on three public datasets. Code: https://github.com/ADMIS-TONGJI/DiffTSP.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DiffTSP, a discrete diffusion-based model that treats triple set prediction (TSP) in knowledge graphs as a generative task, enabling one-pass prediction of a consistent set of missing triples and achieving state-of-the-art results.
AI.motivation: Existing Knowledge Graph Completion mainly predicts a single missing element in a triple, assuming partial information about the missing fact, which is unrealistic for many applications. The more realistic Triple Set Prediction task must infer all missing triples from only the observed graph, but current TSP methods predict triples independently, ignoring dependencies among missing facts and leading to inconsistencies. The paper aims to build a model that can jointly generate a coherent set of missing triples conditioned on the incomplete KG.
AI.method: The authors cast TSP as a discrete diffusion generative modeling problem over knowledge graphs. They define a forward diffusion process that progressively corrupts the KG by masking relational edges (triples), and a reverse process that learns to denoise and reconstruct the full KG from the incomplete graph. To realize this, they design a structure-aware denoising network combining (1) a relational context encoder to capture local and global relational patterns and (2) a relational graph diffusion transformer to model structural dependencies during generation. The model, called DiffTSP, generates the entire set of missing triples in a single pass rather than one-by-one.
AI.result: DiffTSP is evaluated on three public knowledge graph datasets for the TSP task and outperforms prior TSP and KGC baselines, achieving state-of-the-art metrics (e.g., on triple prediction quality and set-level consistency). The experiments demonstrate that modeling dependencies among triples via discrete diffusion leads to better and more coherent triple set predictions.
AI.conclusion: Treating triple set prediction as a discrete diffusion-based generative task allows joint modeling of dependencies among missing triples and enables one-pass generation of the entire missing triple set. The proposed DiffTSP framework, with its structure-aware denoising network, provides a more realistic and effective solution for completing knowledge graphs, achieving state-of-the-art performance on multiple benchmarks and offering a promising direction for generative KG completion methods.

title: MHSafeEval: Role-Aware Interaction-Level Evaluation of Mental Health Safety in Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly explored as scalable tools for mental health counseling, yet evaluating their safety remains challenging due to the interactional and context-dependent nature of clinical harm. Existing evaluation frameworks predominantly assess isolated responses using coarse-grained taxonomies or static datasets, limiting their ability to diagnose how harms emerge and accumulate over multi-turn counseling interactions. In this work, we introduce R-MHSafe, a role-aware mental health safety taxonomy that characterizes clinically significant harm in terms of the interactional roles an AI counselor adopts, including perpetrator, instigator, facilitator, or enabler, combined with clinically grounded harm categories. Then, we propose MHSafeEval, a closed-loop, agent-based evaluation framework that formulates safety assessment as trajectory-level discovery of harm through adversarial multi-turn interactions, guided by role-aware modeling. Using R-MHSafe and MHSafeEval, we conduct a large-scale evaluation across state-of-the-art LLMs. Our results reveal substantial role-dependent and cumulative safety failures that are systematically missed by existing static benchmarks, and show that our framework significantly improves failure-mode coverage and diagnostic granularity.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a new taxonomy and evaluation framework to detect how large language models can cause harm over multi-turn mental health counseling conversations, revealing failures that static, single-turn benchmarks miss.
AI.motivation: Current safety evaluations for LLMs in mental health contexts mostly inspect single, isolated responses, using broad labels or static datasets. This misses how clinically significant harm often emerges cumulatively and in context, through multi-turn dialogue and shifting conversational roles. The authors want a framework that can capture interactional, role-dependent harms that better reflects real counseling scenarios.
AI.method: They first design R-MHSafe, a "role-aware" mental health safety taxonomy that labels potential harms by both clinical harm type and the interactional role the AI counselor plays (perpetrator, instigator, facilitator, enabler). Then they build MHSafeEval, an agent-based, closed-loop evaluation system in which agents engage LLMs in adversarial, multi-turn counseling conversations to elicit and detect harmful behaviors at the trajectory level. The framework uses the role/harm taxonomy to analyze how and when harms arise along the conversation.
AI.result: Applying R-MHSafe and MHSafeEval to multiple state-of-the-art LLMs, they find substantial safety failures that depend on the role the model is playing and that accumulate over multi-turn exchanges. These failures are largely undetected by existing static, single-turn benchmarks. Their framework provides richer coverage of failure modes and more fine-grained, diagnostic information about how harms appear and evolve in dialogue.
AI.conclusion: Role- and trajectory-aware evaluation is necessary to meaningfully assess LLM safety in mental health counseling. The proposed taxonomy and closed-loop, multi-turn evaluation framework surface critical, clinically relevant harms that static evaluations miss, enabling better diagnosis and ultimately informing safer LLM design and deployment for mental health support.

title: Training and Agentic Inference Strategies for LLM-based Manim Animation Generation
summary: Generating programmatic animation using libraries such as Manim presents unique challenges for Large Language Models (LLMs), requiring spatial reasoning, temporal sequencing, and familiarity with domain-specific APIs that are underrepresented in general pre-training data. A systematic study of how training and inference strategies interact in this setting is lacking in current research. This study introduces ManimTrainer, a training pipeline that combines Supervised Fine-tuning (SFT) with Reinforcement Learning (RL) based Group Relative Policy Optimisation (GRPO) using a unified reward signal that fuses code and visual assessment signals, and ManimAgent, an inference pipeline featuring Renderer-in-the-loop (RITL) and API documentation-augmented RITL (RITL-DOC) strategies. Using these techniques, this study presents the first unified training and inference study for text-to-code-to-video transformation with Manim. It evaluates 17 open-source sub-30B LLMs across nine combinations of training and inference strategies using ManimBench. Results show that SFT generally improves code quality, while GRPO enhances visual outputs and increases the models' responsiveness to extrinsic signals during self-correction at inference time. The Qwen 3 Coder 30B model with GRPO and RITL-DOC achieved the highest overall performance, with a 94% Render Success Rate (RSR) and 85.7% Visual Similarity (VS) to reference videos, surpassing the baseline GPT-4.1 model by +3 percentage points in VS. Additionally, the analysis shows that the correlation between code and visual metrics strengthens with SFT and GRPO but weakens with inference-time enhancements, highlighting the complementary roles of training and agentic inference strategies in Manim animation generation.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to best train and run LLMs so they can generate correct Manim code and good animations from text, combining supervised fine-tuning, RL, and agent-style inference with a renderer and docs in the loop.
AI.motivation: LLMs struggle with generating programmatic animations in Manim because it requires spatial and temporal reasoning and knowledge of a niche API that is rare in pretraining data. There is no systematic study of how different training (SFT, RL) and inference-time strategies interact for this text-to-code-to-video task, so practitioners lack guidance on what works best.
AI.method: They build ManimTrainer, a training pipeline that first applies supervised fine-tuning and then Group Relative Policy Optimisation (a form of RL) using a unified reward that measures both code correctness and visual similarity of the rendered video. They also build ManimAgent, an inference pipeline that uses Renderer-in-the-loop (iteratively rendering and self-correcting) and an enhanced variant that injects Manim API documentation during this loop. They then evaluate 17 open-source (<30B) LLMs across nine combinations of training and inference methods on ManimBench, using metrics for render success and visual similarity to reference videos.
AI.result: Supervised fine-tuning mainly improves code quality, while GRPO improves the final visual quality of animations and makes models more responsive to feedback during self-correction in inference. The best configuration is Qwen 3 Coder 30B with GRPO and the documentation-augmented renderer-in-the-loop (RITL-DOC), reaching 94% render success and 85.7% visual similarity, slightly outperforming GPT-4.1 on visual similarity by 3 percentage points. They also find that SFT and GRPO strengthen the correlation between code and visual metrics, whereas inference-time tricks (like RITL/RITL-DOC) weaken that correlation.
AI.conclusion: A combined training and inference approach is crucial for high-quality Manim animation generation: SFT improves code, RL (GRPO) pushes visual fidelity and feedback sensitivity, and agentic inference with renderer and docs in the loop gives additional gains but alters the relationship between code and visual quality. Training-time and inference-time methods are complementary, and properly tuned open-source <30B LLMs can surpass a strong proprietary baseline (GPT-4.1) on this specialized text-to-code-to-video task.

title: Mira-Embeddings-V1: Domain-Adapted Semantic Reranking for Recruitment via LLM-Synthesized Data
summary: Candidate sourcing for recruiters is best viewed as a two-stage retrieval and reranking pipeline with recall as the primary objective under a limited review budget. An upstream production retriever first returns a candidate shortlist for each job description (JD), and our goal is to rerank that shortlist so that qualified candidates appear as high as possible. We present mira-embeddings-v1, a semantic reranking system for the recruitment domain that reshapes the embedding space with LLM-synthesized training data and corrects boundary confusions with a lightweight reranking head. Starting from real JDs, we build a five-stage prompt pipeline to generate diverse positive and hard negative samples that sculpt the semantic space from multiple angles. We then apply a two-round LoRA adaptation: JD--JD contrastive training followed by JD--CV triplet alignment on a heterogeneous text dataset. Importantly, these gains require no large-scale manually labeled industrial training pairs: a modest set of real JDs is expanded into supervision through LLM synthesis. Finally, a BoundaryHead MLP reranks the Top-K results to distinguish between roles that share the same title but differ in scope. On a local pool of 300 real JDs with candidates from an upstream production retriever, mira-embeddings-v1 improves Recall@50 from 68.89% (baseline) to 77.55% while lifting Precision@10 from 35.77% to 39.62%. On a supportive global pool over 44,138 candidates judged by a Qwen3-32B rubric, it achieves Recall@200 of 0.7047 versus 0.5969 for the baseline. These results show that LLM-synthesized supervision with boundary-aware reranking yields robust gains without a heavy cross-encoder.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces mira-embeddings-v1, an LLM-augmented semantic reranking system that boosts recruiter candidate search recall and precision without requiring large-scale human labels.
AI.motivation: Recruiters use a two-stage retrieval + reranking pipeline to find suitable candidates for job descriptions, but existing retrievers struggle to rank qualified candidates high enough, especially under limited review budgets and with roles that share similar titles but differ in scope. There is also a shortage of large, manually labeled training data specific to recruitment, making it hard to train powerful rerankers without heavy cross-encoders.
AI.method: The authors design mira-embeddings-v1, built on an embedding model adapted using LLM-synthesized supervision and a boundary-aware reranking head. They start from real job descriptions (JDs) and construct a five-stage prompting pipeline with a large language model to generate diverse positive and hard negative samples, shaping the semantic space of JDs and CVs from different perspectives. Then they perform a two-round LoRA-based fine-tuning: (1) JD–JD contrastive training to better structure relationships among job descriptions; (2) JD–CV triplet training on heterogeneous textual data to align job descriptions with candidate profiles. Finally, they add a lightweight MLP "BoundaryHead" that reranks the top-K shortlist from a production retriever, focusing on resolving fine-grained distinctions between similar titles with different responsibilities.
AI.result: On a local evaluation of 300 real JDs with candidates from a production retriever, mira-embeddings-v1 improves Recall@50 from 68.89% to 77.55% and Precision@10 from 35.77% to 39.62% compared to the baseline retriever. On a larger global pool with 44,138 candidates evaluated via a Qwen3-32B-based rubric, it raises Recall@200 from 0.5969 to 0.7047, substantially outperforming the baseline.
AI.conclusion: LLM-synthesized supervision combined with a boundary-aware reranking head enables significant gains in recall and precision for recruiter candidate sourcing, without relying on costly large-scale human-labeled datasets or heavy cross-encoders. This demonstrates that structured prompt pipelines and light adaptation of embedding models can effectively improve domain-specific retrieval and reranking performance in recruitment.

title: The implicated scientist: on the role of AI researchers in the development of weapons systems
comment: Presented as an oral talk and a poster at the AI for Peace workshop at ICLR 2026
summary: Artificial intelligence (AI) technologies are increasingly used in modern weapons systems. Notably, these systems have recently been involved in mass killings and destruction at scale. Furthermore, there is currently a strong interest and competition among powerful players to accelerate the proliferation of weapons with automated or AI-based components, a phenomenon known as AI arms race. This competition poses a risk of causing even more deaths and devastation in the future, as well as increased power and wealth inequality. In this work, we aim to shed light on the role of AI researchers as implicated subjects in the harms caused by weapons enabled by AI technologies. We investigate and discuss the specifics of this implication and explore ways to transfigure this position of implication into one of differentiated, long-distance solidarity with the victims of technologically fortified injustices.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how AI researchers are implicated in harms from AI-enabled weapons and explores how they can shift toward solidarity with victims of these technologies.
AI.motivation: AI is increasingly embedded in modern weapon systems, which have been used for mass killings and large-scale destruction. At the same time, an AI arms race among powerful actors accelerates the development and deployment of such systems, raising risks of future devastation and worsening inequalities. Despite this, the ethical and political role of AI researchers whose work underpins these weapons is under-examined; the paper seeks to address this gap.
AI.method: The authors conduct a conceptual and ethical analysis of the role of AI researchers in the development and deployment of AI-enabled weapons. They examine the notion of ‘implicated subjects’ and apply it to AI researchers, then explore theoretical and practical pathways to transform this implicated position into one of critical, long-distance solidarity with affected victims.
AI.result: The paper clarifies how AI researchers are structurally entangled in the harms of AI-enabled weapons, even when they are not direct perpetrators or decision-makers. It identifies specific forms of implication (e.g., through funding structures, institutional affiliations, and dual-use research) and outlines possible practices and orientations that could enable AI researchers to act in solidarity with people harmed by AI-powered warfare and repression.
AI.conclusion: AI researchers occupy a morally and politically implicated position in the harms produced by AI-enabled weapons and the broader AI arms race. Recognizing this implication is a precondition for transforming their stance into one of differentiated, long-distance solidarity with victims. The authors argue that AI researchers should critically reflect on their involvement in military and security applications, resist harmful forms of AI proliferation, and cultivate practices that align their work with those suffering from technologically reinforced injustices.

title: HiRAS: A Hierarchical Multi-Agent Framework for Paper-to-Code Generation and Execution
summary: Recent advances in large language models have highlighted their potential to automate computational research, particularly reproducing experimental results. However, existing approaches still use fixed sequential agent pipelines with weak global coordination, which limits their robustness and overall performance. In this work, we propose Hierarchical Research Agent System (HiRAS), a hierarchical multi-agent framework for end-to-end experiment reproduction that employs supervisory manager agents to coordinate specialised agents across fine-grained stages. We also identify limitations in the reference-free evaluation of the Paper2Code benchmark and introduce Paper2Code-Extra (P2C-Ex), a refined protocol that incorporates repository-level information and better aligns with the original reference-based metric. We conduct extensive evaluation, validating the effectiveness and robustness of our proposed methods, and observing improvements, including >10\% relative performance gain beyond the previous state-of-the-art using open-source backbone models and significantly reduced hallucination in evaluation. Our work is available on GitHub: https://github.com/KOU-199024/HiRAS.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces HiRAS, a hierarchical multi-agent system that improves the robustness and performance of reproducing computational experiments from papers, and proposes a refined evaluation protocol (P2C-Ex) for the Paper2Code benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing large language model-based systems for automating experiment reproduction rely on fixed, linear agent pipelines with weak global coordination, which leads to limited robustness, suboptimal performance, and issues in reliably assessing results due to shortcomings in reference-free evaluation metrics for Paper2Code.
AI.method: The authors design the Hierarchical Research Agent System (HiRAS), where supervisory manager agents coordinate specialized sub-agents across fine-grained stages of the experiment reproduction workflow. In parallel, they analyze the limitations of current reference-free evaluation on the Paper2Code benchmark and develop Paper2Code-Extra (P2C-Ex), a protocol that incorporates repository-level information and more closely mirrors the original reference-based evaluation metric. They then perform extensive experiments to benchmark HiRAS and P2C-Ex against prior approaches using open-source backbone models.
AI.result: HiRAS achieves over 10% relative performance improvement compared to the previous state-of-the-art on the Paper2Code benchmark when using open-source models, and the new P2C-Ex protocol leads to significantly reduced hallucination in evaluation. Overall, their system shows improved effectiveness and robustness in end-to-end experiment reproduction.
AI.conclusion: A hierarchical multi-agent architecture with manager-supervisor coordination (HiRAS) can substantially enhance end-to-end experiment reproduction from papers, especially when paired with a more faithful evaluation protocol (P2C-Ex) that leverages repository-level information. These contributions yield notable gains over prior state-of-the-art systems and produce more reliable, less hallucination-prone evaluations.

title: Learning from Less: Measuring the Effectiveness of RLVR in Low Data and Compute Regimes
summary: Fine-tuning Large Language Models (LLMs) typically relies on large quantities of high-quality annotated data, or questions with well-defined ground truth answers in the case of Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR). While previous work has explored the benefits to model reasoning capabilities by scaling both data and compute used for RLVR, these results lack applicability in many real-world settings where annotated data and accessible compute may be scarce. In this work, we present a comprehensive empirical study of open-source Small Language Model (SLM) performance after RLVR in low data regimes. Across three novel datasets covering number counting problems, graph reasoning, and spatial reasoning, we characterize how model performance scales with dataset size, diversity, and complexity. We demonstrate that (1) procedural datasets allow for fine-grained evaluation and training dataset development with controllable properties (size, diversity, and complexity), (2) under RLVR, models trained on lower complexity tasks can generalize to higher complexity tasks, and (3) training on mixed complexity datasets is associated with the greatest benefits in low data regimes, providing up to 5x sample efficiency versus training on easy tasks. These findings inspire future work on the development of data scaling laws for RLVR and the use of procedural data generators to further understand effective data development for efficient LLM fine-tuning.

AI.tldr: The paper empirically studies how Small Language Models behave under Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) when only small amounts of data are available, using procedurally generated tasks in number counting, graph reasoning, and spatial reasoning.
AI.motivation: Fine-tuning LLMs usually needs lots of labeled data or clear ground-truth answers. Prior RLVR work focuses on large data and compute, which is unrealistic for many practical scenarios with limited annotated data and compute. The authors want to understand how to efficiently fine-tune smaller open-source models in such low-data regimes and how dataset properties like size, diversity, and complexity affect performance.
AI.method: They run a systematic empirical study on open-source Small Language Models trained with RLVR. They create three procedural benchmark datasets (for counting, graph, and spatial reasoning) where they can precisely control dataset size, diversity, and task complexity. They then train the SLMs with RLVR on datasets of different sizes and complexity mixes, and evaluate performance and generalization to harder tasks.
AI.result: (1) Procedurally generated datasets enable controlled training and evaluation with tunable size, diversity, and complexity. (2) Under RLVR, models trained only on lower-complexity tasks are still able to generalize to higher-complexity tasks. (3) In low data regimes, training on mixed-complexity datasets yields the best performance and up to 5x higher sample efficiency compared to training only on easy tasks.
AI.conclusion: Procedural data with controllable properties is a powerful tool for studying and improving RLVR-based fine-tuning of SLMs in low-data settings. Models can generalize from simpler to harder tasks, and mixing task complexities in the training data substantially improves sample efficiency. These insights suggest directions for data scaling laws in RLVR and for designing procedural data generators to achieve more efficient and effective fine-tuning of language models.

title: Reverse Constitutional AI: A Framework for Controllable Toxic Data Generation via Probability-Clamped RLAIF
comment: Accepted to Findings of ACL 2026. 10 pages, 6 figures. Code and data available at https://github.com/ZeroLoss-Lab/R-CAI
summary: Ensuring the safety of large language models (LLMs) requires robust red teaming, yet the systematic synthesis of high-quality toxic data remains under-explored. We propose Reverse Constitutional AI (R-CAI), a framework for automated and controllable adversarial data generation that moves beyond isolated jailbreak prompts. By inverting a harmless constitution into a constitution of toxicity and iteratively refining model outputs through a critique--revision pipeline, R-CAI enables scalable synthesis of multi-dimensional adversarial data without human annotation. Optimizing solely for toxicity-related rewards, however, can lead to reward hacking and degraded semantic coherence. To address this challenge, we introduce probability clamping within reinforcement learning from AI feedback, which stabilizes adversarial optimization while preserving adversarial intent. Experiments demonstrate that R-CAI generates diverse, high-quality toxic data and that probability clamping substantially improves semantic coherence (15%) without sacrificing adversarial strength. Overall, R-CAI provides a fully automated framework for red teaming data generation and systematic safety evaluation of aligned language models.

AI.tldr: R-CAI is a framework to automatically generate high-quality adversarial (toxic) data for red teaming LLMs using an inverted constitution and critique–revision loops, plus a probability clamping trick in RL to keep outputs coherent while still adversarial.
AI.motivation: Safety evaluation of LLMs needs strong, systematic red teaming data, but current approaches rely on scattered jailbreak prompts and expensive human-crafted toxic data. There is limited work on generating controlled, diverse, high-quality adversarial content at scale in an automated way, and naive optimization for toxicity tends to cause reward hacking and incoherent outputs.
AI.method: They introduce Reverse Constitutional AI (R-CAI): start from a harmless constitution and invert it into a toxicity-focused constitution; use this to guide an automated critique–revision pipeline that iteratively refines model outputs into stronger adversarial examples without human labels. For training, they use reinforcement learning from AI feedback with a toxicity-related reward, but add a new mechanism called probability clamping that constrains how much token probabilities can be distorted during optimization, aiming to preserve fluency and coherence while still maximizing adversarial/toxic characteristics.
AI.result: R-CAI produces diverse and high-quality toxic/adversarial datasets without manual annotation. In experiments, adding probability clamping substantially improves semantic coherence of generated adversarial text by about 15% while maintaining the level of adversarial strength. The generated data are effective for red teaming and evaluating safety alignment of LLMs.
AI.conclusion: R-CAI offers a fully automated, scalable method for generating multi-dimensional adversarial/toxic data for LLM red teaming, moving beyond one-off jailbreak prompts. The proposed probability clamping mechanism mitigates reward hacking by stabilizing the adversarial optimization process, yielding coherent yet still adversarial outputs, and thus supports more systematic safety testing of aligned language models.

title: Six Llamas: Comparative Religious Ethics Through LoRA-Adapted Language Models
comment: 51 pages, 14 figures. We present Six Llamas, a comparative study examining whether Llama-3.1-8B models fine-tuned on distinct religious corpora encode systematically different patterns of ethical reasoning. Five LoRA-adapted variants are constructed for Christianity, Islam, Judaism, Hinduism, and Buddhism. For theoretical background on the condensate comparative method, see arXiv:2603.07329
summary:
We present Six Llamas, a comparative study examining whether large language models fine-tuned on distinct religious corpora encode systematically different patterns of ethical reasoning. Six variants of Meta-Llama-3.1-8B are constructed: one unmodified control and five LoRA-adapted models trained exclusively on the sacred and theological texts of Christianity, Islam, Judaism, Hinduism, or Buddhism. All six models are probed with an identical battery of 17 standardized ethical prompts spanning moral dilemmas, game-theoretic scenarios, public policy questions, and moral-psychological self-assessments. To assess robustness and reproducibility, we implement a multi-temperature sampling design spanning ten temperature settings. We compute response consistency metrics, pairwise inter-model agreement rates, temperature sensitivity coefficients across four prompt domains, and run-to-run stability analyses.
  Findings show that LoRA-adapted models produce ethical reasoning patterns that are (a) systematically differentiated from the base model, (b) consistent with the moral logics of their training traditions, (c) structured along interpretable dimensions in moral-philosophical space, (d) core ethical positions remain stable across temperature variations for high-consensus dilemmas. The Trolley Problem achieves 100% consistency across all models and temperatures, while (e) tradition-specific divergence intensifies at higher temperatures in morally contested domains, and (f) the base model exhibits the highest overall response consistency (mean 88.3%), suggesting LoRA adaptation introduces both tradition-specific signal and increased sampling sensitivity.
  The study offers a proof-of-concept for the condensate comparative method using differentially trained language models as instruments for cultural and ethical analysis and identifies specific criteria for falsification and planned extensions.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how fine-tuning the same LLM on different religious texts changes its ethical reasoning, comparing six versions across many moral prompts and temperatures.
AI.motivation: To understand whether and how large language models, when fine-tuned on distinct religious corpora, systematically adopt different ethical reasoning patterns, and to propose a method for using such models as tools for cultural and ethical analysis.
AI.method: The authors take Meta-Llama-3.1-8B and create six variants: one base model and five LoRA-fine-tuned models on religious corpora from Christianity, Islam, Judaism, Hinduism, and Buddhism. They evaluate all six models on 17 standardized ethical prompts covering moral dilemmas, game theory, public policy, and moral-psychological self-assessments. They probe each model under ten different temperature settings, and compute response consistency metrics, pairwise agreement between models, temperature sensitivity coefficients across four prompt domains, and run-to-run stability.
AI.result: LoRA-fine-tuned models differ systematically from the base model and reflect the moral logics of their respective religious traditions. Their responses form interpretable structures in a moral-philosophical space. Core ethical judgments on high-consensus dilemmas (e.g., the Trolley Problem) remain stable across temperatures, achieving 100% consistency. Tradition-specific divergences grow with higher temperatures in morally contested areas. The base model has the highest overall response consistency (mean 88.3%), indicating that religious LoRA adaptation injects tradition-specific bias but also increases sampling sensitivity and variability.
AI.conclusion: Fine-tuning LLMs on different religious traditions produces distinct, stable patterns of ethical reasoning aligned with those traditions, while the original base model remains more consistent overall. The work demonstrates a "condensate comparative" method that treats differentially trained LLMs as instruments to study cultural and ethical variation, and it outlines criteria for falsifying this approach and directions for expanding the method.

title: SPENCE: A Syntactic Probe for Detecting Contamination in NL2SQL Benchmarks
summary: Large language models (LLMs) have achieved strong performance on natural language to SQL (NL2SQL) benchmarks, yet their reported accuracy may be inflated by contamination from benchmark queries or structurally similar patterns seen during training. We introduce SPENCE (Syntactic Probing and Evaluation of NL2SQL Contamination Effects), a controlled syntactic probing framework for detecting and quantifying such contamination. SPENCE systematically generates syntactic variants of test queries for four widely used NL2SQL datasets-Spider, SParC, CoSQL, and the newer BIRD benchmark. We use SPENCE to evaluate multiple high-capacity LLMs under execution-based scoring. For each model, we measure changes in execution accuracy across increasing levels of syntactic divergence and quantify rank sensitivity using Kendall's tau with bootstrap confidence intervals. By aligning these robustness trends with benchmark release dates, we observe a clear temporal gradient: older benchmarks such as Spider exhibit the strongest negative values and thus the highest likelihood of training leakage, whereas the more recent BIRD dataset shows minimal sensitivity and appears largely uncontaminated. Together, these findings highlight the importance of temporally contextualized, syntactic-probing evaluation for trustworthy NL2SQL benchmarking.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SPENCE, a syntactic probing framework to detect and quantify training contamination in NL2SQL benchmarks for large language models.
AI.motivation: Reported high performance of LLMs on NL2SQL benchmarks might be artificially inflated because models may have seen benchmark queries or similar structures during training, undermining the trustworthiness of evaluation results.
AI.method: The authors design SPENCE, which systematically generates controlled syntactic variants of existing NL2SQL test queries from four benchmarks (Spider, SParC, CoSQL, BIRD). They then evaluate several large LLMs on these variants using execution-based accuracy, measuring how performance changes as syntactic divergence from the original queries increases, and compute rank sensitivity via Kendall's tau with bootstrap confidence intervals. They analyze robustness trends aligned with benchmark release dates to infer contamination risk.
AI.result: Models show stronger degradation in execution accuracy and more negative Kendall's tau values on older benchmarks (e.g., Spider) as queries are syntactically varied, indicating higher sensitivity and likely contamination. Newer benchmarks such as BIRD show minimal sensitivity to syntactic perturbations, suggesting low or no contamination.
AI.conclusion: Syntactic robustness patterns, when considered in light of benchmark release times, can reveal likely training data contamination in NL2SQL evaluations. SPENCE provides a principled, temporally contextualized framework for probing and quantifying such contamination, and the study implies that older NL2SQL benchmarks are less reliable indicators of true generalization than newer, less contaminated ones.

title: Using large language models for embodied planning introduces systematic safety risks
summary: Large language models are increasingly used as planners for robotic systems, yet how safely they plan remains an open question. To evaluate safe planning systematically, we introduce DESPITE, a benchmark of 12,279 tasks spanning physical and normative dangers with fully deterministic validation. Across 23 models, even near-perfect planning ability does not ensure safety: the best-planning model fails to produce a valid plan on only 0.4% of tasks but produces dangerous plans on 28.3%. Among 18 open-source models from 3B to 671B parameters, planning ability improves substantially with scale (0.4-99.3%) while safety awareness remains relatively flat (38-57%). We identify a multiplicative relationship between these two capacities, showing that larger models complete more tasks safely primarily through improved planning, not through better danger avoidance. Three proprietary reasoning models reach notably higher safety awareness (71-81%), while non-reasoning proprietary models and open-source reasoning models remain below 57%. As planning ability approaches saturation for frontier models, improving safety awareness becomes a central challenge for deploying language-model planners in robotic systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DESPITE, a large benchmark to evaluate how safely large language models plan for robots, showing that strong planning does not guarantee safe plans and that safety awareness lags behind planning ability, especially in open-source models.
AI.motivation: Large language models are widely used as high-level planners for robotic systems, but it is unclear whether their plans are actually safe, especially when dealing with physical or normative dangers. Existing evaluations often lack systematic coverage of risks and deterministic validation, making it hard to quantify and compare the safety of different models when used for planning in real-world robotic contexts.
AI.method: The authors build DESPITE, a benchmark of 12,279 planning tasks that cover both physical and normative dangers, each with fully deterministic validation so correctness and safety of a plan can be automatically judged. They then test 23 language models (open-source and proprietary, ranging from 3B to 671B parameters and including reasoning and non-reasoning variants) to separately measure planning ability (producing valid plans) and safety awareness (avoiding dangerous plans). They analyze how these two capacities scale with model size and differ across model families and reasoning capabilities, and model their interaction multiplicatively to explain safe task completion rates.
AI.result: The best-planning model fails to generate a valid plan on only 0.4% of tasks but still produces dangerous plans on 28.3% of them, indicating that high planning performance does not imply safety. Among 18 open-source models, planning ability improves dramatically with scale (from 0.4% to 99.3% valid plans), while safety awareness stays relatively low and flat (38–57%). The authors find that safe completion is well-explained by a multiplicative interaction between planning ability and safety awareness, meaning larger models are safer mainly because they plan better, not because they avoid dangers more effectively. Three proprietary reasoning models achieve substantially higher safety awareness (71–81%), whereas non-reasoning proprietary and open-source reasoning models remain below 57%.
AI.conclusion: Safety-aware planning is not an automatic byproduct of better planning capability in large language models: as models get larger, they become much better at solving tasks but not proportionally better at avoiding dangerous plans. For open-source models, safety awareness plateaus despite scale, and even frontier models that plan almost perfectly still produce a significant fraction of unsafe plans. Therefore, as planning performance saturates, improving safety awareness—through model design, training, or external safeguards—emerges as a key open problem for safely deploying language-model-based planners in robotic systems.

title: Forget What Matters, Keep the Rest: Selective Unlearning of Informative Tokens
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026 Main Conference. 17 pages, 9 figures
summary: Unlearning in large language models (LLMs) has emerged as a promising safeguard against adversarial behaviors. When the forgetting loss is applied uniformly without considering token-level semantic importance, model utility can be unnecessarily degraded. Recent studies have explored token-wise loss regularizers that prioritize informative tokens, but largely rely on ground-truth confidence or external linguistic parsers, which limits their ability to capture contextual information or the model's overall predictive state. Intuitively, function words like "the" primarily serve syntactic roles and are highly predictable with little ambiguity, but informative words admit multiple plausible alternatives with greater uncertainty. Based on this intuition, we propose Entropy-guided Token Weighting (ETW), a token-level unlearning regularizer that uses entropy of the predictive distribution as a proxy for token informativeness. We demonstrate that informative tokens tend to have higher entropy, whereas structural tokens tend to have lower entropy. This behavior enables ETW to achieve more effective unlearning while better preserving model utility than existing token-level approaches.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Entropy-guided Token Weighting (ETW), a token-level unlearning regularizer for LLMs that focuses forgetting on informative, high-entropy tokens to preserve model utility.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM unlearning methods often apply a uniform forgetting loss across tokens, which can harm model utility because not all tokens equally contribute to meaning. Recent token-wise regularizers depend on ground-truth confidence or external parsers, which do not fully capture contextual information or the model’s predictive state. There is a need for a token-importance mechanism that is intrinsic to the model and sensitive to contextual uncertainty.
AI.method: The authors introduce ETW, a token-level weighting scheme for unlearning losses, where weights are derived from the entropy of the model’s predictive distribution at each token position. Tokens with higher predictive entropy, interpreted as more informative and ambiguous content tokens, receive larger weights in the forgetting objective, while low-entropy, predictable structural tokens (e.g., function words) receive smaller weights. This rebalances unlearning pressure toward semantically important tokens.
AI.result: Empirical results show that tokens with empirically higher informativeness exhibit higher predictive entropy, while structural tokens correspond to lower entropy, validating the intuition behind ETW. When integrated into unlearning procedures for LLMs, ETW achieves stronger unlearning on target content while preserving overall model utility better than previous token-level regularization approaches that use ground-truth confidence or external linguistic tools.
AI.conclusion: Entropy-guided Token Weighting offers an effective, model-intrinsic way to focus unlearning on semantically important tokens by using predictive entropy as a proxy for informativeness. This leads to more targeted forgetting with less collateral damage to model utility, suggesting entropy-based token weighting as a promising direction for safer and more precise LLM unlearning methods.

title: A Generalized Synthetic Control Method for Baseline Estimation in Demand Response Services
summary: Baseline estimation is critical to Demand Response (DR) settlement in electricity markets, yet existing machine learning methods remain limited in predictive performance, while methodologies from causal inference and counterfactual prediction are still underutilized in this domain. We introduce a Generalized Synthetic Control Method that builds on the classical Synthetic Control Method (SCM) from econometrics. While SCM provides a powerful framework for counterfactual estimation, classical SCM remains a static estimator: it fits the treated unit as a combination of contemporaneous donor units and therefore ignores predictable temporal structure in the residual error. We develop a generalized SCM framework that transforms baseline estimation into a dynamic counterfactual prediction problem by augmenting the donor representation with exogenous features, lagged treated load, and selected lagged donor signals. This enriched representation allows the estimator to capture autoregressive dependence, delayed donor-response patterns, and error-correction effects beyond the scope of standard SCM. The framework further accommodates nonlinear predictors when linear weighting is inadequate, with the greatest benefit arising in limited-data settings. Experiments on the Ausgrid smart-meter dataset show consistent improvements over classical SCM and strong benchmark methods, with the dominant performance gains driven by dynamic augmentation.

AI.tldr: They propose a generalized, dynamic version of Synthetic Control to better estimate electricity consumption baselines for demand response, achieving higher predictive accuracy than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Accurate baseline estimation is crucial for fairly settling Demand Response in electricity markets, but current machine learning approaches underperform and classical Synthetic Control is static and ignores temporal dependence, limiting its predictive power. The authors aim to leverage ideas from causal inference and counterfactual prediction to address these shortcomings.
AI.method: They extend the classical Synthetic Control Method into a Generalized Synthetic Control framework that treats baseline estimation as a dynamic counterfactual prediction task. The method augments the donor pool representation with exogenous features, lagged values of the treated unit’s load, and selected lagged donor signals. This design captures autoregressive behavior, delayed donor responses, and error-correction dynamics. The framework supports both linear and nonlinear predictors, enabling flexible modeling especially in low-data regimes.
AI.result: On the Ausgrid smart-meter dataset, the generalized method consistently outperforms classical Synthetic Control and several strong benchmark models. The primary gains stem from the dynamic augmentation (lags and exogenous variables) rather than from nonlinearity alone.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating dynamic information and richer feature sets into Synthetic Control substantially improves baseline estimation for Demand Response. The generalized SCM framework provides a more accurate counterfactual prediction tool than the classical, static SCM, particularly when data are limited and temporal dependencies are important.

title: Bridging the Reasoning Gap in Vietnamese with Small Language Models via Test-Time Scaling
summary: The democratization of ubiquitous AI hinges on deploying sophisticated reasoning capabilities on resource-constrained devices. However, Small Language Models (SLMs) often face a "reasoning gap", particularly in non-English languages like Vietnamese, where they struggle to maintain coherent chains of thought. This paper investigates Test-Time Scaling strategies for the Qwen3-1.7B architecture within the context of Vietnamese Elementary Mathematics. We introduce Vi-S1K, a high-fidelity reasoning dataset localized via a Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite powered pipeline, and Vi-Elementary-Bench, a dual-resource benchmark for rigorous evaluation. Using an LLM-as-a-Judge protocol, we reveal that the base model possesses robust latent knowledge (Accuracy: 4.05/5.00) but suffers from a severe "formatting gap" in communication. Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) acts as a critical "reasoning unlocker", yielding a 77% improvement in Explanation Quality and bridging the gap between raw calculation and pedagogical coherence. Furthermore, our analysis of prompting strategies uncovers a significant trade-off: structured frameworks like ReAct impose a "cognitive tax" on the 1.7B parameter capacity, degrading performance relative to pure Chain-of-Thought (CoT) combined with Self-Consistency. These findings establish a deployment hierarchy for SLMs, demonstrating that SFT combined with simplified test-time scaling is superior to complex agentic workflows for edge-based reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to get a 1.7B-parameter Qwen3 model to reason well in Vietnamese elementary math on edge devices, introducing new datasets/benchmarks, showing supervised fine-tuning greatly improves explanation quality, and finding that simple CoT + self-consistency works better than complex agent-style prompting like ReAct.
AI.motivation: Deploying strong reasoning AI on small, resource-limited devices is hard, especially for non-English languages like Vietnamese. Small Language Models have latent knowledge but show a reasoning/formatting gap, making their answers incoherent or pedagogically weak. The paper aims to understand how to unlock and evaluate reasoning in small Vietnamese math models using test-time scaling and better training data.
AI.method:
1) Build Vi-S1K, a Vietnamese elementary math reasoning dataset created via an automated localization pipeline using Gemini 2.5 Flash-Lite.
2) Build Vi-Elementary-Bench, a dual-resource benchmark for evaluation.
3) Use an LLM-as-a-Judge protocol to separately score latent knowledge and explanation/formatting quality on Qwen3-1.7B.
4) Apply supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on the reasoning data to reduce the formatting/reasoning gap.
5) Systematically evaluate prompting and test-time scaling strategies (e.g., Chain-of-Thought, Self-Consistency, ReAct) to measure their effect on a 1.7B SLM’s reasoning performance in Vietnamese math.
AI.result:
1) The base Qwen3-1.7B model shows high latent knowledge (4.05/5 accuracy) but poor formatting and explanation quality.
2) SFT substantially improves explanation quality by 77%, aligning step-by-step reasoning with clear, pedagogical explanations.
3) Complex structured prompting frameworks like ReAct impose a cognitive burden on the small model, harming performance compared to simpler Chain-of-Thought plus Self-Consistency.
4) The study establishes a practical hierarchy of deployment strategies for SLMs in Vietnamese math reasoning tasks.
AI.conclusion: Supervised fine-tuning on a high-quality localized reasoning dataset is essential to unlock the reasoning potential of small Vietnamese language models, closing the gap between raw knowledge and usable explanations. For edge deployment, combining SFT with simple test-time scaling methods like CoT + Self-Consistency outperforms more complex agentic approaches such as ReAct, suggesting that lightweight reasoning frameworks are better suited to small models and resource-constrained settings.

title: WorldDB: A Vector Graph-of-Worlds Memory Engine with Ontology-Aware Write-Time Reconciliation
summary: Persistent memory is the bottleneck separating stateless chatbots from long-running agentic systems. Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) over flat vector stores fragments facts into chunks, loses cross-session identity, and has no first-class notion of supersession or contradiction. Recent bitemporal knowledge-graph systems (Graphiti, Memento, Hydra DB) add typed edges and valid-time metadata, but the graph itself remains flat: no recursive composition, no content-addressed invariants on nodes, and edge types carry no behavior beyond a label. We present WorldDB, a memory engine built on three commitments: (i) every node is a world -- a container with its own interior subgraph, ontology scope, and composed embedding, recursive to arbitrary depth; (ii) nodes are content-addressed and immutable, so any edit produces a new hash at the node and every ancestor, giving a Merkle-style audit trail for free; (iii) edges are write-time programs -- each edge type ships on_insert/on_delete/on_query_rewrite handlers (supersession closes validity, contradicts preserves both sides, same_as stages a merge proposal), so no raw append path exists. On LongMemEval-s (500 questions, ~115k-token conversational stacks), WorldDB with Claude Opus 4.7 as answerer achieves 96.40% overall / 97.11% task-averaged accuracy, a +5.61pp improvement over the previously reported Hydra DB state-of-the-art (90.79%) and +11.20pp over Supermemory (85.20%), with perfect single-session-assistant recall and robust performance on temporal reasoning (96.24%), knowledge update (98.72%), and preference synthesis (96.67%). Ablations show that the engine's graph layer -- resolver-unified entities and typed refers_to edges -- contributes +7.0pp task-averaged independently of the underlying answerer.

AI.tldr: WorldDB is a new, deeply structured, programmatic memory engine that outperforms existing systems for long-term conversational agents.
AI.motivation: Existing persistent-memory approaches for LLM agents rely mostly on flat vector-store RAG or flat bitemporal knowledge graphs. These approaches fragment information into chunks, lack robust cross-session identity, don’t cleanly represent supersession/contradiction, and treat edges as passive labels without behavior. There is a need for a memory system that can model hierarchical worlds, track immutable histories, and enforce semantic update rules programmatically.
AI.method: The paper introduces WorldDB, designed around three main ideas: (1) every node is itself a "world" containing an interior subgraph with its own ontology and a composed embedding, recursively; (2) nodes are immutable and content-addressed so edits create new hashes propagating up ancestors, forming a Merkle-style audit trail; (3) edges are active programs with on_insert/on_delete/on_query_rewrite handlers that implement behaviors like supersession, contradiction, and proposed merges, eliminating raw appends. The system includes resolver-unified entities and typed refers_to edges within this graph layer.
AI.result: On the LongMemEval-s benchmark (500 questions over ~115k-token conversational histories), WorldDB paired with Claude Opus 4.7 achieves 96.40% overall and 97.11% task-averaged accuracy. This is +5.61 percentage points over Hydra DB and +11.20 points over Supermemory. It attains perfect recall for single-session assistant behavior and strong results on temporal reasoning (96.24%), knowledge updates (98.72%), and preference synthesis (96.67%). Ablation experiments show the graph layer alone adds about +7 percentage points task-averaged accuracy, independent of the LLM used for answering.
AI.conclusion: WorldDB’s combination of hierarchical world-nodes, immutable content-addressed structure, and programmable edges yields substantially better long-term memory for agentic systems than prior flat RAG or flat bitemporal graph systems. Its graph layer and active edge semantics materially improve performance on long-horizon conversational tasks and reasoning over time, updates, and user preferences.

title: PDDL-Mind: Large Language Models are Capable on Belief Reasoning with Reliable State Tracking
summary: Large language models (LLMs) perform substantially below human level on existing theory-of-mind (ToM) benchmarks, even when augmented with chain-of-thought prompting or probabilistic belief updates. We argue that these failures primarily arise from unreliable implicit state tracking rather than limitations in high-level reasoning. We introduce PDDL-Mind, a neuro-symbolic framework that decouples environment state evolution from belief inference. By translating narrative descriptions into explicit states and actions expressed in Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL), and by verifying action-induced state transitions against a predefined domain, PDDL-Mind provides LLMs with a logically consistent and explicit representation of world states for ToM tasks. Experiments on MMToM-QA, MuMA and FanToM show that PDDL-Mind achieves over 5% absolute accuracy gain over the best existing state-of-the-art method on ToM benchmark questions.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PDDL-Mind, a neuro-symbolic framework that improves large language models’ performance on theory-of-mind tasks by explicitly representing and validating world and belief states using PDDL, leading to more than a 5% accuracy gain over prior methods on multiple ToM benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing large language models underperform humans on theory-of-mind benchmarks, and techniques like chain-of-thought prompting or probabilistic belief updates still leave them far from human-level. The authors posit that this gap is mainly due to unreliable, implicit tracking of environment and belief states within the models, rather than a lack of high-level reasoning capacity. They aim to address this by giving models an explicit, logically consistent representation of evolving world states and agents’ beliefs during narrative ToM tasks.
AI.method: The authors propose PDDL-Mind, a neuro-symbolic framework that separates the modeling of environment state evolution from belief inference. Narrative descriptions are translated into explicit states and actions encoded in Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL). The framework verifies that action-induced state transitions comply with a predefined domain model, ensuring logical consistency. This explicit state representation is then used by an LLM to perform theory-of-mind reasoning, instead of relying on the model’s implicit state tracking alone.
AI.result: On three theory-of-mind benchmarks—MMToM-QA, MuMA, and FanToM—PDDL-Mind improves LLM performance, attaining more than a 5% absolute accuracy increase over the best existing state-of-the-art approaches on benchmark questions. This demonstrates the effectiveness of explicit, verified state modeling for enhancing ToM reasoning in LLMs.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that LLM failures on ToM benchmarks are largely driven by unreliable implicit state tracking rather than innate deficiencies in high-level reasoning. By decoupling environment dynamics from belief inference and encoding them in a verified PDDL representation, PDDL-Mind provides a logically coherent world model that substantially boosts ToM performance. The results suggest that neuro-symbolic approaches with explicit state representations are a promising direction for bridging the gap between LLMs and human-level ToM reasoning.

title: LLM Safety From Within: Detecting Harmful Content with Internal Representations
summary: Guard models are widely used to detect harmful content in user prompts and LLM responses. However, state-of-the-art guard models rely solely on terminal-layer representations and overlook the rich safety-relevant features distributed across internal layers. We present SIREN, a lightweight guard model that harnesses these internal features. By identifying safety neurons via linear probing and combining them through an adaptive layer-weighted strategy, SIREN builds a harmfulness detector from LLM internals without modifying the underlying model. Our comprehensive evaluation shows that SIREN substantially outperforms state-of-the-art open-source guard models across multiple benchmarks while using 250 times fewer trainable parameters. Moreover, SIREN exhibits superior generalization to unseen benchmarks, naturally enables real-time streaming detection, and significantly improves inference efficiency compared to generative guard models. Overall, our results highlight LLM internal states as a promising foundation for practical, high-performance harmfulness detection.

AI.tldr: SIREN is a lightweight guard model that uses internal layer features of LLMs (safety neurons) to detect harmful content more accurately and efficiently than existing guard models.
AI.motivation: Existing guard models typically use only the final-layer representation of LLMs to detect harmful prompts and responses, ignoring safety-relevant signals distributed across internal layers. This may limit detection accuracy, generalization, and efficiency. The authors aim to exploit these rich internal representations without modifying the base LLM to build a more accurate, efficient, and generalizable harmfulness detector.
AI.method: They propose SIREN, which operates on the internal activations of an existing LLM. First, they identify safety-relevant neurons via linear probing across layers. Then, they construct a harmfulness detector by combining these neurons through an adaptive layer-weighted strategy, effectively aggregating safety signals from multiple layers. SIREN acts as a lightweight guard model on top of the LLM, without changing the underlying model parameters, and supports streaming (token-level) inference.
AI.result: Across multiple safety benchmarks, SIREN substantially outperforms state-of-the-art open-source guard models while using about 250× fewer trainable parameters. It also generalizes better to unseen benchmarks, naturally supports real-time streaming detection, and is more inference-efficient than generative guard models.
AI.conclusion: LLM internal states contain rich, exploitable safety-relevant structure that can underpin high-performance, practical harmfulness detectors. By leveraging safety neurons and adaptive layer weighting, SIREN achieves better accuracy, generalization, and efficiency than existing open-source guard models, suggesting that future safety systems should systematically use internal representations rather than only final-layer outputs.

title: Learning to Seek Help: Dynamic Collaboration Between Small and Large Language Models
summary: Large language models (LLMs) offer strong capabilities but raise cost and privacy concerns, whereas small language models (SLMs) facilitate efficient and private local inference yet suffer from limited capacity. To synergize the complementary strengths, we introduce a dynamic collaboration framework, where an SLM learns to proactively decide how to request an LLM during multi-step reasoning, while the LLM provides adaptive feedback instead of acting as a passive tool. We further systematically investigate how collaboration strategies are shaped by SLM and LLM capabilities as well as efficiency and privacy constraints. Evaluation results reveal a distinct scaling effect: stronger SLMs become more self-reliant, while stronger LLMs enable fewer and more informative interactions. In addition, the learned dynamic collaboration strategies significantly outperform static pipelines and standalone inference, and transfer robustly to unseen LLMs.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a dynamic collaboration framework where a small language model (SLM) learns when and how to consult a large language model (LLM) during multi-step reasoning, leading to more efficient, private, and accurate inference than static or standalone approaches.
AI.motivation: LLMs are powerful but expensive and pose privacy issues when used as remote services, while SLMs are efficient and can run locally but lack sufficient reasoning capacity. There is a need for a principled way to combine them so users can get LLM-level performance with lower cost and better privacy by only querying LLMs when necessary.
AI.method: The authors design a framework in which an SLM acts as the primary reasoner and controller: during multi-step reasoning, it decides at each step whether to continue on its own or to query an LLM, and in what form. The LLM is not just a black-box tool but provides adaptive feedback or guidance tailored to the SLM’s current state. They train or learn collaboration policies under different conditions (varying SLM/LLM strengths and constraints on cost and privacy) and then analyze the resulting interaction patterns and performance.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows a clear scaling pattern: as the SLM becomes stronger, it relies less on the LLM, and as the LLM becomes stronger, the collaboration requires fewer calls that are more information-dense. The learned dynamic strategies yield higher task performance and/or better efficiency than static pipelines (e.g., always or never calling the LLM, or fixed-stage calls) and than using either the SLM or LLM alone. The strategies also generalize well when plugged into different, unseen LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic, learned collaboration between SLMs and LLMs is more effective than rigid, static integration schemes. Properly designed, an SLM can learn when and how to request help and how to use feedback from an LLM, enabling cost-efficient and more private use of powerful models without sacrificing accuracy, and these strategies are robust across different LLM backends.

title: OGER: A Robust Offline-Guided Exploration Reward for Hybrid Reinforcement Learning
summary: Recent advancements in Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) have significantly improved Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning, yet models often struggle to explore novel trajectories beyond their initial latent space. While offline teacher guidance and entropy-driven strategies have been proposed to address this, they often lack deep integration or are constrained by the model's inherent capacity. In this paper, we propose OGER, a novel framework that unifies offline teacher guidance and online reinforcement learning through a specialized reward modeling lens. OGER employs multi-teacher collaborative training and constructs an auxiliary exploration reward that leverages both offline trajectories and the model's own entropy to incentivize autonomous exploration. Extensive experiments across mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that OGER significantly outperforms competitive baselines, achieving substantial gains in mathematical reasoning while maintaining robust generalization to out-of-domain tasks. We provide a comprehensive analysis of training dynamics and conduct detailed ablation studies to validate the effectiveness of our entropy-aware reward modulation. Our code is available at https://github.com/ecoli-hit/OGER.git.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OGER, a framework that improves LLM reasoning in RL with verifiable rewards by combining offline teacher guidance and online RL via an entropy-aware exploration reward, leading to better performance and generalization on mathematical and general reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Although RL with verifiable rewards boosts LLM reasoning, current methods struggle to explore novel solution trajectories beyond the model’s initial latent space. Existing approaches either rely on offline teacher signals without deep integration into RL, or on entropy-based exploration that is limited by the model’s capacity. There is a need for a unified framework that leverages both offline expert trajectories and online exploration to enable richer, autonomous reasoning behavior.
AI.method: OGER unifies offline teacher guidance and online RL through a specialized reward modeling framework. It uses multi-teacher collaborative training to generate diverse offline guidance and constructs an auxiliary exploration reward that combines information from these offline trajectories with the model’s own entropy. This entropy-aware reward modulation encourages the model to explore new reasoning paths while still being guided by teacher demonstrations. The method is evaluated using RLVR setups on mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks, with detailed training analysis and ablations.
AI.result: Across multiple mathematical and general reasoning benchmarks, OGER consistently outperforms strong baselines. It yields substantial gains in mathematical reasoning accuracy and demonstrates strong generalization to out-of-domain reasoning tasks. Ablation studies show that both the multi-teacher setup and the entropy-aware exploration reward are key contributors to the improvements.
AI.conclusion: By integrating multi-teacher offline guidance with online RL via an entropy-aware reward model, OGER effectively enhances exploration and reasoning in LLMs under RLVR. The framework not only boosts in-domain mathematical reasoning but also maintains robust generalization to unseen tasks, and its components—particularly the exploration reward design—are empirically validated through extensive analysis and ablations.

title: How Non-Linguistic Is the Indus Sign System? A Synthetic-Baseline Scorecard
comment: 13 pages, 4 figures, 8 tables. Code available from corresponding author upon request
summary: Whether the Indus Valley sign system (c. 2600-1900 BCE) encodes spoken language has been debated for decades.
