This paper introduces a multi-metric discrimination framework that tests the observed Indus corpus against two kinds of computer-generated non-linguistic baseline -- one mimicking a heraldic emblem system, the other an administrative coding system -- each calibrated with Zipfian frequency distributions, positional constraints, and bigram dependencies derived from six attested non-linguistic corpora. The scorecard evaluates four properties central to the Farmer-Sproat-Witzel (2004) critique: text brevity, repeated formulaic phrases, hapax legomenon rate, and positional rigidity. Applying this framework to 1,916 deduplicated inscriptions (584 unique signs, 11,110 tokens) from the ICIT/Yajnadevam digitization, we find that the Indus corpus does not match either baseline cleanly. Across the four metrics examined, the Indus corpus occupies an intermediate position relative to the two baseline families, matching neither cleanly. Neither a heraldic nor an administrative generator can reproduce all four properties at once. We also compare against seven real-world non-linguistic corpora including Sproat's (2014) datasets, finding that no attested non-linguistic system reproduces the full Indus statistical profile either. We replicate key prior results including a Zipf slope of -1.49 and conditional entropy of 3.23 bits. All code and data are publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper tests whether the Indus Valley sign system statistically resembles non-linguistic symbol systems, using a multi-metric comparison against simulated heraldic and administrative codes, and finds that it matches none of them cleanly.
AI.motivation: There has been a long-standing debate over whether the Indus Valley sign system encodes language or is instead a non-linguistic symbol system (e.g., heraldry or administrative codes). Previous critics (notably Farmer, Sproat, and Witzel 2004) argued from properties like short texts and many repeated formulas that the system was non-linguistic. The authors are motivated to provide a more rigorous, quantitative, and multi-metric test of this critique by comparing the Indus corpus to realistic non-linguistic baselines rather than ad hoc arguments.
AI.method: The authors build a multi-metric discrimination framework that compares the Indus corpus to two families of computer-generated non-linguistic baselines: one modeling a heraldic emblem system and another modeling an administrative coding system. These generators are calibrated to match key distributional properties observed in real non-linguistic corpora: Zipfian sign frequencies, positional constraints, and bigram dependencies, derived from six attested non-linguistic datasets. They then evaluate four metrics specifically emphasized in the Farmer-Sproat-Witzel critique: overall text length distribution (brevity), frequency of formulaic repeated phrases, rate of hapax legomena (signs occurring once), and positional rigidity of signs. The Indus corpus consists of 1,916 deduplicated inscriptions (584 distinct signs, 11,110 tokens) from the ICIT/Yajnadevam digitization. They also compare the Indus corpus directly to seven real-world non-linguistic corpora, including those used by Sproat (2014). Key previously reported global statistics such as Zipf slope and conditional entropy are replicated as validation checks. All code and data are released for reproducibility.
AI.result: The Indus corpus does not align cleanly with either family of non-linguistic baselines. Across the four metrics (brevity, formulaicity, hapax rate, positional rigidity), the Indus sign system occupies an intermediate position relative to the heraldic and administrative generators: it is not fully captured by either model. No single configuration of the heraldic or administrative generators can simultaneously reproduce all four observed properties of the Indus data. Likewise, when compared directly to seven real-world non-linguistic corpora (including Sproat’s datasets), none match the complete statistical profile of the Indus corpus. The authors confirm prior estimates such as a Zipf slope of about -1.49 and conditional entropy of around 3.23 bits for the Indus inscriptions.
AI.conclusion: The statistical behavior of the Indus Valley sign system cannot be straightforwardly explained as resembling known types of non-linguistic symbol systems such as heraldic emblems or administrative codes. The system sits in an intermediate regime that existing non-linguistic models and corpora do not reproduce across multiple key metrics, undermining core elements of the Farmer-Sproat-Witzel critique that framed the script as clearly non-linguistic on these grounds alone. While the paper does not definitively prove that the Indus signs encode language, it shows that they cannot be easily dismissed as merely non-linguistic under the tested baselines and suggests that richer or alternative models are needed to capture their structure. The availability of code and data supports further quantitative work on the linguistic status of the Indus script.

title: ClawEnvKit: Automatic Environment Generation for Claw-Like Agents
summary: Constructing environments for training and evaluating claw-like agents remains a manual, human-intensive process that does not scale. We argue that what is needed is not just a dataset, but an automated pipeline capable of generating diverse, verified environments on demand. To this end, we introduce ClawEnvKit, an autonomous generation pipeline that instantiates this formalism from natural language descriptions. The pipeline comprises three modules: (1) a parser that extracts structured generation parameters from natural language input; (2) a generator that produces the task specification, tool interface, and scoring configuration; and (3) a validator that enforces feasibility, diversity, structural validity, and internal consistency across the generated environments. Using ClawEnvKit, we construct Auto-ClawEval, the first large-scale benchmark for claw-like agents, comprising 1,040 environments across 24 categories. Empirically, Auto-ClawEval matches or exceeds human-curated environments on coherence and clarity at 13,800x lower cost. Evaluated across 4 model families and 8 agent harness frameworks, we find that harness engineering boosts performance by up to 15.7 percentage points over a bare ReAct baseline, completion remains the primary axis of variation with no model saturating the benchmark, and automated generation enables evaluation at a scale previously infeasible. Beyond static benchmarking, ClawEnvKit enables live evaluation: users describe a desired capability in natural language and obtain a verified environment on demand, turning evaluation into a continuous, user-driven process. The same mechanism serves as an on-demand training environment generator, producing task distributions that adapt to an agent's current weaknesses rather than being bounded by existing user logs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ClawEnvKit, an automated pipeline that generates diverse, validated claw-like agent environments from natural language, enabling large-scale, low-cost benchmarking and adaptive training via the Auto-ClawEval benchmark.
AI.motivation: Existing environments for training and evaluating claw-like agents are manually created, human-intensive, and do not scale, limiting diversity, scale, and adaptability of evaluation and training. The authors want an automated, reliable way to generate many varied and correct environments from simple user descriptions.
AI.method: They design ClawEnvKit, a three-stage autonomous pipeline: (1) a natural-language parser that extracts structured parameters for environment generation; (2) a generator that constructs detailed task specifications, tool interfaces, and scoring rules; and (3) a validator that checks feasibility, structural validity, diversity, and internal consistency, rejecting or fixing problematic environments. They use this system to automatically build Auto-ClawEval, a large benchmark of 1,040 environments across 24 categories, and then systematically evaluate multiple model families and agent harness frameworks on it.
AI.result: Using ClawEnvKit, they build Auto-ClawEval and show that the automatically generated environments are as coherent and clear as or better than human-curated ones while being 13,800x cheaper to produce. Experiments with 4 model families and 8 agent harnesses show that improved harness engineering can raise scores by up to 15.7 percentage points compared to a basic ReAct setup, that completion ability is the main factor in performance differences, and that no model saturates the benchmark. The system supports generation at scales that were previously impractical manually.
AI.conclusion: Automated, validated environment generation from natural language is a practical and scalable alternative to manual environment design for claw-like agents. ClawEnvKit enables both large-scale static benchmarking (Auto-ClawEval) and live, user-driven evaluation and training, where environments are generated on demand and can adapt to an agent’s weaknesses. This approach turns evaluation into a continuous process and removes the limitations imposed by fixed, human-authored datasets or user logs.

title: QuickScope: Certifying Hard Questions in Dynamic LLM Benchmarks
summary: LLM benchmarks are increasingly dynamic: instead of containing a fixed set of questions, they define templates and parameters that can generate an effectively unlimited number of question variants. This flexibility is valuable, but it makes evaluation expensive -- especially when the goal is not just determining an average score, but reliably identifying a model's weak spots. This paper introduces a new methodology for identifying hard questions in dynamic benchmarks. It leverages COUP, a recent Bayesian optimization algorithm (Graham, Velez & Leyton-Brown, 2026), after introducing several substantive modifications to make the algorithm suitable for practical LLM pipelines. We also wrap it in a tool that supports flexible choices of datasets and utility functions, enabling users to target the kinds of questions they care about (e.g., low-accuracy questions; questions that are unusually hard relative to their measured complexity). In experiments across a range of benchmarks, we show that our method, dubbed $\texttt{QuickScope}$, discovers truly difficult questions more sample efficiently than standard baselines, while also reducing false positives from noisy outcomes.

AI.tldr: Introduces QuickScope, a method to efficiently find the hardest questions in dynamic LLM benchmarks using a modified Bayesian optimization algorithm (COUP).
AI.motivation: Dynamic, template-based LLM benchmarks can generate many question variants, but evaluating enough of them to find a model’s genuine weaknesses is expensive and noisy. There is a need for a principled, sample-efficient way to identify truly hard questions rather than just computing average scores.
AI.method: Adapt and extend the COUP Bayesian optimization algorithm for LLM evaluation settings, including practical modifications suited for real pipelines. Wrap this in a configurable tool (QuickScope) that can plug into different datasets and utility functions so users can define what “hard” means (e.g., low accuracy, difficulty relative to complexity). Use active sampling guided by the BO model to select which question variants to query next.
AI.result: Across multiple dynamic benchmarks, QuickScope finds genuinely difficult questions with fewer model evaluations than standard baselines, and it better avoids falsely labeling questions as hard when their apparent difficulty is due to noise.
AI.conclusion: QuickScope provides an effective, sample-efficient framework for probing LLM weaknesses in dynamic benchmarks, improving both the quality and cost of hard-question discovery compared to conventional approaches.

title: On the Emergence of Syntax by Means of Local Interaction
summary: Can syntactic processing emerge spontaneously from purely local interaction? We present a concrete instance on a minimal system: an 18,658-parameter two-dimensional neural cellular automaton (NCA), supervised by nothing more than a 1-bit boundary signal, is trained on the membership problem of an arithmetic-expression grammar. After training, its internal $L \times L$ grid spontaneously self-organizes into an ordered, spatially extended representation that we name Proto-CKY. This representation satisfies three operational criteria for syntactic processing: expressive power beyond the regular languages, structural generalization beyond the training distribution, and an internal organization quantitatively aligned with grammatical structure (Pearson $r \approx 0.71$). It emerges independently on four context-free grammars and regenerates spontaneously after perturbation. Proto-CKY is functionally aligned with the CKY algorithm but formally distinct from it: it is a physical prototype, a concrete instantiation of a mathematical ideal on a physical substrate, and the systematic distance between the two carries information about the substrate itself.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that a simple neural cellular automaton, trained only with a 1-bit boundary supervision signal on an arithmetic-expression grammar membership task, can spontaneously develop an internal representation that effectively performs context-free syntactic parsing (Proto-CKY), functionally akin to the CKY algorithm.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether complex syntactic processing—traditionally implemented via explicit algorithms like CKY—can emerge spontaneously from local neural computations without explicit structural supervision, and to understand how symbolic-like computation can arise from distributed, low-level learning dynamics.
AI.method: The authors design a two-dimensional neural cellular automaton (NCA) with 18,658 parameters arranged on an L×L grid. They train it on the membership problem for different arithmetic-expression context-free grammars using only a 1-bit supervision signal at the boundary (accept/reject). The NCA updates locally on the grid, and the authors analyze the emergent internal representations after training, including their spatial organization and correlation with gold-standard parse structures (via Pearson correlation) and their behavior under perturbations. They compare the emergent mechanism functionally to the classical CKY parsing algorithm.
AI.result: After training, the NCA self-organizes its internal L×L grid into a structured representation, called Proto-CKY, that: (1) recognizes languages beyond regular (i.e., has context-free expressive power), (2) generalizes structurally beyond the training distribution, (3) shows internal activations that are quantitatively aligned with standard grammatical structure (Pearson r≈0.71 with parse chart structure). This Proto-CKY representation appears robust across four distinct context-free grammars and can re-emerge after perturbations. It behaves in a functionally similar way to CKY parsing while remaining formally distinct from the exact CKY algorithm.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that a purely local, distributed system—a neural cellular automaton with minimal, non-structural supervision—can spontaneously develop a structured, parsing-like internal mechanism with context-free expressive power, akin to CKY. This suggests that symbolic syntactic operations can emerge as physical prototypes of classical algorithms, and that the systematic differences between the emergent Proto-CKY and the ideal CKY algorithm provide insight into how the computational substrate shapes emergent algorithms.

title: Agentic Forecasting using Sequential Bayesian Updating of Linguistic Beliefs
summary:
We present BLF (Bayesian Linguistic Forecaster), an agentic system for binary forecasting that achieves state-of-the-art performance on the ForecastBench benchmark. The system is built on three ideas. (1) A Bayesian linguistic belief state: a semi-structured representation combining numerical probability estimates with natural-language evidence summaries, updated by the LLM at each step of an iterative tool-use loop. This contrasts with the common approach of appending all retrieved evidence to an ever-growing context. (2) Hierarchical multi-trial aggregation: running $K$ independent trials and combining them using logit-space shrinkage with a data-dependent prior. (3) Hierarchical calibration: Platt scaling with a hierarchical prior, which avoids over-shrinking extreme predictions for sources with skewed base rates.
  On 400 backtesting questions from the ForecastBench leaderboard, BLF outperforms all the top public methods, including Cassi, GPT-5, Grok~4.20, and Foresight-32B. Ablation studies show that the structured belief state is as impactful as web search access, and that shrinkage aggregation and hierarchical calibration each provide significant additional gains.
  In addition, we develop a robust back-testing framework with a leakage rate below 1.5\%, and use rigorous statistical methodology to compare different methods while controlling for various sources of noise.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces BLF, a Bayesian Linguistic Forecaster system that achieves state-of-the-art binary forecasting performance on the ForecastBench benchmark by using a Bayesian linguistic belief state, hierarchical aggregation over multiple trials, and hierarchical calibration.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based forecasting systems typically stuff all retrieved evidence into a growing context and lack principled aggregation and calibration, limiting forecast accuracy and robustness. The authors aim to design an agentic LLM forecaster that uses structured belief representations, better trial aggregation, and rigorous calibration to reach or surpass the best existing methods on a standard benchmark, while also evaluating them under a strict, low-leakage backtesting setup.
AI.method: They build BLF around three core components: (1) a Bayesian linguistic belief state, which is a semi-structured record containing probability estimates and concise natural-language evidence summaries, updated iteratively by the LLM instead of appending all raw evidence; (2) hierarchical multi-trial aggregation, where K independent forecasting runs are combined using logit-space shrinkage with a data-dependent prior; and (3) hierarchical calibration via Platt scaling with a hierarchical prior to prevent over-shrinking extreme probabilities, especially when base rates are skewed. They then test BLF on the ForecastBench backtesting questions and perform ablations. Additionally, they construct a robust backtesting framework that minimizes information leakage and uses sound statistical comparisons.
AI.result: On 400 backtesting questions from the ForecastBench leaderboard, BLF surpasses all top public baselines, including Cassi, GPT-5, Grok 4.20, and Foresight-32B. Ablation experiments show that using the structured Bayesian linguistic belief state yields gains comparable to adding web search, and that both shrinkage-based aggregation and hierarchical calibration each further boost performance. Their backtesting framework achieves a leakage rate below 1.5% and supports rigorous statistical comparison across methods.
AI.conclusion: A structured Bayesian linguistic belief state, combined with hierarchical aggregation and calibration, enables LLM-based forecasting agents to achieve state-of-the-art performance on ForecastBench. The belief state is a key contributor to accuracy, not just external tools like web search, and careful probabilistic aggregation plus calibration further improve forecasts. Their low-leakage, statistically robust backtesting framework provides a reliable way to evaluate such systems and supports their performance claims.

title: Latent Abstraction for Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) has become a standard approach for enhancing large language models (LLMs) with external knowledge, mitigating hallucinations, and improving factuality. However, existing systems rely on generating natural language queries at each hop and maintaining a strict architectural separation between retriever and generator, preventing them from leveraging the full representational capacity of the LLM. We propose \textbf{LAnR} (Latent Abstraction for RAG), a unified framework in which a single LLM jointly performs encoding, retrieval, and generation entirely within its own latent space. Rather than generating textual queries, LAnR produces dense retrieval vectors from the hidden states of a designated \texttt{[PRED]} token and uses them to match against encoded document representations from the same model. Furthermore, LAnR adaptively decides when sufficient evidence has been retrieved using a lightweight MLP control head over those same hidden states, eliminating both the separate retriever and explicit token-level stopping reasoning. This design is motivated by our empirical observation that answer token entropy reliably signals retrieval sufficiency. Extensive experiments on six QA benchmarks spanning single-hop and multi-hop settings demonstrate that LAnR outperforms existing RAG methods, while achieving improved inference efficiency through reduced number of retrieval calls and tighter model integration.

AI.tldr: LAnR is a RAG framework where a single LLM handles encoding, retrieval, and generation in its latent space, improving accuracy and efficiency over standard RAG.
AI.motivation: Traditional RAG systems separate the retriever from the generator and rely on natural language queries at each retrieval hop. This limits how well the LLM’s internal representations can be used for retrieval and forces token-level reasoning about when to stop retrieving. The authors aim to better exploit the LLM’s latent representations, reduce architectural complexity, and improve factuality and efficiency.
AI.method: They introduce LAnR, where an LLM produces dense retrieval vectors directly from the hidden state of a special [PRED] token, instead of emitting textual queries. Documents are encoded into the same vector space by the same model, enabling dense matching between query and documents within the model’s latent space. A lightweight MLP control head, operating on the [PRED] hidden state, decides whether sufficient evidence has been retrieved, guided by the empirical link between answer token entropy and retrieval sufficiency. This removes the need for a separate retriever and explicit token-level stopping rules.
AI.result: On six single-hop and multi-hop QA benchmarks, LAnR achieves higher accuracy than existing RAG baselines and requires fewer retrieval calls, demonstrating both performance and efficiency gains. The integrated design yields tighter coupling between retrieval and generation, reducing overhead compared with systems that maintain a separate retriever component.
AI.conclusion: A unified latent-space RAG framework, where one LLM both retrieves and generates and learns when to stop retrieving, can outperform conventional RAG setups that separate retriever and generator. Leveraging hidden-state-derived retrieval vectors and an MLP-based control head improves factual QA performance and inference efficiency, indicating that LLM internal signals like answer token entropy are effective for adaptive retrieval control.

title: MathNet: a Global Multimodal Benchmark for Mathematical Reasoning and Retrieval
summary:
Mathematical problem solving remains a challenging test of reasoning for large language and multimodal models, yet existing benchmarks are limited in size, language coverage, and task diversity. We introduce MathNet, a high-quality, large-scale, multimodal, and multilingual dataset of Olympiad-level math problems together with a benchmark for evaluating mathematical reasoning in generative models and mathematical retrieval in embedding-based systems. MathNet spans 47 countries, 17 languages, and two decades of competitions, comprising 30,676 expert-authored problems with solutions across diverse domains. In addition to the core dataset, we construct a retrieval benchmark consisting of mathematically equivalent and structurally similar problem pairs curated by human experts.
  MathNet supports three tasks: (i) Problem Solving, (ii) Math-Aware Retrieval, and (iii) Retrieval-Augmented Problem Solving. Experimental results show that even state-of-the-art reasoning models (78.4% for Gemini-3.1-Pro and 69.3% for GPT-5) remain challenged, while embedding models struggle to retrieve equivalent problems. We further show that retrieval-augmented generation performance is highly sensitive to retrieval quality; for example, DeepSeek-V3.2-Speciale achieves gains of up to 12%, obtaining the highest scores on the benchmark. MathNet provides the largest high-quality Olympiad dataset together with the first benchmark for evaluating mathematical problem retrieval, and we publicly release both the dataset and benchmark at https://mathnet.mit.edu.

AI.tldr: MathNet is a large, multilingual, multimodal Olympiad‑level math dataset and benchmark for solving, retrieving, and retrieval‑augmented solving of problems, revealing that current top models still struggle.
AI.motivation: Existing math reasoning benchmarks for large language and multimodal models are small, mostly monolingual, and lack diversity in problem types and retrieval tasks. This makes it hard to rigorously evaluate and advance mathematical reasoning and math‑aware retrieval capabilities of modern models. The authors aim to provide a richer, more realistic, and more comprehensive testbed that covers many countries, languages, and competition years, and that also evaluates how well models can retrieve and reuse mathematically related problems.
AI.method: The authors construct MathNet, a dataset of 30,676 Olympiad‑level problems collected from 47 countries, 17 languages, and about 20 years of competitions. Each problem includes expert‑written statements and solutions and spans diverse mathematical domains. They further curate a retrieval benchmark where human experts pair mathematically equivalent or structurally similar problems. On top of this resource, they define three evaluation tasks: (i) direct problem solving by generative models, (ii) math‑aware retrieval by embedding models using the curated pairs, and (iii) retrieval‑augmented problem solving that combines retrieval and generation. They then run systematic experiments with state‑of‑the‑art reasoning and embedding models to measure performance on each task and analyze sensitivity to retrieval quality.
AI.result: On the Problem Solving task, even leading reasoning models underperform perfect accuracy, with Gemini‑3.1‑Pro reaching 78.4% and GPT‑5 at 69.3% on MathNet. On the Math‑Aware Retrieval task, current embedding models have difficulty retrieving mathematically equivalent or structurally similar problems, indicating that existing embeddings are not well aligned with deeper mathematical structure. For Retrieval‑Augmented Problem Solving, performance strongly depends on the quality of the retrieved problems: models such as DeepSeek‑V3.2‑Speciale can gain up to 12% absolute accuracy when supplied with high‑quality retrieved problems, achieving the highest benchmark scores. The dataset and benchmark are released publicly for the community.
AI.conclusion: MathNet substantially advances the evaluation of mathematical reasoning and retrieval for modern AI systems by providing the largest high‑quality, multilingual, multimodal Olympiad dataset and the first dedicated benchmark for mathematical problem retrieval. The results show that state‑of‑the‑art models still struggle on challenging competition problems and that retrieval‑augmented generation is effective but highly dependent on retrieval quality. By releasing both the dataset and benchmark, the authors aim to catalyze progress in math‑capable models and math‑aware retrieval techniques across languages and modalities.

title: GraSP: Graph-Structured Skill Compositions for LLM Agents
summary: Skill ecosystems for LLM agents have matured rapidly, yet recent benchmarks show that providing agents with more skills does not monotonically improve performance -- focused sets of 2-3 skills outperform comprehensive documentation, and excessive skills actually hurt. The bottleneck has shifted from skill availability to skill orchestration: agents need not more skills, but a structural mechanism to select, compose, and execute them with explicit causal dependencies. We propose GraSP, the first executable skill graph architecture that introduces a compilation layer between skill retrieval and execution. GraSP transforms flat skill sets into typed directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) with precondition-effect edges, executes them with node-level verification, and performs locality-bounded repair through five typed operators -- reducing replanning from O(N) to O(d^h). Across ALFWorld, ScienceWorld, WebShop, and InterCode with eight LLM backbones, GraSP outperforms ReAct, Reflexion, ExpeL, and flat skill baselines in every configuration, improving reward by up to +19 points over the strongest baseline while cutting environment steps by up to 41%. GraSP's advantage grows with task complexity and is robust to both skill over-retrieval and quality degradation, confirming that structured orchestration -- not larger skill libraries -- is the key to reliable agent execution.

AI.tldr: GraSP introduces an executable skill-graph architecture for LLM agents that compiles flat skills into typed DAGs with causal structure, yielding more reliable, efficient task execution than prior flat or heuristic approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents now have access to many skills/tools, but benchmarks show that simply adding more skills does not improve and can even hurt performance. The real problem is orchestration: how to select, order, and compose skills with explicit causal dependencies so that agents can reliably complete complex tasks, even when skill retrieval is noisy or over-complete.
AI.method: The paper proposes GraSP, which inserts a compilation layer between skill retrieval and execution. Retrieved flat skills are transformed into typed directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) whose nodes are skills and whose edges encode precondition-effect relationships. GraSP executes this graph with node-level verification to check whether skill effects actually hold. When failures occur, it uses five typed local repair operators to modify only relevant parts of the graph, reducing replanning complexity from O(N) global replans to O(d^h) local adjustments, where d is branching factor and h is repair depth. The approach is evaluated on ALFWorld, ScienceWorld, WebShop, and InterCode with eight different LLM backbones and against several strong agent baselines.
AI.result: Across all four benchmarks and eight LLM backbones, GraSP consistently outperforms ReAct, Reflexion, ExpeL, and flat skill orchestration baselines. It improves task rewards by up to 19 points over the strongest baseline and reduces environment interaction steps by up to 41%. Performance gains increase with task complexity, and the method remains robust under conditions of skill over-retrieval and degraded skill quality.
AI.conclusion: Structured orchestration via executable skill graphs is more important than simply enlarging skill libraries. By compiling skills into typed causal DAGs and repairing plans locally, GraSP enables more reliable, efficient execution for LLM agents, especially on complex tasks and in noisy skill settings, establishing skill-graph compilation as a key architectural component for future tool-using agents.

title: Latent Preference Modeling for Cross-Session Personalized Tool Calling
summary: Users often omit essential details in their requests to LLM-based agents, resulting in under-specified inputs for tool use. This poses a fundamental challenge for tool-augmented agents, as API execution typically requires complete arguments, highlighting the need for personalized tool calling. To study this problem, we introduce MPT, a benchmark comprising 265 multi-session dialogues that cover three challenges: Preference Recall, Preference Induction, and Preference Transfer. We also propose PRefine, a test-time memory-augmented method that represents user preferences as evolving hypotheses. Through a generate--verify--refine loop, it extracts reusable constraints from history and improves tool-calling accuracy while using only 1.24% of the tokens required by full-history prompting. These results indicate that robust personalization in agentic systems depends on memory that captures the reasons behind user choices, not just the choices themselves.

AI.tldr: The paper tackles the problem of under-specified user inputs for LLM tools by proposing a benchmark (MPT) and a memory-augmented method (PRefine) that forms and refines hypotheses about user preferences to improve tool-calling accuracy efficiently.
AI.motivation: LLM-based agents often receive incomplete or under-specified instructions from users, especially about personal preferences needed for accurate API/tool calls. Existing approaches either fail to personalize effectively or rely on expensive full-history prompting. The work is motivated by the need for robust, efficient personalization that can infer and reuse user preferences across multi-session interactions.
AI.method: The authors build MPT, a benchmark of 265 multi-session dialogues capturing three aspects of personalization: (1) Preference Recall (remembering explicitly stated preferences), (2) Preference Induction (inferring implicit preferences), and (3) Preference Transfer (applying preferences across tools/domains). They then propose PRefine, a test-time, memory-augmented framework that represents user preferences as evolving hypotheses. Using a generate–verify–refine loop, the system forms candidate preference hypotheses from dialogue history, verifies them against new context, refines them as needed, and uses the resulting constraints to fill in missing tool arguments. PRefine is evaluated against baselines including full-history prompting and other memory strategies, while tracking both tool-calling accuracy and token usage.
AI.result: PRefine significantly improves tool-calling accuracy on the MPT benchmark compared to baselines, while using only 1.24% of the tokens required by full-history prompting. It handles the three personalization challenges (recall, induction, transfer) more robustly than prior methods, demonstrating that structured, hypothesis-based preference memory is both more accurate and more efficient than naive context replay.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that effective personalization for tool-augmented LLM agents requires memory structures that capture the underlying reasons behind user choices—represented as hypotheses and constraints—rather than just storing raw past choices or entire histories. The proposed MPT benchmark and PRefine method offer a practical path toward scalable, token-efficient, and robust personalized tool calling in multi-session settings.

title: Automatic Slide Updating with User-Defined Dynamic Templates and Natural Language Instructions
comment: To appear in Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026)
summary: Presentation slides are a primary medium for data-driven reporting, yet keeping complex, analytics-style decks up to date remains labor-intensive. Existing automation methods mostly follow fixed template filling and cannot support dynamic updates for diverse, user-authored slide decks. We therefore define "Dynamic Slide Update via Natural Language Instructions on User-provided Templates" and introduce DynaSlide, a large-scale benchmark with 20,036 real-world instruction-execution triples (source slide, user instruction, target slide) grounded in a shared external database and built from business reporting slides under bring-your-own-template (BYO-template) conditions. To tackle this task, we propose SlideAgent, an agent-based framework that combines multimodal slide parsing, natural language instruction grounding, and tool-augmented reasoning for tables, charts, and textual conclusions. SlideAgent updates content while preserving layout and style, providing a strong reference baseline on DynaSlide. We further design end-to-end and component-level evaluation protocols that reveal key challenges and opportunities for future research. The dataset and code are available at https://github.com/XiaoZhou2024/SlideAgent.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DynaSlide, a large benchmark and SlideAgent, an agent-based system for dynamically updating complex, user-authored presentation slides via natural language instructions, while preserving slide layout and style.
AI.motivation: Updating complex, data-heavy business presentation slides is time-consuming and mostly manual. Existing automation approaches rely on rigid templates that don't support dynamic updates of diverse, user-created slide decks or natural language instructions. There is a need for a more flexible, data-grounded system and benchmark to support realistic slide update workflows under bring-your-own-template conditions.
AI.method: The authors define a new task: "Dynamic Slide Update via Natural Language Instructions on User-provided Templates." They construct DynaSlide, a large-scale benchmark consisting of 20,036 triplets of (source slide, user instruction, target slide), all linked to a shared external database and drawn from real business reporting slides under BYO-template conditions. They propose SlideAgent, an agent-based framework that: (1) parses multimodal slide content; (2) grounds user natural language instructions in slide elements and the external data; and (3) performs tool-augmented reasoning over tables, charts, and textual conclusions to modify the slide while preserving its layout and style. They also design evaluation protocols at both end-to-end and component levels to assess performance and identify challenges.
AI.result: DynaSlide provides a large, realistic benchmark for the new task, and SlideAgent serves as a strong baseline capable of updating slide content while keeping the original layout and style. The evaluation protocols reveal performance characteristics of SlideAgent and identify specific challenges and opportunities for improvement in natural language grounded slide updating.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a new, realistic task for automating slide updates from natural language instructions on user-defined templates, provides a large-scale benchmark (DynaSlide) under BYO-template conditions, and introduces SlideAgent as an effective baseline framework. The proposed evaluations highlight remaining challenges, encouraging future research on multimodal understanding, instruction grounding, and tool-augmented reasoning for data-driven slide authoring and maintenance.

authors: H S V N S Kowndinya Renduchintala
title: Heterogeneity in Formal Linguistic Competence of Language Models: Is Data the Real Bottleneck?
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit a puzzling disparity in their formal linguistic competence: while they learn some linguistic phenomena with near-perfect mastery, they often perform below chance on others, even after training on trillions of tokens. In this work, we investigate whether these failures stem from inherent architectural limitations or simply the scarcity of these specific grammatical constructions in web-scale corpora. We pre-train simple GPT-2 Small (124M) models on a 100M-token random sample of the FineWeb corpus and intervene by injecting a minimal amount (1%) of synthetic data targeting specific linguistic phenomena. We find that this targeted intervention substantially improves model performance in 8 out of the 9 worst-performing BLiMP paradigms - notably the accuracy on a specific paradigm, only_npi_scope, surges from 20.9% to 69.4%. Furthermore, we observe that these interventions generally preserve or slightly improve aggregate performance. However, while we also identify a resistant phenomenon, principle_A_c_command, whose performance remains below chance even after our data augmentation, our findings do serve as an optimistic existence proof that even small language models can substantially improve on those linguistic phenomena on which models typically perform poorly, provided the pre-training data contains sufficient exposure to them. This suggests that efforts towards human-scale language modeling may benefit greatly by focusing on data composition. The code to reproduce our results is open-sourced at https://github.com/kowndinya-renduchintala/heterogeneity-in-formal-linguistic-competence.

AI.tldr: The paper studies why LLMs fail badly on some syntactic phenomena and shows that adding a small amount of targeted synthetic data during pre‑training greatly improves performance on most of these hard cases, implying data composition is crucial for human‑scale language modeling.
AI.motivation: LLMs demonstrate heterogeneous formal linguistic competence: they master some grammatical patterns but perform at or below chance on others, even with huge training corpora. It is unclear whether these failures reflect fundamental architectural limits of transformer LMs or simply data scarcity for specific constructions. The authors aim to disentangle these possibilities and to test whether carefully augmenting pre‑training data with rare grammatical phenomena can systematically fix these weaknesses without harming overall performance.
AI.method: They pre-train multiple GPT‑2 Small (124M parameter) models from scratch on a 100M-token random sample of the FineWeb corpus. For experimental conditions, they inject a small proportion (about 1%) of synthetic sentences specifically constructed to instantiate certain challenging linguistic phenomena defined in the BLiMP benchmark (e.g., only_npi_scope, principle_A_c_command). They then evaluate the resulting models on BLiMP paradigms, focusing on the nine worst-performing ones in the baseline model. They compare accuracy with and without targeted synthetic data and assess whether performance gains on targeted phenomena trade off against performance on the rest of BLiMP or aggregate metrics.
AI.result: Targeted injection of about 1% synthetic data focusing on specific hard grammatical phenomena yields substantial performance gains on 8 of the 9 worst-performing BLiMP paradigms. A highlighted example is the only_npi_scope paradigm, where accuracy increases from 20.9% to 69.4% after augmentation. These improvements generally occur without degrading, and sometimes slightly improving, aggregate BLiMP performance. However, at least one phenomenon, principle_A_c_command, remains resistant: performance stays below chance even after synthetic data augmentation.
AI.conclusion: The experiments indicate that many apparent failures of LLMs on difficult grammatical phenomena are not due to inherent limitations of the GPT‑2‑style transformer architecture, but instead to insufficient exposure to those constructions in pre‑training data. Adding a small amount of carefully designed synthetic data can dramatically improve competence on those phenomena without harming overall performance. Nonetheless, some phenomena (e.g., involving principle A and c‑command) remain stubbornly hard, suggesting there may be deeper challenges or that more sophisticated interventions are needed. Overall, the work argues that human‑scale language modeling should pay close attention to data composition, as strategically augmenting pre‑training corpora with rare but linguistically important structures can substantially enhance formal linguistic competence.

title: Domain-oriented RAG Assessment (DoRA): Synthetic Benchmarking for RAG-based Question Answering on Defense Documents
summary: Open-domain RAG benchmarks over public corpora can overestimate deployment performance due to pretraining overlap and weak attribution requirements. We present DoRA (Domain-oriented RAG Assessment), a domain-grounded benchmark built from defense documents that pairs synthetic, intent-conditioned QA (question answering) with auditable evidence passages for attribution. DoRA covers five question types (find, explain, summarize, generate, provide) and contains 6.5K curated instances. In end-to-end evaluation with a fixed dense retriever, general-purpose Language Models (LMs) perform similarly, while a model trained on DoRA (DoRA SFT) yields large gains over the base model (Llama3.1-8B-Instruct): up to 26% improvement in QA task success, while reducing the hallucination rate by 47% in RAG faithfulness scores, supporting contamination-aware regression testing under domain shift.

AI.tldr: DoRA is a domain-specific benchmark for evaluating RAG systems on defense documents, with auditable attribution, and shows that training on it significantly boosts QA performance and reduces hallucinations compared to a base Llama3.1 model.
AI.motivation: Existing open-domain RAG benchmarks based on public corpora can be misleading because models may have seen the data during pretraining and because they often don’t rigorously test whether answers are properly grounded in retrieved evidence. This leads to overestimated performance and poor visibility into hallucinations, especially when deploying in specialized domains. The authors want a contamination-aware, domain-grounded way to evaluate RAG under realistic domain shift.
AI.method: They construct DoRA, a benchmark built from defense-domain documents. The dataset pairs synthetic, intent-conditioned QA instances (questions crafted to reflect different user intents) with specific evidence passages that can be audited for proper attribution. DoRA spans five question types (find, explain, summarize, generate, provide) and totals 6.5K curated examples. They then run end-to-end RAG evaluations with a fixed dense retriever and compare general-purpose LMs to a version fine-tuned on DoRA (DoRA SFT) using the Llama3.1-8B-Instruct base model. They measure QA task success and RAG faithfulness (hallucination) scores.
AI.result: With the same dense retriever, off-the-shelf general-purpose LMs perform similarly to each other on DoRA. However, the DoRA SFT model—fine-tuned on this benchmark—substantially outperforms its base, achieving up to 26% higher QA task success and a 47% reduction in hallucination rate per RAG faithfulness scores.
AI.conclusion: DoRA provides a domain-grounded, contamination-aware benchmark for RAG that emphasizes evidence attribution and supports realistic evaluation under domain shift. Fine-tuning on DoRA yields large improvements in both accuracy and faithfulness, showing that specialized, auditable benchmarks can meaningfully improve RAG reliability in specialized domains like defense.

title: ReCoQA: A Benchmark for Tool-Augmented and Multi-Step Reasoning in Real Estate Question and Answering
summary: Developing agents capable of navigating fragmented, multi-source information remains challenging, primarily due to the scarcity of benchmarks reflecting hybrid workflows combining database querying with external APIs. To bridge this gap, we introduce ReCoQA, a large-scale benchmark of 29,270 real-estate instances featuring machine-verifiable supervision for intermediate steps, including structured intent labels, SQL queries, and API calls. Complementarily, we propose HIRE-Agent, a hierarchical framework instantiating an understand-plan-execute architecture as a strong baseline. By orchestrating a Front-end parser, a planning Supervisor, and execution Specialists, HIRE-Agent effectively integrates heterogeneous evidence. Extensive experiments demonstrate that HIRE-Agent constitutes a strong baseline and substantiates the necessity of hierarchical collaboration for complex, real-world reasoning tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper presents ReCoQA, a benchmark for multi-source information navigation, and HIRE-Agent, a hierarchical agent framework that uses an understand-plan-execute pipeline for complex, real-world reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Current agents struggle with fragmented, multi-source information and there is a lack of benchmarks that capture hybrid workflows involving databases and external APIs, making it hard to evaluate and improve such agents.
AI.method: They build ReCoQA, a large-scale real-estate benchmark with 29,270 instances that provide machine-verifiable supervision over intermediate reasoning steps (intent labels, SQL queries, API calls). They also design HIRE-Agent, a hierarchical agent composed of a Front-end parser, a planning Supervisor, and execution Specialists, following an understand-plan-execute architecture to integrate heterogeneous evidence.
AI.result: Experiments on ReCoQA show that HIRE-Agent is a strong baseline and that using hierarchical collaboration among specialized components significantly improves performance on complex reasoning tasks requiring integration of database queries and API calls.
AI.conclusion: ReCoQA fills a crucial evaluation gap for multi-source, hybrid-workflow agents, and HIRE-Agent demonstrates that hierarchical, modular understand-plan-execute architectures are effective for complex real-world reasoning over heterogeneous information sources.

title: Process Reward Models Meet Planning: Generating Precise and Scalable Datasets for Step-Level Rewards
summary: Process Reward Models (PRMs) have emerged as a powerful tool for providing step-level feedback when evaluating the reasoning of Large Language Models (LLMs), which frequently produce chains of thought (CoTs) containing errors even when the final answer is correct. However, existing PRM datasets remain expensive to construct, prone to annotation errors, and predominantly limited to the mathematical domain. This work introduces a novel and scalable approach to PRM dataset generation based on planning logical problems expressed in the Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL). Using this method, we generate a corpus of approximately one million reasoning steps across various PDDL domains and use it to train PRMs. Experimental results show that augmenting widely-used PRM training datasets with PDDL-derived data yields substantial improvements in both mathematical and non-mathematical reasoning, as demonstrated across multiple benchmarks. These findings indicate that planning problems constitute a scalable and effective resource for generating robust, precise, and fine-grained training data for PRMs, going beyond the classical mathematical sources that dominate this field.

AI.tldr: They propose a scalable way to build Process Reward Model (PRM) datasets using PDDL planning problems, creating ~1M labeled reasoning steps that, when used to train PRMs, improve both mathematical and non-mathematical reasoning over existing datasets.
AI.motivation: Current PRM datasets are costly to annotate, error-prone, and mainly focused on math. LLMs often make step-wise reasoning errors even with correct final answers, so there is a need for large, accurate, and diverse step-level supervision beyond the mathematical domain.
AI.method: They automatically generate logical reasoning traces from planning problems defined in the Planning Domain Definition Language (PDDL). These traces provide step-level signals for whether each reasoning step is correct. They compile a corpus of about one million such steps across diverse PDDL domains and train Process Reward Models on this data, both standalone and in combination with existing PRM datasets.
AI.result: PRMs trained with the additional PDDL-derived data outperform baselines trained only on standard PRM datasets. The improvements are observed on multiple benchmarks and cover both mathematical and non-mathematical reasoning tasks, indicating better step-level evaluation and guidance of LLM chains of thought.
AI.conclusion: Planning problems expressed in PDDL are a scalable, precise, and domain-diverse source of labeled reasoning steps for training PRMs. Incorporating this data yields more robust and fine-grained PRMs than relying solely on traditional math-focused datasets, suggesting that planning-based generation can broaden and strengthen supervision for LLM reasoning.

title: Modeling Multiple Support Strategies within a Single Turn for Emotional Support Conversations
summary: Emotional Support Conversation (ESC) aims to assist individuals experiencing distress by generating empathetic and supportive dialogue. While prior work typically assumes that each supporter turn corresponds to a single strategy, real-world supportive communication often involves multiple strategies within a single utterance. In this paper, we revisit the ESC task by formulating it as multi-strategy utterance generation, where each utterance may contain one or more strategy-response pairs. We propose two generation methods: All-in-One, which predicts all strategy-response pairs in a single decoding step, and One-by-One, which iteratively generates strategy-response pairs until completion. Both methods are further enhanced with cognitive reasoning guided by reinforcement learning to improve strategy selection and response composition. We evaluate our models on the ESConv dataset under both utterance-level and dialogue-level settings. Experimental results show that our methods effectively model multi-strategy utterances and lead to improved supportive quality and dialogue success. To our knowledge, this work provides the first systematic empirical evidence that allowing multiple support strategies within a single utterance is both feasible and beneficial for emotional support conversations. All code and data will be publicly available at https://github.com/aliyun/qwen-dianjin.

AI.tldr: The paper redefines Emotional Support Conversation (ESC) generation to allow multiple support strategies in a single utterance and proposes two generation methods, All-in-One and One-by-One, both enhanced by reinforcement learning-based cognitive reasoning, achieving better supportive quality and dialogue success on ESConv.
AI.motivation: Existing ESC models usually assume a one-to-one mapping between a supporter turn and a single support strategy, but real human helpers often mix several strategies in one utterance. This mismatch limits the naturalness and effectiveness of ESC systems. The authors are motivated to more accurately model real-world supportive communication by enabling multi-strategy content within a single generated utterance and to test whether this complexity is both feasible to model and beneficial for support quality.
AI.method: The authors reformulate ESC as a multi-strategy utterance generation task, where each utterance consists of one or more (strategy, response) pairs. They design two generation paradigms: (1) All-in-One, which predicts all strategy-response pairs jointly in a single decoding step; and (2) One-by-One, which sequentially generates strategy-response pairs until a stopping condition. Both paradigms are augmented with a cognitive reasoning component guided by reinforcement learning, which optimizes strategy selection and response composition based on feedback signals. The models are trained and evaluated on the ESConv dataset under both utterance-level (single turn) and dialogue-level (multi-turn) setups.
AI.result: On the ESConv benchmark, both proposed methods successfully generate multi-strategy utterances. Quantitative evaluations (presumably automatic metrics plus human judgments) show that these methods increase supportive quality and overall dialogue success compared to baselines that restrict each utterance to a single support strategy. The reinforcement learning-based cognitive reasoning further improves strategy appropriateness and coherence of responses.
AI.conclusion: Modeling multiple support strategies within a single utterance in ESC is not only technically feasible but also leads to better emotional support outcomes. The All-in-One and One-by-One multi-strategy generation frameworks, enhanced by RL-guided cognitive reasoning, outperform single-strategy baselines on ESConv at both utterance and dialogue levels. The work provides initial systematic empirical evidence that multi-strategy utterances are beneficial in emotional support dialogue systems and releases code and data to facilitate further research.

title: ltzGLUE: Luxembourgish General Language Understanding Evaluation
summary: This paper presents ltzGLUE, the first Natural Language Understanding (NLU) benchmark for Luxembourgish (LTZ) based on the popular GLUE benchmark for English. Although NLU tasks are available for many European languages nowadays, LTZ is one of the official national languages that is often overlooked. We construct new tasks and reuse existing ones to introduce the first official NLU benchmark and accompanying evaluation of encoder models for the language. Our tasks include common natural language processing tasks in binary and multi-class classification settings, including named entity recognition, topic classification, and intent classification. We evaluate various pre-trained language models for LTZ to present an overview of the current capabilities of these models on the LTZ language.

AI.tldr: Introduces ltzGLUE, the first GLUE-style NLU benchmark and model evaluation suite for Luxembourgish.
AI.motivation: Luxembourgish is an official national language but is underrepresented in NLP resources and benchmarks compared to other European languages, making it difficult to systematically evaluate and improve NLU models for LTZ.
AI.method: Design a GLUE-inspired benchmark for Luxembourgish by creating new tasks and reusing existing datasets. The benchmark covers common NLU/NLP tasks framed as binary and multi-class classification problems, including named entity recognition, topic classification, and intent classification. Then evaluate a range of pre-trained Luxembourgish (and possibly multilingual) encoder language models on these tasks.
AI.result: Provides the first standardized set of NLU tasks for Luxembourgish and reports performance of various pre-trained language models on these tasks, yielding a comparative overview of their strengths and weaknesses for LTZ.
AI.conclusion: ltzGLUE establishes a foundational evaluation benchmark for Luxembourgish NLU and shows the current capabilities of existing pre-trained models, enabling more systematic progress and future research on LTZ language technologies.

title: Employing General-Purpose and Biomedical Large Language Models with Advanced Prompt Engineering for Pharmacoepidemiologic Study Design
summary: Background: The potential of large language models (LLMs) to automate and support pharmacoepidemiologic study design is an emerging area of interest, yet their reliability remains insufficiently characterized. General-purpose LLMs often display inaccuracies, while the comparative performance of specialized biomedical LLMs in this domain remains unknown. Methods: This study evaluated general-purpose LLMs (GPT-4o and DeepSeek-R1) versus biomedically fine-tuned LLMs (QuantFactory/Bio-Medical-Llama-3-8B-GGUF and Irathernotsay/qwen2-1.5B-medical_qa-Finetune) using 46 protocols (2018-2024) from the HMA-EMA Catalogue and Sentinel System. Performance was assessed across relevance, logic of justification, and ontology-code agreement across multiple coding systems using Least-to-Most (LTM) and Active Prompting strategies. Results: GPT-4o and DeepSeek-R1 paired with LTM prompting achieved the highest relevance and logic of justification scores, with GPT-4o-LTM reaching a median relevance score of 4 in 8 of 9 questions for HMA-EMA protocols. Biomedical LLMs showed lower relevance overall and frequently generated insufficient justification. All LLMs demonstrated limited proficiency in ontology-code mapping, although LTM provided the most consistent improvements in reasoning stability. Conclusion: Off-the-shelf general-purpose LLMs currently offer superior support for pharmacoepidemiologic design compared to biomedical LLMs. Prompt strategy strongly influenced LLM performance.

AI.tldr: The paper compares general-purpose and biomedical large language models for helping design pharmacoepidemiologic studies and finds that general-purpose models with structured prompting perform better overall, especially in relevance and reasoning, while all models struggle with ontology-code mapping.
AI.motivation: Pharmacoepidemiologic study design is complex and time-consuming, and there is growing interest in using LLMs to automate or assist this process. However, the reliability and comparative performance of different types of LLMs—especially general-purpose versus biomedical-specialized models—are not well understood. The authors aim to systematically evaluate how well these models support key aspects of protocol development and whether prompt strategies can improve performance.
AI.method: The study benchmarked two general-purpose LLMs (GPT-4o, DeepSeek-R1) against two biomedical fine-tuned LLMs (Bio-Medical-Llama-3-8B and qwen2-1.5B-medical_qa) using 46 real pharmacoepidemiologic protocols from the HMA-EMA Catalogue and Sentinel System (2018–2024). The models were tested on multiple protocol-related questions and evaluated along three dimensions: (1) relevance of their outputs, (2) logic and adequacy of their justification, and (3) agreement between proposed concepts and ontology codes across several coding systems. Two prompting strategies, Least-to-Most (LTM) and Active Prompting, were applied and compared for each model.
AI.result: General-purpose LLMs, especially when used with Least-to-Most prompting, outperformed biomedical LLMs on relevance and justification quality. GPT-4o with LTM prompting achieved a median relevance score of 4 (on the study’s scale) for 8 out of 9 questions in the HMA-EMA protocols. Biomedical models tended to give less relevant answers and weaker or insufficient justifications. Across all models, performance on ontology-code mapping was limited, indicating difficulty in accurately aligning narrative concepts with standardized coding systems. LTM prompting yielded the most consistent gains in reasoning stability across models.
AI.conclusion: At present, off-the-shelf general-purpose LLMs provide better practical support for pharmacoepidemiologic study design than the tested biomedical-specialized models. How the model is prompted—particularly using structured, stepwise approaches like Least-to-Most—has a strong impact on performance. Nonetheless, all evaluated models remain weak at ontology-code mapping, so human oversight is needed for tasks involving standardized coding systems.

title: How Creative Are Large Language Models in Generating Molecules?
summary: Molecule generation requires satisfying multiple chemical and biological constraints while searching a large and structured chemical space. This makes it a non-binary problem, where effective models must identify non-obvious solutions under constraints while maintaining exploration to improve success by escaping local optima. From this perspective, creativity is a functional requirement in molecular generation rather than an aesthetic notion. Large language models (LLMs) can generate molecular representations directly from natural language prompts, but it remains unclear what type of creativity they exhibit in this setting and how it should be evaluated. In this work, we study the creative behavior of LLMs in molecular generation through a systematic empirical evaluation across physicochemical, ADMET, and biological activity tasks. We characterize creativity along two complementary dimensions, convergent creativity and divergent creativity, and analyze how different factors shape these behaviors. Our results indicate that LLMs exhibit distinct patterns of creative behavior in molecule generation, such as an increase in constraint satisfaction when additional constraints are imposed. Overall, our work is the first to reframe the abilities required for molecule generation as creativity, providing a systematic understanding of creativity in LLM-based molecular generation and clarifying the appropriate use of LLMs in molecular discovery pipelines.

AI.tldr: This paper examines how large language models (LLMs) display and can be evaluated for creativity in molecular generation under multiple chemical and biological constraints.
AI.motivation: Molecule generation for drug discovery involves navigating a vast, structured chemical space under many physicochemical, ADMET, and biological constraints. Effective methods must find non-obvious, high-quality molecules while avoiding local optima, which the authors argue is best understood as a need for computational creativity rather than just optimization. Although LLMs can already generate molecules from natural language prompts, it is unclear what kind of creativity they manifest and how to rigorously assess it in this context.
AI.method: The authors conduct a systematic empirical study of LLM-based molecular generation across tasks involving physicochemical properties, ADMET characteristics, and biological activity. They define and operationalize two complementary creativity dimensions—convergent creativity (ability to satisfy and integrate constraints and converge on viable solutions) and divergent creativity (ability to explore and diversify the chemical space). They then analyze how model choices, prompts, and constraint configurations influence these two creativity dimensions and the generated molecules’ properties.
AI.result: They find that LLMs show distinct, measurable creative behaviors in molecule generation. One highlighted pattern is that adding more explicit constraints in prompts can increase the rate of constraint satisfaction (convergent creativity) rather than simply over-restricting the search. The study reveals systematic trade-offs and patterns in how LLMs balance exploration and constraint satisfaction across different molecular design tasks.
AI.conclusion: The work reframes molecular generation requirements as a problem of creativity and shows that LLMs do exhibit structured forms of creative behavior in this domain. By formalizing convergent and divergent creativity and empirically characterizing them, the paper clarifies when and how LLMs should be used within molecular discovery pipelines, offering a principled basis for evaluating and integrating LLM-generated molecules into drug discovery workflows.

title: SignDPO: Multi-level Direct Preference Optimisation for Skeleton-based Gloss-free Sign Language Translation
summary: We present SignDPO, a novel multi-level Direct Preference Optimisation (DPO) framework designed to enhance the alignment of skeleton-based Sign Language Translation. While current skeleton-based models have made significant progress using Maximum Likelihood Estimation, they are primarily constrained by an imitation-based paradigm that lacks discriminative sensitivity to the fine-grained spatio-temporal nuances of sign language, often leading to semantic drift. To address this, SignDPO shifts the optimisation goal from simple sequence mimicry to structured preference alignment across spatial, temporal, and linguistic dimensions. Our framework involves three key designs. First, we introduce a hierarchical perturbation strategy to construct spatial and temporal non-preferred samples at both global and local granularities automatically. Second, we propose a self-guiding mechanism that leverages decoder cross-attention scores to identify and perturb semantically salient skeletal regions, forcing the model to distinguish genuine sign signals from structural distortions. Third, we establish an automated language-level preference generator by fine-tuning a dedicated perturbation model, capturing complex output-level failure modes without manual annotation. Extensive experiments on three widely adopted benchmarks, CSL-Daily, How2Sign, and OpenASL, demonstrate that SignDPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art gloss-free methods and even rivals established gloss-based ones. Our results suggest that multi-level preference alignment is a powerful paradigm for bridging the gap between high-entropy skeletal trajectories and discrete linguistic semantics.

AI.tldr: SignDPO is a new training framework that improves skeleton-based sign language translation by aligning the model with multi-level preferences (spatial, temporal, linguistic) rather than just imitating reference sequences.
AI.motivation: Current skeleton-based sign language translation models are trained mainly with Maximum Likelihood Estimation, which encourages them to mimic reference translations but does not teach them to be sensitive to subtle spatio-temporal details in sign execution. This imitation-based paradigm can cause semantic drift because small but meaningful variations in motion are not discriminatively modeled. There is a need for a method that explicitly guides models to distinguish correct sign patterns from slightly perturbed, incorrect ones across multiple structural levels.
AI.method: The authors propose SignDPO, a multi-level Direct Preference Optimization framework. (1) They design a hierarchical perturbation strategy that automatically creates non-preferred spatial and temporal samples at both global and local levels, serving as negative examples. (2) They add a self-guiding mechanism using decoder cross-attention scores to locate semantically important skeletal regions and then perturb them, encouraging the model to recognize genuine sign cues versus distortions. (3) They train an automated language-level preference generator via a fine-tuned perturbation model to produce non-preferred output texts, capturing complex linguistic error patterns without manual labeling. The model is then optimized to prefer original (correct) samples over these structured perturbations across all three levels.
AI.result: On three benchmark datasets for sign language translation—CSL-Daily, How2Sign, and OpenASL—SignDPO achieves consistently better performance than previous state-of-the-art gloss-free methods and reaches performance comparable to strong gloss-based approaches, demonstrating the effectiveness of multi-level preference-based training.
AI.conclusion: Multi-level preference alignment via Direct Preference Optimization offers a strong alternative to simple likelihood-based imitation for skeleton-based sign language translation. By constructing and leveraging structured perturbations in spatial, temporal, and linguistic spaces, SignDPO makes models more discriminatively aware of fine-grained sign patterns, thereby narrowing the gap between continuous skeletal motion and discrete language output and yielding state-of-the-art performance on several benchmarks.

title: JudgeMeNot: Personalizing Large Language Models to Emulate Judicial Reasoning in Hebrew
comment: To appear in Findings of the ACL 2026
summary: Despite significant advances in large language models, personalizing them for individual decision-makers remains an open problem. Here, we introduce a synthetic-organic supervision pipeline that transforms raw judicial decisions into instruction-tuning data, enabling parameter-efficient fine-tuning of personalized models for individual judges in low-resource settings. We compare our approach to state-of-the-art personalization techniques across three different tasks and settings. The results show that Causal Language Modeling followed by synthetically generated instruction-tuning significantly outperforms all other baselines, providing significant improvements across lexical, stylistic, and semantic similarity. Notably, our model-generated outputs are indistinguishable from the reasoning of human judges, highlighting the viability of efficient personalization, even in low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: They propose a pipeline to turn judges’ past decisions into instruction-tuning data so small, personalized models can mimic each judge’s reasoning style, achieving outputs that look like real judicial decisions, even with limited data.
AI.motivation: Large language models are powerful but generic; they do not reliably reflect the preferences, style, and reasoning patterns of specific decision-makers, especially when only a small amount of data exists per individual. In the judicial context, faithfully reflecting a particular judge’s reasoning is important for research, decision support, and consistency, yet obtaining enough labeled data and supervision for each judge is hard. The authors aim to create a data- and parameter-efficient way to personalize models to individual judges using only their historical decisions.
AI.method: They design a synthetic-organic supervision pipeline: starting from real judicial opinions (organic data), they automatically transform these into instruction-tuning examples, likely by generating prompts, questions, and step-by-step rationales that correspond to each decision. They first perform Causal Language Modeling (CLM) pre-finetuning on the judge’s text to capture style and domain, and then apply synthetically generated instruction-tuning to inject task structure and reasoning. They build personalized, parameter-efficient models for individual judges and compare this to other personalization methods across multiple tasks and settings, evaluating lexical, stylistic, and semantic similarity to real judicial decisions.
AI.result: Across three tasks and settings, the CLM-then-synthetic-instruction-tuning pipeline beats state-of-the-art personalization baselines. Their personalized models more closely match judges’ decisions in wording, writing style, and underlying meaning. Human or automatic evaluations show that the model’s generated justifications are effectively indistinguishable from actual human-judge reasoning, even when only limited data per judge is available.
AI.conclusion: Transforming raw judicial decisions into synthetic instruction-tuning datasets, combined with an initial CLM personalization step, is an effective and data-efficient way to build judge-specific models. This approach outperforms alternative personalization techniques and demonstrates that, even in low-resource scenarios, it is feasible to approximate individual judges’ reasoning and style to a degree that appears indistinguishable from human-authored opinions.

title: Modeling Human Perspectives with Socio-Demographic Representations
summary: Humans often hold different perspectives on the same issues. In many NLP tasks, annotation disagreement can reflect valid subjective perspectives. Modeling annotator perspectives and understanding their relationship with other human factors, such as socio-demographic attributes, have received increasing attention. Prior work typically focuses on single demographic factors or limited combinations. However, in real-world settings, annotator perspectives are shaped by complex social contexts, and finer-grained socio-demographic attributes can better explain human perspectives. In this work, we propose Socio-Contrastive Learning, a method that jointly models annotator perspectives while learning socio-demographic representations. Our method provides an effective approach for the fusion of socio-demographic features and textual representations to predict annotator perspectives, outperforming standard concatenation-based methods. The learned representations further enable analysis and visualization of how demographic factors relate to variation in annotator perspectives. Our code is available at GitHub: https://github.com/Leixin-Zhang/Socio_Contrastive_Learning

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Socio-Contrastive Learning, a method that jointly models annotator perspectives and rich socio-demographic attributes to better predict and analyze subjective NLP annotations.
AI.motivation: In many NLP tasks, annotators legitimately disagree due to differing subjective perspectives. These perspectives are influenced by complex, fine-grained socio-demographic factors, not just single or coarse attributes. Existing work often models either perspectives or demographics in a limited way (e.g., one factor at a time or simple feature concatenation), which fails to capture how nuanced social contexts shape annotator judgments. There is a need for methods that more effectively integrate demographic information with text representations, both to improve prediction of annotator-specific labels and to enable analysis of how demographics relate to annotation variation.
AI.method: The authors propose Socio-Contrastive Learning, a representation learning framework that jointly models annotator perspectives and socio-demographic attributes. It fuses socio-demographic features with textual representations using a contrastive objective rather than simple concatenation. The model learns demographic embeddings and text-based perspective embeddings such that annotators with similar perspectives and socio-demographic profiles are pulled closer in representation space, while dissimilar ones are pushed apart. This joint learning yields richer, structured representations that can be used for prediction as well as downstream analysis and visualization of demographic effects on annotation behavior.
AI.result: Socio-Contrastive Learning outperforms standard baselines that rely on straightforward concatenation of demographic and textual features for predicting annotator perspectives. The learned representations also prove useful for exploratory analysis: they support visualization and inspection of how different socio-demographic factors correlate with, or explain, variation in annotation patterns across annotators.
AI.conclusion: Modeling annotator perspectives in NLP benefits from fine-grained socio-demographic information and from learning them jointly through a contrastive framework. Socio-Contrastive Learning provides a more effective fusion of textual and demographic features than simple concatenation, leading to better prediction of annotator-specific labels and enabling richer analyses of how social factors shape subjective annotation behavior. The released code facilitates application and extension of this approach in future work.

title: Mix and Match: Context Pairing for Scalable Topic-Controlled Educational Summarisation
comment: To be published at the International Conference on Artificial Intelligence in Education (AIED'26)
summary: Topic-controlled summarisation enables users to generate summaries focused on specific aspects of source documents. This paper investigates a data augmentation strategy for training small language models (sLMs) to perform topic-controlled summarisation. We propose a pairwise data augmentation method that combines contexts from different documents to create contrastive training examples, enabling models to learn the relationship between topics and summaries more effectively. Using the SciTLDR dataset enriched with Wikipedia-derived topics, we systematically evaluate how augmentation scale affects model performance. Results show consistent improvements in win rate and semantic alignment as the augmentation scale increases, while the amount of real training data remains fixed. Consequently, a T5-base model trained with our augmentation approach achieves competitive performance relative to larger models, despite using significantly fewer parameters and substantially fewer real training examples.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a pairwise data augmentation method to train small language models for topic-controlled summarisation, showing that scaling augmentation improves performance without increasing real training data.
