We assess the quality of these resources through qualitative and quantitative human analyses, measuring inter-annotator agreement. Additionally, we perform ablation studies to demonstrate the impact of multilingual data, with respect to English only, in VLMs training. Experiments, comprising 3 different models show that using multilingual, multimodal examples for training VLMs aids is consistently beneficial on non-English benchmarks, with positive transfer to English as well.

AI.tldr: They build multilingual training data and evaluation benchmarks for vision-language models in five European languages and show that multilingual multimodal training improves performance, especially on non-English tasks, while also helping English.
AI.motivation: Most existing vision-language models and their datasets are English-centric, which limits their performance and evaluation in other languages. There is a need for both large-scale multilingual multimodal training corpora and rigorous multilingual benchmarks to fairly train and assess VLMs across languages.
AI.method: They propose a regeneration-translation pipeline: (1) regenerate existing PixMo examples with permissively licensed models to create a multilingual training corpus called Multi-PixMo, based on PixMo-Cap, PixMo-AskModelAnything, and CoSyn-400k; (2) translate several popular English VLM benchmarks (MMbench, ScienceQA, MME, POPE, AI2D) into five European languages, combining synthetic generation with manual annotation and validation; (3) evaluate data quality via human qualitative and quantitative analyses including inter-annotator agreement; and (4) run ablation studies training three different models with and without multilingual data to measure impact.
AI.result: They obtain a large multilingual multimodal training dataset (Multi-PixMo) and a set of multilingual benchmark suites for five European languages. Human assessments indicate the translated and regenerated resources are of high quality and reliable. Experimental ablations on three VLMs show that including multilingual multimodal training examples consistently improves performance on non-English benchmarks and even yields gains on English tasks compared to English-only training.
AI.conclusion: Multilingual, multimodal training data and benchmarks substantially enhance the ability of vision-language models to handle non-English languages without harming—and even improving—English performance. The proposed resources and regeneration-translation methodology help close the gap between English-centric VLM development and the need for robust multilingual capabilities.

title: HiGMem: A Hierarchical and LLM-Guided Memory System for Long-Term Conversational Agents
comment: Accepted to Findings of the Association for Computational Linguistics: ACL 2026. Camera-ready version. 10 pages, 2 figures. Code: https://github.com/ZeroLoss-Lab/HiGMem
summary: Long-term conversational large language model (LLM) agents require memory systems that can recover relevant evidence from historical interactions without overwhelming the answer stage with irrelevant context. However, existing memory systems, including hierarchical ones, still often rely solely on vector similarity for retrieval. It tends to produce bloated evidence sets: adding many superficially similar dialogue turns yields little additional recall, but lowers retrieval precision, increases answer-stage context cost, and makes retrieved memories harder to inspect and manage. To address this, we propose HiGMem (Hierarchical and LLM-Guided Memory System), a two-level event-turn memory system that allows LLMs to use event summaries as semantic anchors to predict which related turns are worth reading. This allows the model to inspect high-level event summaries first and then focus on a smaller set of potentially useful turns, providing a concise and reliable evidence set through reasoning, while avoiding the retrieval overhead that would be excessively high compared to vector retrieval. On the LoCoMo10 benchmark, HiGMem achieves the best F1 on four of five question categories and improves adversarial F1 from 0.54 to 0.78 over A-Mem, while retrieving an order of magnitude fewer turns. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/ZeroLoss-Lab/HiGMem.

AI.tldr: A new hierarchical, LLM-guided memory system (HiGMem) improves long-term conversational retrieval by first reasoning over event summaries, then selectively reading detailed turns, achieving higher accuracy with far fewer retrieved turns than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Long-term conversational agents must retrieve relevant information from large histories without flooding the model with irrelevant context. Existing memory systems mostly depend on vector similarity, which often pulls many superficially similar but unhelpful dialogue turns, harming precision, wasting context space, and making memories difficult to manage. The authors are motivated to design a memory system that keeps retrieval selective and inspectable while maintaining or improving recall, especially under adversarial or noisy conditions.
AI.method: The authors propose HiGMem, a two-level event-turn memory architecture. At the higher level, dialogue is summarized into semantic event summaries that serve as anchors. At query time, the LLM first examines these high-level event summaries to reason about which events are likely relevant. Guided by this reasoning, the system then selectively retrieves a focused subset of underlying dialogue turns related to those events, instead of pulling many turns purely by vector similarity. This LLM-guided hierarchical process aims to produce concise, high-quality evidence sets without incurring the large context and computation overhead typical of large vector-based retrieval sets.
AI.result: On the LoCoMo10 benchmark for long-term conversational memory, HiGMem achieves the best F1 scores in four out of five question categories. It notably improves adversarial F1 from 0.54 to 0.78 compared to A-Mem, a prior system, while retrieving about an order of magnitude fewer dialogue turns, demonstrating both higher effectiveness and greater efficiency in retrieval.
AI.conclusion: HiGMem shows that using LLM-guided reasoning over hierarchical event summaries can substantially improve memory retrieval for long-term conversational agents. By first filtering with semantic event anchors and then selectively inspecting related turns, the system provides concise yet reliable evidence sets, outperforming vector-similarity-based baselines both in accuracy and efficiency. This suggests that future conversational memory systems should incorporate LLM-guided, hierarchical reasoning rather than relying solely on flat similarity search.

title: ComPASS: Towards Personalized Agentic Social Support via Tool-Augmented Companionship
summary: Developing compassionate interactive systems requires agents to not only understand user emotions but also provide diverse, substantive support. While recent works explore empathetic dialogue generation, they remain limited in response form and content, struggling to satisfy diverse needs across users and contexts. To address this, we explore empowering agents with external tools to execute diverse actions. Grounded in the psychological concept of "social support", this paradigm delivers substantive, human-like companionship. Specifically, we first design a dozen user-centric tools simulating various multimedia applications, which can cover different types of social support behaviors in human-agent interaction scenarios. We then construct ComPASS-Bench, the first personalized social support benchmark for LLM-based agents, via multi-step automated synthesis and manual refinement. Based on ComPASS-Bench, we further synthesize tool use records to fine-tune the Qwen3-8B model, yielding a task-specific ComPASS-Qwen. Comprehensive evaluations across two settings reveal that while the evaluated LLMs can generate valid tool-calling requests with high success rates, significant gaps remain in final response quality. Moreover, tool-augmented responses achieve better overall performance than directly producing conversational empathy. Notably, our trained ComPASS-Qwen demonstrates substantial improvements over its base model, achieving comparable performance to several large-scale models. Our code and data are available at https://github.com/hzp3517/ComPASS.

AI.tldr: The paper builds a tool-augmented, compassionate dialogue system benchmark and model (ComPASS) for personalized social support, showing tool use improves support quality and that a fine-tuned 8B model can approach larger models.
AI.motivation: Existing empathetic dialogue systems mostly output plain text empathy and lack diverse, substantive support behaviors that users need in real interactions. They are limited in both form (only text replies) and content (weak personalization and actionability). The authors want agents that provide richer, psychology-grounded social support, mirroring human companionship and coping assistance.
AI.method: 1) Define the problem using the psychological framework of social support and translate it into human–agent interaction behaviors. 2) Design about a dozen user-centric, simulated multimedia tools (e.g., for content recommendation, reminders, activities) that cover multiple types of social support. 3) Build ComPASS-Bench, a personalized social support benchmark for LLM agents, via multi-step automatic synthesis of interaction scenarios plus manual refinement. 4) Generate tool-use trajectories on this benchmark and use them to fine-tune Qwen3-8B, creating a specialized model called ComPASS-Qwen. 5) Evaluate multiple LLMs with and without tools on ComPASS-Bench under two settings, assessing both tool-call correctness and final response quality.
AI.result: The evaluated LLMs can generally produce syntactically and semantically valid tool-call requests with high success rates, meaning they learn to invoke tools correctly. However, there is still a substantial quality gap in the final support responses, particularly in personalization and depth. Tool-augmented responses systematically outperform purely text-based empathetic replies on ComPASS-Bench. ComPASS-Qwen, the fine-tuned 8B model, significantly improves over the base Qwen3-8B and reaches performance comparable to several much larger LLMs.
AI.conclusion: Equipping LLM agents with a suite of social-support-inspired tools and training them on a dedicated benchmark leads to more effective, human-like compassionate support than relying on direct empathetic text generation alone. ComPASS-Bench offers a first, structured way to evaluate personalized social support in tool-using agents, and ComPASS-Qwen shows that even mid-sized models can, with appropriate supervision, approach the performance of large models. This paradigm highlights the value of grounded psychological frameworks and tool augmentation for building emotionally supportive AI companions.

title: ArbGraph: Conflict-Aware Evidence Arbitration for Reliable Long-Form Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) remains unreliable in long-form settings, where retrieved evidence is noisy or contradictory, making it difficult for RAG pipelines to maintain factual consistency. Existing approaches focus on retrieval expansion or verification during generation, leaving conflict resolution entangled with generation. To address this limitation, we propose ArbGraph, a framework for pre-generation evidence arbitration in long-form RAG that explicitly resolves factual conflicts. ArbGraph decomposes retrieved documents into atomic claims and organizes them into a conflict-aware evidence graph with explicit support and contradiction relations. On top of this graph, we introduce an intensity-driven iterative arbitration mechanism that propagates credibility signals through evidence interactions, enabling the system to suppress unreliable and inconsistent claims before final generation. In this way, ArbGraph separates evidence validation from text generation and provides a coherent evidence foundation for downstream long-form generation. We evaluate ArbGraph on two widely used long-form RAG benchmarks, LongFact and RAGChecker, using multiple large language model backbones. Experimental results show that ArbGraph consistently improves factual recall and information density while reducing hallucinations and sensitivity to retrieval noise. Additional analyses show that these gains are evident under conflicting or ambiguous evidence, highlighting the effectiveness of evidence-level conflict resolution for improving the reliability of long-form RAG. The implementation is publicly available at https://github.com/1212Judy/ArbGraph.

AI.tldr: ArbGraph is a pre-generation evidence arbitration framework for long-form retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) that builds a graph of atomic claims with support/contradiction links and iteratively propagates credibility to filter unreliable evidence before generation, improving factuality and robustness to noisy or conflicting retrievals.
AI.motivation: Long-form RAG often retrieves noisy, incomplete, or contradictory evidence, which leads to hallucinations and factual inconsistencies. Existing methods typically entangle conflict handling with the generation step (e.g., via verifier-augmented decoding or retrieval expansion), making it hard to explicitly resolve evidence conflicts and to ensure that the generator operates over a coherent factual basis. The authors want a principled way to validate and reconcile evidence before generation, particularly under conflicting or ambiguous sources, so that long-form answers become more reliable, dense, and faithful.
AI.method: 1) Decompose retrieved documents into atomic factual claims using an LLM or extraction module. 2) Construct a conflict-aware evidence graph where nodes are atomic claims and edges represent support or contradiction relationships between claims, possibly with intensity weights. 3) Run an intensity-driven iterative arbitration process over this graph that propagates credibility scores based on the interaction of supporting and contradicting claims, down-weighting inconsistent or weakly supported claims and up-weighting well-supported ones. 4) Use the resulting filtered and re-weighted claim set as the evidence basis for downstream long-form generation, with the generation step separated from and conditioned on the arbitrated evidence. 5) Evaluate this framework on long-form RAG benchmarks (LongFact, RAGChecker) using multiple LLM backbones.
AI.result: Across experiments on LongFact and RAGChecker with different backbone LLMs, ArbGraph yields higher factual recall and information density compared to baseline long-form RAG approaches, while also lowering hallucination rates and reducing sensitivity to retrieval noise. The framework is especially beneficial when retrieved evidence contains conflicts or ambiguity, where it better identifies and suppresses unreliable claims than methods that handle conflicts implicitly during generation.
AI.conclusion: Pre-generation, graph-based evidence arbitration via ArbGraph effectively separates evidence validation from text generation in long-form RAG. By explicitly modeling support and contradiction among atomic claims and propagating credibility before generation, ArbGraph builds a coherent evidence foundation that improves factual reliability, information richness, and robustness to noisy or conflicting retrievals. The consistent gains on standard benchmarks suggest that evidence-level conflict resolution is a promising direction for making long-form RAG more trustworthy and that ArbGraph is a practical, publicly available instantiation of this idea.

title: IceBreaker for Conversational Agents: Breaking the First-Message Barrier with Personalized Starters
summary: Conversational agents, such as ChatGPT and Doubao, have become essential daily assistants for billions of users. To further enhance engagement, these systems are evolving from passive responders to proactive companions. However, existing efforts focus on activation within ongoing dialogues, while overlooking a key real-world bottleneck. In the conversation initiation stage, users may have a vague need but no explicit query intent, creating a first-message barrier where the conversation holds before it begins. To overcome this, we introduce Conversation Starter Generation: generating personalized starters to guide users into conversation. However, unlike in-conversation stages where immediate context guides the response, initiation must operate in a cold-start moment without explicit user intent. To pioneer in this direction, we present IceBreaker that frames human ice-breaking as a two-step handshake: (i) evoke resonance via Resonance-Aware Interest Distillation from session summaries to capture trigger interests, and (ii) stimulate interaction via Interaction-Oriented Starter Generation, optimized with personalized preference alignment and a self-reinforced loop to maximize engagement. Online A/B tests on one of the world's largest conversational agent products show that IceBreaker improves user active days by +0.184% and click-through rate by +9.425%, and has been deployed in production.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes IceBreaker, a system that generates personalized conversation starters to help users begin interactions with conversational agents even when they have no clear query in mind.
AI.motivation: Many users have vague needs but no explicit question when opening a chat with an AI assistant, leading to a “first-message barrier” where conversations never start. Existing work focuses on proactive behavior only after a dialogue is underway, ignoring the initiation stage, which is crucial for increasing engagement and real usage of conversational agents.
AI.method: The authors define a new task, Conversation Starter Generation, and design IceBreaker as a two-step process: (1) Resonance-Aware Interest Distillation that mines session summaries to infer users’ latent or trigger interests; (2) Interaction-Oriented Starter Generation that produces personalized starters optimized via preference alignment and a self-reinforcement loop to improve engagement. The system is evaluated via large-scale online A/B testing on a major conversational agent platform.
AI.result: Deployed at scale, IceBreaker increases user active days by 0.184% and click-through rate on suggested starters by 9.425% in online A/B tests, demonstrating meaningful gains in user engagement and adoption.
AI.conclusion: Proactively generating personalized conversation starters at the very beginning of interaction can effectively overcome the first-message barrier for conversational agents. IceBreaker operationalizes this via interest distillation and interaction-optimized generation, and its real-world deployment and measurable engagement gains show that conversation initiation is a distinct, impactful stage worth dedicated modeling and optimization.

title: Understanding the Prompt Sensitivity
summary: Prompt sensitivity, which refers to how strongly the output of a large language model (LLM) depends on the exact wording of its input prompt, raises concerns among users about the LLM's stability and reliability. In this work, we consider LLMs as multivariate functions and perform a first-order Taylor expansion, thereby analyzing the relationship between meaning-preserving prompts, their gradients, and the log probabilities of the model's next token. We derive an upper bound on the difference between log probabilities using the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality. We show that LLMs do not internally cluster similar inputs like smaller neural networks do, but instead disperse them. This dispersing behavior leads to an excessively high upper bound on the difference of log probabilities between two meaning-preserving prompts, making it difficult to effectively reduce to 0. In our analysis, we also show which types of meaning-preserving prompt variants are more likely to introduce prompt sensitivity risks in LLMs. In addition, we demonstrate that the upper bound is strongly correlated with an existing prompt sensitivity metric, PromptSensiScore. Moreover, by analyzing the logit variance, we find that prompt templates typically exert a greater influence on logits than the questions themselves. Overall, our results provide a general interpretation for why current LLMs can be highly sensitive to prompts with the same meaning, offering crucial evidence for understanding the prompt sensitivity of LLMs. Code for experiments is available at https://github.com/ku-nlp/Understanding_the_Prompt_Sensitivity.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes why large language models (LLMs) are highly sensitive to small, meaning‑preserving changes in prompts, using a first‑order Taylor expansion view of LLMs and gradient/logit geometry, and shows this sensitivity is linked to how similar prompts are internally dispersed rather than clustered.
AI.motivation: Users and practitioners observe that LLM outputs can change drastically when prompts are rephrased while preserving meaning, raising concerns about robustness, reliability, and controllability. There is a need for a principled, mathematical explanation of this prompt sensitivity, beyond empirical metrics, to understand its mechanism and to identify which prompt variations are most risky.
AI.method: The authors treat an LLM as a multivariate function mapping token embeddings to next‑token log probabilities and apply a first‑order Taylor expansion around a prompt to relate changes in prompts to changes in logits/log probabilities via gradients. They derive an upper bound on the difference in log probabilities between two meaning‑preserving prompts using the Cauchy–Schwarz inequality, and empirically study gradient norms, upper bounds, and logit variance under different prompt variants and templates. They further compare their theoretical upper bound with an existing empirical metric, PromptSensiScore.
AI.result: They find that, unlike smaller neural networks that tend to cluster representations of semantically similar inputs, LLMs disperse such inputs in representation/gradient space. This dispersion yields large theoretical upper bounds on the difference in log probabilities for meaning‑preserving prompt variants, explaining why these differences are hard to drive close to zero. Certain kinds of meaning‑preserving changes are shown to introduce higher prompt sensitivity risk than others. The theoretically derived upper bound is highly correlated with the empirical PromptSensiScore metric. Analysis of logit variance indicates that prompt templates usually have a stronger effect on the logits than the specific questions.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs are inherently prone to high prompt sensitivity because they do not internally cluster semantically similar prompts; instead, their gradients and representations for such prompts are dispersed, which mathematically permits large changes in next‑token log probabilities. The proposed analysis framework and upper bound provide a general theoretical interpretation of prompt sensitivity, align well with existing empirical metrics, and highlight that template choices can be more impactful than question wording—offering guidance and evidence for better understanding and potentially mitigating prompt sensitivity in LLMs.

title: River-LLM: Large Language Model Seamless Exit Based on KV Share
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated exceptional performance across diverse domains but are increasingly constrained by high inference latency. Early Exit has emerged as a promising solution to accelerate inference by dynamically bypassing redundant layers. However, in decoder-only architectures, the efficiency of Early Exit is severely bottlenecked by the KV Cache Absence problem, where skipped layers fail to provide the necessary historical states for subsequent tokens. Existing solutions, such as recomputation or masking, either introduce significant latency overhead or incur severe precision loss, failing to bridge the gap between theoretical layer reduction and practical wall-clock speedup. In this paper, we propose River-LLM, a training-free framework that enables seamless token-level Early Exit. River-LLM introduces a lightweight KV-Shared Exit River that allows the backbone's missing KV cache to be naturally generated and preserved during the exit process, eliminating the need for costly recovery operations. Furthermore, we utilize state transition similarity within decoder blocks to predict cumulative KV errors and guide precise exit decisions. Extensive experiments on mathematical reasoning and code generation tasks demonstrate that River-LLM achieves 1.71 to 2.16 times of practical speedup while maintaining high generation quality.

AI.tldr: River-LLM is a training-free framework that enables efficient token-level early exit in decoder-only LLMs by resolving the KV cache absence problem, achieving up to ~2x real speedup without quality loss.
AI.motivation: Although Early Exit can theoretically speed up LLM inference by skipping redundant layers, decoder-only models suffer from the KV Cache Absence problem: skipped layers do not produce key-value states required for future tokens. Existing work either recomputes these states (large latency) or masks them (accuracy loss), so real wall-clock speedups fall short of theoretical gains. There is a need for a method that preserves efficiency and quality without retraining.
AI.method: The paper proposes River-LLM, a training-free framework that adds a lightweight KV-Shared Exit River alongside the backbone. When an early exit is taken, this river naturally generates and stores the missing KV cache entries so that subsequent tokens can still use complete historical context without recomputation. Additionally, the method exploits similarity in state transitions across decoder blocks to estimate cumulative KV errors caused by exiting early, and uses this prediction to decide when it is safe to exit for each token.
AI.result: On mathematical reasoning and code generation benchmarks, River-LLM delivers 1.71x–2.16x practical wall-clock speedup compared to standard inference while keeping generation quality high, indicating that token-level early exit can be realized effectively in decoder-only LLMs without retraining.
AI.conclusion: River-LLM overcomes the KV Cache Absence bottleneck that previously limited early exit in decoder-only LLMs. By sharing and preserving KV states through an auxiliary Exit River and guiding exits via predicted KV error, it converts theoretical layer reductions into real-world inference speedups, all in a training-free manner and with minimal impact on output quality.

title: AlphaContext: An Evolutionary Tree-based Psychometric Context Generator for Creativity Assessment
comment: Accepted by the 64th Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (ACL 2026) Main Track
summary: Creativity has become a core competence in the era of LLMs and human-AI collaboration, underpinning innovation in real-world problem solving. Crucially, the systematic improvement of creativity necessitates scientifically valid assessment instruments. Psychometric research recognizes context-based assessment as an effective way to measure creative thinking. However, high-quality expert-designed contexts remain scarce. Existing LLM-based generators often struggle with insufficient assessment cues, weak narrative coherence, limited stylistic diversity, and poor support for creative thinking. To address these challenges, we propose AlphaContext, an evolutionary tree-based psychometric context generator for creativity assessment. First, the HyperTree Outline Planner formalizes expert-designed outlining as a rule-guided hypertree and performs top-down hierarchical planning. The MCTS-based Context Generator fills the outline via MCTS to balance global structure and local quality. Then, the Evolutionary Context Optimizer evolves contexts with MAP-Elites by repeatedly updating niche elites to jointly improve diversity and quality. Finally, the Assessment-Guided Evolution Refiner simulates virtual participants with diverse styles and recycles weak contexts for further evolution. Experiments show that AlphaContext yields an average improvement of 8% over competitive methods across 6 quality metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces AlphaContext, an evolutionary tree-based system to automatically generate high-quality, diverse psychometric contexts for creativity assessment in the era of LLM-assisted human-AI collaboration.
AI.motivation: Creativity is increasingly important in LLM-augmented problem solving, but its systematic improvement requires valid, reliable assessment tools. Context-based tests are effective for measuring creative thinking, yet expert-crafted contexts are scarce and current LLM-based generators produce contexts with weak cues for assessment, poor narrative coherence, low stylistic diversity, and limited support for eliciting creativity. A method is needed that can automatically generate many high-quality, diverse, psychometrically useful contexts.
AI.method: The authors propose AlphaContext, an evolutionary, tree-based generation framework. (1) HyperTree Outline Planner: encodes expert outlining rules as a hypertree and performs top-down hierarchical planning of the context’s structure. (2) MCTS-based Context Generator: fills in the planned outline using Monte Carlo Tree Search to jointly optimize global structure and local text quality. (3) Evolutionary Context Optimizer: applies MAP-Elites evolutionary search, treating different niches (e.g., styles or types) as elites and iteratively evolving candidate contexts to improve both diversity and quality. (4) Assessment-Guided Evolution Refiner: simulates diverse virtual participants responding to the contexts; contexts that elicit weak creative responses are recycled and further evolved, making the optimization explicitly guided by their value for creativity assessment.
AI.result: In experiments, AlphaContext outperforms competitive LLM-based context generation approaches, achieving on average an 8% improvement across six different quality metrics related to context suitability, coherence, diversity, and support for creative thinking.
AI.conclusion: AlphaContext demonstrates that combining hierarchical planning, MCTS-based generation, and evolutionary optimization with assessment-guided feedback can automatically produce higher-quality, more diverse psychometric contexts for creativity assessment. This offers a scalable way to support creativity testing in human-AI collaboration settings and may serve as a foundation for more robust, data-driven creativity measurement tools.

title: StepPO: Step-Aligned Policy Optimization for Agentic Reinforcement Learning
summary: General agents have given rise to phenomenal applications such as OpenClaw and Claude Code. As these agent systems (a.k.a. Harnesses) strive for bolder goals, they demand increasingly stronger agentic capabilities from foundation Large Language Models (LLMs). Agentic Reinforcement Learning (RL) is emerging as a central post-training paradigm for empowering LLMs with these capabilities and is playing an increasingly pivotal role in agent training. Unlike single-turn token-level alignment or reasoning enhancement, as in RLHF and RLVR, Agentic RL targets multi-turn interactive settings, where the goal is to optimize core agentic capabilities such as decision making and tool use while addressing new challenges including delayed and sparse rewards, as well as long and variable context. As a result, the token-centric modeling and optimization paradigm inherited from traditional LLM RL is becoming increasingly inadequate for capturing real LLM agent behavior. In this paper, we present StepPO as a position on step-level Agentic RL. We argue that the conventional token-level Markov Decision Process (MDP) should be advanced to a step-level MDP formulation, and that the step, rather than the token, should be regarded as the proper action representation for LLM agents. We then propose step-level credit assignment as the natural optimization counterpart of this formulation, thereby aligning policy optimization and reward propagation with the granularity of agent decisions. Finally, we discuss the key systems designs required to realize step-level Agentic RL in practice and preliminary experiments provide initial evidence for the effectiveness of this perspective. We hope that the step-aligned, step-level paradigm embodied in StepPO offers the Agentic RL community a useful lens for understanding agent behavior and helps advance LLMs toward stronger general-agent capabilities.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes StepPO, a step-level reinforcement learning paradigm for training large language model (LLM) agents, arguing that agent decisions should be modeled and optimized at the step level rather than the token level.
AI.motivation: Existing RL methods for LLMs (like RLHF and RLVR) mainly focus on single-turn, token-level optimization and do not capture the multi-turn, interactive, agentic behavior required by modern LLM agents. As agent harnesses pursue more complex tasks, they face challenges such as delayed and sparse rewards and long, variable contexts, making token-centric RL formulations insufficient. There is a need for a framework that better aligns the optimization process with how LLM agents actually make decisions over multi-step interactions.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize LLM agent behavior using a step-level Markov Decision Process (MDP), where each step corresponds to a semantically meaningful agent action (e.g., a turn, a tool call, or a high-level decision) rather than individual tokens. They then formulate a step-level credit assignment mechanism that propagates rewards at the granularity of these steps, aligning policy optimization with the structure of agent decisions. They also outline required system designs for implementing step-level Agentic RL in practice, including how to represent steps, manage long contexts, and handle delayed/sparse rewards.
AI.result: Preliminary experiments demonstrate that step-level RL via StepPO can improve core agentic capabilities such as decision making and tool use in multi-turn settings, providing empirical support that step-level modeling and optimization better capture LLM agent behavior than traditional token-level approaches. The results, while initial, indicate that aligning the RL formulation with step-level actions yields more effective training signals for agentic tasks.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that treating steps, not tokens, as the fundamental actions in RL for LLM agents offers a more natural and effective framework for training general-purpose agents. StepPO’s step-level MDP and credit assignment paradigms better handle delayed and sparse rewards and long, variable contexts typical of agentic scenarios. The authors position StepPO as a conceptual and practical foundation for future work in Agentic RL and argue that adopting a step-aligned perspective can significantly advance the development of stronger, more capable general LLM agents.

title: BhashaSutra: A Task-Centric Unified Survey of Indian NLP Datasets, Corpora, and Resources
summary: India's linguistic landscape, spanning 22 scheduled languages and hundreds of marginalized dialects, has driven rapid growth in NLP datasets, benchmarks, and pretrained models. However, no dedicated survey consolidates resources developed specifically for Indian languages. Existing reviews either focus on a few high-resource languages or subsume Indian languages within broader multilingual settings, limiting coverage of low-resource and culturally diverse varieties. To address this gap, we present the first unified survey of Indian NLP resources, covering 200+ datasets, 50+ benchmarks, and 100+ models, tools, and systems across text, speech, multimodal, and culturally grounded tasks. We organize resources by linguistic phenomena, domains, and modalities; analyze trends in annotation, evaluation, and model design; and identify persistent challenges such as data sparsity, uneven language coverage, script diversity, and limited cultural and domain generalization. This survey offers a consolidated foundation for equitable, culturally grounded, and scalable NLP research in the Indian linguistic ecosystem.

AI.tldr: Survey of NLP resources for Indian languages, consolidating datasets, benchmarks, and models across modalities and tasks.
AI.motivation: There is no dedicated, unified survey focused on NLP resources specifically for Indian languages. Existing reviews cover only a few high-resource languages or treat Indian languages as part of broad multilingual settings, overlooking low-resource and culturally diverse varieties. This gap hinders a comprehensive understanding of the resource landscape and equitable progress in Indian NLP.
AI.method: Conduct a systematic survey of existing NLP resources related to Indian languages, cataloging over 200 datasets, 50 benchmarks, and 100 models, tools, and systems. Organize them by linguistic phenomena, domains, modalities (text, speech, multimodal), and culturally grounded tasks. Analyze patterns in annotation practices, evaluation methodologies, and model design choices.
AI.result: A structured inventory of Indian language NLP resources across multiple modalities and tasks, highlighting coverage for 22 scheduled languages and many marginalized dialects. Identification of patterns and gaps, such as data sparsity, uneven language coverage, script diversity, and limitations in cultural and domain generalization.
AI.conclusion: The survey provides the first unified, consolidated overview of NLP resources for Indian languages, serving as a foundational reference for future research. It underscores the need for more equitable, culturally aware, and scalable resource development, especially for low-resource and diverse linguistic varieties in India.

title: Adversarial Humanities Benchmark: Results on Stylistic Robustness in Frontier Model Safety
summary: The Adversarial Humanities Benchmark (AHB) evaluates whether model safety refusals survive a shift away from familiar harmful prompt forms. Starting from harmful tasks drawn from MLCommons AILuminate, the benchmark rewrites the same objectives through humanities-style transformations while preserving intent. This extends literature on Adversarial Poetry and Adversarial Tales from single jailbreak operators to a broader benchmark family of stylistic obfuscation and goal concealment. In the benchmark results reported here, the original attacks record 3.84% attack success rate (ASR), while transformed methods range from 36.8% to 65.0%, yielding 55.75% overall ASR across 31 frontier models. Under a European Union AI Act Code-of-Practice-inspired systemic-risk lens, Chemical, biological, radiological and nuclear (CBRN) is the highest bucket. Taken together, this lack of stylistic robustness suggests that current safety techniques suffer from weak generalization: deep understanding of 'non-maleficence' remains a central unresolved problem in frontier model safety.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the Adversarial Humanities Benchmark (AHB), showing that transforming harmful prompts into humanities-style text drastically increases jailbreak success rates on frontier models, revealing weak generalization of current safety methods.
AI.motivation: Safety systems are typically evaluated on direct, clearly harmful prompts, but real-world misuse may be couched in oblique, stylistic, or narrative forms. The authors want to know whether safety refusals remain robust when harmful intent is embedded in humanities-style transformations such as literary or narrative rephrasings. They also seek a standardized benchmark, expanding from isolated adversarial examples (poems, stories) to a systematic suite capturing stylistic obfuscation and concealed goals, to better understand systemic risks under frameworks like the EU AI Act.
AI.method: They start from harmful tasks in MLCommons AILuminate and algorithmically or manually rewrite them into humanities-style prompts—e.g., literary, narrative, or otherwise stylistically obfuscated forms—while preserving the underlying harmful objective. These transformed prompts form the Adversarial Humanities Benchmark (AHB). They then evaluate 31 frontier models on both the original and transformed prompts, measuring attack success rate (ASR): the proportion of prompts for which the model provides disallowed harmful assistance instead of refusing. They also categorize harms, including a CBRN (chemical, biological, radiological, nuclear) bucket, and interpret results through a systemic-risk lens inspired by the EU AI Act Code of Practice.
AI.result: On the original MLCommons-style harmful prompts, models show a low ASR of 3.84%, indicating strong refusal behavior on familiar attack forms. However, when prompts are transformed via humanities-style obfuscations, ASR jumps dramatically, with individual transformation methods yielding 36.8%–65.0% ASR and an overall ASR of 55.75% across 31 frontier models. Among harm categories, the CBRN bucket emerges as the highest systemic-risk area under their evaluation framework.
AI.conclusion: Current safety techniques are not robust to shifts in prompt style: they generalize poorly beyond explicit, familiar harmful patterns to humanities-style, obfuscated formulations. The high ASR under AHB indicates that safety systems often rely on surface cues rather than a deep, context-sensitive understanding of non-maleficence. This constitutes a serious open problem for frontier model safety, especially for high-risk domains such as CBRN, and motivates developing methods that can detect and refuse harmful intent even when it is stylistically concealed.

title: LQM: Linguistically Motivated Multidimensional Quality Metrics for Machine Translation
comment: Accepted to ACL 2026; resources available at https://github.com/UBC-NLP/LQM_MT
summary: Existing MT evaluation frameworks, including automatic metrics and human evaluation schemes such as Multidimensional Quality Metrics (MQM), are largely language-agnostic. However, they often fail to capture dialect- and culture-specific errors in diglossic languages (e.g., Arabic), where translation failures stem from mismatches in language variety, content coverage, and pragmatic appropriateness rather than surface form alone.We introduce LQM: Linguistically Motivated Multidimensional Quality Metrics for MT. LQM is a hierarchical error taxonomy for diagnosing MT errors through six linguistically grounded levels: sociolinguistics, pragmatics, semantics, morphosyntax, orthography, and graphetics (Figure 1). We construct a bidirectional parallel corpus of 3,850 sentences (550 per variety) spanning seven Arabic dialects (Egyptian, Emirati, Jordanian, Mauritanian, Moroccan, Palestinian, and Yemeni), derived from conversational, culturally rich content. We evaluate six LLMs in a zero-shot setting and conduct expert span-level human annotation using LQM, producing 6,113 labeled error spans across 3,495 unique erroneous sentences, along with severity-weighted quality scores. We complement this analysis with an automatic metric (spBLEU). Though validated here on Arabic, LQM is a language-agnostic framework designed to be easily applied to or adapted for other languages. LQM annotated errors data, prompts, and annotation guidelines are publicly available at https://github.com/UBC-NLP/LQM_MT.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes LQM, a linguistically grounded error taxonomy and evaluation framework that better captures dialect- and culture-specific MT errors, validated on Arabic dialects and designed to be language-agnostic.
AI.motivation: Existing MT evaluation metrics, both automatic and human (e.g., MQM), are largely language-agnostic and focus on surface-level errors, which makes them inadequate for diglossic languages like Arabic. In such settings, key failures arise from mismatches in language variety, cultural content, and pragmatic appropriateness rather than just form, so a more linguistically and socioculturally sensitive framework is needed.
AI.method: The authors design LQM, a hierarchical error taxonomy with six linguistically motivated levels: sociolinguistics, pragmatics, semantics, morphosyntax, orthography, and graphetics. They build a bidirectional parallel corpus of 3,850 sentences covering seven Arabic dialects from conversational, culturally rich sources. Six LLMs are evaluated zero-shot on MT between these varieties. Expert annotators perform span-level human error annotation with LQM, labeling 6,113 error spans in 3,495 sentences and assigning severity-weighted quality scores. They also compute an automatic metric (spBLEU) for comparison.
AI.result: The study yields a richly annotated dataset of MT errors across seven Arabic dialects, structured by the LQM taxonomy and including severity-weighted scores. It demonstrates that LQM can expose dialect- and culture-specific error types that typical, largely language-agnostic metrics may miss. spBLEU is used as a complementary automatic measure to the human LQM-based assessments.
AI.conclusion: LQM is presented as a linguistically grounded, multidimensional MT evaluation framework that effectively captures sociolinguistic and pragmatic nuances, particularly important for diglossic and dialect-rich languages like Arabic. Although instantiated and validated on Arabic, the framework is designed to be language-agnostic and easily adaptable to other languages. The authors release the LQM-annotated errors, prompts, and guidelines to facilitate further research and broader adoption.

title: MASS-RAG: Multi-Agent Synthesis Retrieval-Augmented Generation
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are widely used in retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to incorporate external knowledge at inference time. However, when retrieved contexts are noisy, incomplete, or heterogeneous, a single generation process often struggles to reconcile evidence effectively. We propose \textbf{MASS-RAG}, a multi-agent synthesis approach to retrieval-augmented generation that structures evidence processing into multiple role-specialized agents. MASS-RAG applies distinct agents for evidence summarization, evidence extraction, and reasoning over retrieved documents, and combines their outputs through a dedicated synthesis stage to produce the final answer. This design exposes multiple intermediate evidence views, allowing the model to compare and integrate complementary information before answer generation. Experiments on four benchmarks show that MASS-RAG consistently improves performance over strong RAG baselines, particularly in settings where relevant evidence is distributed across retrieved contexts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MASS-RAG, a multi-agent framework for retrieval-augmented generation that improves how LLMs use external documents, especially when evidence is scattered or noisy.
AI.motivation: Standard RAG pipelines rely on a single LLM pass to read retrieved documents and answer queries. This often fails when the retrieved evidence is noisy, incomplete, or split across multiple documents, because one monolithic generation step cannot reliably reconcile conflicting or partial information. The authors aim to make RAG more robust and accurate in these challenging retrieval scenarios.
AI.method: MASS-RAG decomposes the RAG pipeline into several role-specialized LLM agents: (1) an evidence summarization agent that condenses each retrieved document, (2) an evidence extraction agent that pulls key facts or claims, and (3) a reasoning agent that interprets and reasons over the extracted/summary information. A final synthesis agent then aggregates these intermediate views to produce the final answer. This multi-agent architecture explicitly structures how evidence is processed and combined before generation.
AI.result: Across four RAG benchmarks, MASS-RAG outperforms strong single-agent RAG baselines. The gains are most pronounced when relevant information is distributed across multiple retrieved contexts or mixed with irrelevant/noisy content, indicating better evidence integration and robustness.
AI.conclusion: Structuring RAG as a coordinated set of specialized LLM agents, rather than a single monolithic generator, leads to more effective use of retrieved evidence. MASS-RAG’s intermediate evidence views and synthesis step help reconcile heterogeneous or distributed information, improving answer quality in challenging retrieval settings.

title: Transition-Matrix Regularization for Next Dialogue Act Prediction in Counselling Conversations
summary: This paper studies how empirical dialogue-flow statistics can be incorporated into Next Dialogue Act Prediction (NDAP). A KL regularization term is proposed that aligns predicted act distributions with corpus-derived transition patterns. Evaluated on a 60-class German counselling taxonomy using 5-fold cross-validation, this improves macro-F1 by 9--42% relative depending on encoder and substantially improves dialogue-flow alignment. Cross-dataset validation on HOPE suggests that improvements transfer across languages and counselling domains. In systematic ablations across pretrained encoders and architectures, the findings indicate that transition regularization provides consistent gains and disproportionately benefits weaker baseline models. The results suggest that lightweight discourse-flow priors complement pretrained encoders, especially in fine-grained, data-sparse dialogue tasks.

AI.tldr: They improve next dialogue act prediction by regularizing model predictions toward empirically observed dialogue-flow statistics, yielding strong F1 gains, especially for weaker models, and transferring across datasets and languages.
AI.motivation: Next Dialogue Act Prediction in fine-grained, data-sparse counselling settings is difficult, and standard pretrained encoders underuse discourse-flow regularities present in dialogue corpora. The authors aim to exploit empirical dialogue-flow statistics to improve robustness and flow-consistency of dialogue act predictions, particularly when data is limited or models are weak.
AI.method: They compute empirical dialogue act transition distributions from a German counselling corpus and introduce a KL-divergence regularization term that encourages the model’s predicted act distribution for the next turn to align with these corpus-derived transition patterns. They plug this regularizer into different NDAP architectures with various pretrained encoders and perform 5-fold cross-validation on a 60-class taxonomy, plus cross-dataset validation on the HOPE dataset. Systematic ablations across encoders and architectures test how much the transition regularization contributes beyond the baselines.
AI.result: Adding the KL transition regularization significantly boosts macro-F1 by 9–42% relative to the baseline depending on the encoder, and it improves quantitative measures of dialogue-flow alignment. Cross-dataset experiments on the HOPE corpus indicate that the gains are not limited to a single language or counselling domain. Ablation studies show that the regularization consistently helps and yields especially large relative improvements for weaker baseline models.
AI.conclusion: Corpus-based discourse-flow priors, implemented via KL transition regularization, are an effective and lightweight way to improve next dialogue act prediction. They complement pretrained encoders, provide robust gains in fine-grained and data-sparse dialogue tasks, and particularly enhance the performance of weaker models while improving dialogue-flow consistency across languages and domains.

title: GSQ: Highly-Accurate Low-Precision Scalar Quantization for LLMs via Gumbel-Softmax Sampling
summary: Weight quantization has become a standard tool for efficient LLM deployment, especially for local inference, where models are now routinely served at 2-3 bits per parameter. The state of the art is currently split into two sets of methods: simple scalar quantization techniques, such as GPTQ or AWQ, which are widely deployed but plateau in accuracy at 3-4 bits per parameter (bpp), and "second-generation" vector- or trellis-quantized methods, such as QTIP, GPTVQ and AQLM, which push the accuracy frontier at low bit-widths but are notoriously hard to implement and to scale, and have gained relatively less traction. In this paper, we ask whether this gap is fundamental, or whether a carefully optimized scalar quantizer can recover most of it. We answer in the affirmative, by introducing GSQ (Gumbel-Softmax Quantization), a post-training scalar quantization method which jointly learns the per-coordinate grid assignments and the per-group scales using a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation of the discrete grid. GSQ matches the cardinality of the relaxation to the small number of levels available in the target bit-width regime (e.g., 3-8 levels for ternary and 3 bpp, respectively), making the relaxation tight and the optimization tractable. Practically, on the standard Llama-3.1-8B/70B-Instruct models, GSQ closes most of the gap between scalar quantization and the QTIP frontier at 2 and 3 bits, while using a symmetric scalar grid with group-wise quantization, and thus fully compatible with existing scalar inference kernels. We further show that GSQ scales to trillion-scale Mixture-of-Experts models such as Kimi-K2.5, where vector-quantized methods are difficult to apply.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GSQ, a Gumbel-Softmax based scalar weight quantization method that closes most of the accuracy gap to advanced vector-quantization schemes at 2–3 bits while remaining simple and kernel-compatible.
AI.motivation: Existing scalar quantization methods (e.g., GPTQ, AWQ) are simple and widely used but lose accuracy below 3–4 bits per parameter, whereas more accurate second-generation vector/trellis quantizers (e.g., QTIP, GPTVQ, AQLM) are complex and hard to deploy at scale. The authors want to know whether this accuracy vs. simplicity trade-off is fundamental, or if a more carefully optimized scalar quantizer can reach similar performance to complex methods at very low bit-widths.
AI.method: They propose GSQ (Gumbel-Softmax Quantization), a post-training scalar quantization algorithm. GSQ jointly optimizes per-weight grid assignments and per-group scaling factors using a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation of the discrete quantization grid. The relaxation is designed to have a cardinality that matches the few quantization levels allowed at low bit-widths (e.g., 3–8 levels for ternary and 3 bits), ensuring a tight relaxation and tractable optimization. GSQ uses symmetric scalar grids and group-wise quantization so it remains compatible with existing scalar inference kernels.
AI.result: On Llama-3.1-8B/70B-Instruct, GSQ narrows most of the accuracy gap between standard scalar quantizers and the QTIP state-of-the-art frontier at 2–3 bits per parameter, despite using only simple scalar grids and group-wise quantization. It also scales to trillion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts models such as Kimi-K2.5, where vector-quantized methods are difficult to apply, demonstrating good scalability and practicality.
AI.conclusion: A well-designed scalar quantizer using Gumbel-Softmax relaxation can recover most of the benefits of more complex vector/trellis quantization methods at very low bit-widths, without sacrificing compatibility with existing scalar inference infrastructures. Thus, the perceived fundamental gap between simple scalar and advanced vector quantization in the 2–3 bit regime is largely bridgeable in practice.

title: Dual Alignment Between Language Model Layers and Human Sentence Processing
summary: A recent study (Kuribayashi et al., 2025) has shown that human sentence processing behavior, typically measured on syntactically unchallenging constructions, can be effectively modeled using surprisal from early layers of large language models (LLMs). This raises the question of whether such advantages of internal layers extend to more syntactically challenging constructions, where surprisal has been reported to underestimate human cognitive effort. In this paper, we begin by exploring internal layers that better estimate human cognitive effort observed in syntactic ambiguity processing in English. Our experiments show that, in contrast to naturalistic reading, later layers better estimate such a cognitive effort, but still underestimate the human data. This dual alignment sheds light on different modes of sentence processing in humans and LMs: naturalistic reading employs a somewhat weak prediction akin to earlier layers of LMs, while syntactically challenging processing requires more fully-contextualized representations, better modeled by later layers of LMs. Motivated by these findings, we also explore several probability-update measures using shallow and deep layers of LMs, showing a complementary advantage to single-layer's surprisal in reading time modeling.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how different internal layers of large language models (LLMs) align with human sentence processing, especially in syntactically challenging cases, and proposes improved measures for modeling human reading times.
AI.motivation: Prior work showed that surprisal from early LLM layers matches human processing in simple sentences, but surprisal underestimates human effort in syntactic ambiguities. It is unknown whether internal LLM layer advantages generalize to such complex cases and how to better model human cognitive load there.
AI.method: The authors compare surprisal and related probability-based measures taken from shallow (early) versus deep (later) layers of LLMs against human reading-time data in English syntactic ambiguity experiments. They examine which layers best predict human cognitive effort and test new probability-update metrics that combine information from multiple layers.
AI.result: For syntactically unchallenging, naturalistic reading, surprisal from earlier LLM layers best predicts human behavior, replicating prior findings. However, for syntactic ambiguity processing, later layers provide a better—though still imperfect—fit to human cognitive effort. Additionally, probability-update measures that leverage both shallow and deep layers outperform single-layer surprisal in modeling reading times.
AI.conclusion: Human–LM alignment depends on processing mode: naturalistic reading resembles weaker, early-layer prediction, while syntactically demanding cases require fully contextualized, later-layer representations. Multi-layer probability-update measures offer a complementary advantage over simple surprisal and may constitute a more accurate framework for modeling human sentence processing across difficulty levels.
